      • ▸How much deforestation is your country importing through trade?
        • 1. Three-quarters of global deforestation is to meet domestic, not international demand
        • 2. The domestic demand story is particularly true for beef, the largest driver of deforestation
        • 3. Rich countries contribute substantially to deforestation through imports: mostly oilseeds, cocoa and coffee
        • 4. Countries like the UK have reduced their imported deforestation over the last few decades

  • Index by Author
CROSSCURRENTS
Week of June 22–27, 2026 · Vol. 1 · Issue 24

Notable Quotes

"So that's how you take down an oligarchy. One bite at a time. It's not fast, and it's not satisfying, at least not at first." — Matt Stoller, BIG ("BOOM: Left-Wing Populists Just Smashed Incumbents in Elections Last Night. But Can They Govern?")

"In the Land of Wars Are Fun, where we get to decide strategy for both sides and then ours always wins, this Iranian reaction was taken as justification for prompting it." — Oren Cass, Commonplace ("Cutting Bait on a Bad War")

"If zero-carbon nuclear fusion technology generating cheap electricity were invented tomorrow, this same half-century-old anti-development coalition would immediately mobilize against it. Stop Big Fusion Now!" — Michael Lind, UnHerd ("In defense of data centers")

"Britain isn't more dysfunctional than other Western democracies. It's merely more honest about it." — Joseph C. Sternberg, WSJ ("Britain's Prime Minister Problem")

"Those who teach you to glorify suffering are the very ones who caused it. The powerful never complain that life is unfair, because they created that unfairness." — Wang Wusi, China Digital Times ("Translation: How Tolerant is China's Internet?")

Themes

Iran's aftermath: who lost, and the Vance turn

The U.S.-Iran ceasefire held this week, so the argument moved off who won to who lost. Every writer reaches for the same word — humiliation — and pins it on a different culprit. That instability is the cluster's organizing shape.

Underneath the disagreement runs a convergence. From the restraint pole and the regime-critic pole alike, the read is identical: the Islamic Republic survived intact, and Washington is now the party asking for terms. The forward edge is the Vance turn — the ascendancy of the restraint faction (the camp that wants the US out of Middle East wars), doubling as a quiet 2028 launch.

  • "Humiliation" is the contested keyword, and it names four incompatible defeats. The word organizes the cluster precisely because no two writers attach it to the same thing. Luttwak pins it on American restraint — force staged in the Gulf and never used. Cass pins it on the war's premise, locating the real humiliation in starting a fight the public would not pay to win. Golkar pins it on Trump's negotiation; Kahn pins it on Netanyahu.

  • The hawk case that restraint lost the war rests on a counterfactual it never proves. Luttwak argues that paratroopers and Marines already in the Gulf could have seized islands and choked off Iran's oil exports, forcing capitulation at low risk. That limited raids would have compelled Iran is asserted, not demonstrated. Cass names exactly this move — deciding the enemy's strategy for him — as the war hawk's central fallacy, which makes the two pieces a direct rebuttal of each other.

  • Across the restraint pole and the regime-critic pole, one verdict converges: the regime survived and the US is now the supplicant. Golkar reads Tehran as running "heroic flexibility" — Khamenei's term for a tactical retreat that preserves the regime — trading a reopened strait and a reported $12 billion in unfrozen assets for nothing structural. Cass concedes the unfavorable terms are the unavoidable cost of a war that failed its own shifting objectives. Kahn catalogs the losers and quotes an Israeli editorial calling the deal a "catastrophic capitulation."

  • Regime change is dead, and the same fact reads as vindication from one pole and catastrophe from the other. Both poles agree Netanyahu was frozen out of the talks and his regime-change war collapsed. Ahmari reads this as vindication, treating Vance's line that "every nation in the region" has a right of self-defense as a measure of how far Israel's standing has slipped. Kahn reads it as catastrophe — Israel demoted from "co-pilot" to "passenger," 156 countries now recognizing Palestine, an ICC arrest warrant constraining Netanyahu's travel.

  • The Vance turn: restraint-faction ascendancy doubling as a 2028 soft-launch. Ahmari presents Vance as the self-styled "team captain of the restrainers," owning the post-war diplomacy after a hot war he privately opposed. The concrete win he touts is a new channel for Iranian Revolutionary Guard and US Central Command officers to manage disputes in Doha. Klippenstein reads the same media tour as calculated distancing from Trump — on apology, immigration tone, and Israel — built on the edge that an elected vice president "can't be fired." His closer, that Democrats should take Vance's "America First moderation" seriously, sets this beside the Democrats'-strategic-reckoning cluster, where Silver reads Newsom's 2028 retreat to the partisan base as frontrunner weakness.

  • The hawk holdouts read one post-ceasefire incident as proof negotiation is folly. McCarthy and Sheehy both treat Iran's June 26 drone strike on a Hormuz cargo ship as confirmation of a categorical claim about regime intent — but a single incident cannot establish that the regime is unappeasable, and the "events vindicate the hawks" frame is not earned. McCarthy goes further, recasting the realist view that US-Iran enmity is downstream of the Israel relationship as a shared "antisemitic" position of the progressive left and a MAGA fringe. The same publication's editors run a structurally identical move against the Mamdani insurgency, casting the left as the existential danger (NR Editors, in the left-populism theme).

  • The war's costs land hardest off-camera, in the Horn of Africa. The Somalia piece is the only one tracing the Hormuz shock onto a third-party economy: fuel up about 77%, freight from India roughly doubled, container costs from Turkey tripled as ships reroute around Africa. It also reads the war as accelerating a great-power scramble for Somalia's Bab el-Mandab coastline — the chokepoint between the Gulf of Aden and the Red Sea — with Israel's recognition of breakaway Somaliland the latest move. It flags its own uncertainty too, reporting that some analysts privately think the famine risk is overstated.

The cluster has stopped arguing about the war and started arguing about its meaning. The settled facts — the regime intact, the US accommodating, regime change abandoned — are no longer contested; only their interpretation is. Each faction narrates the same outcome as confirmation of its prior: the hawk sees humiliating restraint, the restrainer sees vindicated realism, the regime-critic sees a bad deal banked too cheaply. This continues last week's cross-orientation convergence on a giveaway deal, but the live question has migrated from Iran policy to the domestic succession the war accelerated.

That migration matters more than the who-lost argument it sits beneath. The restraint faction now owns both the post-war diplomacy and the most credible 2028 vehicle, while the hawks are reduced to reading single ship-strikes as vindication. The restrainer creed — that American force has limits and cannot remake other societies — is the foreign-policy face of the broader governance reckoning in the Western-governance theme, where the limits of state reach are arriving abroad and at home at once.

Carry-forward: The restraint faction's capture of post-war Iran policy is the thread to watch: the Vance-led camp owns the diplomacy, and the Doha deconfliction channel is the concrete test of whether it survives Netanyahu and Iran's hardliners, the two threats Ahmari names. Last week's open question about who governs the Strait of Hormuz also stays live — McCarthy and Sheehy cite the June 26 drone strike as evidence the waterway is still contested, and the next sixty days of talks will show whether Iran consolidates a recognized governance role or the deconfliction channel keeps the strait open.

↩ Back to TOC

The Mamdani sweep: the left-populist insurgency

All three congressional candidates Mayor Zohran Mamdani endorsed won the June 23 New York Democratic primary. Two unseated party-backed incumbents. The party's own leaders — Jeffries, Schumer, Hochul — delivered no one. The commentary fight is not over what happened but over what it means. Is the left-populist insurgency an asset or a liability for the Democrats? Six pieces from across the spectrum sort along that single axis.

The sharpest split runs between two writers reading the same wins in opposite valences: one as electoral poison, one as the only available answer to oligarchic power. They agree on exactly one thing — that the wins may not translate into governing substance. A second, stranger agreement crosses the left-right divide. An independent-left journalist and a post-liberal editor both read the night as the death of the establishment's power to pick its own candidates.

  • The same wins read as the party's MAGA problem or its only weapon against oligarchy — depending on whether you measure ideology or power. From the electoral pole, the sweep is the Democrats' MAGA. Smith reads the Democratic Socialists of America surge as an insurgent hard-left faction that wins on anti-Trump backlash and repels swing voters. From the power pole, Stoller reads the same wins as a genuine popular revolt against oligarchy and asks only whether the winners can govern. They share one premise — doubt the wins become substance — and split on what it means. For Smith non-delivery reassures; for Stoller it is the tragedy to avert.

  • Left and right agree on the mechanism: the establishment has lost its power to "decide" who its candidates are. Klippenstein reads the night as ordinary voters reaching past party gatekeepers, not as a win for the left or the DSA. He anchors it in George Washington's Farewell Address warning that the "spirit of party" enfeebles self-government. Shullenberger reaches it from the right: 2026 is the Democrats' 2016, the moment the rule that party elites pick the nominees finally fails for them as it failed for Republicans under Trump. One welcomes the erosion as democracy reviving; the other indicts both parties as captured. The valences invert; the diagnosis is identical.

  • Not all of the insurgency travels: material "sewer socialism" wins durably; the purist, concession-averse strain courts the backlash that sank the post-2018 left. Goldberg draws the line inside the win itself. Mamdani's materially-focused "sewer socialism" — early-1900s nuts-and-bolts municipal delivery like transit and rents — builds a durable coalition. The academic-left strain does not: a prison abolitionist who would not say whether murderers should be jailed invites the centrist backlash that collapsed the post-2018 DSA wave. The danger is a movement flush with success deciding it no longer has to choose between the two.

  • The establishment right inverts the anti-oligarchy case: left-populism is more dangerous than right-populism because it aims to destroy the order, not restore it. NR Editors argue right-populism's appeal to preserving the existing order sets outer limits on its excesses, while left-populism aims at the order's destruction and forms "alliances of convenience with Islamists." Their causal account is sociological: a downwardly-mobile, educated-but-underemployed cohort among whom antisemitism recurs as scapegoat. That last move — treating antisemitism as the thread linking the progressive left to a foreign or fringe menace — is the rhetorical structure McCarthy applies in the Iran cluster (Theme 01) to bind Khomeinism to "Death to America."

All six pieces now concede the same two facts: the sweep happened, and the establishment can no longer gate its own primaries. Nobody this week argues the insurgency is a mirage. The live argument has moved to consequences — and it splits cleanly by what each writer measures. Count ideology and swing voters, and the sweep reads as liability. Count power and governing capacity, and it reads as the only available answer.

The implication no single piece states: whether the sweep is an asset or a liability and whether the winners can actually govern are one question asked from two ends. Govern like Mamdani — cheap transit, frozen rents, visible delivery — and the swing-voter alarm quiets while the governing-capacity worry is answered at once. Govern like the purist strain, and both fears are confirmed at once: the swing voters flee, and the oligarchy the insurgents ran against keeps winning anyway. The variable that decides between those outcomes — whether the cohort builds governing competence — is exactly what the argument over asset or liability brackets out. Whether Democrats should embrace, channel, or resist the insurgency is the next question, taken up in Theme 03 by Yglesias and Silver.

Carry-forward: the project's running finding that the donor class's candidate-gating machinery is decaying inside both parties took its clearest Democratic-side confirmation this week. Last cycle the establishment's substitute lever — coordinated press revelations timed to a Maine vacancy window — failed to stop Graham Platner; this week party leaders backing a full New York slate delivered no one, while every Mamdani endorsement carried. What remains untested is the governance step: whether the cohort that beat the gatekeepers can build the legislative competence to convert primary wins into delivered policy. If the New York winners govern materially and durably, the decay is a realignment; if they cannot, the establishment's electability case rebuilds itself.

↩ Back to TOC

The Democrats' strategic reckoning

This cluster is the argument beneath the week's New York primary win — not what the Mamdani sweep means, but what Democrats should do about it. Five pieces, four of them by Yglesias, converge on one diagnosis: Democratic strategy has stopped being about persuasion.

The shared claim is structural, not moral. As contestable statewide races disappeared, persuading a swing voter became a niche skill and keeping the party's own factions happy became the main event. What looks like a character problem — uninspiring frontrunners, a party at war with itself — reads better as the predictable output of a politics that no longer has to move anyone outside its tent.

  • The vanished swing voter, not weak candidates, is the root diagnosis. Once New York could elect a Republican governor, ambitious Democrats reflexively courted persuadable voters who don't follow politics. Yglesias's Schumer-internship memoir recovers the craft: small winnable fights and a problem-solving posture that keeps ideology off the sleeve. As crossover seats vanished, persuasion turned niche and faction-management became the only skill that mattered, producing figures like Newsom and Harris who never won a contested race. Silver makes the same point about Newsom's thin electability case — California is not a swing state, and his margins there underwhelm.

  • Both populisms sell the same fantasy: remove the corrupt and the tradeoffs vanish. Yglesias draws on "stealth democracy" — the public's instinct that political conflict itself signals bad actors, since people of goodwill should just be able to fix things. If conflict is illegitimate, hard tradeoffs must be someone's fault, and electing good people should make them disappear. That premise, he argues, makes the insurgent left's "remove corrupt elites and the free stuff appears" the mirror image of MAGA's "deport immigrants and balance the budget" — unlike real Nordic social democracy, which runs on explicit tradeoffs.

  • Newsom's embrace of an unpopular Biden is a costly signal to the party's biggest faction. Silver splits the party into three factions: the left, the abundance liberals (the build-more, supply-side wing), and the largest group, the "#Resistance Libs." Those voters — older, college-educated, partisan more than ideological — believe Democrats lost in 2024 only by not fighting hard enough. The taxonomy is the connective tissue to the week's Mamdani sweep: the left faction just won New York, and Newsom is courting the bigger one. Praising a president at 30% favorability is expensive, which is exactly what makes it a loyalty signal — it locks down that faction's floor in a primary that rewards faction-management over broad appeal. Silver calls the retreat a sign of weakness, not strength.

  • The party blames the frontman to avoid repudiating the program. Yglesias, in a podcast with Jerusalem Demsas, argues the "everyone's annoyed at Joe and Jill" consensus is cope: it lets progressives condemn the man while keeping the left-wing economics intact. His sharper charge is about how the program was built — Biden won the 2020 primary as the Obama-continuity moderate, then handed economic and regulatory policy to the Warren wing, a "brain transplant" under cover of continuity. "Bidenism," on this reading, was a faction-satisfaction product — assembled to keep the coalition's wings content rather than to win an argument with the median voter.

The cluster turns on a move Yglesias makes against himself. The popularist case has always been majoritarian — give voters what they say they want. But the midterm essay arrives at the opposite conclusion about the party's own electorate: "democracy should exist between the parties rather than within them." The activist primary voter, on this logic, is the mechanism that keeps selecting faction-pleasers over persuaders, so the fix is to route around that voter — win red-leaning general elections, not safe-seat primary fights.

That tension maps onto the week's other Democratic story. The Mamdani sweep is read, in the left-populist insurgency cluster, as the healthy collapse of donor-class gatekeeping — voters, not insiders, deciding primaries. This cluster reads the same collapse from the other side: once the gate is gone and the primary electorate is the activist base, the candidates it produces have rarely had to persuade anyone who could vote against them. Both sides agree the establishment lost control; they disagree on whether what replaces it can win outside the tent.

Carry-forward: the running donor-class-enforcement-decay question — whether a party that can no longer gate its primaries can still field candidates who win general elections — gets two data points this week. Newsom's retreat to the #Resistance-Lib floor, and the diagnosis that the party has lost the craft of swing-voter persuasion, both bet that the post-gatekeeping primary electorate rewards faction loyalty over crossover appeal. The November red-leaning midterm results, and the Maine Senate race in particular, are the near-term test of whether that bet is right.

↩ Back to TOC

Data centers, the grid, and energy politics

The data-center backlash has become a top-tier energy-politics fight running on two separate rails this week. One rail is American electricity bills. The other is European carbon and water. What is new is where the demand now originates.

On the US rail, the grievance is concrete: who pays for the grid the AI build-out requires. The call for a national moratorium now comes from inside senior energy policymaking, not only from activists. On the European rail, AI is named directly as a climate antagonist during a record June heatwave. Two reframes cut across both rails. One holds that the backlash is really hostility to industrial development as such, dressed in shifting local rationales. The other holds that the binding constraint is the queue to connect power to the grid, not any shortage of power.

  • The backlash runs on two rails: US electricity bills and European carbon and water. On the American side the fight is cost allocation, and a senior House Energy and Commerce Democrat now demands a national moratorium until air, water, and power-bill harms are prevented (Zeitlin). On the European side, during a record heatwave, national outlets name AI directly as the climate villain — Mediapart calls it an "ecological ogre" being prioritized over the climate, and El País flags its thirst for energy and water (Euro|topics). The same build-out generates two grievances that rarely meet.

  • Lind's "astroturf" reframe fails on both the cost evidence and the geography. The split is over whether the backlash tracks real harm or signals identity. From one pole, Lind reads it as urban-progressive virtue-signaling and "more astroturf than grass-roots," resting on a Gallup inversion where opposition peaks among urban Democratic women and bottoms out among the rural Republican men nearest the sites (Lind). The evidence cuts the other way twice. On cost: analysts find little national price effect, but New Jersey's PJM grid is the real exception, where the market monitor pinned $23 billion in added capacity-auction costs on data-center load (Zeitlin). On geography: the documented rejections are red and rural — farm owners and county boards in Indiana, Iowa, and Ohio (Holzman).

  • The farmland argument is a weapon migrating from the anti-renewables fight, and may boomerang onto clean power. Farmland protection is becoming the data-center fight's national watchword, the same argument that slowed solar and wind (Holzman). The twist is who wields it: the anti-monopolist left as much as the right. The spillover risk is concrete — Eau Claire County, Wisconsin just adopted a "farmland preservation plan" discouraging utility-scale solar and data centers in one move. Food and Water Watch calls that overlap "a coincidence"; the bundled restriction sits against the denial.

  • The market-design "fix" relocates the distributional fight; it doesn't dissolve it. Reframing the whole dispute, Tabarrok argues the binding constraint is the queue to connect generation to the grid, not a generation shortage — fixable by auctioning connection rights instead of first-come queuing, plus flexible interconnection for loads that can power down during the few annual peak hours (Tabarrok). The auction is efficient and sidesteps the moratorium. But it quietly answers the who-pays question in favor of the hyperscalers — the largest cloud operators — because a connection auction routes scarce grid access to the deepest pockets. That is the same cost-allocation choice the ratepayer rail is fighting over, recast as engineering.

  • Same diagnosis, opposite prescriptions: harness the animus or defeat it. Lind and Yglesias agree the loudest complaints — water use, consumer electricity prices — are overblown or narrow, leaving a small legitimate core (Lind; Yglesias). They split on what to do with the public's dislike. Lind wants to break the anti-development coalition. Yglesias wants to ride it: a data-center carbon tax pegged to the global social cost of carbon — the worldwide damage per ton emitted — passable precisely because voters hate both carbon pricing and data centers. The ownership-side version of the same build-out runs through Bernie Sanders's plan to tax AI equity into public hands (Bruenig, Theme 06).

The two rails almost never touch. The American argument is about who pays for the grid the build-out needs. The European argument is about a planetary externality — carbon and water during a heatwave. Only the carbon-tax proposal bridges them, and it does so by exploiting the first rail to fund the second: it harnesses Americans' dislike of data centers to price the warming their power consumption imposes on the global poor.

Underneath both rails is a supply-side fact the cluster does not name. The demand-side surge in compute is hitting a grid and an electric-technology base — batteries, power electronics, transmission gear — where China, not the US, now sets the pace (Theme 09, Smith). The fight over who pays to connect the next gigawatt is, one layer down, a fight over an industrial base the US is no longer leading. The interconnection queue Tabarrok wants to auction is downstream of whether America can build the hardware to clear it at all.

Carry-forward: the datacenter-buildout-resistance thread (local resistance as the binding constraint on the US AI build-out) takes a new turn — the national-moratorium demand now comes from a senior House energy-policy Democrat, not only red-state Republicans or activists, which makes the platform genuinely bipartisan; what to watch is whether the PJM $23-billion grievance becomes the template other regional grids cite. Tabarrok's connection-auction proposal is a fresh instance of distributional-choice-laundered-as-technical (a contested who-pays choice dressed as market design): an auction routes grid access to the deepest pockets while presenting itself as neutral efficiency, so what to watch is whether any actual interconnection-reform rule prices that distributional effect or hides it.

↩ Back to TOC

America at 250: the Founding, citizenship, and the courts

America's 250th birthday arrived in the same week as two 6–3 Supreme Court immigration wins and a new federal rule on who may claim a migrant child. Commentators treated all three as one question: who belongs to the American polity, and on what principle. The anniversary set the stakes that the rulings then tested.

The sharpest division runs inside the right, not across the aisle. Two writers in the same outlet defend a members-only citizenship from founding principle — by opposite routes. Alongside that principle fight, the Court's two immigration wins get defended as plain-text statutory reading rather than policy hardball.

  • The right's case for bounded citizenship splits over its ground: shared creed or inherited trait. Both writers want a members-only polity. They disagree on what makes someone a member. From one pole, Anton grounds citizenship in the Founding's claim that all men hold equal natural rights. That premise, he argues, makes the political compact particular and invitation-only, and it leads him to reject the Right's "blood and soil" as a category error. From the other, Upham reclaims "blood, soil, creed" — descent (jus sanguinis), birthplace (jus soli), and religion — as the legitimate criteria a polity uses to define who belongs.

  • A Reconstruction-era theory of membership becomes a present-day argument against racial preferences. Upham relocates the Constitution's anti-discrimination guarantee from the Equal Protection Clause to the Fourteenth Amendment's Privileges or Immunities Clause — the guarantee of a citizen's basic legal standing. His reading: blood, soil, and creed may legitimately define who is a citizen, but once inside, any burden tied to them is a "badge of alienage." From that he infers that racial preferences such as affirmative action are unconstitutional. The same categories that bound the polity at its edge become injustice when applied among its members.

  • The Court's two immigration wins read as plain-text statutory application, not policy hardball. McLaughlin and Rubenfeld converge that two 6–3 rulings turn on textualism — reading a statute by its enacted words. In the asylum case, a migrant turned back in Mexico has not "arrived in" the country, so the statutory hearing right never triggers. In the Temporary Protected Status case — a humanitarian status for nationals of designated countries — a statutory bar on judicial review means courts may not review. The majority fractured rightward: Justice Thomas would have stripped equal-protection scrutiny from immigration entirely, which the majority declined. No roster writer mounted a developed left-legal rebuttal this week.

  • The membership fight, rendered as administrative procedure: who may claim a migrant child. Fahlberg's original reporting shows the principle turning into paperwork. A forthcoming Department of Health and Human Services (HHS) rule would tighten sponsor vetting for unaccompanied migrant children. It narrows acceptable identity proof to 13 fixed documents, ends background-check waivers, and adds physical checks for gang tattoos. The trigger is a run of fraud-and-abuse cases, including a "super sponsor" who filed multiple false claims. HHS concedes the cost — genuine relatives will find it harder too — and argues the fraud risk is worth it.

All three strands answer the question the anniversary forces: on what basis is membership drawn, and who decides. The principle fight supplies the grounds, the rulings supply the method, and the rule supplies the paperwork. The connective tissue is the Court itself. The same Roberts majority that left-leaning analysts have been reading as selectively applying neutral rules, the right this week reads as finally applying written law plainly — one Court, opposite diagnoses by pole.

That the diagnosis cuts both ways is what separates the textualist claim from motivated reasoning. The pending birthright-citizenship case, Trump v. Barbara, turns on jus soli — citizenship by birthplace. McLaughlin, who is defending this week's wins, still predicts the administration loses it, because the Fourteenth Amendment's plain words run against abolishing birthright citizenship. Textualism is a method, not a team: when the enacted words favor the administration it wins, and when they do not, the same method says it loses.

Carry-forward: the project's running question about whether the Roberts Court applies rules even-handedly or selectively now has a conservative-legal mirror to set against the left's selective-application reading — the same rulings the left reads as selective, the right reads as plain textualism. Watch whether more right-of-center legal writers adopt the plain-textualism framing. The cleaner test is the birthright ruling: when it lands, does it vindicate McLaughlin's prediction that the same method which delivered this week's wins costs the administration that case?

↩ Back to TOC

AI: ownership, regulation, and the liberal order

This cluster asks who governs AI, not what the next model can do. Three answers compete this week, and none of them is "the market." One camp wants the public to own a stake in the frontier labs outright. A second holds that the state already gates frontier models, or soon will. A third says standing back is itself a sweeping act of governance — and the wrong kind.

The organizing shape is a convergence across opposite traditions. A socialist case for public equity and a classical-liberal critique of laissez-faire reach the same conclusion: the state must shape AI, not stand back. The two poles agree on almost nothing except that the hands-off option is no longer available.

  • Public ownership of AI need not mean the government buying companies — a one-time tax paid in stock does it. Sanders would tax AI providers half their stock into public hands, turning Anthropic, OpenAI, and a Gemini spin-off into part-public firms (Bruenig). The reframe that does the work: this is not the state buying companies but a one-time tax payable in stock — roughly $1 trillion transferred — producing ordinary partial state-owned enterprises on the model of Norway's Equinor. The argued advantage is real-time control: an equity stake governs a fast-moving frontier sector faster and at finer grain than statute or rulemaking can.

  • "AI is already regulated" is asserted as present fact, but it describes an anticipated review regime, not current US law. Frontier models above certain capability or compute thresholds will inevitably face government review before release — so "we now have de facto AI regulation," argues Box CEO Aaron Levie. Cowen amplifies the claim and adds that he is glad AI progressed "under the wire" before any tightening. The premise is asserted as present fact, yet current US law gates compute and chip exports, not model releases. The strategic map that follows — US gatekeeping leverage, a slower release cadence, open-weight models (those released for anyone to run) as relative winners — rides on a review regime that is anticipated, not enacted.

  • Laissez-faire toward an industry bidding for world government is itself a top-down project to remake the world. The contradiction sits inside classical liberalism itself (Shullenberger). Its leading builders, following Nick Bostrom, are racing toward a "singleton" — a single global decision-making agency — as the implicit prize of an AI arms race, while conceding (Musk, not just "woke" labs) that the result is a "permanent underclass" living on universal basic income. Standing back from that is not neutrality; it enables exactly the technocratic world-government the creed exists to oppose. AEI convening a Council on AI Ethics — a free-market stalwart turning to bioethics — reads as a symptom of the right's split between AI accelerationists and social conservatives.

  • A socialist and a classical-liberal critic converge: the state must shape AI, not stand back. Two opposite political traditions reach one conclusion. From the socialist side, Bruenig wants the public to own half the frontier labs outright. From a free-market outlet, Shullenberger argues that standing back is itself the most sweeping intervention imaginable. Opposite vocabularies, one finding: AI is not a normal competitive market the state can safely leave alone. What remains in dispute is the form of state involvement, not whether there will be one.

  • AI auto-writing social-science papers is mostly false today — the live question is how soon it won't be. A translated Chinese hype post claims a Stanford tool turns a raw dataset into a complete, publication-ready paper with reproducible code in 30 minutes (Cowen). Cowen's literal verdict is the operative content: "Mostly that is not true," perhaps an attempt to demoralize. But his follow-up is the load the bullet carries — "will it never ever be true? In two years be true?" If AI can author empirical research on that horizon, none of the ownership and regulation fights above are premature.

The four pieces map a spectrum, but the spectrum has narrowed. A decade ago the AI-governance question was whether the state should be involved at all. This week even the libertarian-adjacent pole concedes that it will be: Cowen is not arguing against pre-release review, only glad it arrived late. Once the free-market side's position is "I'm relieved we got the models out before the regulation," the market-discipline answer has quietly left the table. What is left is a fight over instruments — equity, pre-release review, or recognizing laissez-faire as itself a state project.

Two of those instruments are "make AI pay society" proposals in different clothes. Bruenig's stock tax extracts ownership; in the data centers, the grid, and energy politics theme, a single-sector carbon tax on data centers (Yglesias) extracts an externality price instead. That theme's backlash over the grid and where data centers get built is the same build-out's physical side, where the public is already refusing to host the compute these labs run on. The ownership fight and the energy fight are two faces of one question: on what terms does society let the build-out proceed?

Carry-forward: the AI thread's shift from capability to control sharpens this week into a near-consensus that markets will not govern AI alone — socialist, classical-liberal, and libertarian poles now argue over the form of state involvement rather than its necessity. What would test this: whether any single instrument materializes first — a Sanders-style equity tax clearing committee, a formal pre-release model-review rule replacing today's compute-and-chip export gates, or a data-center moratorium or carbon tax pricing the build-out. The convergence holds only until the market-discipline position finds a serious new defender, and this week it had none.

↩ Back to TOC

The West can't govern itself: Britain, Europe, state capacity

Two events frame the week's Britain cluster: the tenth anniversary of the 2016 Brexit vote, and Keir Starmer's resignation as prime minister. The pieces converge from opposite poles on one diagnosis. The Western state has been hollowed out and no longer answers to whoever nominally controls it. Britain is the test case; several writers argue the condition is West-wide.

The convergence is the cluster's organizing finding: three writers, three orientations, one verdict that the machinery of government cannot be steered. The disagreement is only about what does the hollowing. From one pole, an external force — the EU's gravitational pull on a mid-sized economy that left it. From the other, an internal one — a permanent administrative state, or a "para-state" of courts and agencies, that outlasts every elected government. The lone counter-model on offer, Hungary, is itself collapsing as this is written.

  • Three orientations, one diagnosis: the state won't respond to its nominal controllers. From the post-liberal Leave pole, Harrington names it directly — since Cameron every PM hits the same wall, "the machinery of government won't be steered," so voters keep swapping the steering wheel while the machine trundles on. From the constitutionalist Remain pole, Sumption locates the unsteerable force outside Britain, in the EU's gravitational dominance. From the French left-realist pole, Aurelien calls the condition "Policy Management" — nearly all effort goes to procedure, almost none to substance. Sternberg universalizes it: not unique British dysfunction, just Britain being "more honest about it."

  • A Leaver and a Remainer in the same magazine reach the same verdict: Brexit didn't "take back control." Sumption and Harrington published in UnHerd the same week, on the same anniversary, having voted opposite ways in 2016 — yet both conclude the slogan failed. They differ only on why. For Sumption, "Take Back Control" rested on a "primitive" view of sovereignty: the EU takes ~40% of UK exports while the UK takes ~10% of the EU's, so Britain must follow EU standards either way, now with no vote in writing them. For Harrington, leaving only sped up the churn of prime ministers without restoring the state's capacity for "joined-up policy" — coordinated, cross-department governance.

  • The mechanism others only gesture at has a name — a "para-state" of courts and agencies outside elected control. Blair transferred power to a "para-state" of independent courts, the Human Rights Act, regulators, quasi-governmental "Quangos" (arm's-length public bodies), and an independent Bank of England (Pinkoski). So a social-democratic revival on behalf of Leave voters was structurally impossible whoever won the vote. The showcase is the 2019 Miller ruling, in which the Supreme Court struck down the government's suspension of Parliament. This is the concrete machinery beneath Harrington's "unsteerable machine" and Aurelien's "Policy Management." Pinkoski's other half — that Brexit revived ethnic self-identification over civic identity — links to this week's citizenship cluster in America at 250 (Upham).

  • Britain's failed-PM parade is the visible edge of West-wide ungovernability, not British exceptionalism. Six PMs in a decade reads as honesty, not unique dysfunction (Sternberg): the parliamentary system is a "ready vehicle" for mid-term leadership change, so the instability other democracies hide shows on the surface. The underlying tensions are shared — cultural rifts layered on socioeconomic splits, mass immigration, a growth-strangling welfare state, worsening dependency ratios (more retirees per worker). France is paralyzed; the AfD leads German polls. This extends the project's running finding that Europe's legacy-center parties are losing to insurgents built on the charge that the managerial class can't solve voters' problems.

  • The diagnoses bottom out in conceptual blindness: states can't anticipate what their worldview rules out. Beneath the cluster's "what" sits a "why" (Aurelien): governments are chronically "surprised" because "to anticipate something, you must believe it is possible," and a "plodding, materialist, reductive conception of politics" cannot grasp religion, nationalism, or how China, Russia, and Iran actually reason. "Policy Management" — procedure crowding out substance — is the symptom; conceptual blindness is the cause. His launching example is what he characterizes as the failed US/Israeli campaign against Iran, offered as illustration, not the argument's subject.

  • The lone counter-model, Hungary, is collapsing — and it lost on competence, not on too little state power. Dreher, leaving Budapest after four years, offers Hungary as the state that did fight back against left-captured institutions — the move he says Trump and DeSantis copied. But Orbán just lost: the incoming Tisza government is purging Fidesz institutions and reversing course. Dreher's own evidence undercuts the model — Fidesz fell on a stalled economy and cronyism, and lavish pronatalism (5–6% of GDP) raised fertility only briefly. Note the genre: the persuasion runs on four-years-in-Budapest firsthand texture more than on the argument it advances.

Look at what each diagnosis actually locates as the failure. Not a shortage of authority — a shortage of durability. The hollowed state can still act; what it cannot do is make a decision stick, sustain it, or correct it. This restates last week's Europe finding: the binding constraint on Cummings's Whitehall and Macron's treaty-binding was never constitutional permission to decide, but the capacity to make the decision last.

The cluster's deepest tension is self-cancelling. Every writer who diagnoses the hollow state then prescribes a cure that needs the very capacity they say is gone — counter-revolution, rejoining on EU terms, restoring substance over procedure. Harrington says the quiet part out loud: she calls for a counter-revolution while doubting Britain "even retains the state capacity" to mount one. Dreher's Hungary was the one place a state did reassert control, and it lost the next election on everyday economic competence. The remedy and the disease turn out to be the same organ.

Carry-forward: the european-center-exhaustion thread now has a state-capacity corollary: the insurgent vehicles winning across Britain, France, and Germany inherit the same unsteerable machine, so winning power and wielding it are separate problems. Hungary is the test — the one insurgent that actually governed, then lost on competence, suggests the machine defeats challengers from inside as reliably as it defeats incumbents. Watch whether any incoming European government — Andy Burnham's likely Labour succession, or post-Orbán Tisza — demonstrates the "joined-up" governing capacity the cluster says has atrophied, or merely resets the churn.

↩ Back to TOC

Who controls reality: knowledge, memory, and the therapeutic

Six pieces converge on one anxiety: how a shared sense of reality gets manufactured, captured, or distorted. The natural lens is the claim that Americans no longer disagree mainly about facts. They disagree about what the agreed facts mean.

Williams calls this interpretive polarization — rival mental maps that filter the same events through incompatible stories of salience and blame. The other five pieces are instances of the same problem in specific domains: therapeutic culture, recovered-memory therapy, podcasting, and Wikipedia's governance. Only one offers a remedy rather than a diagnosis — Alexander's case for handing a contested judgment to a crowd forecast no single gatekeeper controls.

  • The technique manufactures what it claims to find. Two unrelated domains share one structural move. Mental-health awareness campaigns are iatrogenic — they cause the distress they treat — with a 2025 trial showing ADHD materials make healthy people believe they have the disorder (Frawley). Recovered-memory therapy implants the abuse it claims to surface, matching Loftus's false-memory paradigm that makes roughly 15% of subjects "remember" invented events (deBoer). The diagnostic tool produces its own object.
  • Self-sealing belief is the shared engine. The interpretations that survive are the ones built to resist disproof. The most resilient stereotypes absorb confirming evidence and screen out the rest, because they can't be falsified (Williams). Recovered memory runs the same "meme logic" — remembering proves abuse, not-remembering proves repression (deBoer). Non-falsifiability isn't a side effect; it's what lets a folk theory spread and outlast correction.
  • Knowledge institutions get captured by the gatekeepers who run them. Wikipedia never adopted a community charter, so roughly 400 mostly anonymous admins interpret vague rules with little accountability — a governance vacuum that, on this telling, turned a neutrality project into a narrow-establishment mouthpiece (Sanger). The case leans on his co-founder standing as its main warrant. It converges with Williams's pessimism on remedies: an objective knowledge institution works only if trusted, and it can itself be captured.
  • Conspiracism is the algorithm's default setting. The podcast "superstructure" replaces adversarial interviews with genial conversations, where pressing a guest to substantiate a claim reads as rudeness — rewarding a baseline assumption that everything is staged and a "they" is always scheming (Tracey). The Epstein-files frenzy is the case study, from a Cabinet official floating blackmail theories unchallenged to a "game-theoretical" claim that Epstein is still alive. Tracey concedes mid-essay that the medium is content-neutral, which narrows his own monocausal title.
  • The lone proposed fix: a check no single gatekeeper controls. Against five diagnoses, one remedy. Alexander treats a crowdsourced forecasting index as a partly-trustless test of whether his own alarm about US democracy is just bias — trustless meaning it doesn't depend on trusting any one authority (Alexander). The fix doesn't escape the cluster's problem: whoever picks the 153 questions reintroduces the expert-judgment bias the design meant to remove. That loops straight back to the capture finding.

Across the cluster, a reflexive pattern: the genre keeps reproducing the epistemic problem it diagnoses. Tracey writes a monocausal "podcasts ruined America" essay, then concedes the medium is neutral and the real target is an algorithmic layer. Williams diagnoses interpretive capture as a symmetrical, both-sides failure, then breaks his own symmetry by locating the cause in the left's institutional dominance. Sanger indicts gatekeeping while resting his case on the gatekeeper-like authority of being a co-founder.

Only Alexander steps outside the loop, and only by distrusting himself first. His remedy works because it offloads the judgment to a mechanism designed to resist the bias it checks — and even he concedes the mechanism smuggles the bias back in at the question-selection stage. The pattern across all six: naming a distorted reality is itself an interpretive act, and most of these writers can't perform it without enacting the vice they name.

↩ Back to TOC

The industrial map: deglobalization, tariffs, and the China challenge

This cluster reads geopolitics from the factory floor. The shared premise: as globalized markets retreat, the real currency of power is industrial capacity — who makes the physical goods and controls the supply chains that build them. Three pieces map that contest; a fourth turns to the human cost beneath the state that is winning it.

The three industrial-strategy pieces converge on one diagnosis and split on one prescription. China is out-building the West across the technologies that decide the next era, and the Western reply, where it exists, is tariffs without the factories the tariffs were meant to protect. The open split is whether national self-sufficiency is a working hedge or a thesis running ahead of its results.

  • China is winning the race that builds the physical world, even as the US keeps its lead in AI. The contested resource is the "electric stack" — batteries, permanent-magnet motors, and power electronics — now converging into one supply chain that builds EVs, drones, and robots. China makes most of the world's batteries, magnets, motors, drones, and EVs; the US is an also-ran kept alive by Tesla Smith. The claim that bites: AI mastery won't deliver the physical world — a data center full of geniuses can't build a drone without the stack. This is the supply side of an energy-industrial story whose demand side runs through Theme 04 (Data centers, the grid, and energy politics), where AI compute drives the electricity buildout Pallone.

  • Tariffs without the factories are "a wall around an empty lot." Tariffs are only part one; the part two that deploys the revenue into rebuilding the industrial base never arrived Lynn. The test case is commercial aerospace: COMAC, China's state aircraft maker founded in 2008, is breaking the Boeing-Airbus duopoly with a narrow-body jet already in domestic service and a captive market of hundreds of millions. The underrated edge isn't efficiency — state economies waste capital — but the patience to fund a 20-year plan in a capital-intensive industry while Boeing wobbles after the 737 MAX and the US has no next-generation jet program.

  • National refining capacity is offered as a deglobalization hedge, but the "vindication" claim outruns its evidence. Mexico's post-2018 push to refine its own fuel — buying a Texas refinery for Pemex, the state oil company, and building a new one — lifted refinery throughput 44% and cut US gasoline imports, framed as proof that the Strait-of-Hormuz price shock vindicated betting against globalized oil markets Díaz del Castillo. The evidence doesn't carry the weight: President Sheinbaum still needed a fiscal intervention to hold prices down, and the case answers the opposition's cost-overrun objections by labeling them a neoliberal worldview rather than rebutting them.

  • The state out-building the West is one its own citizens describe as "tolerant" of unaccountable elites. Industrial capacity measures what a country can make, not whether its system serves the people inside it. A dissident essay inverts former Global Times editor Hu Xijin's lament about declining online "tolerance": China in fact leads the world in tolerance — of lead-poisoned children, contaminated infant formula, and occupants burning in malfunctioning electric cars, while bereaved families stay "emotionally stable" and the responsible firms never apologize Wang. The same electrified economy that makes China the supply-chain winner is one whose production costs — in safety, in accountability — are absorbed silently by its citizens.

Set side by side, the cluster's three industrial pieces agree on the diagnosis and overreach on the cure. The West can't build — tariffs without factories, an aircraft champion wobbling, a refining base playing catch-up — is the strong, convergent finding. That self-sufficiency works is the weaker one: Lynn's four-point program is a wish list, and the Mexico case needed a price subsidy the "vindication" framing elides.

The CDT piece is the cluster's necessary corrective. Reading power off industrial capacity alone treats "who makes the most batteries" as the whole scoreboard. The electric cars in Smith's winning stack are the same cars Chinese citizens are, in Wang's account, burning to death inside — a reminder that capacity and a system worth emulating are different measurements.

Carry-forward: the open question of which axis best measures US-China power — industrial and AI-deployment capacity, financial-system insulation, or military strength — gains direct evidence this week for the industrial measure. Smith's electric-stack finding says the physical-world race is already lost to China even where the US leads in models, which would make "who builds the atoms" the axis that decides the next decade. The claim that deglobalization is a durable trend (Mexico, plus Indonesia and Nigeria) is testable: watch whether national refining and manufacturing self-sufficiency keeps rising through the next oil-price shock, or whether the fiscal cost of holding it together — the subsidy Sheinbaum still had to pay — pulls these economies back toward the global market.

↩ Back to TOC

Cross-theme insights

Crosscurrents synthesis — June 22-27, 2026

In domain after domain this week, the actors who used to control an outcome lost the power to do it. The faction that wanted America out of its Middle East wars took ownership of Iran policy from the hawks who started the war. Democratic party leaders who once anointed nominees delivered no one, while their own incumbents fell. The rule that "the party decides" its primaries failed for Democrats, as it had already failed for Republicans. Britain shed another prime minister; Hungary's lone illiberal counter-model lost power and is being dismantled by its successor.

The loss of control is only half the finding. The other half: whatever inherits the vacuum keeps discovering that removing an incumbent and steering the result are two different powers. The insurgents who swept New York's primaries are met at once with the question of whether they can govern. The British state that voters keep handing new drivers answers to none of them. The restraint faction that captured Iran diplomacy holds a channel that, by its own champion's account, turns on a galaxy-sized if. This extends what the project has tracked for two weeks — that the binding constraint on Western institutions is not the authority to decide but the capacity to make a decision last. This week that incapacity migrates from the order being displaced to the forces displacing it.

How the themes interlock

The collapse of the gatekeepers runs on both party coalitions and across the left-right divide at once. The week's strongest convergence is between writers who agree on nothing else. Reading New York's primary sweep, an independent-left journalist and a post-liberal-right editor reach the same diagnosis: the establishment has lost its power to decide who its candidates are. Klippenstein calls the night a revolt of ordinary voters against party gatekeepers, not a win for the left. Shullenberger calls 2026 the Democrats' 2016 — the year the rule that elites pick nominees finally fails for them as it failed for Republicans under Trump. One welcomes the erosion as democracy reviving; the other indicts both parties as captured; the diagnosis is identical. The loudest disagreement about the sweep sits on top of the same premise. Whether the wins are the party's MAGA liability (Smith) or its only available weapon against oligarchy (Stoller), both writers doubt the winners can govern — which is the lead's question again. The same collapse runs the Iran cluster: the restraint faction's capture of post-war policy is the foreign-policy establishment losing its lock on war decisions (Ahmari), with the hawks reduced to reading a single June 26 drone strike as vindication (McCarthy). This is the clearest Democratic-side confirmation yet of the project's running finding that the donor class's candidate-gating machinery is decaying inside both parties.

The capacity the Britain cluster mourns is the one the AI clusters demand — and the two sets of writers never meet. Four writers from incompatible traditions converge that the Western state can no longer be steered by whoever nominally controls it. They split only on what does the hollowing. A constitutionalist Remainer locates the force outside Britain, in the EU's gravitational pull (Sumption). A post-liberal Leaver locates it inside, in a "para-state" of courts and agencies that outlasts every elected government (Harrington, Pinkoski). A French left-realist calls the condition "Policy Management" — procedure crowding out substance (Aurelien) — while a Wall Street Journal columnist universalizes it as a West-wide failure Britain is merely "more honest about" (Sternberg). What they describe is not a shortage of authority but a shortage of durability: the state can still act, but it cannot make a decision stick. That is precisely the capacity the AI clusters take for granted. A socialist and a classical-liberal critic converge from opposite poles that the state must shape AI rather than stand back — Bruenig wants the public to own half the frontier labs through a one-time tax paid in stock; Shullenberger argues that laissez-faire toward an industry racing to build a single global decision-making agency is itself a sweeping intervention. Neither set of writers reads the other. The data-center fight is where they collide on the ground: a senior House energy Democrat now demands a national moratorium (Zeitlin), while Tabarrok would auction grid-connection rights — a fix that routes scarce capacity to the deepest pockets while presenting itself as neutral engineering.

One build-out, two seams the clusters leave unjoined. The data-center backlash is the demand side of a story whose supply side runs through the industrial-map cluster, and the two are told apart. The compute surge hits a grid and an electric-technology base — batteries, motors, power electronics — where China, not the US, now sets the pace (Smith). The fight over who pays to connect the next gigawatt sits one layer above a fight over an industrial base the US no longer leads. The second seam is ownership. Two proposals to make AI pay society travel in different clothes: a one-time stock tax that hands the public a stake in the labs (Bruenig), and a single-sector carbon tax on data centers pegged to the global cost of warming (Yglesias). One extracts equity, the other an externality price; both answer the same question — on what terms society lets the build-out proceed.

Beneath the politics, the deepest fights are boundary fights: who belongs, and who gets to say what is real. The America-250 cluster argues membership, and its sharpest split is inside the right — between grounding citizenship in a shared creed (Anton) and grounding it in inherited traits of descent, birthplace, and religion (Upham). The knowledge cluster argues reality: how a shared sense of the facts gets manufactured or captured, from awareness campaigns that create the distress they treat (Frawley) to a Wikipedia run by unaccountable gatekeepers (Sanger). Both are versions of the week's organizing question — who controls the boundary, and what happens when the old controllers lose their grip. The membership fight and the reality fight are the gatekeeper collapse rendered as first principles.

↩ Back to TOC

What's missing

Wire check. Significant events in the window drew no roster engagement. The deadliest is literal: two earthquakes near Caracas killed more than 900 people, with later counts running past 1,400 — the strongest Venezuelan tremor in a century, leading every wire outlet by the weekend — and not one roster writer touched it. The same is true of two accountability stories. The John Bolton guilty plea and Leon Black's walkout from the House Epstein hearing both sit inside the selective-enforcement thread the project has tracked for two months. Bolton's plea matters because it "stood up to legal scrutiny" where the James and Comey prosecutions look political — the even-handedness test the thread turns on — yet no roster writer engaged it. A subtler miss is internal to the Iran cluster. It opens by declaring the ceasefire held and the argument moved to "who lost." But by June 27 the US was striking Iran again after a cargo-ship drone attack, and Iran was striking back; the premise was overtaken inside the window the cluster covers.

Perspective check. The standing gaps hold. No roster writer is of the working class or the displaced workforce the energy and industrial clusters describe, now many consecutive weeks. The Iran cluster has interpreters but no one with Middle East operational expertise to judge whether the deconfliction channel — the new line for Iranian and US military officers to manage disputes — or the ceasefire itself can hold, and the June 27 strikes exposed that gap at once. And the week's largest human event, the Venezuela quake, found no roster writer in or near Latin America; the only Global-South reporting came from the Horn of Africa (Somalia) and China.

↩ Back to TOC

What the mainstream press is getting wrong

The New York sweep is a gatekeeping story, not a "socialism" story. The press frames the primary as a left or Democratic Socialists win — NPR's weekly wrap files it under "Democratic Socialists made gains," and the conservative register treats it as the party going "off the socialist deep end" (NR Editors). The roster's cross-pole reading reaches what the ideology frame misses: the operative event is the establishment losing its power to gate its own primaries, a collapse visible from the left (Klippenstein) and the right (Shullenberger) alike. The same press scan carries the Republican mirror it never connects — Trump's loyalty enforcement (NPR, "Trump's demands for loyalty are causing friction") and the Louisiana Senate runoff (NBC) are the GOP version of the identical gatekeeping fight. The press covers two separate party stories; the roster shows one phenomenon on both coalitions.

The European heatwave and the AI build-out are the same story. BBC ran the record heat as a meteorological and health crisis ("Heatwave breaks more records in northern and central Europe"), with no connection to the compute boom straining the same grids. The roster makes the link the Anglophone wire drops. On the European rail, national outlets name AI directly as a climate antagonist — Mediapart's "ecological ogre," El País on its thirst for energy and water (Euro|topics). On the American rail, the same demand surge becomes a grid-cost fight in which a senior House energy Democrat demands a national moratorium (Zeitlin). The AI-climate connection surfaced in the roster only through the French and Spanish press; the Anglophone wire and the roster's Anglophone core both missed it — the week's clearest mutual blind spot, caught by the non-Anglophone feed.

The durable Iran story is the domestic succession, not the ceasefire's survival. BBC and NBC run the tactical register, with headlines like "US strikes Iran after attack on cargo ship" (BBC). The roster treats the settled facts — regime intact, Washington accommodating, regime change abandoned — as no longer contested, and points to the consequential shift: the restraint faction now owns post-war diplomacy and the most credible 2028 vehicle (Ahmari, Klippenstein), while the hawks read a single ship strike as vindication. The press asks whether the ceasefire holds; the roster shows the war's most durable output is who now owns American Iran policy.

Press absence. The HHS rule tightening who may claim an unaccompanied migrant child (a Free Press exclusive) and the data-center cost finding — the PJM grid's $23 billion data-center surcharge — appear nowhere in the seven press-scan outlets.

↩ Back to TOC

Carry-forward

This week opens two structural findings, refines two, and advances two more in the log.

  • ai-governance-state-not-market (new) — The AI-governance debate has stopped being about whether the state should shape AI and become a fight over which instrument; the market-discipline position had no serious defender this week.

    • Warrant: A socialist case for public equity (Bruenig on Sanders's stock tax) and a classical-liberal critique of laissez-faire (Shullenberger) converge that the hands-off option is gone. Even the libertarian-adjacent pole is only relieved that models shipped before review tightened, not arguing against review (Cowen).
    • Implication: Over 12-36 months the AI fight is over instruments — an equity tax, a pre-release review rule, a moratorium or carbon tax — not over whether the state is involved. The convergence holds only until a serious market-discipline defender re-enters.
    • Test: Whether any single instrument materializes first: a Sanders-style equity tax clearing committee, a formal pre-release model-review rule, or a data-center moratorium or carbon tax. A second signal is whether a credible defense of leaving AI to the market reappears.

  • restraint-faction-owns-postwar-iran (new) — The Iran war's most durable domestic output is the restraint faction's capture of both post-war policy and the most credible 2028 vehicle, with the US now negotiating as the party asking for terms.

    • Warrant: Across the restraint pole and the regime-critic pole, one verdict converges — the Islamic Republic survived intact and Washington is accommodating (Golkar, Cass, Kahn). Ahmari presents Vance as "team captain of the restrainers," owning the diplomacy. Klippenstein reads the same media tour as a 2028 soft-launch built on an elected vice president who "can't be fired."
    • Implication: The foreign-policy face of the broader governance reckoning — that American force has limits — now has an institutional carrier and a succession vehicle, while the hawks are reduced to reading single incidents as vindication.
    • Test: Whether the Doha deconfliction channel survives Netanyahu and Iran's hardliners (the two threats Ahmari names); whether the June 27 strikes harden into renewed war or stay contained; whether Vance's "America First moderation" consolidates as a 2028 pitch. Successor to the resolved question of whether time favored the US or Iran; pair with the open Strait-of-Hormuz governance dispute.

  • datacenter-buildout-resistance (refined) — The political ceiling on the US AI build-out is now a genuinely bipartisan platform, with the national-moratorium demand coming from inside senior energy policymaking rather than only from activists or red-state Republicans.

    • Warrant: Pallone, ranking Democrat on House Energy and Commerce, calls for a national moratorium until air, water, and power-bill harms are prevented — joining Sanders-AOC and New York's pending moratorium. The cost grievance has one hard instance: the PJM grid's independent monitor pinned $23 billion in added capacity-auction costs on data-center load. The documented local rejections are red and rural (Holzman), cutting against Lind's "astroturf" read.
    • Implication: The resistance is a cross-coalition constraint, not a partisan one, so the compute build-out's binding ceiling is political as much as physical.
    • Test: Whether other regional grids produce PJM-style cost attributions; whether any moratorium passes at state or federal level; whether the farmland argument that slowed solar and wind boomerangs onto clean power.

  • donor-class-enforcement-decay (refined) — The party establishment's machinery for gating its own primaries is decaying inside both coalitions; this week delivered its clearest Democratic-side confirmation.

    • Warrant: Every congressional candidate Mamdani endorsed won the June 23 New York primary, two unseating incumbents, while Jeffries, Schumer, and Hochul delivered no one (Klippenstein, Stoller). Last cycle the establishment's substitute lever — coordinated press revelations timed to a Maine statute window — failed to stop Platner; this week a fully backed slate failed outright. The GOP mirror runs in parallel (Trump's loyalty enforcement; the Louisiana runoff).
    • Implication: Once the gate is gone, the primary electorate is the activist base, which selects for faction loyalty over crossover appeal (Silver on Newsom, Yglesias on the vanished swing voter) — so winning primaries and winning general elections become separate problems.
    • Test: Whether the New York winners govern materially and durably (Goldberg's "sewer socialism" — everyday municipal delivery of transit and rents — versus the purist strain); the November Maine result; whether additional 2026 primaries repeat the pattern.

The week also advances the standing finding that contested distributional choices keep getting dressed as neutral engineering — Tabarrok's grid-connection auction routes scarce access to the deepest pockets while presenting itself as efficiency — and adds direct evidence for the industrial measure of US-China power, with Smith's electric-stack finding putting the physical-world race ahead of any AI-model lead.

↩ Back to TOC

Meta-finding

The mode that did the most work this week was the interpretive framework, specifically convergence across incompatible poles. The week's hardest findings are not numbers but agreements between writers who share no priors: that the Western state cannot be steered (four orientations), that AI cannot be left to the market (socialist and classical-liberal), that the establishment has lost its power to gate primaries (independent-left and post-liberal-right). Material data was confirmatory rather than load-bearing — the PJM $23 billion grid figure, China's electric-stack market shares, the Gallup polling inversion all sharpen findings the frameworks already carried. Historical analogy did real work where it supplied a mechanism rather than a mood: the para-state Blair built, "The Party Decides" falsified for Democrats a decade after it failed for Republicans. The week's sharpest single observation is reflexive, from the knowledge cluster: the writers diagnosing how a distorted sense of reality gets manufactured keep enacting the vice they name. A monocausal essay on podcasts concedes mid-way that the medium is neutral; a both-sides theory of polarization breaks its own symmetry; a gatekeeping indictment rests on the authority of being a co-founder. Naming a distorted reality is itself an interpretive act, and most of these writers cannot perform it cleanly. That is a finding about the limits of this week's dominant mode, not a verdict on it.

↩ Back to TOC

Optimist's take

Crosscurrents optimist's take — June 22 to June 27, 2026.

The synthesis reads the week as a study in things that can't be steered: a ceasefire that banks Iran's gains, a Western state hollowed past the point where elections reach it, a party establishment that has lost the power to pick its own candidates, an AI build-out arriving faster than the grid or the law can absorb it. Steering is the synthesis's organizing worry. Rotate the same evidence toward what is getting easier to do, and a different week appears — one in which obstacles that read as diffuse decline are turning into specific, fixable problems. Five world-facts survive striking every writer's name from the page.

Starting something is at a record high in the two largest rich economies on the page, and the cost of doing it has collapsed. New US business registrations set a monthly record every month this year — 2.9 million through May, the strongest five-month start ever recorded — and France is posting record business creation of its own, led by micro-entrepreneurs. The deeper fact is cost. One person with off-the-shelf payments, storefront, cloud, and bookkeeping software, now plus AI, can do what a small staff used to require. Recent cohorts are reaching real revenue faster than any before them: the 2025 class hit a million dollars in cumulative sales within a year at three times the rate of the 2019 class. Registrations and platform revenue are not the same as surviving firms with employees, so this is dynamism's leading indicator, not its confirmation. But the barrier to trying has fallen faster than almost any structural metric the synthesis tracks, and it fell on price, not policy.

The physical hardware of decarbonization is getting cheap and abundant, and grids are already rebuilding themselves around it in response to nothing but price. One layer beneath the data-center fight sits a supply fact: batteries, motors, and the power electronics that move electricity are being mass-produced at a scale that is driving their cost down worldwide. And the market is responding without being told to. In Texas's grid, as solar capacity grew from 4 to nearly 30 gigawatts, the midday value of power fell to roughly a third of the peak price — a signal that storage was needed — and developers built it, taking the system from almost no battery output to 8.6 gigawatts of discharge. The remaining constraint, the years-long queue to connect new power to the grid, turns out to have a known fix: auction the connection rights instead of awarding them first-come, and let loads that can power down during the few annual peak hours connect flexibly. The synthesis is right that the US may not capture the industrial base behind it, a genuine strategic loss. But the world still gets cheaper batteries and solar out of it, wherever the factories sit.

The largest single driver of deforestation is domestic demand, which makes it a problem a single government can actually solve — and the rich world's imported share has already fallen by two-thirds. The intuition that wealthy consumers drive forest loss through their imports turns out to be mostly wrong. About three-quarters of global deforestation serves demand inside the country doing the clearing, beef most of all, where Brazil's cleared land overwhelmingly feeds Brazilians. That is the optimistic finding, because domestic-demand deforestation sits inside one government's policy reach rather than requiring the near-impossible coordination of global trade. And where rich countries do contribute through imports, the trend is already good: Britain's imported deforestation has dropped by roughly two-thirds since the early 2000s, with similar declines in France and Germany. The caveats are real — the US has not declined, and the dataset measures deforestation risk through national averages, not specific sourcing — but a problem that is national and already falling is far more soluble than the assumption that rich-world imports drive forest loss implied.

A hot war between the United States and Iran stopped at a ceasefire instead of escalating — and the thing that stopped it was democratic accountability, not strategy. Strip the who-lost argument and a plain fact remains: the shooting stopped, regime change was abandoned rather than pursued through occupation, and a standing channel now exists for Iranian and American military officers to defuse incidents directly in Doha. The way the exit came is the more durable green shoot. The war ended because the public would not bear the cost of "winning" it, which is the old republican principle that a war needs consent, not just will. The same capacity to detect a mistake and reverse it that the comparative record rewarded last week showed up here in real time. The fragility is honest: the deal favors Iran, the arrangement turns on a very large if, and a drone strike on a Hormuz cargo ship days after the ceasefire shows the strait is still contested. A war that ended is still better than a war that widened.

The tools for enforcing law against concentrated power are being rebuilt, and some of them now sit directly in citizens' hands. The clearest instance is a just-filed California class action against Walmart and other chains over fuel-pricing software alleged to have raised gas prices by up to thirty cents a gallon. The suit was made possible by a new state law banning shared pricing algorithms and enforceable by private citizens, not only regulators, and assembled by former federal antitrust staff. That is an enforcement lever that did not exist a year ago. Alongside it runs a fact about democratic responsiveness: in New York, ordinary voters overrode a coordinated party establishment to choose their own nominees, with leaders who backed a full slate delivering none of it. Whether the winners can govern is genuinely unresolved — even the writer making the case is mildly pessimistic that the cadre to wield these tools at scale exists. But the levers are being written, and the gatekeepers' grip is weaker than the declension story assumed.

What the synthesis has right, and the path. The deal favors Iran; the Western state can act but cannot make a decision stick; the party that lost its gatekeepers may field candidates who cannot win or cannot govern; the build-out is outrunning the grid and the law. None of that rotates away. But the same evidence supports five world-claims, and they share a shape. Record new-firm formation, cheap clean hardware, a nationally tractable deforestation problem, a war ended by accountability, and citizen-enforceable antitrust are all cases of an obstacle turning legible — from diffuse "decline" into a specific bottleneck with an identified fix. The grid queue has an auction. The subway's runaway tunnel cost traces to a single unsubstantiated 1970 fire-safety rule that European systems safely doubled. The path runs through whether those fixes get adopted: whether interconnection reform prices the connection rather than hides it, whether the new-firm surge converts into surviving employers, whether the Doha channel survives its first real test. Each is observable within two to four years, and none requires the synthesis to be wrong. The quiet finding is that this was a week of problems getting smaller and better-specified, not larger — and a problem you can name precisely is a problem you can begin to fix.

↩ Back to TOC

What the Administration Would Say

Crosscurrents loyalist's take — June 22 to June 27, 2026

Last week's defense of the Iran memorandum rested on a wager: the deal's verdict could not be rendered yet, and the next sixty days would grade it. The first returns are in, and they run the administration's way more than against it. The ceasefire held; no wider war arrived. The cleanest version of the case came not from a White House surrogate but from a skeptic. Oren Cass, no loyalist, credits the president with "the rare, right, and thankless thing" in cutting bait on a war that had failed its own shifting objectives. That is the defense in one line. The administration ended a war the prior consensus had no exit from, declined the ground assault to reopen Hormuz its critics would also have condemned, and absorbed the cost rather than doubling the bet.

Concede what the week still charges. The convergence across the roster — the regime survived intact, and Washington is now the party asking for terms — is real, and so are the costs: the unfrozen assets, the Strait clause if it means what its text suggests. But the deal's structure answers the worst of them. The Gulf investment it dangles before Iran is contingent on compliance, not paid up front. That is the correction the administration always said it wanted over Obama's 2015 deal: the money is withheld until behavior earns it, leverage retained rather than spent. A defender can call that a better instrument than the JCPOA and mean it.

The hawks' rejoinder is the June 26 drone strike on a Hormuz cargo ship, read as proof that negotiating with the regime is folly. The roundup flags the move itself: one incident cannot establish that a regime is categorically unappeasable, and "events vindicate the hawks" is asserted, not earned. The honest read inverts it. A post-ceasefire incident is exactly what the new Doha channel — direct contact between Revolutionary Guard and Central Command officers — exists to manage. The test of the deal is not whether incidents occur. It is whether the machinery to contain them holds. One drone strike absorbed without a return to war is that machinery working, not failing.

The week's largest gift to the administration is not anything it did. It is what the opposition became.
