The same coalition that elected this president in 2024 ran against the politics now sweeping the other party's primaries. The roundup does not need a loyalist to make the point. A center-left economist calls the result the Democrats' own MAGA; a sympathetic columnist concedes that the academic-left strain — a candidate who would not say whether murderers should be jailed — invites exactly the backlash that sank the post-2018 left. The administration's bet in 2024 was that a working-class, multiracial coalition could be held against a party drifting toward its affluent activists. That bet looks more durable this week, not less, because the drift is now winning primaries the party leadership tried to stop.

The strongest single defense is the Court. Two 6-3 immigration rulings landed, and the case for them is not policy muscle but plain text. A migrant turned back in Mexico has not "arrived in" the United States, so the statutory hearing right never triggers; a statute that bars judicial review of a humanitarian designation means courts may not review it. A Yale law professor, no friend of the administration, reaches the same verdict as National Review, and adds the inconvenient fact that the metering practice began under Obama in 2016. The clinching move belongs to the conservative defending the wins: he predicts the administration loses the pending birthright-citizenship case on the very same method, because the Fourteenth Amendment's words run against it. Textualism is a method, not a team. When the enacted words favor the administration it wins; when they do not, the same reading says it loses. A defense that survives that asymmetry is not motivated reasoning.

The same instinct runs through the build-out the opposition wants to halt. New business formation is at a record — 2.9 million new firms through May, the strongest five-month start ever logged — and the data-center construction the moratorium movement is fighting is real industrial activity, not a press release. The administration's answer to the energy strain is to make the new loads pay their own way rather than push the cost onto households. Here the honest defender stops short of triumph. The cost-allocation fight is genuine: the grid serving New Jersey really did book $23 billion in added capacity costs to data-center load, and the question of who pays is not yet settled. The deeper charge lands too. Tariffs without the factories they were meant to protect are, in one critic's phrase, "dreamy industrial nostalgia," and the build half of the plan has not arrived; the retreat on electric vehicles sits in real tension with a race for batteries and motors that China is winning. The momentum is defensible. The follow-through is a promissory note.

Two items this week no honest defender should try to rescue. The federal terrorism case against fifteen Minnesota protesters is collapsing in court — dismissals, acquittals, an affidavit that recurs across cases — and a prosecution that cannot survive a courtroom is not enforcement of the law; it is overreach the law is correcting. Defending it would forfeit the credibility the textualism argument earns. And the warning from a conservative leaving Hungary should be read as written: Orbán, the one figure who actually fought the captured institutions, lost his next election on a stalled economy and cronyism, because squeezed voters will not tolerate corruption or loyalty rewarded over competence. "I hope the White House is paying attention" is the right note to take seriously, not deflect.

So the wager stands, narrowed and honest. The administration ended a war without starting a larger one, won its immigration cases on the statute's own words, and watched its opposition validate the coalition that elected it — while overreaching on domestic prosecutions and lagging on the industrial build it promised. The next quarter grades the rest: whether Iran stalls the nuclear talks as predicted, whether the factories follow the tariffs, and whether the administration heeds the Hungarian lesson before its own economy delivers it.

Rebuttal

Crosscurrents rebuttal — June 22 to June 27, 2026

Three of the loyalist's moves survive contact and are granted here before the rest are tested. The framework choice was the right one. Ending a war the prior consensus had no exit from, and declining the ground assault to reopen Hormuz by force, is defensible at the price the deal carries — the concession last week's rebuttal already made, and Cass's "rare, right, and thankless thing" names it cleanly (Cass). The deferred structure of the Gulf investment is a genuine design improvement: money promised against future compliance is a better instrument than money handed over at signing. And the loyalist is right that the hawks have not earned their conclusion — one post-ceasefire drone strike cannot establish that the regime is categorically unappeasable, exactly as the roundup flags against McCarthy and Sheehy. A defense that names its own indefensibles — the Minnesota prosecutions, the unbuilt factories — has earned a hearing on the rest.

The hearing is where three of the four load-bearing defenses strain, each on the loyalist's own materials.

Start with conditionality, the claimed advance over Obama's 2015 deal. The virtue the loyalist names — "the money is withheld until behavior earns it, leverage retained rather than spent" — describes only the tranche Iran is content to wait for. The relief that matters now was not withheld. The roundup's own ledger has the regime already collecting the reported $12 billion in unfrozen assets and sanctions easing, in exchange for reopening a strait that was open before the war (Golkar). That is money released at signing — the precise JCPOA structure the deal is sold as correcting. The contingent $300 billion in Gulf investment is real and really deferred, but it is not what keeps the regime, in Golkar's phrase, economically relieved and politically intact this quarter. The improvement holds for the part Iran can afford to wait on and fails for the part doing the work. Conditionality is both true and not load-bearing.

Then the inversion, the week's most ingenious move. The loyalist reads the June 26 drone strike as "the machinery working, not failing": a post-ceasefire incident is what the Doha deconfliction channel exists to absorb, and absorbing one without a return to war is the channel doing its job. Grant the symmetry the loyalist correctly draws against the hawks — one incident proves no categorical claim. The trouble is that the loyalist waives the same standard for itself. If a single strike cannot prove the regime unappeasable, a single strike absorbed cannot prove the channel holds either. The loyalist applies "one data point settles nothing" to the hawks and "one data point vindicates" to itself. Worse, the frame has no failing condition inside it. Any Iranian strike short of open war now reads as the channel succeeding, which means no incident the strait could produce could ever count against the deal. And it proves too much: a reading on which Iran attacking Hormuz shipping is evidence the arrangement works cannot simultaneously be evidence the strait was secured. The channel "working" and the skeptics being right — Iran keeping a contested, fee-extracting role over the waterway — turn out to name one outcome. This is last week's deferral returning under a new name: the grade promised for "the next sixty days" has been redefined so the data arrives pre-graded.

The textualism defense is the strongest of the four, and most of it stands. Within the four corners of the two 6-3 rulings, the method is not motivated reasoning: a hostile Yale professor and National Review reach the same verdict (Rubenfeld), the metering practice began under Obama, and the conservative defending the wins predicts the administration loses the pending birthright case on the identical reading (McLaughlin). Predicting your own side's defeat by your own method is a real falsifiability test, and the synthesis was glib to wave the rulings off as hardball. But the defense quietly changes the subject. "Textualism is a method, not a team" is a claim about the Court — six justices reading enacted words — and the loyalist spends it as a claim about the administration. Those are different actors, and the administration's own conduct in the same document runs the other way. The Minnesota terrorism case the loyalist flatly abandons is the administration enforcing law by "indicators" — anti-Americanism, anti-capitalism, anti-Christianity — so elastic the U.S. Attorney could not define the charge and conceded bodily harm "is not the measure" (Klippenstein). That is enforcement by political identification of enemies, the antithesis of reading text plainly. The neutrality is real, and it belongs to the Roberts majority — not to the party that wins in front of it while prosecuting its opponents by criteria no statute contains. McLaughlin's predicted birthright loss is the Court's discipline, not the administration's, which brought that case to win it.

Last, the opposition's radicalization, which the loyalist calls "the week's largest gift" — and frames, tellingly, as "not anything it did." A mandate defense whose strongest evidence is what the other party became has conceded that the administration's own week did not supply the vindication. Durability is a two-sided ledger: the 2024 working-class coalition holds only if the alternative repels it and the incumbent delivers enough to keep it. The loyalist banks the first half and, in the same essay, concedes the second is unmet — tariffs without the factories (Lynn), the electric-vehicle retreat, the build-out reduced to a "promissory note" — then endorses the Dreher warning that squeezed voters desert on a stalled economy and cronyism (Dreher). Those two beliefs collide. Coalition durability is asserted on exactly the ground — material delivery — the loyalist admits has not been laid, and the Hungarian lesson it takes seriously is the one that says non-delivery dissolves the coalition however repellent the opposition looks. The defense has slid from "the administration is doing what voters elected it to do" to "the other team is worse," the weakest move available, because it concedes the principal's performance. It also reads the New York sweep selectively: the roundup's finding is an anti-gatekeeper revolt that both Shullenberger and Klippenstein read as hitting both parties — 2026 as the Democrats' 2016. The loyalist keeps the half that flatters its coalition and drops the half that says the same decay is coming for its own.

Read together, the surviving defenses are real but smaller than the frame that carries them. The textualist neutrality belongs to the Court; the retained leverage belongs to the tranche Iran can wait on; the deconfliction "working" belongs to a bar lowered until the skeptics' own forecast counts as success; the mandate's durability belongs to the opposition's drift, not the administration's delivery. Each thing the loyalist asks the reader to credit the administration for turns out to be true of something one actor over — the Court, the deferred money, the repellent challenger. The reader exits not on whether the framework beat the quagmire, which it did, but on a defense that keeps locating its vindication next to the administration rather than in it.

↩ Back to TOC

Access Issues & Limitations

access_report: 64 saved articles, 0 flagged — no truncation, paywalled stubs, byline mismatches, or duplicates among the saved files. Subscribed/gated sources were captured in full via login (Compact ×6, Free Press ×5, National Review ×4, Persuasion ×3, NYT + WSJ via NYPL ProQuest, UnHerd ×5).

Dropped or unreachable (no stubs kept)

Source Item Reason
War on the Rocks Hormuz / Open-Strait / nuclear-AI (3) Member-gated; ~430-word previews only, no login configured.
Courrier International Strait-of-Hormuz dispatch Paywalled (55-word stub); Iran amply covered elsewhere.
3 Quarks Daily "Why the West stopped making land" Link-blog stub (159 words), not a full essay.
The Atlantic (25 in feed) No subscription — not fetched; the left-legal SCOTUS voice is thinner this week as a result.
Persuasion Mounk, "The Good Fight Club" Podcast episode; blurb-only, no transcript → dropped per the podcast-description-only rule.
Chartbook (Tooze) Chartbook 453 Verbatim reprint of his 2006 book chapter, no current-affairs through-line; his other output was all "Top Links" roundups.

No posts this week

Ezra Klein, Zeynep Tufekci, Scholar's Stage, Capitolism (Lincicome), Commentary, Murtaza Hussain, and the on-demand manual roster (Sun Liping, Aourid, Dalio, Maitra, Frank, Mills).

Notes for future runs

  • WSJ is hard-blocked in the Chrome extension (wsj.com navigation fails). Route Sternberg/WSJ through the NYPL ProQuest U.S. Newsstream collection (reachable on the same EZProxy session — worked cleanly this week).
  • Free Press email delivery uses different slugs than the website (newsletter digests, not standalone-article slugs), so fetch_from_email keep_only on website slugs returns nothing. Browser capture against the already-logged-in Substack session is the reliable path for specific FP picks.
  • Content mix: comfortably above the primary-reality floor (Fahlberg's HHS exclusive, Sanger firsthand, African Arguments ×2, Heatmap/Pallone reporting, Klippenstein document-driven, Ritchie/Tabarrok/Bruenig data, Potter engineering). Non-Anglophone supply chain present (African Arguments ×2, China Digital Times, Euro|topics, Compact/Mexico).

Article Summaries

Iran's aftermath: who lost, and the Vance turn

Iran has humiliated America’s military

Edward Luttwak · 2026-06-21 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The February–April 2026 US–Iran war and the vague Trump-brokered ceasefire/"deal" that ended it.

Luttwak argues the war's outcome was set not by Iran's capabilities — which he calls primitive (its missiles less lethal than 1944 German V2 rockets) and its statecraft self-destructive (military spending diverted from water and power left the regime near systemic collapse) — but by Washington's refusal to use force it had already positioned. He claims the 3,000 paratroopers and 5,000 Marines staged in the Gulf could have seized uninhabited islands and coastal tracts in low-risk, helicopter-supported commando raids to keep shipping lanes open while cutting off Iran's oil-export revenue from Kharg Island, and that no one ever actually proposed the full ground invasion that "boots on the ground" panic imagined. That panic — which he pins partly on "isolationist backwaters and White House Vice Presidential staffers" — nullified US air power and let the Revolutionary Guards "win with very few fighters and very few small boats." He roots the paralysis in a structural "post-heroic" transformation caused by a fertility collapse to 1.6, which he says leaves every soldier an only-son "Private Ryan" no family will risk, degrading US strength "down to European levels." But the analysis flags the piece's operative claim as an untested counterfactual: that limited commando seizures would have forced Iranian capitulation is asserted, not demonstrated, and a peer piece in this same cluster (Cass) names exactly this "decide strategy for both sides" reasoning as the war hawk's central fallacy.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is Luttwak doubling down on his decades-old "post-heroic warfare" thesis — that low-birthrate societies will not spend soldiers' lives — and bending the Iran war to fit it. The clever move is to agree with the restraint camp that the war was a US defeat while inverting the usual lesson: the failure, for Luttwak, was too little force, not too much. That lets him keep both his contempt for the 2003 Iraq war (which he testified against) and his insistence that this war could have been won cheaply.

The structural problem is that the piece's decisive claim is an unfalsifiable counterfactual dressed as operational fact. The commando plan — seize islands, choke Kharg Island's oil terminal, protect tankers, all at "minimal" risk — is simply asserted to be both low-cost and war-winning; the very threat he names (mines dropped from any dhow or fishing boat, anti-tank missiles fired at passing tankers) cuts against the "minimal risk" premise, since dispersed irregular mining is exactly what light infantry on empty islands cannot reliably stop. "Humiliation" and "degraded to European levels" are the kind of deterrence claims that can absorb any outcome. And the fertility-to-defeat causal chain is monocausal determinism: the same "post-heroic" public backed precision strikes and a decapitation campaign enthusiastically, so the constraint is plainly on occupation and casualties, not on fighting as such — a distinction Luttwak collapses. There is also an internal tension: he documents US precision success and an Iranian regime on the brink, then calls the result a rout, locating the entire defeat in a single road not taken.

In the cluster, Luttwak is the hawk pole, and Cass answers him almost line-for-line: Cass's "Land of Wars Are Fun, where we get to decide strategy for both sides and then ours always wins" is a precise description of Luttwak's commando fantasy, and Cass's republic-vs-empire point — that public support is a binding constraint, not a failure of nerve — directly refutes the post-heroic framing as a category error ("if my bubby had balls she'd be my zayde"). Note too that "humiliation" is the cluster's contested keyword: Luttwak pins it on American restraint, Cass on the war's premise, Golkar on Trump's negotiation, and Kahn (via Haaretz) on Netanyahu — the same word, four incompatible referents.

↩ Back to TOC

Cutting Bait on a Bad War

Oren Cass · 2026-06-26 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The February 2026 US–Iran war and its negotiated wind-down, plus hawkish commentary (AEI's Matthew Continetti in the WSJ, former VP Mike Pence) insisting the US should have pressed on or "secured concessions first."

Cass argues the pro-war camp runs an "infinite war" strategy — the martingale gambler who keeps doubling his bet — which can only fail by losing nerve or running out of money, conveniently letting hawks blame any defeat on the decision to stop. He insists wars are won by achieving strategic objectives, not by tallying explosions: the US destroyed ships, missiles, and nuclear infrastructure, but by making the war existential for Iran (decapitating its leadership and declaring regime change the goal) it guaranteed the predictable Hormuz closure and missile barrages, then failed its shifting objectives because the public would not bear the costs of "winning." He rejects the hawks' "time is a flat circle" move of citing Iran's lashing-out as retroactive justification for the war that provoked it, and dismisses Pence's "secure concessions first" as a category error that mistakes the language of domestic policy (where one controls outcomes) for a two-sided conflict. His governing principle is republican: public support is as essential as munition stockpiles, and a leader's duty to persuade the citizenry — not merely to will victory — is what distinguishes a republic from an empire. He credits Trump for doing "the rare, right, and thankless thing" in cutting bait, faulting only his attempt to sell a bad deal as great, and locates the real "humiliation" in the failure of the war itself, not the unfavorable terms now unavoidable. (The Friday newsletter's back half — prediction-markets/Meta "Arena," a Korea guest-worker paper, the housing bill, reindustrialization links — is standard curation, not part of the Iran argument.)

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is the strongest and most rigorous piece in the cluster, and it is Cass's American Compass realism — outcomes over slogans, costs reckoned honestly — translated cleanly to foreign policy. The two-sided-conflict insight (the enemy gets a vote, so an "effective strategist always presumes his opponents will take the action he least wants"), the martingale analogy for "infinite war," and the republic-vs-empire framing are genuinely clarifying, and crucially they are falsifiable in a way the "humiliation" and "deterrence" claims swirling around them are not. The piece's central diagnostic — that the war failed from a structural imbalance in preparation, clarity of objective, and willingness to bear costs, not from insufficient resolve — reframes the whole debate around a constraint (public consent) that the hawks treat as a variable they can will away.

Read against Luttwak in this same cluster, Cass is a near-line-by-line rebuttal. Luttwak's thesis — that low-risk commando force would have won and the only failure was post-heroic timidity — is exactly the "Land of Wars Are Fun, where we get to decide strategy for both sides and then ours always wins" fantasy Cass dissects, and Cass's "if my bubby had balls, she'd be my zayde" is the precise answer to "we could have prevailed if only we'd been willing to do more." Cass even concedes the hawks' real fear — that Iran "emerges more credibly powerful" (the Iran-sponsored-terror worry Kahn also raises) — but his point is that this was baked the moment the war was launched without public support: the available alternative was never "win," it was "don't start."

The one soft spot worth flagging for the synthesis: Cass asserts that the bad deal is "already baked... not of the negotiating team's skill or the president's social media posts," which conveniently immunizes the restrainer position against any negotiation critique in the same move he accuses hawks of using to immunize theirs — the symmetry deserves notice. And crediting Trump for cutting bait while blaming the same Trump's war is a tension he manages gracefully but does not fully dissolve. Within the cluster he converges with Golkar (coercion can't transform a survival-regime; the US is now the supplicant) and with Kahn (the war was the error), stands against Luttwak (more force), and shares Ahmari's restrainer valence while rejecting Ahmari's optimism — for Cass the exit is correct but the deal is plainly bad and should be sold as such.

↩ Back to TOC

Trump Lost Both the War and the Negotiation

Saeid Golkar · 2026-06-25 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The June 2026 US–Iran memorandum of understanding ending the February 2026 war, and Trump and Vance's framing of it as a diplomatic victory.

Golkar, an Iran scholar and regime critic (senior advisor at United Against Nuclear Iran), contrasts the June 2025 Israel–Iran war — a genuine setback that humiliated Tehran — with the February 2026 US confrontation, in which Washington's overwhelming superiority and maximal objectives failed to compel any ideological, regional, or strategic change in the Islamic Republic. He argues Iran leveraged one of its few remaining cards — closing the Strait of Hormuz to spike global energy-market fears — to pressure Washington into a quick exit, and that the resulting MOU trades real concessions (sanctions easing, the reported release of $12 billion in frozen Iranian assets) for Tehran merely reopening a strait that was open before Trump attacked. His framework: military force can degrade capabilities but cannot impose ideological transformation on a revolutionary regime whose overriding objective is survival, so Tehran is running Khamenei's "heroic flexibility" — a tactical retreat to preserve the regime and wait out conditions. The irony he presses is that Trump, who condemned Obama's 2015 deal for trading sanctions relief for too little, has now accepted an even less favorable settlement that leaves the regime politically intact and economically relieved.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is a solid, mechanism-first piece from a United Against Nuclear Iran (UANI) and Middle East Forum analyst — hostile to the regime, but the argument is structural rather than merely denunciatory. Its strongest element is the survival-maximizing model of revolutionary regimes: because Tehran treats regime survival as the overriding goal, it can absorb extraordinary punishment and wait, which means coercive air power can wreck capacity without producing the political surrender the campaign was for. The "lost the war AND the negotiation" framing is sharpened by Trump's own past taunt that Iran "never wins wars but never loses negotiations," and the "heroic flexibility" anchor (Khamenei's 2013 coinage for tactical accommodation that preserves strategic aims) is aptly applied.

Two calibrations. First, "lost the war" is doing rhetorical work the body partly walks back: the US did destroy Iranian military capacity and decapitate its leadership (as both Luttwak and Kahn note in this cluster), so the defensible claim is that Washington lost the political contest — Golkar is clear on this, but the title flattens it into a military verdict it doesn't fully earn. Second, the piece leans on unverified Iranian-sourced figures (the $12 billion in frozen assets), which he appropriately hedges ("if these reports turn out to be true"). The "incompetent administration" attribution is asserted more than shown, and is arguably the weaker leg: his structural argument — energy leverage plus a survival regime — would hold even under competent execution, which makes it the more durable claim.

In the cluster, Golkar converges with Cass and Kahn on the outcome — the regime survived or even tightened its grip, and the US is now the supplicant — but his diagnosis is distinct. Where Cass blames American domestic political constraint (no public will to bear the costs) and Kahn blames Netanyahu's isolation strategy, Golkar blames the mismatch between coercive tools and a regime built to outlast them. He stands directly opposite Luttwak, who reads the same defeat as a failure of nerve rather than a failure of premise, and opposite Ahmari, who reads the same MOU as a win; for Golkar, that MOU is precisely the "negotiation" Trump lost.

↩ Back to TOC

Israel Is the Big Loser of the Iran Deal

Sam Kahn · 2026-06-23 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The June 2026 US–Iran memorandum of understanding (MOU, signed mid-June) and Netanyahu's attempt to claim the war's goals were achieved.

Kahn opens with a long roster of losers — the Iranian people first, then Trump, the Republican Party, the Gulf states, US global reputation — before naming Israel and Netanyahu's foreign policy the absolute loser, since the Iran war was Netanyahu's bid for regime change and its collapse exposes the ruin of an isolation-first strategy. He marshals Israeli reaction (a Times of Israel editorial calling the deal a "catastrophic capitulation," far-right finance minister Bezalel Smotrich's denunciation) and Trump's profane turn on Netanyahu ("he has no fucking judgment") after an Israeli strike on Beirut nearly scuttled the deal. He situates this in a decades-long pattern of US presidents — Clinton, Biden, Obama's staff — privately cursing Netanyahu, then argues the post–October 7 goodwill was squandered: 156 countries now recognize Palestine, an ICC arrest warrant constrains Netanyahu's travel, and US public sympathy has shifted toward Palestinians for the first time. The strategic spine: Netanyahu's plan was to alienate every ally until Trump was the only one left and then act freely — but Trump's short attention span and willingness to "flip" turned Israel from "co-pilot" to "mere passenger," frozen out of the US–Iran talks, with an October Israeli election as the possible reset.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

The analytical spine here is sound and well-evidenced: an isolation-maximizing posture is structurally dangerous for a small state, and staking everything on one mercurial patron is a single point of failure that duly failed. Kahn supports it with hard markers — Israel frozen out of the truce talks (NYT's "co-pilot" to "mere passenger"), the ICC warrant blocking Netanyahu from the G7, the recognition cascade, the Gallup sympathy flip — rather than vibes. As an interpretive essay about grand strategy, it largely lands.

Watch the rhetoric, though. The centerpiece litany of presidents cursing Netanyahu (Clinton's "who's the fucking superpower here?", Biden's "bad fucking guy," the Obama-staff adjective list down to "chickenshit") is entertaining color but is closer to argument-by-anecdote and ad hominem than to evidence; the case stands without the profanity reel, which is doing affect work, not analytic work. There is also a mild superlative tension — "first and foremost" the Iranian people, then "the absolute unquestioned loser is Israel" — that resolves only if you read the first as most-sympathetic and the second as most-strategically-consequential; "absolute unquestioned loser" is itself the unfalsifiable-superlative register this whole cluster trades in.

In the cluster, Kahn and Ahmari report the same facts — Netanyahu frozen out, Israeli regime-change dead — with opposite valence: Kahn's Israeli catastrophe is Ahmari's restrainer vindication. Kahn and Golkar agree on most of the loser-list (Iranian people, Trump, Gulf states) but Kahn centers Israel where Golkar centers the American political defeat. And he shares with Cass the conviction that the war itself was the error, though Kahn frames it through Israeli grand strategy rather than Cass's American republic-vs-empire constraint.

↩ Back to TOC

JD Vance: Our new relationship with Iran

Sohrab Ahmari · 2026-06-25 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Original reporting — a post-summit interview with VP JD Vance aboard Air Force Two after the June 2026 Lake Lucerne (Switzerland) US–Iran peace talks.

Ahmari, granted access on the flight home, presents Vance as cautiously upbeat over a concrete win: an agreed channel for officers of Iran's Revolutionary Guards (IRGC) and US Central Command (CENTCOM) to "deconflict" disputes in Doha, which Ahmari argues could "remake the face of the Middle East." The piece's through-line is that a kinetic war paradoxically enabled a "quasi-normalization" of US–Iran relations, and that this vindicates Vance — the self-styled "team captain of the restrainers" — who had to defend a hot war he privately opposed by owning the diplomacy that followed it. Vance defends the new memorandum of understanding (MOU) against the charge that it is merely a weaker version of the JCPOA (Obama's 2015 nuclear deal), calling it a "generic, foundational" document and claiming Iran is now offering more rigorous inspections and the "elimination" of its enriched-uranium stockpile. Ahmari recasts a missing-handshake "snub" that hawks called a humiliation as Iranian domestic anxiety rather than hauteur, and reads Vance's line that "every nation in the region" has a right of self-defense as an astonishing index of how far Israel's standing has slipped. He names two threats to the arrangement — Netanyahu, whose regime-change "dreams" are now just dreams, and Iran's hardliners — while conceding the whole thing turns on "a galaxy-sized if."

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is access journalism in the reported-texture mode, and the texture does argumentative work. Ahmari notes he speaks Persian and has "nearly 20 years covering the region," then deploys overheard Iranian chatter, a malfunctioning-elevator joke, and Usha Vance reading on the bed to authenticate a thesis the on-record material can't carry on its own. The single overheard line — one diplomat's "inshallah, we'll get something done" — is made to support the large claim of "a genuine hunger for a deal that the mullahs' cold public statements rarely betray." That is selection-as-argument: anecdote standing in for a trend.

The piece is openly sympathetic to Vance and functions largely as a vehicle for Vance's own narrative — folding "the Iranian subplot into a longer narrative arc in which all things work together for the good" — a spin Ahmari is self-aware about ("for now, he's partially managed to pull the story back to his preferred plot line"). That self-awareness is the piece's integrity check, and it has two genuinely sharp observations independent of Vance's framing: that Washington, not just Tehran, is now the party pushing de-escalation, and that an American leader publicly invoking a non-Israeli nation's right of self-defense is a real marker of shifted alignment. The weakness is that the optimistic valence rests almost entirely on Vance's characterization of the MOU and on the existence of one as-yet-untested deconfliction channel.

In the cluster, Ahmari shares the factual core with the deal's critics — Netanyahu frozen out, Israeli regime-change dead, the Islamic Republic intact as a "permanent fixture" — but flips the valence: where Golkar and Kahn read that same outcome as capitulation and Israeli catastrophe, Ahmari reads it as restrainer vindication. He is the optimist-restrainer to Cass's grim-restrainer (Cass agrees the exit is the right call but insists the deal is plainly bad and the war the real humiliation). Notably, the "humiliation" of the missing handshake is the same word that travels across the cluster with opposite referents; Ahmari is the writer working to defuse it rather than wield it.

↩ Back to TOC

JD Vance Distances Himself From Trump

Ken Klippenstein · 2026-06-22 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: JD Vance's week-long media tour (The View, the Diary of a CEO podcast, The Blaze, Megyn Kelly, and Ross Douthat's New York Times podcast) ostensibly selling the Iran War.

Having watched the appearances, Klippenstein reads a consistent pattern of subtle distancing from Donald Trump aimed at a 2028 presidential run. He catalogs six tells: praising the admission of error ("you always have to acknowledge when you're wrong") — un-Trumpian; apologizing on The View for the "childless cat ladies" remark; citing immigration, Trump's signature issue, as an example of their "different styles"; declaring "I'd rather be too charitable than too cynical," against Trump's grievance politics; on Israel, calling it the "junior partner" to the American "senior partner," rejecting identical US-Israel goals, and telling Douthat "you can't just kill your way out of" every problem; and on Iran, ruling out fracturing the country into a "Persian Libya," which Klippenstein reads as pre-positioning a defense for not warning Trump about the Strait of Hormuz. The structural argument is that Vance's unique edge is being an elected VP who "can't be fired," unlike his rival Marco Rubio. The closer: Democrats should take Vance's "America First moderation" seriously as a pitch to the center, sincere or not.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is Klippenstein off his usual document-driven national-security beat and into media-watching political punditry, and it should be read as such. The mode of argument is pattern-assembly from public statements, and on those terms it is competently observed and plausibly argued. The freshest and most durable point is structural, not interpretive: the elected Vice President is constitutionally insulated from the "executive purge" that constrains appointed rivals like Secretary of State Rubio — a genuine asymmetry that does reshape 2028 primary calculus. The Israel material is the most concrete evidence, with the "junior partner"/"senior partner" framing and the pointed refusal of the word "antisemitism" reading as real, quotable departures from administration messaging.

The structural weakness is the frame's unfalsifiability: every Vance utterance is decoded as deliberate Trump-distancing, yet the same statements are equally consistent with a VP loyally softening the administration's image for hostile audiences — the "big-tent" outreach Klippenstein himself concedes — or with ordinary message discipline. His hedge, "whether or not he means a word of it," quietly admits the piece cannot distinguish a 2028 soft-launch from in-role good-cop messaging, but it treats ambiguous evidence as confirmatory anyway. The "Persian Libya" item is the weakest link: opposing the total collapse of a 90-million-person state is conventional realism, not obviously a critique of Trump, and folding it into the distancing pattern is mild scope inflation. There is no Straussian dimension — Klippenstein states his thesis (Vance is running, and distancing from Trump) directly and repeatedly, so it reads on literal content. Net: a sharp piece of political observation carried by one genuinely useful institutional insight, weakened by an interpretive lens that bends all evidence one way.

↩ Back to TOC

The Hormuz Strait Drone Attack and the Folly of Negotiating with Iran

Andrew C. McCarthy · 2026-06-26 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Iran's June 26, 2026 drone attack on a cargo ship in the Strait of Hormuz, and the Trump administration's post-ceasefire diplomatic posture (Vice President Vance's "deconfliction"/Doha-channel remarks).

McCarthy argues the February 2026 combined US-Israeli strikes killed Ayatollah Ali Khamenei and many Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) commanders but produced no regime change — the 1979 Khomeinist regime endures, having swiftly named the possibly-incapacitated (or dead) son Mojtaba Khamenei as successor precisely to signal continuity. His core premise is essentialist: the regime is by founding design an apocalyptic sharia-supremacist project whose anti-Americanism is doctrinal rather than transactional, with "Death to America" predating and independent of its hostility to Israel (he invokes bin Laden's post-Soviet pivot to target the US as the remaining superpower). From this he concludes the administration's "really cool" face-to-face meetings and "forever wars" framing are self-defeating — "the enemy gets a vote," and a regime that has lately killed ~40,000 of its own to hold power cannot be a stable treaty partner that two-thirds of the Senate could ratify. His prescription is regime change as the strategic objective pursued through every lever of US power (sanctions, pariah-status, occasional force on the model of Reagan's 1988 tanker-war strikes), explicitly not invasion or occupation. He also recodes the realist/restraint view — that US-Iran enmity is largely downstream of the Israel relationship — as a shared "antisemitic" claim of the progressive left and a MAGA fringe.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is McCarthy doubling down on a position he says he and fellow Iran hawks have held "for decades," and the piece earns its place as the maximalist hawk pole of the Iran cluster (alongside Luttwak). Its spine is a doctrinal-essentialist claim: because the regime's legitimating ideology is anti-American apocalypticism, no incentive structure can move it. The strongest version is hard to dismiss on its own terms — a regime so constituted does have weak incentives to make durable deals, and the post-strike survival plus the rushed Mojtaba installation (as McCarthy presents them) genuinely support "no regime change happened."

The structural weakness is that the thesis is close to unfalsifiable as stated. "Death to America is foundational" plus "the enemy gets a vote" means any Iranian conciliation reads as a "deceptive negotiating ploy" and any aggression reads as confirmation — there is no Iranian behavior that would count as evidence against the framework. The essentialism also does heavy lifting: by collapsing IRGC strategic self-interest, factional politics, and the regime's own admission that it has 90 million disaffected subjects into a single theological will, McCarthy forecloses the possibility his own facts suggest — that a regime this damaged and this domestically hated might be coercible precisely because bare survival, not revolution-export, has become its operative goal.

The prescription is the thinnest part. "Regime change, but not invasion — turn every lever to make it a pariah and eventually break it" is asserted, not specified, and it is essentially the maximum-pressure approach whose decades-long track record (sanctions plus isolation without collapse) the piece does not reckon with. The Reagan tanker-war and Trump nuclear-strike precedents he cites are episodic military actions, not a theory of how sustained external pressure topples a regime that has just demonstrated it can absorb decapitation and survive. Finally, the paratext doing the most rhetorical work is the early move linking "the mainstream Democratic Party (antisemitic progressives)" and an "antisemitic fringe of Trump's MAGA base" into one coalition: it lets McCarthy treat the realist claim that US-Iran enmity is substantially downstream of Israel as bigotry rather than a foreign-policy disagreement, pre-empting the strongest non-hawk objection by recoding it. That is a category-substitution, not an argument, and it is the analytically weakest paragraph in an otherwise forceful essay.

↩ Back to TOC

Two Drinks with . . . the Senator Warning Trump That Iran 'Cannot Be Trusted'

Audrey Fahlberg · 2026-06-26 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Trump's G7 remarks calling Iran's leaders "very rational" and "not radicalized," amid US–Iran ceasefire negotiations; Free Press "Two Drinks with…" interview/profile.

In a profile built around drinks in his Senate office, Fahlberg interviews Montana senator Tim Sheehy — a Navy SEAL combat veteran on Senate Armed Services — who pushes back on Trump's suggestion that Iran's leadership has moderated. Sheehy argues that whatever the personnel changes after Israel and the US killed the regime's prior leaders, its worldview is unchanged ("they still want us dead"), so any deal "cannot be a piece of paper and a bunch of trust." He grounds the claim in personal experience — friends killed by Iranian explosively formed penetrators (EFPs) in Iraq, memorial bracelets he still wears — and contrasts Trump's deal (which he says guarantees Iran up to $300B in Gulf investment contingent on compliance) favorably against Obama's JCPOA, which he says funded the "beast." He credits Trump (the 2020 Soleimani strike, the 12-day war) while warning negotiators not to trust the regime. The piece also reports intra-GOP friction (a Trump–Cassidy shouting match, a war-powers resolution that narrowly failed 47–50–1) and closes with Sheehy insinuating that Iran-skeptic conservative media (e.g., Tucker Carlson) may be foreign-funded. But the analysis flags that the piece's persuasive work is genre, not argument: it offers a single post-ceasefire incident (a Hormuz cargo-ship attack) as editorial "vindication" of a categorical claim about regime intent, and lets Sheehy's unsupported foreign-funding insinuation against fellow conservatives stand unchallenged — so the "Iran can't be trusted, as events proved" frame should not be treated as established.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is an access-journalism profile, and the reported texture — the Coors Banquet, the silver bracelets, the Arlington burial — is doing the argumentative work. Sheehy's combat biography functions as the warrant for a policy claim ("don't trust Iran") that is, on its merits, conventional hawkish boilerplate. The substantive content is thin and effectively unfalsifiable (stay skeptical); the value of the piece is the texture plus genuine intra-GOP color (the luncheon blowup, the precise war-powers vote math).

The reporter's thumb is visible. Fahlberg validates her subject's frame editorially: "His fears were warranted. The day after our interview, the Iranians attacked a cargo ship in the Strait of Hormuz." A single subsequent incident is offered as vindication of a categorical claim about regime intentions — a post hoc confirmation that a profile, unlike an argument, can get away with. The selection of one corroborating event stands in for proof.

The closing insinuation is the weakest move and goes unchallenged in the piece. Sheehy suggests Iran-skeptic conservatives may be "paid" and that "Middle Eastern friends have invested a lot of money in our university networks and media landscape to push a narrative" — an unsupported claim of foreign-funded disloyalty aimed at fellow conservatives. Its irony is unremarked: the deal Sheehy praises is itself underwritten by Gulf money. And the strongest counter to his stance — that maximal distrust forecloses any negotiated constraint, and that the deal's conditionality already encodes his "don't fund the beast" concern — goes unvoiced, because the format isn't built to host a rebuttal.

↩ Back to TOC

Somalia in the Crosshairs: the Consequences of the Iran War on Somalia

Surer Mohamed Nisar Majid Ahmed Ibrahim Faduma Abukar · 2026-06-24 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: the US-Israel war on Iran and the blockade/closure of the Strait of Hormuz, read for its spillover into the Horn of Africa.

The piece argues that geographic proximity, poverty, and political fragility make Somalia unusually exposed on two tracks. Geopolitically, the war re-underlines the strategic value of maritime chokepoints, and Somalia's politics are already shaped by competition over its coastline near the Bab el-Mandab Strait (the gateway between the Gulf of Aden and the Red Sea); regional powers (UAE, Ethiopia, Turkey, Egypt, Israel) have raced to build commercial and military ports and to invest in domestic factions, with Israel's December 2025 recognition of the breakaway region Somaliland — including an Israeli base — and possible US normalization with Eritrea cited as the latest moves likely to deepen fragmentation and undermine fragile statebuilding. On cost of living, an import-dependent economy is absorbing fuel prices up ~77%, freight from India (its main rice/sugar/flour source) roughly doubled, and container costs from Turkey tripled as ships reroute around Africa; this lands hardest on the displaced and poorest amid a pre-war famine risk from poor rains and aid cuts. The authors note mitigating factors — remittances (30-50% of GNP, reaching ~40% of households) are unaffected and livestock exports stayed stable through a near-normal Hajj — and, unusually, flag that "some analysts were privately warning that the risk of famine was being over-stated."

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

The value here is the alternative information supply chain, not a contrarian thesis. While Anglophone commentary frames the Iran war's economic fallout through Hormuz and oil futures, this piece reports it through the price of nails for a youth recreation pitch, tuk-tuk fares, and a named importer's freight-cost data — a materialist, periphery-level vantage the Western press almost never supplies. The chokepoint-competition frame (Bab el-Mandab as "the next Hormuz") is plausible and anchored in specific, verifiable recent events: Turkey's deep-sea drilling vessels at Mogadishu, UAE investment in Berbera, the Israel-Somaliland recognition. The cost-of-living half is the stronger half — concrete, near-term, and evidenced.

The geopolitical half is more speculative, leaning on a chain of soft forecasts ("likely to generate further scrambling," "may well further intensify," "could be further entangled"). These are reasonable structural predictions rather than demonstrated causation, and the two halves sit somewhat loosely together — a well-evidenced near-term price story bolted to a longer-horizon fragmentation thesis. What raises the piece's credibility above typical NGO-adjacent crisis reporting is its self-correction: it explicitly catalogs the mitigants (remittances insulated from the Gulf war, stable livestock sales, food supply "minimally disrupted… so far") and surfaces the dissent that famine risk may be overstated. That willingness to undercut its own alarm is rare and worth carrying into the synthesis as a model of calibrated regional reporting. Not flawed; the contribution is the data and the vantage, not a forecast that can be scored.

↩ Back to TOC

The Mamdani sweep: the left-populist insurgency

Mamdani Sweeps; Party Weeps

Ken Klippenstein · 2026-06-24 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: the June 23 New York Democratic primary, in which all three congressional candidates endorsed by NYC mayor Zohran Mamdani won (two unseating party-backed incumbents) while Democratic leaders Hakeem Jeffries, Chuck Schumer, and Gov. Kathy Hochul failed to deliver a single race.

Klippenstein rejects the media framing of the night as a win for the left, the Democratic Socialists of America (DSA), Mamdani, or Bernie Sanders; he reads it instead as a victory for "the common voter" refusing party-approved operators. He pre-empts the "it's only New York" dismissal by arguing the lesson isn't that the city is turning left but that even there, voters reached past the gatekeepers — evidence the New York/DC party's monopoly on candidate selection is waning nationwide. He generalizes the indictment to both parties (predicting neither will recover) and anchors it in Washington's Farewell Address, which warns that the "spirit of party" enfeebles administration and eventually drives an exhausted public to "seek security and repose in the absolute power of an individual" — a "Sound familiar?" gesture toward Trump. He closes with his recurring thesis that the press won't cover the "tyranny of party control" because, like the national-security state, it is "an appendage of it," pivoting to a subscription/GoFundMe pitch for independent journalism.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

The piece's signature move is reframing: Klippenstein converts an outcome the left, the DSA, and the Sanders world are all claiming as their own into a content-neutral, anti-gatekeeper revolt. This serves his standing brief — he is reliably adversarial toward institutional power, usually the national-security state, here ported onto the party machine. The reframing has real purchase. The losers are genuine establishment heavyweights (Jeffries, Schumer, Hochul), and the "even in New York, voters ignored the anointed picks" observation captures something true about establishment weakness.

But the framework strains against the very facts it cites. The winners were a coordinated slate — Mamdani-endorsed candidates — which Stoller, writing the same news cycle, describes as a DSA "machine" with a "kingmaker." Voters did not reach past gatekeepers into some unmediated common will; they switched to a rival, ascendant faction with its own endorsements, organization, and litmus tests. Klippenstein's "common voter vs. party operators" binary cannot see the new operators, because admitting them would collapse the anti-party frame. The Washington citation papers over the same gap: the Farewell Address warns that faction leads an exhausted public toward "the absolute power of an individual," yet a charismatic individual's endorsement slate sweeping a primary fits that dynamic at least as well as it opposes it. The "Sound familiar?" points at Trump while the piece is celebrating a different charismatic kingmaker.

The closer recycles Klippenstein's characteristic structural claim — the media cannot discuss "the tyranny of party control" for the same reason it cannot discuss the national-security state, "because it is an appendage of it" — immediately followed by the subscribe/GoFundMe pitch for "genuinely independent" journalism. That self-credentialing is the operative paratext: the payoff is less an analysis of the primary than a positioning of Klippenstein outside the system he describes. This is not Straussian — the thesis is stated plainly throughout — so it reads on literal content as a sharp restatement of a real anti-establishment mood that under-theorizes what replaced the establishment.

↩ Back to TOC

Mamdani Is Trumpifying the Democrats

Geoff Shullenberger · 2026-06-25 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The June 2026 New York City primaries — Zohran Mamdani's mayoral win, Graham Platner's Maine Senate primary, and three "mini-Mamdani" NYC congressional victories (Claire Valdez over Brooklyn Borough President Antonio Reynoso, plus two against incumbents).

Shullenberger reads these upsets through a decade-long GOP parallel: Dave Brat's 2014 primary defeat of Eric Cantor over immigration prefigured Trump's 2016 hostile takeover, while Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez's 2018 defeat of Joe Crowley looked like a Democratic "Brat summer" that fizzled. The Democratic establishment held the line — Cynthia Nixon, Zephyr Teachout, and Bernie Sanders all repelled — right through Kamala Harris's 2024 run, vindicating the 2008 political-science book The Party Decides for Democrats long after Trump had falsified it for Republicans. The takeaway of 2026, he argues, is that for Democrats too "the party" no longer decides. He casts immigration (GOP) and support for Israel (Democrats) as structurally parallel wedge issues: in each case the populist charge is that resources meant for "us" flow to a disfavored foreign group, sometimes framed in ethnic terms, and in each case bigotry accusations (racism then, antisemitism now) prove counterproductive as a "battering ram." He suggests Trump, having "settled into" the establishment he once wrecked, is now at his most vulnerable, and reads Trump's affection for Mamdani as "paternal recognition." Bracketing his own mixed view of the socialists' substance (some positions "reasonable," others "bizarre and repellent"), he welcomes the erosion of establishment gatekeeping as "fundamentally salutary": "Democracy lives, despite the best efforts of its self-proclaimed defenders."

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

The structural spine is a real and well-observed divergence. Both parties produced a neophyte-beats-grandee upset in the same window (Brat 2014, AOC 2018), but the GOP establishment then collapsed at presidential scale in 2016 while the Democratic establishment absorbed every left challenge for another eight years. Shullenberger's "the party decides" obituary — invoking the 2008 Cohen-Karol-Noel-Zaller thesis that internal elites, not voters, pick nominees — is a genuinely useful frame for dating that divergence, and the secondary observation that Trump has now "settled into" the establishment he overthrew (and is weaker for it) is a sharp, non-obvious read of his current position.

The load-bearing and most contestable move is the immigration/Israel equivalence. To make the two insurgencies one "populist formula," Shullenberger compresses both into "resources meant for 'us' going to a disfavored foreign group," which lets him equate the antisemitism charges against Mamdani with the racism charges against Trump's restrictionism and predict both will fail as a "battering ram." The flattening does a lot of work: anti-immigration restrictionism reallocates membership in the polity itself, whereas opposition to U.S. support for Israel is a foreign-policy dispute about an ally abroad — collapsing the two to "us vs. disfavored foreigners" is what makes the parallel feel tight, and it is precisely the symmetry that should be resisted. It is also a both-sides move characteristic of Compact's house style: treating "save democracy" Democrats as hypocritical gatekeepers and bracketing the substance of what the insurgents believe.

The other weakness is the sampling. The evidence base for declaring the national "party decides" thesis dead is a NYC mayoral race plus a handful of deep-blue New York House primaries — terrain uniquely favorable to the socialist left, where insurgents enjoy structural advantages that don't generalize to the median Democratic district. Shullenberger hedges ("where exactly the Trumpification will lead will likely take a decade"), but the headline claim outruns the sample. None of this makes the piece flawed at the core; it is a defensible interpretive analogy whose central claim survives, with a contestable equivalence and an over-extrapolated conclusion to flag. It is not Straussian — the operative valuation (proceduralist celebration of establishment erosion, explicitly decoupled from agreement with the agenda) is stated plainly in the kicker, so it is analyzed on literal content.

↩ Back to TOC

Democrats Are Done With Caution

Michelle Goldberg · 2026-06-24 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The June 2026 New York City Democratic congressional primaries, in which all three candidates endorsed by Mayor Zohran Mamdani won.

Goldberg opens with the weakest of the three winners, Darializa Avila Chevalier — a prison-abolitionist Democratic Socialists of America (DSA) member who opposed all deportations and would not say whether murderers should be jailed — yet who beat Congressional Hispanic Caucus chair Adriano Espaillat on a "left-wing tsunami." She reads this, plus progressive wins in Seattle, Washington D.C., and Maine (Graham Platner's Senate-nomination upset; Matt Dunlap in a Trump-won district), as a "Democratic version of the Tea Party": a base radicalized by Trump's second term, furious at a feckless establishment, and turning against Israel after Gaza. She frames the 2026 midterms as a national test of the populist left's "theory of victory" — that Democrats lost by offering timid, corporate-friendly incrementalism. But she cautions this is anxious ground: the DSA also surged after Trump's first term (AOC, Tlaib, Omar in 2018), then collapsed amid sectarian Palestine infighting and a centrist backlash (Jamaal Bowman and Cori Bush primary losses), and Blueprint Research found swing voters saw Kamala Harris as soft on crime/border and "too focused on identity politics." Her central distinction: Mamdani's pragmatic, materially-focused "sewer socialism" builds, whereas Avila Chevalier's "academic leftism" — rigid, concession-averse, refusing trade-offs — invites backlash, and the danger is a movement flush with success thinks it doesn't have to choose.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

Goldberg is a center-left NYT columnist sympathetic to progressive goals but reliably anxious about electability, and this piece doubles down on that signature move: hand-wringing from the left flank. Its analytical payload is the sewer-socialism-vs-academic-leftism distinction (Mamdani's focus on tangible material concerns like rent and transit, versus a purist activism that refuses concessions to "ordinary people's moral intuitions"), and the 2018-2024 historical parallel is genuinely informative and falsifiable rather than merely rhetorical.

The structural tension is that the column wants both to celebrate and to warn, and the warning rests on a contested causal claim — that "progressive overreach" cost Democrats in 2024 — which she sources to Blueprint polling but which the populist-left figures she quotes (Justice Democrats' Usamah Andrabi, who blames Harris's "uninspiring centrism") directly dispute. Goldberg airs the disagreement honestly but does not adjudicate it; the column's real empirical question — did the left or the center lose 2024? — is left open, so the "danger" framing is asserted more than demonstrated. This is not a fundamental flaw but a deliberate hedge.

The strongest opposing view, which she gives voice to, is Wisconsin's Francesca Hong: that electability is subjective and the left-right map fails to capture voters who moved Sanders→Trump→Mamdani. Goldberg concedes it lands ("Hong is right that many voters can't be mapped onto a neat left-right spectrum") yet keeps her anxiety — the appropriately honest position for a columnist who cannot resolve the question her own piece poses.

↩ Back to TOC

The Democrats have their own MAGA now

Noahpinion · 2026-06-26 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: the New York Democratic primary, in which Mamdani-endorsed congressional candidate Darializa Avila Chevalier and two others won (unseating incumbents), set against a broader Democratic Socialists of America (DSA) electoral surge in blue cities.

Smith opens with a dossier of Avila Chevalier's deleted social-media posts — her group Columbia University Apartheid Divest (CUAD) calling for "the total eradication of Western civilization," "Death to America" in Persian, calls to abolish police and prisons and nationalize industries — to argue an extremist on par with Marjorie Taylor Greene is headed to Congress as a Democrat. He insists this isn't a fluke: DSA candidates are winning across blue cities, so the Democrats "now have their own MAGA," an insurgent hard-left populist faction opposing the establishment. He explains the rise on two tracks: right-wing extremism enables left-wing extremism (anti-Trump backlash opens electoral space), and mainstream liberalism, though it mostly succeeded on policy (welfare expansion, the Affordable Care Act, gay marriage), failed in subtler ways — overproducing a downwardly-mobile credentialed "precariat" (a "revolution of rising expectations"), lacking a foreign policy, failing to stop Trump, then alienating both base and swing voters through "wokeness." He argues the two historic American lefts — European-transplant class socialism and homegrown identitarian progressivism — have fused, with Palestine (not Trump) as the animating spark, because it is the one wedge the establishment cannot triangulate: Biden adopted much of Bernie Sanders's economic agenda yet socialists hated him more, since the movement prizes who controls the party over policy wins. Smith predicts the DSA will govern pragmatically (citing Mamdani abandoning costly promises and backing small business) while keeping Palestine as an immovable litmus test, elevating what he calls "Islamo-leftism" (Islam invigorating the leftist cause, not the reverse) and, where it gains foreign-policy power, leaning toward Russia and China and against Ukraine and Taiwan. He holds the movement wins more on backlash than genuine popularity, since its ideas repel swing voters even as Democratic primary voters keep moving left.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

Smith's own causal apparatus undercuts his headline. His 2016 thesis was that "Trump happened because conservatism failed" — MAGA grew from the wreckage of Reaganite economics, foreign policy, and social conservatism. But he concedes mainstream liberalism succeeded. To preserve the MAGA parallel he must relocate the "failure" into subtler registers: overproduction of a downwardly-mobile credentialed class, the absence of a foreign policy, the failure to stop Trump, woke overreach. Several are genuinely illuminating — the precariat point ("revolution of rising expectations") is his strongest, most materialist move. But they describe a movement arising from success-plus-disappointment, not collapse, which is a different engine than MAGA's. The analogy survives at the level of form (insurgent, anti-establishment, backlash-fueled, base-popular but swing-toxic) while flattening content: it equates a faction Smith predicts will "govern surprisingly pragmatically" with the movement he elsewhere in the piece calls a "walking disaster."

The piece's most interesting claim is also its hedge. Smith calls the DSA "as much of an extremist as anyone associated with the MAGA movement," then predicts it will shed its maximal program in office (Mamdani dropping government grocery stores, supporting small business) because "American socialism is ideologically plastic" and "what matters is winning elections." This is close to self-refuting: if the economic radicalism evaporates on contact with governance, the MAGA-grade danger has to be re-housed, and Smith re-houses it in (a) foreign policy — predicted Russia/China sympathy, hostility to Ukraine and Taiwan — and (b) a counterfactual: "would the DSA implement Venezuela-style socialism if they controlled the Presidency and both houses of Congress? Probably, yes." That counterfactual is unfalsifiable and carries the load-bearing alarm precisely because the observable evidence (pragmatic mayors) points the other way.

The strongest analytic contribution is the Palestine argument: that it, not Trump, is the animating spark because it is the one issue the establishment cannot triangulate. Biden adopted much of Bernie's economic agenda (loan forgiveness, climate spending) yet socialists hated him more, which Smith explains through factionalism — control of the party matters more than policy wins. That is a sharp resolution of a real puzzle. It sits on a weaker foundation, though: the whole piece is anchored by Avila Chevalier's deleted tweets, the single most extreme available exhibit, generalized to characterize a faction's ideology. Smith pre-empts the "lone wacky person" objection by gesturing at the DSA wave, but the move from "one primary winner posted unhinged things online years ago" to "the Democrats have their own MAGA" is selection-as-argument — the synecdoche carries weight the systematic evidence (membership charts, socialism-vs-capitalism polling) does not quite supply on its own.

Finally, "Islamo-leftism": Smith introduces a contested neologism and spends a footnote distancing it from "Judeo-bolshevism," insisting leftism (not Islam) is "in the driver's seat." Adopting the term while disavowing its nearest analogue is a tell that he knows it's radioactive; the analytical payload (Muslim professionals and converts gaining Democratic influence at the expense of older Black-Christian and Jewish leaders) could be stated without the loaded coinage, which imports more than the evidence licenses. On the week's central axis, Smith is the pole that reads left-populism as an extremist liability riding backlash — toxic to swing voters — against Stoller, who treats the identical wins as a legitimate anti-oligarchy turn whose only open question is governing capacity. The two are not symmetric: Stoller argues about power and capability, Smith about ideology and electability, so they largely talk past each other. But they converge on one point — skepticism that the wins translate into substance — and split on its valence: for Smith the non-delivery is reassuring (the radicalism is contained), where for Stoller it is the tragedy to be averted.

↩ Back to TOC

BOOM: Left-Wing Populists Just Smashed Incumbents in Elections Last Night. But Can They Govern?

Matt Stoller · 2026-06-24 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: the June 23 primaries in New York and Maryland, where left-wing challengers ousted Democratic incumbents — DSA's Claire Valdez beat Brooklyn borough president Antonio Reynoso by 25+ points; Darializa Avila Chevalier unseated Congressional Hispanic Caucus chair Adriano Espaillat — wins framed in the press as a triumph for "kingmaker" NYC mayor Zohran Mamdani.

Stoller reads the results as part of a popular turn against oligarchy (citing an NBC poll where 54% agreed "nothing really matters because powerful people will always do whatever they want," and Brandeis and a Daron Acemoglu model on the tension between concentrated wealth and democracy). But he argues the real risk is not that elections get canceled — it's that the new leaders will lack the tools to govern, citing Alexander Hamilton outmaneuvering a populist Congress that wouldn't learn how Treasury worked, and Maryland Gov. Wes Moore signing an industry-written "surveillance pricing" ban that does nothing. He answers his own "can they govern?" question with a worked example: a just-filed California class action against Walmart and other chains over Kalibrate "fuel pricing software" — algorithmic "Price Fixing Tech" that allegedly raised gas prices by up to 30 cents a gallon — built on a new state law (AB 325) banning common pricing algorithms and enforceable by private citizens, assembled by ex-FTC enforcers, state-agency evidence, and anti-monopoly journalism. The lesson: oligarchy is beaten "one bite at a time" through law, detail, and enforcement, by building a cadre that knows the levers — capacity the U.S. hasn't had in a populist environment since the 1970s. Stoller is candidly "mildly pessimistic" about whether the new winners can build it before 2029.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

Stoller's signature is the mechanism-first, show-the-receipts case study, and the Kalibrate section is both the piece's payload and its best argument: a concrete account of how algorithmic price coordination ("Price Fixing Tech") works, why it raises prices specifically where competition exists (the German and Canadian findings), and how one lawsuit was assembled. The "atoms not bits" texture — AB 325's statutory hook in the 1940 Socony-Vacuum precedent, its private right of action, the ex-Lina-Khan FTC litigators at Simonsen Sussman, the California Energy Commission evidence, the Capitol Forum reporting that exposed the company — is the actual demonstration that anti-monopoly governance is a learnable craft: "nobody knew how to do this kind of work in 2021... they do now."

But there's a quiet bait-and-switch on the headline. The piece is built around "can they govern?" and opens by diagnosing a capability deficit in the newly elected populists ("well-meaning but information thin new leaders"). The worked example, however, illustrates governance by an entirely different cast — private litigators, holdover enforcers, state agencies, journalists — none of them the freshly elected DSA winners. That is defensible as a thesis (governance is an ecosystem, not the heroics of individual electeds), but it sidesteps the question actually posed: whether Mamdani's slate specifically can do this work. Stoller's honest "I'm mildly pessimistic" and "I'm not sure there's enough time... before 2029" concede the gap rather than close it, which paradoxically strengthens the piece's credibility even as it leaves the title's promise unmet.

The case doubles as a BIG origin story. Stoller foregrounds that his outlet flagged Price Fixing Tech for years (Lee Hepner's 2023 piece; his own 2014 Naked Capitalism post), and that Hepner helped write AB 325 — selection-as-argument that casts the anti-monopoly movement, and his own shop, as the protagonists of the populist-governance story. It's earned given the track record, but worth seeing as positioning. Note too the epistemic hygiene that distinguishes Stoller from the alarm-merchants: "as Louis Brandeis ostensibly said," the Acemoglu paper labeled a "cheeky theoretical framework" rather than proof, the theory-to-primary link offered as something that "suggests" tension rather than causes it. He doesn't oversell the model.

On the week's central axis, Stoller is the pole that treats left-populism as the legitimate — perhaps the only — answer to oligarchy, with the open question being not whether the ideas are sound but whether the movement can build the technical-legal capacity to deliver. Against Smith, who reads the same primaries as the Democrats' MAGA and an extremist liability, the two pieces share exactly one premise — skepticism that electoral wins automatically become substance (Smith via "promises that never get implemented," Stoller via the capability gap) — and split on valence: for Smith the likely non-delivery is reassuring, because it means the radicalism stays contained; for Stoller it is the tragedy to be averted.

↩ Back to TOC

Democrats Go Off the Socialist Deep End

NR Editors · 2026-06-24 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: the June 23, 2026 New York Democratic primaries (Zohran Mamdani- and DSA-backed wins).

The Editors read the results — Darializa Avila Chevalier toppling Rep. Adriano Espaillat (chair of the Congressional Hispanic Caucus), Claire Valdez beating Borough President Antonio Reynoso by 20 points, and Brad Lander beating Rep. Dan Goldman by 30 in a race centered on Goldman's support for Israel — as proof that radical-left populism is now an "epidemic," no longer the containable outbreak it briefly seemed after Jamaal Bowman and Cori Bush lost in 2024 and pundits declared "Peak Woke" over. Their causal account is sociological: the new candidates win by trading working-class, black, and Hispanic support for affluent white voters, reflecting a historically dangerous class of educated-but-underemployed, downwardly mobile resentful young people ("grown adults sponging off their parents") among whom antisemitism recurs as the scapegoat of "envious and conspiratorial minds." Their central comparative thesis: right-populism appeals to preserving or restoring the existing order, which sets outer limits on its excesses, whereas left-populism aims at the order's destruction, forms alliances of convenience with Islamists out of shared animus toward Western civilization, and is driven by a "vanguard of the young, idle, and discontented" — revolutionary furies that "rage before they burn out." They cite Goldman's concession-speech warning about "antisemitic tropes" and Chevalier's "Israel doesn't exist!" and flag-wiping quotes as evidence.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is the NR editorial in pure institutional-conservative mode, and its function in the roundup is exactly that: the establishment Right's voice in the left-populism cluster, the counterpoint to whatever Mamdani-sympathetic or left-populist sources argue. Its strongest analytic content is the coalition-composition observation — that DSA-backed candidates increasingly win by overperforming with affluent, white, educated voters while underperforming with the poor, black, and Hispanic voters they claim to champion. If the cited results hold up, that is a real and citable pattern, and it is the empirical core other sources in the cluster have to answer.

The structural problem is that the organizing metaphor — "epidemic," "symptoms," "spread," "too late to stop" — does argumentative work the evidence does not carry. A handful of results in the most left-wing urban electorate in the country are generalized to "the mainstream Democratic Party" without the national data that would distinguish a citywide realignment from a party-wide one. The Editors note that anti-Trump-brand candidates (George Conway) also lost, reading it as the base "looking past the president," but that observation cuts against the epidemic frame as easily as for it — it could equally read as primary voters rewarding a policy program over pure anti-Trump positioning.

The weakest move is the psychologizing. The "downwardly mobile, resentful, sponging off their parents" portrait and the Le Bon-style "young, idle, and discontented" vanguard substitute a class-character diagnosis for any account of why these candidates' actual program (affordability, housing, anti-corporate populism) is winning votes — explaining the candidates by the alleged pathology of their supporters rather than engaging the substance. That is the mirror image of the move conservatives rightly object to when it is aimed at the MAGA base, and the asymmetry claim built on it — that right-populism is self-limiting while left-populism is uniquely existential — is asserted rather than argued, sitting uneasily beside NR's own decade of warnings about right-populist excess (acknowledged here in a single clause and then minimized). The antisemitism thread is the most substantive specific charge because it is anchored to particular statements (the Goldman-race dynamics, Chevalier's quotes) rather than to a sociological type, and it lands harder than the class-resentment theorizing for exactly that reason.

↩ Back to TOC

The Democrats' strategic reckoning

The paradoxes of populism

Matthew Yglesias · 2026-06-26 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: a Sunday-thread reader mailbag (anchored by reader Amos Karlsen's question about contradictions in public opinion), plus the week's Democratic Socialists of America primary wins in New York City.

Yglesias opens by downplaying the NYC DSA victories — the moderates they beat were themselves well left of center, so "I don't know what the stakes are," and the real work is building the next moderate-reformist thing rather than defending out-of-fashion progressives. The intellectual core answers a reader's puzzle (voters reject tradeoffs yet think in zero-sum terms; they are cynical about incumbents yet credulous about unrealistic promises) via the "stealth democracy" thesis of political scientists Elizabeth Theiss-Morse and John Hibbing: most people believe political conflict itself signals that bad actors are involved, since people of goodwill should simply be able to solve problems. That premise, he argues, reconciles the paradoxes — if conflict is illegitimate, then hard tradeoffs must be the work of corrupt forces (immigrants, billionaires, USAID, Israel), and electing "good people" should make problems vanish. He extends the frame to argue the insurgent left's "get rid of corrupt politicians and we all get this stuff for free" promise is the mirror image of MAGA's "deport immigrants and balance the budget," and is quite unlike actual Nordic social democracy, which rests on explicit tradeoffs (cheaper social goods, pricier consumer goods). Shorter mailbag answers cover urban Democrats' statewide ceiling (a reformer profile beats an establishment-custodian one), why only executives have an incentive to deliver results, nuclear cargo ships (not worth the cost premium), build-to-rent housing, eminent domain (allow voluntary sale, never force redevelopment), and what Democrats should do if they win Congress.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This doubles down on Yglesias's standing popularist-abundance pattern rather than departing from it; the mailbag format simply scatters it across topics. The "stealth democracy" frame is the strongest element — a genuine and portable explanation — and to his credit he applies it symmetrically to left and right rather than scoring only against his usual targets. The structural-symmetry move (insurgent-left free-stuff promise = MAGA deport-and-balance promise) is a real insight, and the Nordic point — that actually existing social democracy is built on conscious tradeoffs, not their elimination — is a fair corrective to a common rhetorical sleight.

The framework's weakness is its near-unfalsifiability: once "voters treat conflict as evidence of bad faith" is granted, almost any public attitude can be retrofitted to it, and Yglesias never specifies what observation would count against the thesis. The reading is also self-confirming in a way that always lands on his prior — every example resolves to "moderate reformers building the next thing are the real story." Note too the recurring move by which left disappointment is pre-empted: the Obama-"betrayed-them" passage frames anyone let down by a progressive champion as simply having "met reality," which forecloses the possibility that the champion compromised more than necessary. The opening dismissal of the NYC results ("I don't know what the stakes are") sits in productive tension with his companion piece "The narrow path to saving the republic," where insurgent-left candidates in swing states are treated as potentially fatal; the reconciliation (safe-seat insurgents are harmless, swing-state ones aren't) is coherent but underlines how much of the framework is keyed to electability math rather than the substance of the disagreements. The "results" answer — that the incentive structure of backbench legislators in safe seats militates against problem-solving, leaving executives as the only real constituency for outcomes — is the sharpest, most concrete passage and feeds the synthesis well.

↩ Back to TOC

What I learned as Chuck Schumer’s intern

Matthew Yglesias · 2026-06-23 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: original commentary — a memoir of Yglesias's 2001 internship in Senator Chuck Schumer's office, framed as recoverable lessons for a Democratic Party that has stopped thinking about persuasion.

Yglesias recalls a less-polarized era when New York had a Republican governor and NYC a Republican mayor, so every statewide office was contestable and ambitious politicians reflexively thought about swing voters.
