He draws several lessons from Schumer's shop: persuadable voters don't follow politics (so the high-value play was getting Schumer onto local TV like WIVT in Binghamton via a Joe Torre blood-donation event, not the Sunday shows watched by hard-to-move junkies); people like small wins (the Verizon *66/*69 hidden-fee crusade, an FCC letter that made for a big press conference despite being trivial); and Schumer was comfortable both with the wins being small and with a "problem-solving" posture rather than an ideological one. He contrasts this with the modern anti-scam tradition of Elizabeth Warren, Rohit Chopra, and Lina Khan, who "wear their ideologies on their sleeve" and earned a reputation as anti-business crusaders, where Schumer would have banned the abusive practice and then said nice things about everyone. The structural diagnosis: as swing seats, crossover voters, and ticket-splitting disappeared, persuasion became a niche pursuit and coalition-management became the only thing that matters — producing politicians like Newsom and Harris who never beat a Republican in a contested race. The lesson is that "the purpose of fighting is to win," and you don't win contested voters by seeming stubborn.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

The credential is the argument here, and that is the thing to watch. "What I learned as Chuck Schumer's intern" converts a contestable strategic claim — that Democrats should be tonally moderate problem-solvers rather than ideological fighters — into received wisdom from a master practitioner, authorized by firsthand proximity. The reported texture (fax machines, speed-dial codes, *66/*69, WIVT Binghamton, the "Ask your parents" footnotes) is charming and does real rhetorical work: it disarms, recasting a thesis as a fond reminiscence that is harder to argue with than an argument would be. This is paratext doing argumentative labor, and it doubles down on Yglesias's popularism while disguising it as memoir.

On the merits, the durable insight is the media-habits point — that the voters worth persuading are precisely the ones not watching political programming — which is well-illustrated and broadly correct, and the causal story (contestable statewide races bred persuasion-oriented politicians; their disappearance bred coalition-managers) is a genuine structural observation rather than nostalgia alone. The weak link is the Warren contrast, which is tendentious and self-serving: Schumer's *66 stunt and Warren's consumer-protection work are arguably the same genre of populist consumer advocacy, differing mainly in affect and branding — a point Yglesias half-concedes ("Warren struck Schumer as a kindred spirit") before drawing a hard line between "problem-solving" and "ideological-warrior" modes. The distinction may therefore be more about packaging than substance, and the anecdote launders an aesthetic preference (genial, low-key, bipartisan-flavored) into a strategic law. There is also an availability problem the piece doesn't face: the prescription is "be like 2001 Schumer," but his companion essay "The narrow path to saving the republic" concedes the information environment and the supply of contestable seats are structurally different now — so the very conditions that made the Schumer style rational may no longer be on offer, which leaves the nostalgia as a diagnosis without a clearly usable cure. Read alongside this week's other two posts, the three form a self-reinforcing set: the memoir supplies the credential, "The paradoxes of populism" supplies the theory (stealth democracy), and "The narrow path" supplies the application — a tidy closed loop worth noticing in its own right.

↩ Back to TOC

The narrow path to saving the republic

Matthew Yglesias · 2026-06-25 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: the state of the 2026 midterms (original commentary), framed against the broader arc of Trump's "no guardrails" second term.

Yglesias states plainly that he believes the future of American democracy has genuinely been in the balance, cataloging second-term abuses — an unfit FBI, repeated purges of military commanders, Bill Pulte's power abuses at the Federal Housing Finance Agency rewarded with promotion to Director of National Intelligence, pardons foreclosing accountability for allies, and ordered prosecutions of enemies. The saving grace, he argues, is that Trump "fumbled the bag" through voluntary overreach and self-inflicted inflation, handing the opposition an opening. But the progressive movement, he charges, "has done less than nothing," pouring energy into blue-on-blue primaries in safe districts instead of the red-leaning races that actually decide control; the broad center-left media, he notes, is far more curious about NYC primaries than about beating Republicans in red areas. The remedy: flip both houses of Congress, which would signal American elites not to surrender to authoritarianism. He then tours the map — House control hinges on gerrymandering and 2028 redistricting in blue states plus the Georgia governor's race; the Senate is a "paradox" where tough states look better than expected (Peltola in Alaska, Turek in Iowa, Talarico in Texas) while a Maine layup got hard (Platner) and Michigan became a "fiasco" (El-Sayed, a "Bernie factionalist," likely to win a swing-state primary). He credits party leaders' under-appreciated tactical work (Jeffries on discharge petitions and message discipline; Schumer's Peltola recruitment) but concedes "80 percent of what's keeping Democrats in the game here is Trump blundering," and faults the party for attempting no real rebrand. He closes — invoking Max Weber's warning against the political actor who crumbles when the world proves "too stupid or too base" — on cautious hope that a "narrow path" remains.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

The piece pretends to escape Yglesias's standing popularist pattern — it opens in the register of existential democratic alarm rather than electoral tactics — but the operative content quickly converts the backsliding frame into his familiar prescription: stop fighting blue-on-blue primaries and run electable moderates in winnable races. That conversion is the central structural problem. The catalogue of authoritarian abuses (FBI, military purges, weaponized prosecutions, the Pulte promotion) does little independent argumentative work; the remedy he derives from it — Democrats winning Congress — is identical to what he would prescribe with no democratic emergency at all. "Saving the republic" is thereby collapsed into "Democrats winning the midterms," a substitution the piece never defends; it leaves unexamined whether Democratic institutional conduct is also implicated and what becomes of the "republic" frame if Democrats win but the underlying dynamics persist.

The honesty is the strongest feature, and it is real. "80 percent of what's keeping Democrats in the game here is Trump blundering" is a striking refusal to overclaim agency for his own preferred strategy, and it cuts against the temptation to read every Democratic prospect as vindication of popularism. The state-by-state analysis is concrete and well-informed (the gerrymandering math following the demise of the Voting Rights Act, the Georgia-governor's-race-as-redistricting-lever point), not vibes. The strongest opposing view — that the blue-on-blue primaries he disdains are how a party actually relitigates its direction, and that "win red-leaning races with moderates" is the same advice he gives every cycle regardless of conditions — lands partially: his framework does have a near-unfalsifiable shape (Democratic wins confirm popularism; losses prove they didn't listen hard enough). But the gerrymandering structural claims are specific and falsifiable, and the candidate reads are granular enough to be checkable, which keeps the piece from floating free. The Weber tag (from "Politics as a Vocation," on the ethic of responsibility) and the "less than nothing" charge against the progressive movement are rhetorical overstatement — he immediately credits the No Kings protests — but they are framing, not load-bearing.

↩ Back to TOC

Ranking Joe Biden’s mistakes

Matthew Yglesias · 2026-06-25 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Ben Terris's New York Magazine piece on the Bidens' attempted return to public life, and the broader Democratic "everyone's annoyed at Joe and Jill" mood.

This is a transcript of Yglesias's podcast The Argument with co-host Jerusalem Demsas, structured as dueling rankings of the Biden administration's mistakes. Yglesias's organizing thesis is that a superficial consensus blaming the Bidens personally masks the real question: was Bidenism a sound program let down by an aged frontman, or a flawed construction whose substance should be repudiated? He wants to "condemn Bidenism, not Joe Biden the man," and treats most anti-Biden venting as "cope" — a way for progressives to distance from the administration without abandoning the left-wing economics they still endorse. His signature charge is that Biden won the 2020 primary as the Obama-continuity moderate but then handed the economic and regulatory jobs to Elizabeth Warren protégés (the "Bidenist-Warrenists"), a "brain transplant" under the guise of continuity. Demsas supplies a contrasting heuristic — she assigns blame by the process behind a decision, so she pardons the American Rescue Plan ("you listened to economists and it didn't pan out — that's life") but faults choices flowing from Biden's ego and his Obama-era resentment. Their converging top-tier mistakes: the economy/inflation, running for reelection, the Gaza/Israel posture, immigration disorder, and picking Kamala Harris (which Yglesias folds into a broader "identity over merit" staffing pattern, e.g. Becerra at HHS, Karine Jean-Pierre). They diverge sharply on two of Yglesias's contrarian positions: that Biden was right to run again (an open primary would have produced Harris anyway and pulled the field further left) and that the "genocide complicity" framing of Biden's Israel policy is factually wrong and overblown — a long, unresolved exchange that Demsas pushes back on, arguing Biden's public embrace of Netanyahu was a major tactical error. A closing "peer review" segment discusses a paper ("Bachelors Without Bachelor's") showing marriage decline concentrated among non-college women and driven by male employment and incarceration, reframing the "childless cat lady" culture-war story as a working-class material problem.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is Yglesias in his most characteristic register — popularist (run on what polls well, downplay what doesn't) and abundance-minded — and it doubles down on rather than departs from that standing pattern: the diagnosis is that Democrats lost by ceding their governing machinery to the activist left, and the cure is a return to neoclassical economics, cost-of-living focus, cultural moderation, and managed-but-real immigration enforcement. The "cope" frame is his recurring rhetorical engine — opponents' stated reasons (Biden's age, the family's egos) get recoded as displacement activity that conveniently spares the left positions he wants jettisoned. It is genuinely illuminating as applied to the Warren-staffing thesis, the transcript's strongest and most specific claim: the contrast between winning a primary as the Obama-continuity candidate and then staffing the regulatory agencies with the intra-party critics of Obamaism is a real, underexamined tension, and the anecdote about Treasury lacking anyone who could narrate the Silicon Valley Bank collapse in real time gives it teeth.

The frame's weakness is that "cope" is close to unfalsifiable: any criticism of Biden that isn't "abandon left-wing economics" gets absorbed as evasion, which lets Yglesias treat his own policy preferences as the only non-cope reading of 2024. The most load-bearing empirical claim — that the American Rescue Plan added roughly 2 points of inflation and that those 2 points were "the difference between winning and losing in 2024" — is asserted, not demonstrated, and sits awkwardly next to Demsas's better-evidenced point that inflation was global and the consensus pro-stimulus view was reasonable ex ante. Yglesias half-concedes this by admitting he supported ARP at the time, which makes the retrospective severity a little convenient.

The most interesting genuine departure is his anti-democratic turn on the "should he have run again" question: "democracy should exist between the parties rather than within them," and an open primary would only have produced a more left-tied Harris. This breaks from the small-d majoritarian instincts elsewhere in his work, and Demsas lands the obvious objection — that the value of an open process is precisely to litigate and discredit bad ideas in public, which is the reckoning Yglesias says the party still needs and hasn't had. The long Gaza exchange is where the dialogue format works against him: it surfaces his discomfort honestly (he is "torn," feels "critical of Israel," yet recoils from the "genocide" consensus), but the operative move — "it's Hamas's fault, they could have surrendered," plus bracketing Egypt's refusal of refugee outflows — is asserted against Demsas's narrower and harder-to-dismiss point that a state waging a war with no articulated endgame bears the burden of justification. Neither resolves it, and both half-acknowledge that the conversation enacts the very dynamic ("Israel takes over the entirety of the conversation") Yglesias is complaining about. The piece's value to the roundup is less any single verdict than the explicitness with which it maps the intra-Democratic fault line: whether the party's problem was Biden's age or Biden's program.

↩ Back to TOC

What is Gavin Newsom doing?

Silver Bulletin · 2026-06-23 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Gavin Newsom's decline in 2028 Democratic nomination polling (25% to 15% since January) and prediction markets (35.5% to 23.3%), plus his recent public embrace of Biden and Biden's legacy.

Silver attributes the slump mainly to reversion to the mean — Newsom's early support reflected name recognition, and he lacks a persuasive case for the nomination — with Kamala Harris and Sen. Jon Ossoff gaining ground. The puzzle Silver probes is why Newsom loudly praises Biden ("one of the most successful presidents in the last century") and hosts Hunter Biden on his podcast despite Biden's 30% favorability — lower than his exit approval, and concentrated almost entirely among hardcore Democrats (71% of Democrats, but 20% of independents, vs. Obama's 57% overall and 96% among Democrats). He reads it as a costly signal to the "#Resistance Libs," which he frames as the party's largest faction (older, college-educated, female-leaning, partisan more than ideological) who believe Democrats lost 2024 only by not fighting hard enough and because Harris was a Black woman, and who feel Biden was unfairly pushed aside. Newsom — an all-caps fighter, a "winner," and (left unstated) a good-looking white guy — resolves each of their grievances, securing a high floor in the Democrats' proportional primary. Silver calls this a sign of weakness: a better play would pair Obama nostalgia with a change message, which Ossoff or Pennsylvania Gov. Josh Shapiro can make more credibly given their swing-state overperformance, whereas Newsom's electability case is weak (California is not a swing state and his margins there underwhelm). He concludes Newsom could still win as the "least-worst" choice, like Biden in 2020 or Kerry in 2004 — a formula that tends to produce mediocre, triangulating general-election nominees.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

The three-faction taxonomy — The Left, the Abundance Libs, and the #Resistance Libs — is the piece's most portable contribution and the connective tissue to the week's other Democratic-direction pieces: the Mamdani/DSA wave is Silver's "Left," Newsom is courting his "#Resistance Libs," and the Abundance Libs (Ezra Klein's camp, named for his book on governance failure in blue states like California) are the third pole. The characterization of #Resistance Libs as partisan-more-than-ideological — older, college-educated, female-leaning, convinced Democrats lost 2024 only by not fighting hard enough and because Harris was a Black woman, and that Biden was unfairly railroaded — is sharp and does real explanatory work for the otherwise-puzzling Biden embrace.

The costly-signal reading is elegant: Newsom praises an unpopular Biden because it's costly, signaling to #Resistance Libs that "you were right about 2024." It is well-supported by the favorability data Silver marshals (Biden at 30% overall, 71% among Democrats, 20% among independents; Obama at 57% and 96%). The vulnerability is that the intent-reading resists falsification: Silver earlier reads Newsom's heterodox moves (Charlie Kirk on the podcast, a rightward shift on trans athletes, opposing California's wealth tax) as a candidate "trying to break out of the establishment box," then reads the Biden embrace as locking that box shut — both behaviors get absorbed by the theory. He fairly flags the strategy as "contradictory," but that also means nearly any Newsom move could be retrofit to the thesis.

Consistent with Silver's pattern (prediction-market-anchored, hype-deflating, contrarian-centrist), the piece is calibrated rather than a hatchet job: he credits the redistricting referendum as "substantive and smart," judges Newsom "no worse than a toss-up" against JD Vance, and rates his raw talent above Kerry's or Biden's. The best-supported empirical claim is that the electability argument is weak — California is not a swing state, his margins there underwhelm, and Ossoff and Shapiro have actual swing-state overperformance to point to. The closing line is the operative one: the #Resistance Lib floor could carry Newsom to a Biden-2020/Kerry-2004 "least-worst" nomination and a "thoroughly mediocre" general-election candidacy — i.e., the establishment's safest path is also the one that reliably produces its weakest nominees. Read against the week's left-populist surge, the piece is the establishment counterpoint: while the DSA wins primaries on anti-oligarchy energy, the establishment frontrunner's defensive crouch is to relitigate Biden for the loyalists.

↩ Back to TOC

Data centers, the grid, and energy politics

Key House Democrat Calls for a National Data Center Moratorium

Matthew Zeitlin · 2026-06-24 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: the Wednesday House Energy & Commerce markup of data-center bills (the Ratepayer Protection Act among them), and Zeitlin's own prior-day reporting arguing that bill is designed less to curb data centers than to insulate them from ratepayer-cost backlash.

Zeitlin reports that Frank Pallone, the New Jersey Democrat who is ranking member of House Energy & Commerce, called the markup proposals a "useful first step" but "not nearly enough," instead demanding "a national AI data center moratorium" until harms to air, water, and power bills can be prevented. Pallone cited home-state momentum (an Asbury Park resolution, New Brunswick's rejection of a project) and joins prior moratorium supporters — Sanders and Ocasio-Cortez's national bill, and New York's one-year moratorium awaiting Gov. Hochul's signature — but is the first so central to national energy policy. Zeitlin grounds the politics in opinion data: a majority now backs a moratorium (40% strongly), and the share blaming data centers for electricity-price rises jumped from 28% (August) to 53% (last month). Crucially, he flags the perception-vs-evidence gap himself: analysts find "little consistent relationship" nationally between data-center construction and price increases — but New Jersey's PJM grid is a real exception, where the independent market monitor attributed $23 billion in added capacity-auction costs to "existing and forecast data center load." Pallone dismissed intermediate fixes — FERC interconnection reform ("months, if not years") and the voluntary Ratepayer Protection Pledge ("toothless," "no enforcement") — as inadequate.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

The reporting is clean and notably disciplined about its own evidentiary limits. Zeitlin's spine is the framing he carried over from the prior day: the Ratepayer Protection Act is best read not as an anti-AI measure but as a move to defuse the ratepayer politics that threaten the buildout — and Pallone's moratorium call is offered as confirmation that a key Democrat sees those narrower bills as insufficient cover. That's a clean read of the legislative signaling.

The piece's strongest feature is that it refuses to launder the polling into causation. The 28%-to-53% jump in public blame is presented as a perception shift, immediately checked against analysts' finding of weak national correlation, and then complicated honestly with the PJM exception — where the $23 billion figure makes the data-center-to-bills story locally real rather than vibes. This is the rare energy story that holds "the public believes X," "the national data doesn't robustly support X," and "but in this one large grid X is documented" in frame simultaneously. No fundamental-flaw flag is warranted; the author marks the gap rather than papering over it.

What the piece leaves unprobed — fairly, given its length — is whether a moratorium is the instrument that fits the grievance. The documented harm (PJM capacity costs, transmission bottlenecks) is a cost-allocation problem; the Ratepayer Protection Pledge and FERC interconnection reform are aimed squarely at it, while a construction moratorium is a blunter tool that also halts the AI buildout for reasons unrelated to bills. Pallone elides that by treating "the promises haven't worked yet" as grounds for the maximal option. For the roundup's data-center axis, this is the backlash pole at its most institutionally serious — the empirical, ratepayer-grounded case for a pause — and the direct foil to Lind's "astroturf" dismissal: the grievance Zeitlin documents in PJM is exactly the "legitimate" concern Lind concedes at the top before spending his essay attributing the opposition to displaced anti-capitalism.

↩ Back to TOC

Data Centers Have a Farmland Problem, Too

Jael Holzman · 2026-06-26 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Food and Water Watch's blog post "The AI Data Center Boom Is Coming for Farmers" (June 2026) and the broader wave of US local fights over siting data centers on agricultural land.

Holzman, who covers local infrastructure opposition for Heatmap, reports that farmland protection is becoming a national watchword in the data-center debate, mirroring its role in the anti-renewables movement — but driven now largely from the anti-monopolist left rather than the right. She documents farm owners rejecting developer offers, Republican legislators in Pennsylvania and elsewhere proposing to block "prime farmland" sales, and county-level rejections (Kosciusko County, Indiana; Linn County, Iowa; Clinton County, Ohio) in heavily-farmed areas. Food and Water Watch's Mitch Jones frames the threat as competition for water, higher electricity costs, and distorted land values, but says the group's core motive is anti-monopoly and small-farmer protection. Holzman's analytical move is to press Jones on whether the farmland frame is borrowed from the anti-renewables playbook and whether it will spill over to restrict solar and wind too — noting Eau Claire County, Wisconsin just adopted a "farmland preservation plan" discouraging both utility-scale solar and data centers at once. Jones denies any connection, calling overlapping restrictions "a coincidence." The reporting leaves that denial sitting against the bundled-restriction evidence.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is a short reported "spotlight," not an argument essay, so the right test is whether the reporting earns its framing — and it largely does. Holzman's load-bearing observation is that "protect farmland" is a transferable piece of rhetorical technology: an argument that proved effective against solar and wind is now being fitted onto data centers, with the side effect (intended or not) of letting counties restrict both in a single ordinance. The Eau Claire County example is the strongest evidence, and Holzman is right to keep it in frame even after Jones insists the convergence is coincidental. The tension between a sources's stated motive (anti-monopoly) and the structural effect of the frame they're deploying (a generic anti-siting tool) is exactly the kind of gap good reporting surfaces without resolving.

The piece's notable limit is that nobody in it quantifies the farmland actually at stake. Data centers have small physical footprints relative to utility-scale solar, so "farmland loss" may be doing more political than acreage work here — the Austin Ahlman quote (an independent House candidate in Nebraska) reframes it as populist anxiety about "control long term," which reads as the truer driver than literal tomato acreage. The story is the politics, and Holzman is candid about that.

For the roundup's data-center cluster, this is the coalition-mapping piece: it shows the backlash is cross-ideological (anti-monopolist left plus rural/populist right) and that its frames are migrating. That cuts directly against Michael Lind's "in defense of data centers" claim that the opposition is essentially the urban progressive party line — Holzman's reporting locates much of the energy on the right and among small-farm populists. It also corroborates Lind on one narrow point he would seize: the same coalition machinery that restricts data centers ends up restricting renewables, which is his "anti-development as such" thesis viewed from the other side.

↩ Back to TOC

In defense of data centers

Michael Lind · 2026-06-25 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: New York's one-year data-center moratorium (awaiting Gov. Hochul's signature) and the national moratorium bill from Sen. Bernie Sanders and Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez.

Lind — a self-described national-developmentalist who routinely casts progressive environmentalism as anti-production — concedes upfront that water-use and electricity-price concerns are legitimate and deserve regulation (and that anti-regulation tech accelerationists are equally misguided), then argues the moratorium movement is fundamentally about something else. His core evidence is a May Gallup survey showing an inversion: opposition is strongest among urban Democratic women (56% strongly oppose) and weakest among the rural Republican men (39%) who actually live where data centers get built. If the movement tracked genuine local exposure, he reasons, the pattern would reverse; that it doesn't means much opposition is "virtue-signaling" and the emerging "progressive party line." Hence his slogan, borrowed from the 1960s Left, "the issue is not the issue": the real objection is to industrial development as such, the same coalition (he argues) that opposed nuclear power, fracking, Keystone XL, and New Deal hydropower dams, each time stacking multiple rationales to please different factions (his "three-fer"/"four-fer"). He likens smearing data centers via Elon Musk to the earlier guilt-by-association linking Keystone to the Koch brothers. The "good news," he says, is that the coalition is fragile — split between neoliberal greens and degrowthers, at odds with construction unions, and undercut by non-ideological "local preservationists" (e.g., Nantucket residents fighting offshore wind) whom he calls the bigger long-run threat to growth. Closing refrain: "A country is not a museum."

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This piece doubles down on Lind's signature frame rather than departing from it, and its quality is uneven: one genuinely sharp empirical jab wrapped in a thesis that is partly unfalsifiable and in tension with his own concessions. The strong move is the Gallup demographic inversion. If opposition to a locally-sited industrial facility is higher among people far from any such facility than among those who would host one, the "practical local concerns" story really is incomplete, and partisan/ideological sorting is doing real work. That is a fair, falsifiable point that the moratorium camp has to answer.

But three structural problems undercut the larger argument. First, "astroturf" is the wrong word for what he describes and he never earns it: astroturf means manufactured, corporate- or elite-funded fake grassroots, and Lind offers zero funding evidence — only ideological-coalition continuity. He is using "astroturf" to mean "ideologically motivated," which is a different and weaker claim. Second, the thesis is in tension with his own opening concession: he grants that water and electricity-cost harms are real, then spends the essay treating essentially all opposition as displaced hostility to development. But if the harms are real, rural Republicans being less worried is at least as consistent with information environment or not-yet-being-affected as with the urban worry being fake. Third, "the issue is not the issue" is deployed unfalsifiably — any opposition to any project can be reframed as displaced anti-development, and Lind never specifies what evidence would count against it.

The frame is also selectively sourced. Lind's whole "progressive party line" story sits awkwardly next to the actual 2026 data-center backlash, which (as Heatmap's reporting on the farmland fights shows) is substantially driven by the populist right and anti-monopolist left, not just urban progressives — a fact his coalition narrative simply omits. And he never engages the one concrete mechanism the serious backlash centers on: the PJM ratepayer-cost story (the independent market monitor's $23 billion attribution), which is precisely the "legitimate" concern he waves at before dismissing its advocates. He wants to concede the substance and discredit the people raising it.

Where the piece is genuinely strong is the coalition-fragility analysis, which is falsifiable and well-exampled: the split between neoliberal greens and degrowthers, the labor-vs-environmentalist clashes (Plumbers and Pipefitters backing Keystone), and the preservationist-vs-industrial-renewables fault line (Nantucket Residents Against Turbines) are real and load-bearing. His consistency challenge to radical Greens — that if Big Data Center is bad for the environment, so are Big Wind, Big Solar, and Big Transmission — is a fair pressure on selective environmentalism, and it dovetails (from the opposite valence) with Holzman's reporting that some counties now restrict solar and data centers in the same ordinance. Not Straussian: the thesis is stated plainly throughout. For the cluster, this is the defense pole — the counterweight to Pallone's moratorium and the European climate-left framing — strongest as coalition analysis, weakest where it converts a real partisan-sorting finding into a blanket claim that the underlying grievances are fake.

↩ Back to TOC

Works in Progress: Grid Connection Auctions

Alex Tabarrok · 2026-06-26 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Chris Gillett's essay in the latest issue of Works in Progress on grid-connection reform.

The argument: the US already has ample generation capacity waiting to come online, so the real bottleneck is connecting it to the grid, where supply must equal demand at every instant. The core flaw is that interconnection uses a first-come, first-served queue, so high-value projects wait years behind less important ones — Gillett's analogy is an airport where small Cessnas can bump 747s. The proposed fix is auctioning connection rights (citing Vickrey's congestion-pricing work and the classic Rassenti–Smith–Bulfin combinatorial auction for airport slots), plus flexible interconnection for curtailable loads like data centers that can disconnect during the few annual peak hours rather than wait years for firm service. Gillett also shows how market prices rebalance energy sources: in ERCOT, as solar grew from 4 to 29.8 GW, the midday (1pm) power value fell from 92.9% to 38.7% of the peak-hour price, signaling demand for storage — to which developers responded, taking ERCOT from near-zero to 8.6 GW of battery discharge.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

The post's strength is correctly relocating the constraint: the energy debate fixates on building generation, but Gillett (via Tabarrok) shows the marginal megawatt is stranded by allocation, not scarcity — a mechanism-design problem with a mechanism-design answer. The auction proposal sits on solid ground; Vickrey's congestion pricing and the Rassenti–Smith–Bulfin experimental combinatorial auctions for airport slots are the canonical precedents, and the flexible-interconnection point is the most immediately relevant to the data-center buildout, since hyperscalers' willingness to curtail during rare peaks could free firm capacity for everyone else. The ERCOT price-shape data is a clean demonstration that price signals already coordinate the storage response without central planning.

The unexamined objection is distributional and political-economy: auctions allocate by willingness-to-pay, advantaging deep-pocketed incumbents and hyperscalers over small or community generators, and the existing first-come queue persists partly because RTO/FERC governance and incumbent interests resist reform. The airport-slot analogy is instructive precisely because slot auctions have been proposed for decades and rarely adopted, for the same political reasons. Tabarrok doesn't engage who loses, but as market-design diagnosis the post is sound and pairs directly with the week's data-center/energy thread.

↩ Back to TOC

Should data centers pay a carbon tax?

Matthew Yglesias · 2026-06-24 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The wave of anti-data-center backlash (water use, electricity prices, tax abatements) and Bernie Sanders's proposal to half-nationalize frontier AI labs.

Yglesias opens by dismissing most data-center complaints as overblown — water and electricity-price fears are misplaced, local tax revenue is real — leaving two legitimate issues: tax abatements (minor) and carbon emissions (serious). His core conceptual move is that "reasoning backwards" from temperature targets is misguided: a genuinely useful new way to consume electricity (illustrated by his "National Cancer Shield" thought experiment) simply means the globally optimal level of emissions has risen, and AI, being economically valuable, is such a technology. He argues the "AI bubble" narrative is partly motivated reasoning — a way to avoid admitting either that hitting climate targets now costs more (for the sake of "polar bears") or that more warming should be accepted. He adds a sharp distributional point against Sanders: the gains from AI can be shared domestically, but the warming costs fall on the global poor (the tropics, Nigeria, Bangladesh) who cannot simply migrate to cold cities the way Americans can. His proposal pairs a "clean energy abundance" agenda (renewables, geothermal, nuclear) with a data-center carbon tax pegged to the global social cost of carbon, plus penalties on US firms using foreign non-priced data centers — exploiting the fact that voters hate both carbon pricing and data centers to pass a policy he concedes is otherwise "deader than the dodo."

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This doubles down on Yglesias's abundance-liberal priors — pro-growth, pro-AI, skeptical of degrowth-flavored environmentalism — but with two features that lift it above his usual register. First, the asymmetry that the social cost of carbon is borne globally while AI's gains accrue nationally is a genuinely clarifying argument, and turning it against Bernie Sanders's "trillions for all of us" framing (where "us" quietly means Americans who would own the labs) is the piece's strongest moment. Second, he concedes more ground to the climate movement than he usually does — granting that climate change is serious and the climate-AI nexus is "worth taking seriously" — which makes the essay read as a real attempt to price an externality rather than a reflexive "green groups are out to lunch" take, even as he can't resist that gibe.

The structural tension is that the proposal exploits a public animus he has just spent several paragraphs arguing is irrational and overblown. He would defend this as correcting a real externality regardless of the public's confused reasons, but the design — a carbon tax narrowly on one sector — sits in tension with the economics he usually champions. Carbon is carbon: a single-sector levy carries the deadweight cost of discouraging an activity he insists is valuable, without the efficiency of economy-wide coverage, and it singles out data centers by public mood rather than by carbon intensity (a gas plant emits the same CO2 whether or not it powers a GPU). His claim that "there would be no consumer-facing price here at all" is the weakest link: a tax on data-center power raises the cost of AI and cloud services, which is ultimately borne by their users — he half-admits this by calling it "a burden on economic development."

The strongest opposing view is therefore that a data-center-only carbon tax is close to the worst of both worlds: it accepts the political framing that AI energy demand is uniquely problematic (which he denies on the merits) in order to smuggle in a Pigouvian tax (a charge set equal to the harm an activity imposes on others). The honest version of his own logic points to an economy-wide carbon price; the data-center version is a second-best political gambit dressed as a clever hack. To his credit, he flags the framing openly ("two wrongs make a right?") and treats it as provocation as much as proposal, so the piece is best read as a serious distributional argument with a deliberately cheeky policy kicker rather than a fully worked-out plan.

↩ Back to TOC

Heatwave in Europe: have the politicians failed?

Euro|topics (curated debate) · 2026-06-25 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: a record-breaking June 2026 heatwave across Europe — many regions on top-level heat alert — and IPCC chair Jim Skea's warning that such conditions will recur.

Euro|topics, the EU-funded press digest, curates fourteen national commentaries under the question "have the politicians failed?" The dominant pole is a climate-urgency, often anti-capitalist framing concentrated in the French press: L'Humanité (FR) calls government inaction "criminal" and casts anti-capitalist forces as the only ones able to fight; L'Obs (FR) warns 2027 presidential candidates who duck climate will be "criminal accomplices"; Mediapart (FR) indicts profit-first production models and singles out AI, "that ecological ogre," being prioritized over the climate; El País (ES) likewise flags AI's "thirst for energy and water" alongside fossil-fuel phase-out; Corriere della Sera (IT) insists "the laws of physics are indifferent to our discussions." A second, pragmatic pole argues mitigation alone is insufficient and adaptation has been neglected: Iltalehti (FI) and L'Opinion (FR) press long-term, heat-resilient infrastructure and warn that paralysing the country at every 35°C spell is unsustainable, while De Standaard (BE) names the "trees vs. air conditioning" choice a false dichotomy and De Volkskrant (NL) wants systemic change over AC. A global-justice strand (Kurier, AT, on the lethal "wet-bulb" threshold already nearing in South Asia and West Africa; Ta Nea, GR, on prevention vs. crisis-management) and a thin contrarian note (The Daily Telegraph, GB, "stop overreacting and enjoy the sunshine") round it out, with Finnish outlets (Helsingin Sanomat) musing that a warming Europe makes Finland a "coolcation" paradise.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

As an aggregator entry the "argument" is the editorial selection and framing, so the analysis is best aimed at the shape of the curation. Euro|topics poses a binary — failure vs. adaptation — but the assembled spread tilts heavily toward urgency: the French outlets in particular slide from "climate is an emergency" to "anti-capitalism is the only adequate response," and the only outright skeptical voice (the Telegraph's "enjoy the sunshine") is positioned as the lone outlier. Read as selection-as-argument, the digest presents climate alarm as the European consensus and dissent as eccentric — a fair reflection of the continental op-ed center of gravity, but worth flagging as a framing choice rather than a neutral sampling.

The most analytically useful thread for the roundup is that two of the urgency-pole outlets — Mediapart (FR) and El País (ES) — independently name AI and data-center energy demand as the climate villain of the moment, citing the tech lobby's request that the EU soften climate targets to prioritise AI. That connects this European climate debate directly to the US data-center backlash cluster in the same window, but from the opposite entry point: where Pallone and the moratorium camp attack data centers via ratepayer bills, the European left attacks them via carbon and water. It also sharpens the contrast with Michael Lind's defense — the "ecological ogre" framing is exactly the anti-industrial-development reflex Lind says is being recycled, here applied to AI in real time.

The sharpest single contribution is De Standaard's (BE) naming of the "trees vs. air conditioning" false dichotomy, which identifies the polarization mechanism itself rather than picking a side; the adaptation camp (Iltalehti, L'Opinion) is, stripped of valence, the European mirror of Lind's "a country cannot come to a standstill" pragmatism, minus his anti-progressive politics. The genuinely candid notes are the uncomfortable ones the consensus usually omits: Helsingin Sanomat's frank observation that a hotter Europe makes Finland a relative winner, and Kurier's wet-bulb point that Europe's "emergency" is already South Asia's baseline. No flaw flag applies — this is a digest, evaluated on coverage and framing, both of which are broad if consensus-weighted.

↩ Back to TOC

America at 250: the Founding, citizenship, and the courts

Tom West’s Founding

Michael Anton · 2026-06-26 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Thomas G. West's book The Political Theory of the American Founding, a natural-rights reading of the Founders, taken up as an America-250 touchstone.

Anton — a Claremont/Hillsdale "West Coast Straussian" and Trump-administration figure — reviews West (a student of Harry Jaffa) as having proved, by finding consensus across public Founding-era documents, that the Founding's core is not an "amalgam" of clashing traditions (the dominant scholarly thesis, associated with the Bailyn/Wood "republican synthesis") but a coherent doctrine of all men's equal natural rights. The reasoning chain: equal natural rights entail a reciprocal duty to respect others' rights, so "liberalism" and "republicanism," rights and duties, equality and liberty are not in tension; the social compact is therefore necessarily particular and "invitation-only" (no world government, open borders, or empire); and "form must fit matter" — universal principles must suit a particular people's character and circumstances. Anton uses this to refute two errors at once: the left reading that natural equality mandates redistribution, racial quotas, and open borders, and a faction of the Right that dismisses equal natural rights as dangerous "propositionism" (America as a mere creed). He defends Locke against Leo Strauss's esoteric reading (Locke as a hidden acquisitive Hobbesian, "life is the joyless quest for joy") by arguing Strauss deliberately exaggerated to revive serious study of the ancients, and that the Founders were practical statesmen, not Locke-followers. Anton then sets aside West's scholarly refusal to "evaluate" and flatly asserts the Founders' theory is true, citing America's two-century success and a "longing for justice" that nature will eventually vindicate. The closer frames restoration as urgent but uncertain, the American "matter" degraded by decades of "fundamental transformation."

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is a laudatory review essay, and Anton opens by disclosing thirty years of friendship with West, a shared Hillsdale post, and shared teachers, then asking "to be believed" — a paratextual move that names the logrolling charge in order to pre-empt it without neutralizing it; the review never seriously entertains that West could be wrong. Set that aside and the load-bearing contribution is real: the form/matter device (form = the regime and its informing principles; matter = the actual people, territory, customs, religion) is a genuinely illuminating way to hold "all men are created equal" together with a bounded, particular polity. It explains how the same writers can assert universal natural rights and a membership-restricted compact without contradiction, and it is the essay's most transportable idea.

The method, though, is the recurring problem. West's (and Anton's) procedure is to "look for consensus" in public documents and to treat scholars who foreground Founder disagreement (the "amalgam"/multiple-traditions schools) as obscuring a deeper unity. But a method designed to find consensus will find it, and public ratification rhetoric is precisely the genre built to paper over disagreement. This is the mirror image of the sin Anton indicts in others — rummaging private letters (e.g., Jefferson's "wall of separation") for present-day validation: selection-as-argument cuts both ways, and the essay never concedes that its consensus might be partly an artifact of which documents it privileges.

Two further structural weaknesses. First, the framework is built to absorb counterevidence: slavery becomes proof of the natural-equality principle (the thing that abolished it) rather than a tension within the Founding, and the closing teleology — a "longing for justice" that nature will "inevitably" vindicate — is unfalsifiable, since improvement confirms nature and decline merely means the wrong side gained the upper hand. Second, the defense of Locke against Strauss doesn't refute Strauss so much as decline his game: Strauss's claim was that Locke's moderate surface conceals radical depths, so citing Locke's moderate surface texts (the Letter Concerning Toleration, Reasonableness of Christianity) answers a different question, and Anton's supporting conjecture — that Strauss was deliberately overstating and later softened — he himself concedes is "plausible, if unprovable."

The essay's pivot is its most revealing. Anton notes that West "violates his own prohibition" against evaluation, infers that West secretly means to vindicate the Founders as true, and then drops West's caution entirely to answer the normative question himself ("Yes."). So West's "Evaluation presupposes knowledge" functions as a Trojan horse — perform the "neutral" reconstruction, then cash it out as "the cure for what ails us exists." The closing pragmatic argument (America succeeded, so the principles must be true — "my money's on the first" of because/in-spite-of/unconnected) is a false trichotomy dressed as a wager, not an argument, about an overdetermined outcome. By the project's Straussian rule this piece is not Straussian: the operative claims (natural rights are true; they ground a restrictionist, bounded polity against both left leveling and right "blood and soil"; restoration is the urgent task) are all stated directly, including the one-word "Yes." Its synthesis value is as the week's clearest statement of the Claremont project to ground Trumpist nationalism in natural right rather than ethnonationalism — and it sits in instructive tension with Upham's same-week, same-outlet embrace of "blood, soil, creed" as legitimate membership categories: two routes to a bounded-membership politics, one explicitly rejecting "blood and soil," the other reclaiming it.

↩ Back to TOC

Blood, Soil, Creed—and the Immunities of American Citizenship

David R. Upham · 2026-06-25 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The long-running originalist dispute over which 14th-Amendment clause forbids racial discrimination, and present-day fights over affirmative action and the boundaries of citizenship, timed to the America-250 moment.

Upham, a constitutional-law scholar, argues that the conventional answer — the Equal Protection Clause — is wrong, because by text and original understanding that clause bars unequal enforcement (the denial of "the protection of the laws," i.e., Southern officials' refusal to shield freedmen from violence), not unequal laws. The correct source is the Privileges or Immunities Clause: "immunities" (Latin in + munia) means exemptions from munia (burdens, taxes), so the clause bars laws that treat a citizen like an alien. Combing the 1866-era record (Bingham, Sherman, Howard, the Chicago Tribune, conventions of "colored citizens"), Upham finds officials named only three illegitimate bases for burdening a citizen — heredity/race/caste (blood), geography/state-or-national origin (soil), and creed/religion — and observes these are precisely the categories by which, per Aristotle, a polity defines membership (a community of households sharing a territory under a common constitution). His central paradox: what is legitimate at the border of the citizenry (defining who is in) becomes injustice among citizens (any burden, even "a one-penny racial tax," is a "badge of alienage"), though these same categories stay legitimate for distributing political rights (loyalty oaths, voting). He extends this: discriminatory distribution of tax-funded benefits is an indirect burden, so racial preferences (affirmative action) would be unconstitutional — while conceding the framers tolerated segregated Northern schools as a "real but relatively minor violation" excused by the "contingent necessity" of white prejudice, to be ended "with all deliberate speed." The link to today's birthright-citizenship fight is oblique: Upham's "blood" (jus sanguinis, citizenship by descent) and "soil" (jus soli, citizenship by birthplace) are the classic membership criteria, but the essay's operative payload is the immunities framework and its anti-preference inference, not the executive-order litigation.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

Judged as an originalist interpretive argument, this is dense and largely well-built. The textual point that Equal Protection bars unequal protection/enforcement rather than unequal laws is a genuine, underappreciated reading shared by the broader Privileges-or-Immunities revival (the clause having been largely dormant since the Slaughter-House Cases gutted it in 1873). The in/munia etymology gives the "immunities of citizenship" concrete content — exemption from burdens — and the three-categories finding is backed by an unusually thick evidentiary apparatus for a short essay. The strongest single move is the membership/burden asymmetry: blood, soil, and creed legitimately gate who becomes a citizen, yet become "badges of alienage" when used to burden someone already a citizen — "a duty at the border of the citizenry becomes a crime among the citizenry." That elegantly explains why the same categories can be licit for naturalization and political rights (oaths, voting) but illicit for taxation or regulation. Upham also handles the obvious counterargument well: rather than claim the framers were "blind" to sex equality (the Calabresi-Rickert reading), he shows sex equality was a live 1860s issue they knew about and chose not to constitutionalize — which strengthens, not weakens, his argument from what they actually said.

The framework strains hardest exactly where it must reconcile its anti-discrimination logic with the framers' own tolerance of segregated schools in the very Northern states (New York, Pennsylvania, Ohio) they promised would be "unaffected." Upham's answer — that segregation was a "real but relatively minor violation" suspended by the "contingent necessity" of white prejudice, pending desegregation "with all deliberate speed" — is the essay's most speculative passage, running explicitly on "the framers probably would have said something like the following" and "I think it probable." The cleaner originalist inference from the same facts points the other way: if the framers tolerated segregation, the likeliest reading is that they did not understand the amendment to forbid it — not that they understood it to forbid it but waived the rule for prejudice. Importing Brown's remedial "all deliberate speed" (1955) back into 1866-67, and closing with an inverted echo of George Wallace's "segregation forever," is rhetorically deft but reconstructs a counterfactual where the record is silent.

The contemporary payload — that the immunities framework "may invalidate all racial preferences" — rests on the "indirect burden" move: any discriminatory distribution of tax-funded benefits is an indirect munia on disfavored taxpayers. But that lever proves too much. By the same logic, any unequal distribution of any tax-funded benefit on any of the three bases would be an indirect burden — including geography, which Upham must then carve out as permissible "by the nature of the good" (schooling as an extension of the household). The carve-out suggests the indirect-burden principle is being switched on for race and off for geography on grounds reached elsewhere, not generated by the principle itself. And the foundational claim — that "no participant…mentioned any other form of inequality" — is an argument from silence, the weakest form of originalist evidence, since silence is consistent with many explanations besides a deliberate three-category limit.

None of this sinks the core: the in/munia reading and the membership-versus-burden asymmetry are defensible and genuinely illuminating, which is why no fundamental-flaw flag is warranted. The piece is best read as a sophisticated originalist brief — its selection of evidence is method-dependent in the same way Anton's "look for consensus" is — aimed at present fights (the post-SFFA affirmative-action settlement; the citizenship-boundary debate) rather than at the birthright-citizenship executive order it never directly engages. For the synthesis it pairs naturally with Anton: both are America-250-timed right-intellectual essays grounding a bounded-membership politics in Founding/Reconstruction principle, but where Anton explicitly rejects "blood and soil" in favor of equal natural rights, Upham reclaims "blood, soil, creed" as legitimate membership categories — a live intramural dispute on the intellectual Right over whether American peoplehood is creedal, ascriptive, or both.

↩ Back to TOC

A Clean Sweep for Trump and Written Immigration Law

Dan McLaughlin · 2026-06-25 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: the Supreme Court's June 2026 immigration rulings — Mullin v. Doe (Temporary Protected Status revocation), Mullin v. Al Otro Lado (asylum "metering" at the border), and Tuesday's Blanche v. Lau (excluding criminal aliens pre-conviction).

McLaughlin reads two 6–3 Justice Alito opinions as straightforward textualism vindicating Congress's written law over judicial improvisation. In Mullin v. Doe, the 1990 TPS statute explicitly bars judicial review of designation/termination decisions and mandates reviews every 18 months; lower courts (including the Ninth Circuit) reviewed anyway, and Alito held the bar means what it says and that "process" challenges are a pretext for reviewing the merits. On the equal-protection claim (that revoking Haiti's TPS reflected racial animus), McLaughlin argues Trump's across-the-board terminations — 13 so far, almost all for non-European countries — defeat any selective-animus theory under either standard of review. In Mullin v. Al Otro Lado, the asylum statute (8 U.S.C. §1158) lets an alien who "arrives in the United States" apply; Alito held that interdicting migrants in Mexico before they cross means they have not "arrived," dismantling Justice Sotomayor's "threshold" reading as a word Congress never wrote and supporting the point with running-back/end-zone analogies. Justice Thomas concurred in both, urging broader holdings (that equal protection does not apply to immigration at all; that the no-review bar is jurisdictional). McLaughlin's frame: these are wins for "written immigration law," and the clean sweep may soften the Court's expected loss for Trump in the pending birthright-citizenship case, Trump v. Barbara.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is McLaughlin in his characteristic register — a doctrinally careful, partisan-but-rigorous explainer that treats textualism as self-evidently correct and the dissents as bad-faith evasion. Its real strength is the precise anchoring of each holding to specific statutory language: the §1158 "arrives in the United States" parsing and the TPS no-judicial-review clause are quoted in full, and the textualist claim is genuinely the operative one in both Alito opinions rather than a gloss the column imposes. For the synthesis this is the textualist pole of the SCOTUS/immigration cluster, and (per the project's Straussian rule) it is not Straussian — the operative claim sits on the surface, in the title.

The structural tell is that the piece does the thing it accuses the dissents of doing: it adopts a tone of incredulity ("almost comical," "laughable," "leaving the rest of us to wonder how anybody could refuse to accept it") that substitutes for engaging the dissents' strongest form. Kagan's argument — that a court can adjudicate whether the Secretary completed the statutorily-required pre-decision steps without reviewing the unreviewable final "determination" — is a real administrative-law distinction (whether procedural preconditions are severable from a review-barred decision is exactly the kind of question textualists split on), and McCarthy-style hand-waving ("utterly divorced from reality") is not a refutation. Likewise Sotomayor's manipulability worry — that the executive can nullify a statutory asylum right simply by physically blocking the doorstep of a port with capacity to process the claim — is a substantive consequence the column recodes as a mere category error about the word "threshold."

The most revealing inclusion is the Thomas concurrence, reported approvingly-neutrally: positions (equal protection categorically inapplicable to immigration, citing continuous national-origin discrimination 1789–present; ordering aliens admitted as a separation-of-powers violation) well to Alito's right that signal where the piece's sympathies sit even as it foregrounds the narrower textualist holding. Where the column is genuinely strong is the institutional critique that "temporary" TPS designations have run 16–35 years and that the Biden administration locked in extensions days before leaving office — a fair point the left-legal counter-reading has to answer. It is weakest as analysis precisely where it is strongest as advocacy: it never concedes that "plain meaning" did contestable work, even though the majority itself fractured on the equal-protection jurisdiction question (Barrett and Gorsuch declined to join subpart III(A)), which quietly undercuts the clean-textualism framing the title sells.

↩ Back to TOC

The Supreme Court Read Immigration Law Plainly. And the Media Freaked Out.

Jed Rubenfeld · 2026-06-26 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The Supreme Court's decision in Mullin v. Al Otro Lado (Thursday) and the New York Times headline characterizing it; Free Press column.

Yale law professor Jed Rubenfeld defends the Court's 6–3 ruling upholding "metering" — the practice of immigration officers turning migrants away on the Mexican side of the border before they can claim asylum — as a straightforward textual reading. The asylum statute grants a hearing to anyone who has "arrived in" the US; Alito, for the majority, held that a migrant stopped before setting foot on US soil has not "arrived in" the country (his football-and-front-door analogies). Rubenfeld stresses two framing points: metering began under Obama in 2016, not as a Trump invention, and the Times headline ("Supreme Court Allows Trump to Block Asylum Seekers") obscures both that origin and the textual basis. He walks through Sotomayor's dissent — its catalog of migrant deaths, its "arriving in Penn Station" counterexamples, and its strongest point, that reading "arrive in" to require crossing renders the statute's separate "physically present" clause superfluous — and rebuts each, conceding the superfluity argument is "slightly better" but citing precedent that Congress sometimes writes superfluous text. He concludes the majority's reading "can't be called crazy" and that Sotomayor's "more people will die" rhetoric isn't justified when the text permits the result.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is an interpretive legal essay, and on its own terms it is mostly fair. Rubenfeld lays out the dissent's actual arguments — and concedes its best one — before answering them, which is more than most advocacy columns manage. Within textualism his reading is defensible: "arrive in" plausibly denotes completed arrival, and the airline-stowaway hypothetical gives the "arrives in" clause independent work to do, blunting the superfluity objection without relying solely on the weak "Congress is sometimes redundant" canon.

The weakest part is the framing device, not the law. "The media freaked out" inflates ordinary headline compression into ideological distortion. "Supreme Court Allows Trump to Block Asylum Seekers at Border" is a defensible description of the ruling's practical effect — the case arose from Trump-era enforcement and the holding does let DHS turn away asylum seekers — even if it omits the Obama origin and the textual reasoning. A headline cannot carry a statutory-interpretation seminar; treating its compression as proof of a politicized press is the column's own thumb on the scale, the mirror image of the distortion it decries.

Rubenfeld also under-engages the strongest opposing view, which isn't Sotomayor's wordplay but the purposivist / non-refoulement one: the asylum right (and the treaty commitment behind it) is arguably defeated if officers can station themselves a few feet outside the line precisely to prevent "arrival." He declares consequences categorically irrelevant to interpretation — a contestable jurisprudential premise, not a neutral one. A purposivist reading holding that "arrive in" should not be construed to let the government engineer non-arrival is also "not crazy." His real, sustainable claim is that the textualist reading is permissible; he overstates it into the assertion that it is nearly compelled and the alternative illegitimate. (Pairs with NR's McLaughlin "Clean Sweep" as the textualist pole against the birthright-citizenship cluster.)

↩ Back to TOC

EXCLUSIVE: HHS Proposes Stricter Standards for Claiming Unaccompanied Migrant Children

Audrey Fahlberg · 2026-06-26 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: A forthcoming HHS / Office of Refugee Resettlement proposed rule (expected to publish Friday) plus prior Free Press reporting on trafficking of unaccompanied alien children (UACs); original reporting.

Fahlberg reports that HHS is set to codify stricter sponsor-vetting for unaccompanied alien children — minors with neither legal status nor a guardian — administered through the Office of Refugee Resettlement (ORR). The proposed rule would raise proof-of-income and proof-of-identity standards (narrowing acceptable ID to 13 fixed documents, dropping foreign birth certificates and marriage licenses), expand background checks, prohibit background-check waivers, and direct ORR to examine children for gang tattoos. The stated trigger is a series of fraud-and-abuse cases, including an Ohio child-smuggling indictment of three Guatemalan nationals (one sentenced for sponsoring a girl he falsely claimed as his sister, then abusing her), plus the Biden-era backlog of trafficking/abuse hotline calls. HHS concedes a tradeoff — tighter rules make it harder for genuine relatives to claim children — but argues the risk is worth curbing "super sponsors" who file multiple fraudulent applications. The only outside voices quoted are restrictionist (RJ Hauman of the National Immigration Center for Enforcement), who calls it the most consequential UAC protection since the 2008 Wilberforce Act and says Congress must go further.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

As reporting on a primary document, the piece does its basic job. The documented core is solid: a specific, citable proposed rule with concrete provisions, anchored to a real federal indictment and to cited inspector-general and congressional findings (the ORR Integrity & Accountability Team's October 2024 identification of a couple who fraudulently sponsored 15 children under multiple aliases). It tells you what the rule changes and the rationale HHS offers.

The framing, though, is one-sided in a way worth flagging for downstream use. Every named outside source is restrictionist; no immigrant-advocacy or child-welfare voice is quoted to contest the conceded downside (harder reunification for genuine relatives, longer custody for kids). The piece surfaces the tradeoff only in HHS's own cost-benefit voice, and the deck ("Human trafficking and abuse surged under the old rules") asserts a causal claim the body supports only anecdotally.

One figure deserves a caution. The body describes "thousands of children" in the Biden-era hotline backlog, but Hauman is quoted invoking "hundreds of thousands of children the HHS inspector general still lists as missing." The widely circulated HHS number (~85,000+ children the agency could not reach by follow-up contact) conflates "unreachable by phone" with "missing/trafficked," and the article passes the much larger "hundreds of thousands" figure through without reconciling it against its own "thousands." For a reporting piece that's not disqualifying, but readers should treat "hundreds of thousands missing" as a contested advocacy figure rather than an established count.

↩ Back to TOC

AI: ownership, regulation, and the liberal order

The Bernie Sanders AI Sovereign Wealth Fund Proposal

Matt Bruenig · 2026-06-26 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Bernie Sanders's New York Times op-ed and subsequent legislation proposing that AI providers hand 50% of their stock to a government-run sovereign wealth fund (SWF).

Bruenig first reframes the proposal: rather than an SWF (diversified holdings for financial return), it would create partial state-owned enterprises (SOEs) — like Norway's Telenor, Equinor, and DNB — by converting Anthropic, OpenAI, and a Gemini spin-off into roughly half-public firms. Crucially, the 50% stake comes not from purchasing shares but from a one-time tax payable in stock (about $1 trillion transferred), which he says answers the most common "confusion" criticism that Sanders wants to spend public money buying companies. He then rebuts three substantive objections. To the claim that AI inference wastes land, water, electricity, and chips, he answers that this collapses into a judgment about AI's usefulness, which (per usage growth) is contestable but does not bear on the public-versus-private ownership question. To Dean Baker's worry that public ownership hands an abusive executive (Trump) more power, he replies that Trump already coerces purely private firms — citing CBS, and Trump's shutdown of Anthropic's flagship Fable model despite the government owning no stake — while his power over existing SOEs (the Postal Service, TVA, Amtrak) and his own ~$27 billion equity portfolio has yielded little beyond a U.S. Steel "golden share" intervention. To the worry that ownership creates a regulatory conflict of interest, he notes this contradicts Baker's argument — one says the state-owner will have too much power, the other too little — and that real-world SOEs are neither uniformly rapacious nor uniformly inept. He concludes that state ownership offers faster, finer-grained, real-time control than statute or rulemaking — exactly what a high-risk frontier sector like AI warrants.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This doubles down on Bruenig's signature method (People's Policy Project; data-driven socialist policy analysis): precise definitional reframing plus catching critics in self-contradiction. Two moves genuinely land. The "confusion" correction is correct and clarifying — many critics did read the plan as a cash purchase when it is a stock-denominated tax, which changes the fiscal arithmetic entirely. And the catch that the "too much government power" objection (Baker) and the "regulatory conflict / too little oversight" objection are mutually contradictory is a sharp dialectical point: both cannot be the operative fear at once, and naming the state-as-owner's oscillation between "rapacious capitalist" and "doddering central planner" exposes a real incoherence in the discourse.

The soft spot is that the Trump reassurance does not scale. Bruenig argues that because Trump did little with legacy SOEs (TVA IT jobs), a worst-case executive is not worth worrying about. But the Postal Service, TVA, and Amtrak are low-salience, non-strategic legacy entities; a 50%, voting-rights stake in the three leading AI labs is a vastly higher-leverage asset, and the inference from "he ignored Amtrak" to "he would be harmless controlling frontier AI" is weak. His own Fable example cuts against him: if an administration can already shut down a flagship model "with the flick of a pen" without owning the company, then ownership adds direct, normalized control on top of the coercion that already exists — which is Baker's worry sharpened, not refuted. Regulatory coercion is at least visible and contestable; ownership control is more direct and harder to challenge.

He also under-engages two things. He reframes the materialist inference critique (compute, water, grid) as merely "is AI useful," which under-describes it — the strongest version is an externality / opportunity-cost argument (social cost may exceed private benefit) that survives even if users find AI useful. And by answering each critic in isolation, he never confronts the strongest combined objection: that concentrating ownership and regulatory authority over a high-stakes frontier in one executive creates a single point of capture, which is more dangerous precisely because the sector is dangerous. His honest answer — "it depends on the goals and priorities of the relevant officials" — is the whole ballgame, and he treats it as settling the matter when it actually reopens it. Still, as a rebuttal essay it is disciplined and mostly persuasive; the comparative SOE evidence is real and the definitional cleanup is a public service.

↩ Back to TOC

Aaron Levie on current implicit AI regulation

Tyler Cowen · 2026-06-26 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: A social-media post by Aaron Levie (CEO of Box) arguing AI has entered an era of implicit regulation.

Levie's premise is that frontier models above certain capability or compute thresholds will inevitably face government review before release — so "we now have de facto AI regulation" already. From that he draws four implications: (1) the US gains gatekeeping power over who accesses frontier intelligence, which holds only as long as the US stays at the frontier and isn't surpassed; (2) a review backlog slows the rapid iterative release cadence, trading frequent small model jumps for bigger, slower step-functions — with a bear case that those small jumps fueled the innovation flywheel; (3) other nations gain more incentive to pursue sovereign AI to avoid dependence on US access, especially given China's progress; (4) open-weight models become relative winners, as the base sovereign AI gets built on and the channel still releasable without the same controls. Cowen concurs, says he long expected this, and is pleased AI made so much progress "under the wire" before regulation tightened. Note: the "already regulated" premise describes an anticipated trajectory more than current US law, which gates compute and chip exports, not model releases.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

The load-bearing premise — "we now have de facto AI regulation" via mandatory government pre-release review — is asserted as accomplished fact when it is better described as a trajectory. There is no formal US statute requiring capability- or compute-gated pre-release review as of mid-2026; what exists is chip/compute export control plus voluntary lab commitments. Levie collapses "this looks inevitable" into "here we are," and Cowen accepts the framing. Once granted, the four implications are reasonable scenario-sketching, and two are durable regardless of whether formal review actually materializes: the sovereign-AI hedging incentive and the open-weights tailwind both follow from any tightening of US-controlled access. The sharpest internal tension is one Levie names but doesn't resolve — US gatekeeping "works as long as we remain at the frontier" — because the entire control regime is self-undermining the moment a rival (China) reaches parity, which is precisely the scenario that motivates everyone else's sovereign-AI hedge.

Cowen's tell is the "under the wire" line: it reveals an accelerationist relief that capabilities advanced fast before regulation could clamp down, consistent with his standing view that protecting the US lead is the priority. This is link-commentary, not argument, and reads best as scenario-mapping rather than a claim about current law — its value is the four-way decomposition (access control / release cadence / sovereign hedging / open-weights), not the contestable premise that the regime already exists.

↩ Back to TOC

AI and the Crisis of ‘Classical Liberalism’

Geoff Shullenberger · 2026-06-26 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: the American Enterprise Institute's new Council on AI Ethics founding document (published in The New Atlantis) and Dean W. Ball's public defense of his shift toward supporting AI "guardrails."

AEI — a laissez-faire stalwart rather than a religious-conservative hub — convened a Council on AI Ethics whose founding document brackets practical and regulatory questions in favor of philosophical reflection, invoking bioethicist Leon Kass's "wisdom of repugnance"; Shullenberger reads this as a sign of the right's deepening "cleavage" (Greg Conti's term) between tech accelerationists and social/religious conservatives. More interesting to him is a contradiction within the free-market wing's own "classical liberal" framework, which he illustrates through Dean Ball — former Trump AI advisor and author of its laissez-faire AI Action Plan — who "opposes almost all AI regulation" yet co-wrote a New York Times op-ed urging "appropriate guardrails" against catastrophic risk. Ball described his fascination with AI eventually "conducting the core functions of the state" (enabling "new architectures of civilization") as an expression of his classical liberalism, while conceding it is a "top-down project to remake the world." Shullenberger identifies those "new architectures" as Nick Bostrom's "singleton" — a single global decision-making agency — which is the implicit prize of an AI arms race aimed at "decisive strategic advantage." His thesis: classical liberalism demands minimal intervention because competition supposedly makes everyone richer and statism is the slippery slope to tyranny, but AI leaders (Elon Musk, not just "woke" Anthropic) now predict a "permanent underclass" requiring UBI, and many builders, following Bostrom, believe they are constructing exactly the technocratic world-government the creed warns against — so laissez-faire toward AI is itself a top-down project to remake the world. He suggests this should push classical liberals to rethink the relationship between human flourishing and the state, but expects they won't.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

The internal-critique structure is the essay's real contribution and it is textually earned. Rather than rehearsing the familiar "fusionist" split on the right (the business-vs-religion alliance Shullenberger calls the two legs of the fusionist stool), he catches Ball's own words doing the contradicting — labeling a bid for AI-run statecraft a "classical liberal" enthusiasm while admitting in the same breath that it is the "top-down project to remake the world" the creed exists to resist. Pairing that with Bostrom's definition of the "singleton" (a single global decision-making agency, from his 2014 book Superintelligence) makes the point concrete rather than rhetorical. For a writer whose recurring beat is the failure of secular and progressive tech criticism — he has argued elsewhere that "tech criticism failed" and that religious thinkers see AI more clearly than the left's "irritable mental gestures" — this is a characteristic and well-built move: locate the operative critique in conservative/religious thought and show the free-market right intellectually unequipped for the moment.

The structural weakness is scope inflation: scaling one faction's contradiction up to "the crisis of classical liberalism." The essay slides from "some Bostrom-influenced builders intend a singleton, and one classical liberal is ambivalent about it" to "the creed is in crisis." But a classical liberal can coherently back competitive markets and oppose a monopolistic firm's bid for a decisive-strategic-advantage singleton — that anti-concentration-of-power stance is the tradition's core, not a betrayal of it. Ball's own fear shows the position is available; Shullenberger frames an unresolved tension as an unresolvable contradiction.

The load-bearing premise on the other prong is industry self-description. The "permanent underclass"/UBI argument treats AI leaders' speculative claims (Musk's tweet, Altman-style forecasts) as if they establish that the technology will fail the "makes-everyone-richer" premise. These are predictions by interested parties, and the apocalyptic register doubles as marketing — the same hype that sells the product is here recruited to refute classical liberalism. The "crisis" is partly an artifact of taking the builders' grandiosity at face value; if the singleton is one faction's eschatology rather than the industry's destiny, the crisis shrinks to "boosters who haven't reconciled their commitments." Honest caveat about what the piece is: it is diagnostic, not prescriptive — Shullenberger explicitly punts the constructive suggestion ("too much to ask for now"), so the payoff is the "Oops!" gotcha rather than a developed alternative. Not Straussian: the thesis is stated outright in the body and the closer.

↩ Back to TOC

Translated from the Chinese

Tyler Cowen · 2026-06-25 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: A translated Chinese-language post promoting CoPaper.AI, a tool from Stanford's REAP team (attributed to Prof. Ross Griebenow) that claims to auto-generate empirical papers.

The quoted post calls this the "Cursor moment for academia" — referencing Cursor, the AI code editor that transformed software development — and claims CoPaper.AI takes a raw dataset and within 30 minutes produces a complete DOCX paper with reproducible Stata/R/EViews code and publication-quality charts.
