It allegedly chains EDA (exploratory data analysis), variable definition, and econometric modeling (OLS through difference-in-differences, regression discontinuity, and causal forests) in an agent workflow, threatening "paper mills," "data drones," and ghostwriters with obsolescence and leaving humans only to define the problem. Cowen's own gloss is the operative content: "Mostly that is not true, so perhaps the Chinese are trying to demoralize us," followed by the leading questions — "But will it never ever be true? In two years be true? Less?"

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

The Straussian rule does not apply here. Cowen states his operative view directly in the closer: the tool's claims are "mostly not true" now, but the directional bet — the part he actually cares about — is the timeline to when they will be, which he telegraphs by collapsing his own rhetorical questions toward "less than two years." The questions are leading, but the claim is on the surface, so it reads literally: discount the hype, take the trajectory seriously. The "trying to demoralize us" line is a half-joke that doubles as a frame — even if the post is psyop, the correct response is a clock, not relief.

What's worth naming is the asymmetry Cowen is exploiting. The hype post is plainly overstated: end-to-end "raw data → publishable causal paper in 30 minutes" misrepresents what is actually hard about empirical social science (identification strategy, data construction, robustness, and defending causal assumptions are the labor; running the regression is not), and "100% reproducible" code does not make a research design valid. But Cowen's move is to concede all of that and still extract the unsettling question, which is the genuinely interesting content: the automatable layer (EDA, boilerplate estimation, charting, code) is exactly the "entropy-increasing drudgery" that fills the low-quality end of empirical output, so even a partial version pressures the paper-mill tail of the distribution well before it touches frontier work. The piece is link-commentary; its value is the calibrated question, not the quoted claims.

↩ Back to TOC

The West can't govern itself: Britain, Europe, state capacity

How Britain lost control

Jonathan Sumption · 2026-06-22 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: the tenth anniversary of the June 2016 Brexit referendum (extracted from his book The Brexit Effect, 2016–2026, Cambridge University Press).

Jonathan Sumption — a former UK Supreme Court justice and medieval historian — reads Brexit through Britain's long foreign-policy tradition: since Elizabeth I, Britain has sought to prevent any single power from dominating the European continent, an instinct the Foreign Office mandarin Eyre Crowe called "almost a law of nature" in 1907. That balance-of-power aim, he argues, could once be pursued from outside but by the late 20th century could only be pursued from inside the EU, the dominant continental bloc. He contends that "Take Back Control" rested on a "primitive" view of sovereignty: joining a multilateral treaty is an exercise of sovereignty — trading some autonomy for greater collective weight — not a surrender of it. The economics, he says, are asymmetric and unchanged by exit: the EU takes ~40% of UK exports while the UK takes only ~10% of the EU's, so Britain must comply with EU standards either way, but now with no say in setting them ("influence has been traded away for nothing"). Post-2020 trade deals merely replicate what membership already provided, and the fisheries "win" proved nearly worthless because ~40% of the British catch lands in the EU and most stocks migrate across the boundary. The "special relationship" with the US is a "threadbare comfort blanket": under Trump and likely after him, Washington demands clientage, and outside the EU Britain — unlike the bloc — has no leverage to refuse, so its subservience "seems likely to be more complete and longer lasting." His forecast: Britain won't rejoin, but will be drawn into ad hoc agreements on the EU's terms, gaining "a modest degree of influence… substantially less than the control" membership conferred.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This doubles down on Sumption's settled persona — the constitutionalist Remainer-realist who treats British self-conception as chronically over-inflated. The essay's real strength is conceptual: the insistence that a multilateral treaty is an exercise rather than a surrender of sovereignty cuts cleanly through the "Take Back Control" frame, and the trade-asymmetry data (the 40%/10% split) gives the abstraction empirical teeth. The fisheries case — Britain has the legal right to exclude EU vessels but can't use it because most of its catch lands in the EU and most species migrate across the maritime boundary — is a well-chosen synecdoche for the gap between formal autonomy and practical control, which is the whole thesis in miniature.

The structural weakness is a symmetry Sumption doesn't acknowledge. He mocks "British Cassandras" for repeatedly and wrongly predicting EU collapse — they "have always underrated the political will to sustain it" — yet his own load-bearing forecast is the mirror image: that "powerful pressures" will push the EU "ultimately towards the creation of a federal superstate." That outcome (fiscal union, common debt issuance, a unified capital-markets union) has been predicted and feared for decades and remains halting; treating it as near-inevitable is exactly the confident structural determinism he faults in others. The argument needs EU consolidation to be inexorable for Britain's marginalization to read as tragic rather than merely costly, and that premise does a lot of quiet work.

The other soft spot is the treatment of Trump as revelation rather than contingency. Sumption is careful — he allows Trump is "impulsive and wayward" — but then converts a single presidency into a permanent condition ("the same voters will be around in 2028"; the Democrats won't reverse it), so Atlanticism is retroactively declared never to have been viable. The strongest opposing view is that the US–UK relationship has survived many transactional and isolationist phases and that the intelligence and nuclear cooperation Sumption concedes is still "critical" is stickier than his "comfort blanket" image allows. Even so, as a realist account of relative weight — Britain offers access to ~70m consumers, the EU to ~450m — the core claim that a mid-sized economy 20 miles off the French coast cannot escape its continental gravity is hard to refute, and the piece never pretends Brexit can be reversed, only that its cost compounds.

Notably, Sumption and Mary Harrington — writing in the same publication on the same anniversary from opposite sides of the 2016 vote (he a Remainer, she a Leaver) — converge on the same verdict: Britain did not take back control. Sumption locates the failure externally (the EU's gravitational dominance); Harrington locates it internally (an unsteerable permanent state). That a confirmed Remainer and a committed Leaver reach the same conclusion through opposite diagnoses is the kind of cross-orientation convergence worth carrying into the synthesis.

↩ Back to TOC

Don’t shed tears for Keir

Mary Harrington · 2026-06-22 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Keir Starmer's resignation as Prime Minister, announced the Monday before publication after a weekend of press speculation; also the tenth anniversary of the Brexit referendum.

Mary Harrington — a post-liberal, self-described reactionary-feminist UnHerd columnist — opens with a loaded indictment of Starmer's record (rendered as rhetorical questions invoking "two-tier Keir," censorship, abortion, and the abolition of jury trials) but pivots to argue the specifics don't matter. Her thesis: since David Cameron, every prime minister has hit the same wall — "the machinery of government won't be steered" — so voters keep swapping the steering wheel (the PM) while "the machine just keeps trundling along." She reframes the 2016 Leave vote as fundamentally about state capacity: her fear was that outsourcing policy to Brussels would atrophy Britain's ability to do "joined-up policy," like a muscle wasting from disuse. But Brexit, she concedes, didn't restore responsiveness; it only sped up the churn of prime ministers. Her sharpest evidence: within months Starmer — "a proceduralists' proceduralist" — was complaining about the bureaucracy's "tepid bath of managed decline" in terms "scarcely distinguishable from Dominic Cummings," which she reads as confirmation the anti-"Blob" critique is real. She predicts Andy Burnham will succeed him but, per a Manchester journalist's profile, is "weaker on joined-up governance," while the rest of Labour is wedded to "business as usual." Her conclusion: not revolution but "counter-revolution" against Tony Blair's "constitutional vandalism" is needed — though she openly doubts Britain "even retains the state capacity" to pull one off.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

The core diagnosis is stronger than its packaging. The observation that prime-ministerial churn is a symptom of an unsteerable state rather than a series of individual failures is a genuine structural insight, and the Starmer-sounds-like-Cummings point is the essay's best move: when a left-establishment, proceduralist PM and the arch-Brexiteer outsider converge on the same "Blob" diagnosis (the "Blob" being the permanent civil-service establishment Cummings has long blamed for blocking reform), the convergence is itself evidence the critique tracks something real rather than partisan grievance. The "muscle atrophy" framing — that delegating policy to Brussels would erode the domestic capacity to make it — is a clean articulation of the strongest version of the Leave-as-state-capacity argument.

The packaging undercuts it, though. The opening catalogue — "full-term abortion, censorship, digital surveillance, the abolition of trial by jury," "soaring youth unemployment," "farm suicides," "two-tier Keir" (the charge that Starmer policed disorder asymmetrically) — is asserted as settled fact through a volley of rhetorical questions, a selection-as-argument a reader should not absorb as neutral record; each is a contested framing presented as obvious. There's also a tension Harrington half-acknowledges: if the problem is purely structural (the machine resists any driver), the moral indictment of Starmer personally is in friction with her own line that he "was ultimately a victim of the same problem as all the others." She wants both the structural alibi and the personal prosecution.

To her credit, the essay declines the usual move of papering over its remedy-gap. The "counter-revolution" she calls for is left almost entirely unspecified — who would carry it out, by what mechanism — and she names that hole herself: whether Britain "retains the state capacity to make a success of such a counter-revolution, or could muster enough aligned operatives." That honesty is the difference between this and a pure declinist lament. The unfalsifiable-decline reflex is still present — "more mass immigration, more Net Zero, more decline. More unrest" is a forecast shaped to fit her priors — and the "it'll be Burnham" prediction leans on a single profile and one homelessness-scheme anecdote to carry a large claim about his governing capacity. But the central structural claim survives, and it converges directly with two other pieces this week: Sumption reaches the same "no control was regained" verdict from the Remainer side, and Aurelien's account of "Policy Management" (procedure displacing substance) hollowing out policy-making is the same hollowed-state thesis from a French ex-diplomat's vantage. The shared object — Western state capacity — is a strong cross-orientation thread.

↩ Back to TOC

Britain's Prime Minister Problem

Joseph C. Sternberg · 2026-06-25 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Prime Minister Keir Starmer's announcement that he will leave office, and the expected succession of Manchester mayor Andy Burnham.

Britain has churned through six prime ministers in a decade, but Sternberg argues this is honesty, not exceptional dysfunction — Britain "isn't more dysfunctional than other Western democracies. It's merely more honest about it." He grants Starmer's particular unsuitability (a legalistic manager who failed even at purging Labour antisemitism and rose only because of Tory incompetence) but argues no leader could have done better: the Conservatives cycled through five PMs across five distinct platforms — Cameron's compassionate conservatism, May's consensus moderation, Johnson's flamboyant populism, Truss's new Thatcherism, Sunak's "desiccated technocracy" — none of which built a durable coalition. Burnham, the likely successor, offers energy and retail skill but no discernible policy program and cannot resolve Labour's split between pragmatic centrists and "reality-detached socialists." Sternberg attributes Britain's more-visible instability to its parliamentary system (a ready vehicle for mid-term leadership change, used historically as a "political safety valve") and its fragility as a political-geographical entity (Scottish/Welsh regionalism, the 2014 independence referendum's 55-45 result, Brexit, Northern Ireland). But the core tensions — cultural rifts layered on socioeconomic splits, exacerbated by mass (often illegal) immigration, a welfare state whose taxation and regulation "strangle growth," and deteriorating dependency ratios — are shared across the West (France paralyzed; the AfD leading German polls). His conclusion: Britain probably can't be governed right now, and neither can its peers.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

Sternberg writes the WSJ's "Political Economics" column and reliably attributes Western malaise to welfare-state economics; this piece doubles down on that house view while giving the "honest, not exceptional" framing real analytical work. Its strengths are compression and two effective rhetorical moves: the Texas-secession analogy (if 45% of Texans had voted to leave the U.S. twelve years ago, "America would remain in crisis today") usefully defamiliarizes the Scotland vote, and the catalogue of five distinct Tory platforms tidily demonstrates that the problem outlasts any single leader or ideology.

The structural weakness is the move common to decline-thesis writing: it bundles disparate causes — regionalism, immigration, the welfare state, demographics — into one undifferentiated "ungovernability" and asserts rather than weighs them. The welfare-state-strangles-growth claim is offered as self-evident without engaging the obvious counterevidence (high-tax, high-welfare Nordic states that are neither low-growth nor ungovernable), which actually undercuts the column's central thesis: if some high-welfare states remain stable, the causes of British instability are more specific than a universal Western condition allows. At 770 words this is forgivable op-ed compression rather than a fundamental flaw, but the diagnosis stays gestural.

The strongest opposing view cuts against Sternberg from inside his own essay: the parliamentary "safety valve" he names is precisely institution-specific. The U.S. and France do not replace leaders mid-term this way because their institutions forbid it — which suggests Britain's visible churn is a feature of its constitutional design, not merely a more honest readout of a shared malaise. He half-concedes this ("only by accident has the U.K. come to suffer a more obvious instability") but then leans back on the universalist frame, leaving the tension between "Britain is exceptional in form" and "Britain is typical in substance" unresolved.

↩ Back to TOC

How Brexit Brought Back Ethnic Politics

Nathan Pinkoski · 2026-06-23 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: the ten-year arc of Brexit (the 2016 referendum through the Starmer government) and, specifically, David Goodhart's class-based "somewheres vs. anywheres" interpretation in The Road to Somewhere (2017).

Pinkoski argues Brexit falsified three twentieth-century presuppositions, each pointing toward a new twenty-first-century practice. First, that managed mass democracy could be steered to progressive ends: Cameron, the careless "heir to Blair," broke with Blairite caution by calling a referendum and lost it despite the "Project Fear" campaign (Mark Carney's Bank of England, the IMF, Obama's "back of the queue" warning). Second, that a state divides cleanly into elected government and implementing administration: Blair had transferred power to a "para-state" of independent courts, the Human Rights Act, regulators, quasi-governmental "Quangos," and an independent Bank of England, so a social-democratic revival on behalf of Goodhart's "somewheres" was structurally impossible — the 2019 Miller prorogation ruling, in which the Supreme Court overrode the Crown-in-parliament, is his showcase of judicial sovereignty, and the Tories, he says, only scaled up para-state funding after 2016. Third, and the title claim: that Britain divides on class, not ethnicity. He contends civic-nationalist elites preferred class-based explanations because they preserved a "postethnic" Britain, but ethnic politics was already resurgent — Scottish nationalism (which he reads as ethnic self-demarcation, fueled rather than dampened by devolution) and the rise of "elsewheres," immigrant-origin communities with sectarian arrangements that the somewhere/anywhere binary cannot capture. He predicts the white British majority will increasingly self-identify by shared ancestry rather than civic values, and that every future prime minister must manage ethnic politics within para-state constraints. Brexit, he concludes, revealed these problems but resolved nothing.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

Pinkoski (a senior fellow at the Center for Renewing America, a national-conservative think tank, and a translator of Alasdair MacIntyre's biographer) writes squarely in the post-liberal Compact register. The essay's architecture — three falsifications, each yielding a new political practice — is genuinely elegant, and the para-state analysis is its strongest stretch: the reading of Miller (the 2019 case voiding Boris Johnson's prorogation of parliament) as a transfer of sovereignty from the Crown-in-parliament to the judiciary is a defensible interpretation, and the observation that devolution gave Scottish nationalism more institutional surface on which to differentiate itself, rather than placating it, is empirically plausible. The spotting of an analytic gap in Goodhart — that Scottish nationalists (progressive-talking, but economically like northern Leave voters) and immigrant-origin "elsewheres" both break the somewhere/anywhere binary — is a real contribution.

The load-bearing weakness is that the entire title thesis rests on an elastic use of "ethnic." Pinkoski stretches one word to cover three very different phenomena — Scottish sub-national nationalism, British-Muslim communal/sectarian organization, and an emergent white-majority ancestral identity — and treats their unification under "ethnic politics" as demonstrated rather than asserted. The Scottish case is the weakest link: describing Scottish nationalism as "the ethnically homogenous population of Scotland demarcating itself from an English ethnicity" reclassifies a largely civic, national, and historical distinction as racial-ethnic by fiat, because the thesis requires it. Here the framework does too much work.

There is also a symmetry problem he never confronts. The essay convincingly charges class-based readings with being "alluring because they did not challenge civic nationalism" — i.e., motivated reasoning — but his own ethnic-politics frame is at least as congenial to national-conservative priors, which make ethnic mobilization the deep truth of politics; the suspicion he turns on Goodhart applies symmetrically to him. His closing prediction — that the white majority will shift from civic to ancestral self-understanding — is presented as near-inevitable, is effectively unfalsifiable on the essay's own terms, and reads less as forecast than as a future the tradition welcomes. The "elsewheres" coinage does sharp descriptive work but smuggles a normative frame ("resident aliens," kin "far away from Britain") that essentializes immigrant-origin Britons as permanently external. The piece is a strong diagnosis of the para-state and a provocative taxonomy, undercut by a key term carrying more weight than its evidence supports.

↩ Back to TOC

While We Were Sleeping.

Aurelien · 2026-06-24 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: what he characterizes as the failure of the recent US/Israeli campaign against Iran and a resulting "one-sided" US–Iran Memorandum of Understanding — used as the launching example, not the subject.

Aurelien — a pseudonymous former European diplomat and NATO official — builds a taxonomy of why governments are repeatedly "surprised" in international affairs. He distinguishes several types: surprise-at-failure (a wished-for outcome generates a chain of necessary-but-false assumptions, as he argues the Iran air campaign did, and as Operation Barbarossa and Iraq 2003 did); surprise-by-indifference and surprise-by-ignorance (not bothering, or being unable, to learn the actual situation — the Falklands junta, Iraq's Sunni-Shia divide); surprise-by-complacency (the 1979 fall of the Shah, where Western embassies talked only to Westernized elites and could not imagine a religious-political movement); genuine unknowables (Pearl Harbor's last-minute torpedo fuses); and surprise-as-state-change (sudden collapses — 1989, plus a string of armies that disintegrated on contact: Mali, Congo, Mosul/ISIS, Afghanistan, Assad's Syria in 2024). His diagnostic core: "governments" don't get surprised, individuals do, drowning in a daily blizzard of unsourced information that structured analysis cannot triage at scale; and policy-making has been progressively replaced by what he calls "Policy Management," in which nearly all effort goes to procedure and almost none to substance. The deepest cause, he argues, is conceptual blindness — "to anticipate something, you must believe it is possible" — and a "plodding, materialist, reductive conception of politics" that cannot grasp religion, nationalism, or how China, Russia and Iran actually think. He closes with caveats: history is "terrifyingly contingent," nothing is deterministic, and some surprises are genuinely excusable.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

The taxonomy is the essay's genuine value, and several pieces of it are sharp. The "backwards logic" anatomy of the Iran air campaign — the desired political outcome (regime collapse) silently generating a cascade of assumptions that had to be true (the state is fragile, the targets are hittable, the population will rise) because otherwise the objective is unattainable — is a real and well-described cognitive failure mode. The information-saturation account, dramatized through a Foreign Ministry desk officer's day consumed by meetings and message-clearing with no time left to think, is concrete and credible, and the "Policy Management" coinage (procedure displacing substance) names something readers of any bureaucracy will recognize.

But two structural problems run through it, both flagged in this project's standing cautions about this writer. The first is self-credentialing as argument: the essay's authority rests on insider texture — "I'm reliably informed that there are people in Washington…," "people who had been in the UN Mission in Haiti… told me," "I sat in many stuffy rooms in 1991-92" — so the reader is asked to credit conclusions on the strength of an anonymous author's claimed proximity to the rooms. The second is near-unfalsifiability. The conceptual-blindness thesis can absorb any outcome: a missed prediction is blindness, and a correct one is dismissed (those who foresaw Communism's end were "generally right for the wrong reasons"). Likewise "Policy Management" is asserted as a recent secular decline while he concedes "it was always like this to some degree" — with no measure offered for the deterioration. A framework that explains every result equally well explains none in particular.

The topical hook is also the most contestable empirical claim and should not be carried downstream as settled: that the Iran campaign was an "ineffective" US "effective surrender" via a "one-sided" Memorandum of Understanding is asserted, not argued, and reflects Aurelien's consistent prior against US power projection; whether the 2025–26 Iran episode was a US defeat is precisely a live dispute among other roster writers. The example selection reinforces this — the catalogue is almost entirely Western failures-to-foresee, building a West-in-decline narrative even as he gestures that the failing "is not unique to the West." What partly redeems the piece is its self-awareness: the distinction he draws between doing politics (real decisions with real consequences, which breeds conservatism) and merely commenting on it ("that's not our job, we're only here to criticise") is a jab that implicates his own genre. Read against his pattern, this is Aurelien doubling down — the taxonomic essay, the insider voice, the institutional-decay thesis — but with enough honest hedging at the close to land better than his more sweeping pieces. Worth noting the week's coincidence: Aurelien invokes Operation Barbarossa as his archetype of surprise-at-failure in the same week Adam Tooze reprints his deep economic history of the same campaign — the same event used as a cognitive parable on one hand and a colonial-economic anatomy on the other. His "Policy Management" decay thesis also converges with the British "unsteerable state" diagnosis in Harrington and Sumption.

↩ Back to TOC

Rod Dreher: What I Learned in Hungary

Rod Dreher · 2026-06-26 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Viktor Orbán's April electoral defeat and the incoming Tisza / Péter Magyar government's reversal of his policies; reflective essay after four years living in Budapest.

Rod Dreher, leaving Budapest after four years at the government-funded Danube Institute, offers a chastened verdict: Orbán's Hungary was neither the "semi-fascist" caricature of Western media nor the model its admirers imagined. He argues the "illiberal democracy" label was a self-inflicted misnomer (Hungary's 2011 Fundamental Law is, he says, a liberal-democratic document) and that Orbán's real project was rejecting a "globalist" liberalism hostile to Christianity and nation. His central concession is empirical: Orbán's lavish pronatalist spending (5–6% of GDP) raised fertility only temporarily (1.23 to a 1.61 peak, then back down), which Dreher reads as proof the birth-dearth is "a spiritual and religious crisis," not a material one. He documents Hungary's deep secularization (93% Christian in 1992 to 42.5% by recent census, with few churchgoers) and argues "cultural Christianity" cannot re-Christianize a people. Fidesz lost, he says, on a stalled economy and cronyism — captured by a friend's morning-after "Fuck Fidesz" — and the new Tisza government is now purging Orbán-linked institutions, cozying to the EU, and abandoning the anti-migration and pro-Christian stances. He concludes Hungary "is over" as a MAGA model, but extracts lessons: politics follows culture; the right learned from Orbán that state power can fight left-captured institutions (Trump's university moves, DeSantis's in Florida); and, pointedly, that economically squeezed voters won't tolerate corruption and loyalty-over-competence governance — "I hope the White House is paying attention."

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

Calibrated against Dreher's pattern, the concessions are real and to his credit. He is the Benedict Option author and a one-time Orbán enthusiast who relocated to live the experiment, so admitting that aggressive pronatal spending didn't durably move fertility — and declaring Orbánism dead as an exportable model — is a genuine, falsifiable-flavored update that cuts against the pronatalist-policy enthusiasm common on his side. But the concession operates at the level of tactics and personnel (Fidesz corruption, failed subsidies) while the deeper worldview — civilizational decline driven by secularism and consumerism, the spiritual-crisis diagnosis, the politics-follows-culture axiom — is reinforced, not revised. It is a partial departure presented as hard-won wisdom; the core frame doubles down.

The key empirical inference overreaches. Dreher leaps from "pronatal subsidies didn't durably raise fertility" to "therefore the crisis is spiritual, not material" — but his own text supplies a material confound: fertility "sank back to the doldrums" in Hungary's "stalled post-Covid economy," and he allows "no doubt for other reasons as well" before asserting the spiritual reading anyway. The failure of one policy lever (cash subsidies) doesn't establish that material factors are not "at the core"; cost-of-living, housing, and economic insecurity are material explanations he waves past to reach the conclusion his priors prefer. The spiritual diagnosis may be right, but it is asserted, not earned from the fertility data he marshals.

The essay also leans on dispatch-texture as argument. The vivid scenes — the St. Stephen's Day drone-crown dissolving into a cross over a secular crowd, the farewell dinner's "spiritual desert," the 30-something who wants a "Magyar Sweden" of "shopping, traveling, and sexual freedom" — are selected to confirm the de-Christianization thesis, and the "Magyar Sweden" generational verdict is one pessimistic conservative's secondhand characterization of an entire cohort offered as evidence (selection-as-argument). The strongest and most current move, though, is stated plainly rather than Straussianly: Dreher turns Hungary into a warning to Trump's Washington — that governance "driven more by loyalty than competence," plus corruption, is what cost Orbán power despite his cultural wins. That from-the-right caution is the piece's live contribution, and it lands precisely because Dreher earned it by watching a national-conservative project he wanted to succeed collapse on ordinary-governance failures rather than on the ideological grounds its critics had predicted.

↩ Back to TOC

Who controls reality: knowledge, memory, and the therapeutic

Right and Left Are Seeing Two Different Worlds

Dan Williams · 2026-06-25 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: the scholarly and political "different worlds" / factual-polarization debate (drawing on Williams's own prior work; a version first appeared at his Conspicuous Cognition blog).

Williams, a philosopher of epistemics at the University of Sussex, first grants the optimistic revisionist case that bare factual disagreement is smaller than feared, on three grounds: misinformation and fake-news prevalence has been overstated; better survey designs (paying respondents for accuracy, offering "don't know" options, screening trolls) shrink apparent factual error; and the "backfire effect" plus irrational-tribalism findings largely failed to replicate, with people in fact updating on evidence. But he argues this optimism is premature because "facts do not interpret themselves." Drawing on Walter Lippmann's Public Opinion (1922) — the "pseudo-environment," the "pictures in our heads," "we define first and then see" — he names the deeper problem interpretive polarization. His central illustration is a paired, inverted set of cases: the UK murder of white student Henry Nowak by a Sikh man (whose "I can't breathe" body-cam death the populist right read as proof of a "two-tier" anti-white justice system and the left read as an isolated tragedy not to be "politicized"), and George Floyd's 2020 death, where the interpretive valences of right and left were exactly reversed. His key claim is that the most resilient "stereotypes" are the hard-to-falsify ones — interpretations (like "two-tier justice") that gobble up confirming and screen out disconfirming evidence — and that they are never neutral, projecting "our own value, our own positions and our own rights." On remedies he is pessimistic: Lippmann's technocratic "organized intelligence" fix is fragile because knowledge institutions can themselves be captured by pseudo-environments, and even objective ones work only if trusted — and trust has eroded, partly, he argues, through the left's institutional dominance and a strand of progressive thought that replaces objectivity with activism.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is Williams in his strongest mode: a clean conceptual reframing that dissolves a confused debate by relocating where the disagreement actually lives. The factual-versus-interpretive distinction is genuinely clarifying, and the inverted-cases structure is the essay's best move — by showing the same "I can't breathe" frame deployed with opposite political valence by opposite coalitions (right-coded for Nowak, left-coded for Floyd), he demonstrates that the operative variable is the interpretive grid, not the facts and not even a fixed partisan disposition toward a given type of fact. The Lippmann retrieval is apt and well-deployed, and the observation that the most durable frames are precisely the unfalsifiable ones is a real contribution to why fact-checking under-performs.

The essay's central tension is that it half-escapes and half-reproduces the symmetry its own framework implies. For most of its length the argument is rigorously even-handed: both coalitions inhabit pseudo-environments, both are "in a sense wrong," both invert their salience-rules between Floyd and Nowak. But the remedies section breaks that symmetry, locating the trust collapse specifically in "the left's growing institutional dominance" and progressive activism displacing objectivity. That may be correct, but it is asserted rather than argued, and it sits awkwardly with the framework: if pseudo-environments are general features of mediated cognition, a one-sided causal story about institutional capture is itself the kind of selective salience the essay warns against. The strongest objection — that "make institutions more impartial" is exactly the technocratic fix he has just shown to be self-undermining — he half-concedes ("easier to diagnose than to prescribe") without resolving.

A second limit is that the framework risks proving too much. If all pseudo-environments are "in a sense wrong" and the resilient ones are unfalsifiable, the essay needs but never supplies a criterion for ranking them; Lippmann's "not all are equally wrong" is invoked and then dropped. Without that criterion, "interpretive polarization" can flatten the difference between an interpretation that tracks reality better and one that tracks it worse into mere variation in framing — which would undercut Williams's own evident view that some readings (e.g., the empirically checkable components of "two-tier justice") are better than others. As a diagnostic the essay fully lands, and it is the cluster's framework piece — the meta-level account of why a given week's partisan-press divergence looks the way it does, and a useful lens for the synthesis's "different worlds" thread. As prescription it is candidly thin, and the one place it abandons its own symmetry (the remedies' partisan causal story) is the place to read most skeptically. Not flawed — it is an interpretive essay, and a strong one — but the asymmetry in the cure is the seam.

↩ Back to TOC

Stop Talking About Mental Health

Ashley Frawley · 2026-06-25 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: an April 30, 2026 OECD report claiming more than one in five people across OECD and EU countries experience a mental disorder and calling for more prevention and awareness spending.

Frawley argues the "talk more about mental health" consensus is self-defeating: awareness campaigns manufacture the very distress they purport to treat. She grounds this in philosopher Ian Hacking's "looping" account of interactive human kinds — labels do not passively describe people, they create new ways of being — and in a lineage of therapeutic-culture critics (Philip Rieff, Thomas Szasz, Christopher Lasch, Frank Furedi). She marshals recent empirical support: a 2025 randomized controlled trial in which ADHD-awareness materials raised healthy people's belief they had the disorder, an Australian depression-labeling study, and a 2026 review of eleven experiments finding awareness materials consistently lower diagnostic thresholds and increase symptom-scanning, with young people most affected. The Dutch NEMESIS surveys (diagnosable disorder rising from 17.4% to 26.1% between 2007–09 and 2019–22, alongside a 2016 national awareness campaign) and England/Wales incapacity-claim growth (2.9 million in 2019 to 4.5 million in 2025) are offered as the pattern at scale. Her political claim is that governments cling to the mental-health frame because it justifies almost any policy (e.g., Starmer's under-16 social-media ban smuggling in age verification the public had rejected) and because it converts material conflicts — she cites EU "resilience" support for farmers whose 2023–24 tractor protests targeted actual EU policy — into technocratic problems, removing "who gets what and who decides" from politics. She ends on a hopeful note: in the Eurobarometer survey behind the OECD's figure, respondents ranked material conditions above awareness campaigns for their wellbeing.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This doubles down on Frawley's established therapeutic-culture critique (her prior work on happiness and wellbeing discourse, in the Furedi / Centre for Parenting Culture Studies tradition). What distinguishes it from the usual cultural lament is a genuinely strong evidentiary base: the looping-kinds mechanism plus the RCT and meta-review evidence make the iatrogenic claim more than vibes-level, since the experiments isolate the labeling effect from genuine pathology. The materialist twist — depoliticization, farmer suicides versus farmer tractor protests, "who decides" — grafts a left-coded grievance critique onto a frame usually deployed from the right, and that cross-coding is the piece's most interesting move and Compact's signature.

The central tension is that Frawley wants the measured rise in disorder to be artifactual (awareness/labeling inflating the numbers) and simultaneously wants material conditions to be the true driver of distress. These pull against each other. If worsening material conditions are genuinely making people unwell — as her closing Eurobarometer point and the incapacity-claims surge both suggest — then much of the rise is real distress, not manufactured by labels, which softens "stop talking about mental health" into the milder "stop mislabeling material suffering." She gestures at reconciliation (the frame displaces material politics) but never settles whether the NEMESIS and incapacity figures reflect inflation or real immiseration; the "perhaps uncoincidentally" linking the 2016 Dutch campaign to the rise is correlational, and she concedes prevalence is hard to measure before leaning on the rise anyway.

The strongest opposing view — that under-treatment and stigma remain real, and that some labeling produces appropriate help-seeking rather than harm — is partly blunted by her best evidence: the RCTs inducing healthy people to self-diagnose isolate iatrogenesis from genuine illness, so the mechanism largely lands even if its magnitude is contestable. Worth flagging as paratext: her affiliation with MCC Brussels (a Hungarian-government-linked institute) sits behind the anti-technocratic, anti-EU framing, though it does not undercut the empirical core.

↩ Back to TOC

Recovered Memories Aren't Real

Freddie deBoer's Blog · 2026-06-24 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: the dueling lawsuits over Amy Griffin's bestselling 2025 memoir The Tell — in which Griffin describes recovering, via psychedelic-assisted therapy, a childhood sexual-assault memory — including Joleene Altum's countersuit-spawning claim that Griffin stole her story.

deBoer argues that "recovered" or "repressed" memories as conceived in pop psychology — trauma automatically sealing a memory whole into an inaccessible vault for years, then disgorging it intact under therapy — do not exist, and that this is the considered view of the experimental memory-science community rather than a fringe position. He traces the idea to Freud and the nineteenth-century dissociation tradition, then identifies its core defect: a non-falsifiable "meme logic" in which remembering proves abuse and not-remembering proves repression. He marshals four lines of evidence: rebranding "repression" as "dissociative amnesia" doesn't change the claim; trauma science finds horrifying events are remembered too well (stress hormones consolidate memory — psychologist Richard McNally), the opposite of repression; a 2025 review found the neuroimaging case for dissociative amnesia thin (small samples, no control groups, activity better explained by ordinary intentional suppression); and the "recovery" techniques closely match Elizabeth Loftus's false-memory implantation paradigm (lost-in-the-mall, meeting Bugs Bunny at Disney), which leads on the order of 15% of subjects to "remember" invented events. He notes that mental-health professionals are no more skeptical than laypeople — skepticism concentrates among cognitive/legal researchers — and documents the human cost via the George Franklin, Gary Ramona, and Paul Ingram cases and the Sybil/Shirley Mason "multiple personality" fraud.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is deBoer in evidence-marshaling mode, and on the merits the piece tracks the cognitive- and legal-psychology mainstream accurately: Loftus's lost-in-the-mall and Bugs-Bunny implantation studies, McNally on the hyper-consolidation of traumatic memory, the false-memory literature, and the Franklin/Ramona/Ingram cases are all real and fairly characterized. The contrarian-sounding headline names a claim that is, in its strong form, well supported — the specific mechanism the popular picture requires (a trauma-triggered unconscious sealing-away of an otherwise-intact memory, recoverable years later) is precisely the part the evidence doesn't bear, and deBoer is careful to distinguish it from ordinary forget-then-be-reminded. Matching critique to mode: this is an empirical argument and it clears the bar. The legal/due-process upshot — that a recovered memory can't ground a conviction because it is unfalsifiable and yields no physical evidence — is the firmest part of the piece.

Worth flagging a structural tension in deBoer's posture. He is normally the roster's anti-credentialist, suspicious of "the experts agree" as a move; here his case leans explicitly on expert consensus ("not a fringe contrarian position but rather the consensus of experts"). He squares this by splitting the professions — cognitive and legal memory researchers are skeptical, practicing clinicians are not — and siding with the sub-field most exposed to the courtroom consequences. That distinction is defensible, but it is also convenient: it lets him invoke consensus where it favors him and dismiss it (clinicians) where it doesn't. The piece also slots neatly into his standing "Trauma Culture" skepticism (the territory of his forthcoming All In Your Head), so it doubles down on a known prior rather than departing from one.

The gap is in the steelman. deBoer's cleanest rhetorical win is the non-falsifiability point, but the crude "not remembering proves repression" meme he rebuts is not the strongest pro-recovery position; that would be betrayal-trauma theory (Jennifer Freyd) and the small corroborated-recovered-memory literature, which posit a narrower mechanism for abuse by a depended-upon caregiver. He doesn't engage that version by name — his ordinary-forgetting carve-out gestures toward it but doesn't adjudicate the live edge of the debate. The ~15% implantation rate is also load-bearing, and he hedges it appropriately ("on the order of"), though it varies considerably across studies and paradigms. None of this undermines the core claim, but the prose is tidier than the underlying science: a genuinely contested boundary is presented as fully settled.

↩ Back to TOC

I Co-Founded Wikipedia. Now I'm Banned for Life.

Larry Sanger · 2026-06-26 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Sanger's own indefinite ban from Wikipedia (Monday), following rejection of his WikiProject Intellectual Diversity application; firsthand commentary.

Larry Sanger, who co-founded Wikipedia in 2001 and left in 2002, recounts being permanently banned and uses the episode to argue the site has drifted from neutrality into a "globalist, academic, secular, and progressive" mouthpiece of a narrow liberal establishment. He attributes the drift to a governance vacuum: Wikipedia never adopted a community charter and is instead run by a small class of mostly anonymous admins (~400 active) interpreting vague rules with no real accountability. After his 2025 "Nine Theses" reform proposal failed to gain traction, he organized WikiProject Intellectual Diversity (WPID), a "big tent" for excluded viewpoints — Hindus, confessional Christians, Israelis, conservatives, libertarians, but also "TERFs, alternative medicine practitioners, and climate skeptics" — recruiting roughly 30 volunteers via X. The application drew a pile-on; he was charged with "canvassing" (for noting the debate on X) and "bludgeoning" (for arguing his defense too vigorously), tried at the Administrators' Noticeboard, and banned by an Arbitration Committee member using a pseudonym. He details the process's lack of due process — no fixed charges, accusers serving as judges, no presumption of innocence — and notes that even Jimmy Wales agreed his X post wasn't canvassing. He closes by proposing alternatives (article-rating tools, Grokipedia, a decentralized "Encyclosphere") and insisting Wikipedia needs competitors.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

The firsthand ban account is the strong, well-sourced core. Sanger names the deciding admin handle ("ScottishFinnishRadish"), quotes the opposing comments, cites Wales conceding the canvassing charge was baseless, and even reports that Grok's read was more balanced than the human pile-on — verifiable specifics, not vibes. His procedural critique of the Administrators' Noticeboard (self-selected prosecutors, charges that accrete from anything you say, accusers doubling as judges, "bludgeoning" as a catch-22 against self-defense) is a recognizable and probably accurate description of how Wikipedia's quasi-judicial culture operates, and the leadership-anonymity point (only ~15% of leaders disclose identities) is a real governance concern.

But the load-bearing claim — systematic ideological bias — rests on co-founder authority plus a handful of cherry-picked examples (the GOP "right-wing to far-right" label, disfavored Christian and Hindu sources), not on demonstration. The self-credentialing does work the argument doesn't: "I Co-Founded Wikipedia. Now I'm Banned for Life" frames a routine sanction against an active recruitment-campaigning editor as a kind of parricide, when Sanger has been an estranged outside critic for over two decades. The founder identity supplies emotional and moral weight that the empirical bias case — contestable on every example — does not independently earn.

The clearest internal tension is WPID's own membership list. Bundling "Hindus, confessional Christians, Israelis, conservatives" (plausibly excluded on viewpoint grounds) with "alternative medicine practitioners and climate skeptics" (excluded on reliability/epistemic grounds) collapses the very distinction his neutrality argument needs. Wikipedia's "undue weight" and "fringe" rules are an epistemic gatekeeping mechanism, not merely an ideological one; treating viewpoint-diversity and the inclusion of alt-medicine as the same project hands his critics their strongest rebuttal — that "intellectual diversity" would relitigate settled reliability standards. His neutrality-as-symmetry premise — that on every contested topic the truth sits midway, so a "right-wing to far-right" label is per se bias rather than a sourced characterization — is asserted, not defended, which is exactly where the opposing case lands.

↩ Back to TOC

How Podcasts Ruined America

Michael Tracey · 2026-06-24 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Original commentary, pegged to Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick's October 2025 New York Post podcast claims about Jeffrey Epstein (and his May 2026 House Oversight walk-back, "I was just speculating for a podcast") and the 2025-26 "Epstein files" media frenzy.

Tracey argues that the podcast "superstructure" has displaced the adversarial "interview" with the genial "conversation" — a format in which pressing a figure to substantiate claims reads as a breach of etiquette — so public figures now choose comfortable hosts (Joe Rogan, Theo Von) over outlets that might cross-examine them. Lutnick is the opening exhibit: a sitting Cabinet official floating that Epstein was "the world's greatest blackmailer" with no pushback, even from a New York Post interviewer ostensibly operating journalistically — evidence, Tracey says, that podcast norms have seeped into traditional settings. He traces a lineage from Rogan's early JFK/UFO/lost-civilization banter to a "default epistemic conspiracism": a baseline assumption that everything is staged and a "they" is always scheming, rewarded by the algorithm regardless of left or right coding. The 2024 Trump campaign, he argues, operationalized this "Rogan-seeded podcast ideology" (with Elon Musk, RFK Jr., and Tulsi Gabbard as surrogates), promising Deep State revelations and an Epstein "client list" — setting up the inevitable 2025 backlash (Pam Bondi's nonexistent "list on my desk") when the payoff never materialized. He catalogs the resulting frenzy: the "pizza/grape soda" code-word theories (even at the journalism-adjacent show Breaking Points, via Krystal Ball and Saagar Enjeti), and former biology professor Bret Weinstein's claim, derived from a proprietary "game-theoretical" formula, that Epstein is still alive. The set-piece is Tracey's own Web Summit panel ("Should we get rid of podcasts?"), where he asked Weinstein whether he had reviewed the autopsy photos in the very files he discusses; Weinstein dodged and accused him of an ambush. Tracey closes by comparing Rogan's "just a comedian / just a podcaster" shield to Jon Stewart's, arguing Rogan is far more influential and that the medium offers no incentive for correction. Notably, he concedes mid-essay that the podcast is a "content-neutral medium" and that what he actually wants to abolish is the current algorithmic superstructure — narrowing the title's monocausal "podcasts ruined America" claim.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

The reporting carries the piece. The Lutnick exhibit is clean — a sitting Commerce Secretary literally telling Congress "I was just speculating for a podcast" is a near-perfect illustration of the accountability gap — and the interview-vs-conversation distinction is a real analytic point: the conversational frame makes any pushback feel like a rudeness, so the format selects for figures who won't probe. The Web Summit confrontation gives the essay first-hand, falsifiable texture (the autopsy photos exist in the released files; Weinstein's "Epstein is alive" claim is checkable against them), and the conspiracism is documented with specific quotes rather than gestured at. The core diagnosis — that a genial, metrics-driven medium with no correction mechanism rewards conspiracist epistemology — survives.

It survives, however, in a much narrower form than the title asserts, and Tracey concedes as much: the body grants that the podcast is "content-neutral" and that he is really indicting the "currently existing superstructure," not the medium. "How Podcasts Ruined America" is itself the kind of monocausal, attention-grabbing overclaim the podcast economy rewards — the headline overshoots the argument the author is willing to defend.

The deeper tension is that the essay performs several of the moves it diagnoses. It is selection-as-argument: a parade of worst cases (Weinstein, Eddie Bravo's "they're eating babies," Roger Avary) is used to characterize an entire medium, which is the same totalizing leap ("everything is X") Tracey attributes to conspiracism. Its register — "sanity-contaminating," a "colossal engine of nonstop hysteria-fomentation," America "ruined" — is exactly the affect-laden, algorithm-friendly rhetoric it criticizes, and the authorial pose (the lone journalist who walks onstage to confront the conspiracist cabal) mirrors the brave-truth-teller genre of the podcasts themselves. The paratext does this work openly: the sign-off "Michael Tracey is a journalist. He writes at mtracey.net" credentials him against "Podcast Land" while eliding that he, too, operates in adjacent independent / X-native media — the clean traditional-vs-podcast binary is partly self-serving.

Finally, the causal story runs one way only. Tracey credits legacy journalism with a "truth-seeking imperative" while conceding it is not "immune" to audience capture, but he doesn't grapple with the obvious reverse arrow: podcasts' credibility is itself partly a response to legacy media's own accountability failures (the Russiagate coverage he names elsewhere, etc.), which is a large part of why audiences defected. He also lumps very different operations — Rogan's freeform conspiracism, Breaking Points' journalism-adjacent format, the New York Post's podcast, Tucker Carlson, Weinstein's Dark Horse — under one "podcast" causal banner, which papers over their distinct incentives. The result is a piece whose best material (Lutnick, the interview/conversation distinction, the Weinstein dustup) is sharp and earned, wrapped in a frame that overclaims and enacts the very pathologies it names.

↩ Back to TOC

The Metaculus Democracy Threat Index

Scott Alexander · 2026-06-25 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: the Metaculus US Democracy Threat Index (a new forecasting product built on 153 democracy-relevant questions, curated by the group Bright Line Watch) and prior reader challenges that his Trump-era alarm might be "Trump Derangement Syndrome."

Wanting an objective check on his own judgment, Alexander reviews the Metaculus index, which aggregates crowd forecasts (a recency-weighted median) instead of relying on the expert panels that make traditional democracy indices easy to dismiss as biased. He credits its advantages — transparency, crowdsourcing, and finer-grained probabilities (a 10% vs. 50% chance of canceled elections rather than a binary "not yet") — while naming two residual risks: vulnerability to a coordinated crowd attack (partly mitigated by obscurity and by medians' robustness versus means) and question-selector bias, since whoever chooses the 153 questions reintroduces the expert-judgment problem the design meant to remove. His example — a "memecoin bribery" question that is currently Republican-coded — shows how a "neutral" question set still encodes 2025's partisan asymmetries; he argues the index gains value over time because a frozen question set resists optimization as biases drift across years. He distrusts the index's "historical backfill" (which retroactively scores 2025-invented questions against pre-2025 reality) but finds the forward-looking forecasts believable and "reassuring": forecasters expect democracy not to worsen over Trump's term, and the projected 2027-2028 threat level fell from 47% to 39% after the November special elections went smoothly (with Trump's preferred candidates losing) and a grand jury failed to indict Letitia James. His wish list: duplicate questions on Polymarket/Kalshi for attack-resilience, a policy for adding questions, and more active forecasters.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is Alexander in careful methodological-review mode, and the epistemic hygiene is real. He separates the believable data (the forward forecasts) from the unreliable (the historical backfill, which retroactively scores questions invented in 2025 against what had already happened by 2021-2024 — many explanations besides declining democracy). He names the attack and selector-bias vectors precisely, and lands a genuinely sharp point: a frozen question set resists partisan optimization because today's biases correlate only weakly with tomorrow's, so a fixed 2025 set can't be tuned to 2035's asymmetries. The memecoin example is a crisp demonstration that question selection is never value-free.

The load-bearing move worth scrutiny is converting forecaster sentiment into a present-tense "health of democracy" score and treating it as an independent check on his own alarm. Forecasting future events (will elections be canceled) is falsifiable; reading the current aggregate probability as an objective measure of how threatened democracy is right now is closer to measuring crowd belief. And the crowd is not independent of Alexander: Metaculus forecasters skew toward his own rationalist/effective-altruist-adjacent milieu (the discussion he links lives on the EA Forum), so a reassuring reading risks confirming that his bubble shares his priors rather than correcting them. The "TDS check" framing is the operative paratext — the index is recruited as external arbiter, and it conveniently returns the reassurance the framing was hoping for.

Two further calibrations. First, superforecasting's track record is strongest on geopolitical questions with usable base rates and weakest on novel, reflexive, adversarial institutional questions — which is exactly the category of democratic backsliding; "forecasters expect democracy won't get worse" is reassuring only if you grant calibration on the rare regime-level events where forecasting is least tested. Second, there is a tension Alexander half-notices: the trustless property depends on a fixed question set, but his own wish list asks for "a policy on how new questions will be added," and any evolution reintroduces the selector bias he flagged. None of this is fatal — the piece is unusually honest about its own limits, and its central claim (this is a better-designed but still-biased index than expert panels) survives. The fair reading is that the index is a real transparency improvement, not the objective arbiter the TDS framing wants it to be.

↩ Back to TOC

The industrial map: deglobalization, tariffs, and the China challenge

China is winning the other tech race

Noahpinion · 2026-06-24 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Ryan McEntush's a16z essay on electric tech "unifying" manufacturing and the "late aerospace republic," plus a Simon Johnson/Elisabeth Reynolds Bloomberg piece and a New York Times article on US-China electric-tech competition; framed through Paul Kennedy's The Rise and Fall of the Great Powers.

Using Kennedy's thesis that great powers rise by mastering the era's key technologies (and fall when those are disrupted), Smith argues the US AI lead — he cites The Economist's estimate of an eight-to-ten-month private-benchmark gap over Chinese models — is real but not the only race that matters. The "other race" is electric technology: electricity powering the physical world via three turn-of-century inventions — modern batteries, permanent-magnet motors, and power electronics — which have broken electricity out of fine-control uses into high-power applications (EVs, drones, robots) and are "unifying" once-separate manufacturing supply chains into a single stack ("a drone is just a phone with a couple of extra parts"). China dominates this stack — most of the world's batteries, magnets, motors, drones, EVs, and humanoid robots, plus rare-earth supply (Deng's legacy) and Xi's targeted "new three" industrial policy (EVs, lithium-ion batteries, solar) — and is electrifying its whole economy toward becoming the "first electrostate," while the US is an "also-ran" kept in the game only by Tesla. Smith faults American complacency (conservatives dismissing EVs as "hippie-dippie bullshit," Ford/GM's "innovator's dilemma," the GOP "war on EVs" cutting demand, attacks on solar, NIMBY blocks on transmission) and argues that mastering AI won't by itself confer mastery of the physical world — "a billion geniuses in a data center" won't deliver drones and robots without the electric stack. He discloses that he invested in Western Magnetics, a US electric-motor startup whose sector his policy conclusion favors.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

The descriptive core is solid and well-sourced: China's dominance of batteries, EVs, solar, drones, rare earths, and electric motors is not in serious dispute, and Smith's real contribution is the synthesis — treating modern batteries, permanent-magnet motors, and power electronics as one coherent "electric revolution" rather than three separate industries, and noticing that it collapses formerly-distinct supply chains into a single manufacturing stack. The atoms-vs-bits framing (AI as the culmination of "bits" progress; electric tech as the thing finally breaking the post-1970s stagnation in "atoms") is clarifying, and the Johnson/Reynolds "invent it here, make it there" point on drones is concrete and damning. This is quintessential Smith — abundance-liberal, sector-targeted industrial policy, China-hawk who nonetheless credits Chinese execution — doubling down on a thesis he has built across earlier posts.

The contestable leap is from "China leads electric manufacturing" to "this is a race America is losing in the great-power sense." Smith stacks two clean dichotomies — an AI/bits race (US winning) versus an electric/atoms race (China winning) — but his own analysis says the two converge (software-defined, AI-integrated electric platforms; a robot is AI embodied). Once they converge, the strongest counter he names but doesn't rebut is the "smile curve": the US AI/autonomy lead might capture the high-margin design-and-software layer of embodied tech while China is left as the lower-margin hardware supplier — in which case owning the AI layer is the more defensible moat, and "a billion geniuses in a data center" is closer to decisive than he allows. He asserts that atoms still matter rather than demonstrating that hardware dominance beats autonomy dominance where the strategic value actually accrues.

Two further strains. The Kennedy scaffold is applied selectively: Kennedy's central mechanism is fiscal-economic overstretch (powers over-extend relative to their economic base), which Smith brackets in favor of the "master the key technology" reading — convenient, since the overstretch thesis would also flag China's real-estate, debt, and demographic problems. And "master the era's technology → become the great power" is a near-unfalsifiable technological determinism: the Netherlands→Britain illustrations are post-hoc, and the closer ("some Paul Kennedy of the future may conclude...") is an unfalsifiable hedge that asserts decline without quantifying its stakes the way the AI section does. Finally, the Western Magnetics disclosure is properly made but worth weighting — the essay's policy ask (a US industrial push on motors and magnets) directly favors the author's investment, so the most interested-party-aligned claims deserve a touch more independent corroboration than the piece supplies. None of this dents the descriptive core; the gap is between a well-evidenced "China dominates electric manufacturing" and a more asserted-than-shown "and that means American decline."

↩ Back to TOC

Tariffs Are Only Half the Battle

Matthew Lynn · 2026-06-25 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: the May 2026 Trump–Beijing visit and China's confirmed order of ~200 Boeing aircraft, plus reports China is slowing Airbus approvals to pressure the EU into buying its domestic COMAC jets.

Matthew Lynn — a British markets columnist writing in Commonplace, a magazine that champions industrial policy over free-market orthodoxy — argues tariffs only make sense as the first half of a plan whose second half (deploying tariff revenue to rebuild the industrial base) has never arrived. He treats commercial aerospace as the decisive test case: COMAC, China's state aircraft maker founded in 2008 to break the Boeing-Airbus duopoly, now has its C919 narrow-body in domestic service and a CR929 wide-body (with Russia) in planning, backed by a captive market of hundreds of millions and the patience to take a 20-year view. Boeing, meanwhile, is "visibly wobbling" after the 737 MAX crisis, and the US has no credible next-generation jet program. Lynn concedes state-directed economies carry real inefficiencies (China's property crisis) but argues that in capital-intensive, long-lead strategic industries the ability to fund a 20-year plan is a genuine advantage the West underrates. He prescribes four things: a properly funded next-gen aircraft program; a serious (better-managed) supersonic/hypersonic program; a strategic aerospace manufacturing cluster modeled on Silicon Valley or Baden-Württemberg autos; and a decision about whether Boeing is a national strategic asset warranting a structured government partnership short of nationalization. His warning: tariffs without the build-out are "dreamy industrial nostalgia" — barriers around industries the US spent 30 years neglecting.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

The central argument is sound and is the heterodox-right house position Commonplace exists to make: protection without investment regrows nothing. The part-one/part-two framing is crisp, and the COMAC trajectory is marshaled accurately — a state entrant with a guaranteed domestic market, leverage over debtor nations, and the willingness to absorb decades of losses is precisely the competitor a tariff wall alone cannot stop. The supporting data (China at ~53% of global shipbuilding tonnage against five US-built ships in 2022; Boston displaced as a biotech hub) does legitimate work establishing that the neglect is broad, not aerospace-specific.

Two soft spots. First, the entire "part two" rests on tariff revenue being both large and durable, which Lynn flags ("assuming they survive legal challenges") but doesn't reckon with at the level of incidence: tariff revenue is substantially a tax on US importers and consumers, so "deploying the revenues" is partly recycling a domestic levy, not capturing foreign money. Second, the supersonic/hypersonic prescription is the weakest link in a piece that warns against nostalgia — Concorde, the Anglo-French supersonic jet he invokes, failed on economics and physics (fuel burn, the overland sonic-boom bans that confined it to ocean routes, a thin premium market), not merely "management," and "the science has improved" waves past constraints (overland boom bans still stand) that remain decisive. A call for a "better-managed Concorde" is itself a flash of the industrial nostalgia the essay diagnoses.

There's also a mild internal tension Lynn mostly contains: the China property crisis he cites as evidence that state direction breeds "inefficiencies and distortions" is itself a product of the same "take a 20-year view and fund it lavishly" model he wants Washington to emulate, so the cautionary example slightly undercuts the prescription. The strongest opposing view — that the US lacks the state capacity and cross-administration political durability to sustain a 30-year industrial program — is not engaged; Lynn assumes the will can be summoned ("nor should it prove impossible"). But the diagnostic core lands, and the piece is most persuasive precisely where it is least prescriptive, in naming the empty lot behind the wall. It belongs in the week's US–China industrial-competition thread rather than the British/European cluster, with the Airbus/COMAC angle as its one European hook.

↩ Back to TOC

Mexico's Oil Strategy Is Paying Off

Germán S. Díaz del Castillo · 2026-06-26 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Original commentary occasioned by the June 2026 Strait-of-Hormuz closure and resulting ~40% global oil-price spike, set against Mexico's long-running energy-policy debate.

Díaz del Castillo opens with the 2017 "gasolinazo" panic — fuel hoarding after a gas-price hike — as proof that Mexico, though theoretically able to meet its own ~1.75 million-barrels-per-day demand, was left vulnerable by aging, underused refineries (its six "legacy" plants ran under 40% capacity) and growing dependence on US fuel imports (~800,000 bbl/day in 2018). He credits the post-2018 self-sufficiency push by President Andrés Manuel López Obrador (AMLO) — acquiring the Texas Deer Park refinery for state oil company Pemex, building the Olmeca/Dos Bocas refinery, modernizing existing plants — with a Q4 2025 throughput jump of 44% year-over-year and a 5% cut in US gasoline imports. He frames opposition objections (Dos Bocas's $12 billion cost overrun, underperformance, safety issues) as the worldview of NAFTA-era "neoliberals" trained at ITAM (Autonomous Technological Institute of Mexico) who trust the global market. The Hormuz price shock, he argues, vindicated AMLO's intuition that globalized oil markets are fragile and US unipolarity is ending — even though his successor, President Claudia Sheinbaum, still had to make a fiscal intervention to hold prices down. He closes by placing Mexico within a broader self-reliance trend (Indonesia, Nigeria), declaring the peak era of globalized markets over.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is a materialist, non-US-perspective energy essay in an interpretive-with-statistics mode, and its strongest contribution is supplying a concrete causal story — gasolinazo panic → infrastructure diagnosis → state-led self-sufficiency program → recent throughput gains — rather than the usual abstract deglobalization gesture. The framing of Mexico as one node in a wider self-reliance turn (alongside Indonesia's Prabowo agenda and Nigeria's shift to net gasoline exporter) is a genuinely interesting lens.

The central weakness is that the "vindication" verdict does not follow from the author's own evidence. The tell is the conceded fiscal intervention: if domestic refining had actually insulated Mexico, Sheinbaum would not have needed to subsidize to keep pump prices down during the Hormuz spike. Refined-product prices track the global crude benchmark regardless of where the gasoline is refined, so self-sufficiency in refining capacity does not by itself decouple Mexican consumers from world price shocks. The episode he cites shows continued exposure, not vindication.

The piece is also boosterish in its selection. It treats the +44% throughput (off a very low base) and the 5% import cut as decisive while folding the $12 billion overrun, underperformance, and safety problems into "opposition objections" to be dismissed rather than weighed, and it omits Pemex's standing as one of the world's most indebted oil companies — the strongest financial case against the refinery program. The Nigeria example also fits poorly: that turnaround was driven by the privately built Dangote refinery, not a state-led Pemex-style push. The net is a real and underexplored argument about deglobalization whose "paying off / vindicated" headline outruns what the evidence here can carry.

↩ Back to TOC

Translation: How Tolerant is China’s Internet?

SW · 2026-06-24 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: former Global Times editor Hu Xijin's October complaint that public discourse in China has narrowed because "society has become less tolerant" — a critique Hu carefully wrapped in praise for "the constitutional order led by the Communist Party of China."

In this translated WeChat essay (surfaced and annotated by China Digital Times, the anti-censorship outlet), the dissident writer Wang Wusi accepts Hu's word — "tolerance" — and turns it back on him. Far from intolerant, Wang argues, China "leads the world in tolerance": citizens tolerate lead-poisoned kindergartners, contaminated infant formula, and occupants burning to death in malfunctioning Xiaomi electric cars ("giant four-wheeled smartphones"), while bereaved families stay "emotionally stable" and responsible parties — Xiaomi's Lei Jun, Tencent — never apologize, reform, or stop suing critics. Hu, caricatured as a maggot or "Egyptian catfish" thriving in filth, helped build the environment he now mourns; his and others' calls for "tolerance" are really demands that the public excuse the powerful. The closing thesis: "those who teach you to glorify suffering are the very ones who caused it." CDT's editor frames the piece as a salvo in the "Hu is part of the problem" camp and flags its in-text censorship evasions — 国家 ("country") rendered "xx" and 统治 ("ruling") as "Z" inside a Brecht quotation — plus the host account's serial deletion and rebirth ("New New New Silence").

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

The contribution is the non-Anglophone epistemic world in primary-source form: this is an artifact of Chinese online dissent, not Western reportage about China, and it rewards reading on two levels. As argument, Wang's move is a clean ironic inversion — by adopting Hu Xijin's own vocabulary and redefining "tolerance" as the public's forced, learned passivity in the face of elite impunity, he flips a complaint about speech-narrowing into an indictment of unaccountability. That is sharper than it looks: it refuses Hu's premise that intolerance is the disease and relocates the pathology to excess tolerance and the self-interested elites who preach it. The extended Hu-as-maggot conceit is invective rather than analysis, and the piece is openly polemical, not evidentiary — but the concrete referents (the Tianshui lead-poisoning case, the Sanlu melamine-milk scandal, the Xiaomi SU7 fires and 100,000-car recall) are real and carry the load.

The genuinely instructive layer is paratextual, and it is why the piece belongs in a roundup: the form is the argument. The censorship-evasion glyphs ("xx," "Z") and the "serially reincarnated" account name are themselves the evidence — Wang can only describe China's intolerance of speech by performing the evasions that intolerance forces, so the typography demonstrates narrowed discourse more viscerally than any explainer could. A necessary caveat for the synthesis: this is a single anonymous dissident voice, selected and curated by an explicitly anti-censorship outlet, so it is an adversarial, non-representative sample of Chinese opinion — its value is existence-proof and texture, not distribution. Worth noting alongside it is CDT's flagged observation about Hu's own rhetoric — couching a critique of speech restriction inside praise for Party-led "constitutional order" to disarm censors and Western critics at once — which is itself a useful specimen of how even establishment-adjacent figures must encode dissent.

↩ Back to TOC

Standalone Pieces

Title Author Publication Summary
US Subways Build Too Many Cross Passages Brian Potter Construction Physics (Potter) Potter argues US subways are needlessly expensive partly because the NFPA 130 fire code mandates egress cross-passages every 800 feet — more than twice as tight as Europe's standard — with no measurable safety payoff, and the requirement should be relaxed.
Against urban planning Matthew Yglesias Slow Boring Yglesias argues that prescriptive urban planning is mostly destructive over-complication — markets handle real-estate tradeoffs better — and the profession's one real contribution, the street grid, barely matters.
Is It Time for a New Sexual Revolution? Ross Douthat NYT Perry, in conversation with Douthat, argues the sexual revolution reset relations between the sexes to men's advantage, and that a "reactionary feminism" grounded in real biological difference points toward restraint, marriage, and choosing partners as if contraception didn't exist.
Toy Story 2 is the Best Toy Story Because It's Actually For Children Freddie deBoer's Blog deBoer deBoer argues Toy Story 2 is the best of the franchise precisely because it stays inside a child's emotional world, while the critically beloved Toy Story 3 trades in nostalgia and mortality — adult emotions, made for the critics handing out the prizes.
Safety Nets Have Strings Attached Oren Cass Commonplace American Compass's newsletter argues that fraud enforcement protects rather than threatens the safety net — program integrity is "prior to" the fight over program size — so progressives who dismiss fraud scrutiny as "a stalking horse for cruelty" are the safety net's worst enemies.
New Business Formation is Surging–Again. Alex Tabarrok Marginal Revolution Tabarrok reads record new-business formation in the US and France as the early stage of an AI- and software-enabled "experimental economy" reversing the long dynamism decline.
The Lessons of Alan Greenspan's Life Ryan Young National Review (publication) Young eulogizes Greenspan as a rare ideas-and-politics figure whose central lesson is that rule-based monetary policy (a de facto Taylor rule) was his great success — and his failure to formalize it, then abandon it, helped seed the 2008 crisis.
Tenuous Ties Sam Pressler Commonplace Pressler argues that working-class men without degrees aren't simply disconnected but live on "tenuous ties" — a single friend, mentor, or group that becomes a single point of failure — and that the remedy is collective re-embedding, not individual self-blame.
Minnesota “Antifa” Terrorists Charged Ken Klippenstein Ken Klippenstein Klippenstein argues the Minnesota indictment of 15 anti-ICE protesters as "Antifa" domestic terrorists is NSPM-7-driven "pre-crime" against a category the government cannot define, and reads it against a record of collapsed prosecutions.
How Liberals Misread MAGA Gregory Conti Compact Conti argues Laura Field's Furious Minds fails as a guide to MAGA because its condemnatory typology ignores the right's real fault lines — above all the tech-right/AI accelerationist split — while Harvey Mansfield's academic critiques have quietly become conventional wisdom.
Beyond SAF and RSF: Why Sudan’s War Cannot Be Understood Through Two Actors Alone Eglal Hamid African Arguments (publication) Hamid argues the SAF-versus-RSF binary that frames Sudan's war and its peace talks obscures how governance has been redistributed to local civilian networks, traditional authorities, and autonomous commanders who actually run daily life.
How much deforestation is your country importing through trade? Hannah Ritchie Sustainability by Numbers (Ritchie) Ritchie shows, using a new trade-embodied-deforestation dataset, that roughly three-quarters of global deforestation serves domestic rather than international demand — puncturing the assumption that rich-world imports are the main driver.
Should People Avoid Whole-Body Screening Info? Scott Alexander ACT Alexander builds a back-of-envelope QALY model putting whole-body MRI screening right at the cost-effectiveness threshold, then argues that the model's unverifiability — not its result — is what justifies doctors' default recommendation against it.

US Subways Build Too Many Cross Passages

Brian Potter · 2026-06-25 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Original commentary — written for the Institute for Progress (IFP) "Transit Abundance Playbook," a collection of 15 proposals to lower US transit construction costs and timelines.
