Potter isolates one mechanical driver of US transit cost: cross-passages, the side tunnels connecting twin rail bores that provide fire egress, which the National Fire Protection Association's transit standard (NFPA 130) requires be spaced no more than 800 feet apart. He explains the cost mechanism — cross-passages can't be cut by the efficient tunnel-boring machine and instead need drill-and-blast or roadheaders, running from roughly $100,000 to $5–10 million each — and traces the 800-foot figure to a 1970 Atlanta MARTA design assumption about how far passengers could walk before "flashover." European standards permit up to 1,640 feet (London's Elizabeth line runs ~1,600, up to 2,275), roughly double, and many European and Japanese metros use few cross-passages at all. He argues there is little evidence the tighter US spacing improves safety: both systems are exceptionally safe, London and New York have near-identical and vanishing fatality records, the NFPA itself admits a "lack of technical substantiation," and analyses (a JICA study for Cairo's Metro Line 4; consultant Mosen) put the cost-benefit ratio above 10. He notes the existing engineering-analysis "escape hatch" is too burdensome to use, leaving 800 feet the de facto rule, and recommends NFPA revise the standard (next edition 2029) or that the FTA, states, and agencies modify it directly to allow ~1,600-foot spacing.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Claude's Response:

This is the "atoms not bits" / cost-disease beat at its best, doing exactly what the genre is for: tracing an aggregate cost problem (US tunneling running multiples of Europe's) to a specific, legible code provision, pinning that provision's origin (a 1970 MARTA flashover-walking-distance assumption), quantifying its marginal cost, and pointing at the precise revision mechanism (the NFPA 2029 cycle; FTA and state adoption modifications) that could change it. The argument is modest and well-scoped — Potter claims cross-passages are one driver, not the whole US–Europe gap — and he pre-empts the obvious rebuttal by conceding the standard already contains an engineering-analysis escape hatch, then explaining persuasively why burden-of-proof and project-delay risk make agencies default to 800 feet regardless. The European track record at 1,640 feet is the cleanest evidence that the tighter US figure buys little.

The soft spot is the structure of the safety case, which is largely an absence-of-evidence argument: low aggregate fatality rates, no past incident that tighter spacing would have prevented, and the NFPA's own admission. That is reasonable, but the hazard cross-passages guard against — a fire stranding a train between stations — is a low-frequency, high-consequence tail event, and an empirical fatality record can't strongly distinguish 800 from 1,640 feet in either direction; the strongest pro-tight-spacing case is precautionary and tail-risk-based, not frequency-based, and Potter's body-count framing doesn't fully meet it. He does partly answer the mechanism (the JICA point that modern trains are largely noncombustible and can usually limp to a station), which is more probative than the fatality tallies. Note too that this is advocacy written for an abundance-agenda playbook, so it argues toward a predetermined conclusion — but the sources are real, the reasoning is transparent, and the cost-benefit-ratio-above-10 claim, if it holds, is close to decisive. No fundamental flaw; the lone weak seam is treating a tail-risk safety question as settled by aggregate statistics.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Against urban planning

Matthew Yglesias · 2026-06-22 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Original commentary, framed around a college SimCity seminar taught by a former mayor (and a linked discussion of mandatory ground-floor-retail rules).

Using the city-building game SimCity as a foil, Yglesias argues that real-world urban planning is more complicated than the game "overwhelmingly in bad ways" — an over-complication of what actually needs planning. He grants the profession one genuinely good idea, the coherent street grid with a hierarchy of road widths, but immediately discounts it as ancient (Roman Paris had one) and not very important, noting that London and Boston function fine with chaotic streets. His central charge is that planning denies tradeoffs and ignores the virtues of markets: cities mandate ground-floor retail (producing unleasable, zero-value spaces) while banning mixed-use and multifamily housing on most of their land, and pile on setbacks, "anti-massing" rules, and shadow restrictions that have nothing to do with how beloved neighborhoods actually work. He contends the risks are asymmetric — under-planning creates modest waste that high or low land values can self-correct, while over-prescriptive planning creates massive waste in every dimension. The "city as engine" close argues that planners treat the city as an art project (the always-sunny rendering) rather than an economic flywheel, and that a neighborhood not overcrowded at peak times is necessarily undercrowded most of the time — so overcrowding is a sign of success, not a planning failure.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Claude's Response:

This is core abundance/YIMBY (yes-in-my-backyard, pro-housing-supply) Yglesias and doubles down on his land-use wheelhouse, deploying two of his sharper recurring arguments. The asymmetric-risk framing — under-planning is cheaply correctable, over-planning is not — is a clean way to shift the burden of proof onto prescriptive rules, and the "if it's not overcrowded at peak, it's undercrowded the rest of the time" observation is a genuinely good point about how aestheticized planning imagines a city frozen at its most photogenic moment. The mandatory-retail-while-banning-mixed-use contradiction is a real and well-chosen hypocrisy.

The main structural problem is a mismatch between the title and the argument. "Against urban planning" promises a wholesale case, but the essay's own SimCity setup opens with the strongest justification for planning — separating residences from polluting power plants and industry — which is a textbook externality and coordination problem that markets handle poorly. Yglesias never rebuts that case; he pivots to softer targets (setbacks, anti-massing, retail mandates, shadow rules) that concern aesthetic prescription rather than externality management, and lets a sound critique of design micromanagement stand in for a far broader claim it doesn't support. The defensible thesis ("against over-prescriptive design rules") is the motte (the easily-defended fallback position); "against urban planning" is the bailey (the sweeping claim actually being advanced).

Two specific moves are weaker than they look. The argument that park-less neighborhoods self-correct because low land values make retrofitting easy waves away the parcel-assembly and holdout problem, which is exactly what makes building parks in built-up areas hard regardless of land price. And his concession on infrastructure — roads, rail, and transmission rights-of-way do need to be laid out in advance — actually cuts against his "even the street grid barely matters" line, since getting a network and its rights-of-way wrong is precisely the kind of error that is enormously costly to fix later, which is an argument for some forward planning. The core claim that markets are good at the dense web of real-estate tradeoffs is sound and is his strongest material; the essay would be more persuasive titled for what it actually argues. Because it is interpretive rather than empirical, the right test is internal consistency, and on that test the framework is applied past its domain — onto exactly the externality and coordination cases where the market story is weakest.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Is It Time for a New Sexual Revolution?

Ross Douthat · 2026-06-25 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Original commentary — a Douthat "Interesting Times" podcast conversation (full edited transcript) with Louise Perry (Wall Street Journal columnist, author of "The Case Against the Sexual Revolution"), revisiting her 2020 book amid intensifying male-female political and cultural alienation.

This is a long podcast conversation rather than a written argument. Perry defines the sexual revolution as both ideological and material, the latter driven by the pill, which she says made women "temporarily sterile" and that invisibility "a different creature." Her core case: men and women differ physically and, on average, psychologically — men have a stronger drive toward casual sex while women disproportionately bear its costs (unplanned pregnancy, sexual violence) — so a hookup culture is structurally worse for women even if some women seek it. She traces the feminist critique from Andrea Dworkin and Catharine MacKinnon (whom she calls prescient on pornography, thinking of women as a Marxist "class") losing the "sex wars" to an individualist, choice-maximizing sex-positivity, then revived by #MeToo. She argues sexual violence is "primarily biological," not ideological, so consent workshops are "no match for pornography," and that this paradoxically rehabilitates Dworkin/MacKinnon's anti-pornography logic. On the present, she diagnoses "heteropessimism" (women's antagonism toward heterosexual relationships born of being burned by hookup culture), locates young women — not men — as the group shifting most sharply left, blames the internet for exposing private homosocial talk and turbocharging mimetic radicalization, and reads tradwives as angry and sad for the same reasons as progressive feminists but with different, equally-online and self-contradictory solutions (e.g., "free birthing"). Her prescriptions are individual: women should behave "as if contraception doesn't exist," choosing the man not the moment and reserving sex for prospective fathers, while using contraception within marriage to space births. Douthat repeatedly presses her on whether sexual restraint is compatible with female emancipation, "Australian beach" liberal norms, gay and lesbian people, and avoiding slut-shaming; Perry largely concedes she can offer critique but cannot "be the architect of an entirely new sexual culture," expecting "the religious are going to inherit the earth" while hoping individuals can still nearly "have it all" through agency. The conversation closes on her self-described "tragic outlook": no clear solutions at scale, but real ones at the individual level.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Claude's Response:

Douthat is a Catholic-conservative columnist who frequently hosts ideological fellow-travelers, but the value of this episode is that he functions as a genuinely probing interlocutor rather than an amplifier. The intellectual payload is the running argument over whether female emancipation and sexual restraint are separable or fundamentally opposed: Douthat keeps forcing Perry to confront the tension between her chastity ideal and the modern conditions she concedes are permanent (the pill, the internet, women in the workforce, the existence of gay and lesbian people, "Australian beach" norms of public female freedom). Perry's honest answer — "I don't know" — is the conversation's real finding, and the episode is stronger for letting the irresolution stand instead of manufacturing a synthesis.

Two of Perry's moves are doing outsized load-bearing work and deserve flagging. First, her claim that sexual violence is "primarily biological," which she uses both to dismiss consent education and to revive Dworkin/MacKinnon's case for banning pornography; she states this as fact with little evidence, it is the most contestable premise in the conversation, and Douthat presses it far less than he presses the chastity question. Second, her "behave as if contraception doesn't exist" prescription collides with her own enforcement problem: she concedes that historically such restraint norms were enforced through slut-shaming and sexual double standards she explicitly dislikes — a genuine internal tension she does not resolve, captured in her admission that she can offer critique but not architect a culture. Her appeal to "the religious are going to inherit the earth" also quietly converts a normative case into a demographic prediction, which sidesteps whether the ethic is desirable by asserting it is merely inevitable.

The strongest opposing view, which Douthat voices well, is the false-consciousness objection: women are politically free actors choosing these arrangements, so a framework premised on the revolution being "bad for women" condescends to them. Perry's reply — heteropessimism as evidence of real damage, plus the sharper structural point that "if feminist cultures turn out to be cultures that cannot reproduce themselves, then the political project dies within a generation" — partly lands but does not fully answer the autonomy challenge. The reproduction-of-cultures argument is the most durable thing she says and the one most portable beyond the gender debate; the rest is best read as honest, well-tested critique that repeatedly declines to become a program.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Toy Story 2 is the Best Toy Story Because It's Actually For Children

Freddie deBoer's Blog · 2026-06-22 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Original commentary, occasioned by the release of Toy Story 5 (which deBoer's family skipped) and the durable critical consensus that Toy Story 3 is the best entry in the series.

deBoer concedes that Toy Story 3 is the most accomplished film by every craft metric a defender would reach for, but argues it is nonetheless not the best, because its operative emotions — nostalgia, mortality, the end of play (Andy leaving for college, the heroes joining hands before the incinerator) — are adult emotions, and the film is engineered to make grown-ups feel grown-up things. Toy Story 2, by contrast, inhabits a child's worldview without ever winking over kids' heads at the parents: at its core is a chase movie that resolves a primal fear of abandonment in terms a seven-year-old understands — your friends come for you, the villain is beaten, you go home. He argues that making a film genuinely for children is the harder, rarer accomplishment, since adult tears for lost childhood are easy to provoke. He closes with a cultural lament: children's culture has been "colonized" by adults — animated films stuffed with knowing winks pitched past the kid to mom and dad, and franchises like Star Wars curdled into scripture for grown men who treat canon as a moral cause.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Claude's Response:

A characteristic deBoer move, transposed from politics to film criticism: the populist-against-the-credentialed-gatekeepers frame ("made for the people handing out the prizes") aimed at the critical establishment rather than the PMC. The aesthetic argument is genuinely sharp, and he argues it honestly — conceding TS3 is the more accomplished and "serious" film, and distinguishing "a movie I can't enjoy" from "a movie I think is bad," which keeps the piece from collapsing into pure contrarianism. The for-children-versus-for-critics binary is clean and does real explanatory work, but it also does a lot of work: TS2's own most famous beat, Jessie's "When Somebody Loved Me," is itself a celebrated adult tearjerker, and the Prospector's bitterness rhymes with TS3's Lotso — so the seam between the two films is blurrier than the thesis allows. deBoer pre-empts this by reading the Jessie song as operating "inside a child's emotional world," which is arguable but convenient.

The load-bearing and most contestable claim is the closing cultural-decline thesis — that movies truly for children "have all but vanished" and children's culture has been colonized by arrested-development adults. It is asserted rather than demonstrated, resting on a thin set of examples (the quippy-animated-movie register; the Star Wars canon wars), and it is the same flatter-the-reader's-sense-that-things-were-better-before gesture he diagnoses in the critics who reward TS3's nostalgia. As cultural criticism it lands as a fun, well-argued provocation rather than a load-bearing empirical claim — which is the right weight for it here.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Safety Nets Have Strings Attached

Oren Cass · 2026-06-22 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The Trump administration's Task Force to Eliminate Fraud (chaired by VP Vance), catalyzed by the Minnesota benefit-fraud exposure, and federal moves to tighten Medicaid/SNAP/unemployment eligibility and audit states.

This is the "Understanding America" weekly newsletter from Oren Cass's American Compass (the byline is Cass; the lead essay is signed "Daniel"). The core argument: a generous safety net rests on a reciprocal "compact" — resources taken from workers reach those who genuinely cannot work — and fraud has two victims, the taxpayer whose contribution is stolen and the real beneficiary whose program is looted. Fraud's deeper cost is trust, "the basic currency of the safety net": once the public stops believing benefits reach only the qualified, support for generosity curdles into resentment. The post-liberal payoff is that "program integrity is prior to the fight over parameters" — whatever a program's proper size, all should agree it must be honestly run — so the progressive reflex of "waving off fraud as a stalking horse for cruelty" actually betrays the dependent citizen. The remainder of the newsletter is a curated link roundup (a tech-governance retrospective; "Bad Studies" critiques of a Yale Budget Lab immigration-productivity paper and an anti-tariff paper; America-250 polls; EU-China trade), not standalone arguments. Note for downstream writers: the lead essay relays VP Vance's "$250 billion lost to fraud" figure with no scrutiny — the same credulity the newsletter mocks two sections later in its "Bad Studies" takedowns — and frames front-end eligibility screening and federal record-demands as neutral "integrity" while eliding their predictable exclusion of eligible claimants.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Claude's Response:

The genuinely strong move is the decoupling of program integrity from program size. It is hard to argue against honest administration, and reframing fraud enforcement as pro-safety-net (defending the compact) rather than anti-welfare is the strongest available version of the conservative case — and a characteristically American Compass one: post-liberal in accepting generous social insurance while insisting it carry obligations (the "strings" of the title). The "two victims" and "trust as the safety net's currency" framings are rhetorically efficient and not wrong on their own terms; unpunished fraud does erode the political coalition for generosity.

But the piece's own newsletter undercuts it. In its "Bad Studies" section it mocks center-left and center-right reports for taking "the form of serious economics" while amounting to "an unpersuasive press release" — yet the lead essay passes along the administration's "$250 billion lost, $10 billion recovered" figure with no scrutiny at all. That is the kind of number that typically conflates "fraud" with "improper payments" (eligibility-paperwork errors, not theft), and the asymmetry — adversarial toward studies that cut against the house view, credulous toward numbers that support it — is exactly the motivated-reasoning pattern the section claims to police.

The framing's pacifying work is the phrase "integrity is prior to parameters." True in the abstract, it is deployed here to present as neutral-administrative a set of measures that are also substantive: demanding enrollee records "to screen out…immigrants here unlawfully," freezing Medicaid funds for states judged "noncompliant," withholding unemployment administrative funds — a federalism power-play and an immigration-enforcement vector, not just bookkeeping. Crucially, front-end eligibility screening predictably produces false negatives (eligible people denied), not only fraud prevention; the administrative-burden literature is the steelman of the Left objection the piece caricatures as mere "cruelty" talk. By treating all resistance as fraud-tolerance, the essay dismisses the strongest counterargument — that aggressive front-end verification narrows access for the very dependent citizens it claims to defend — rather than answering it. The result is a sound principle (run programs honestly) doing double duty as cover for an access-contracting agenda whose costs go unmentioned.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

New Business Formation is Surging–Again.

Alex Tabarrok · 2026-06-25 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Original commentary, citing Registered Agents Inc's Business Formation Report and Stripe Economics data.

The chain: new business formation jumped in 2020, leveled off after the pandemic but stayed historically high, and has surged again over the past ~18 months. Registered Agents Inc reports that every month in 2026 set a formation record, with 2.9 million new businesses through May (the strongest five-month start on record). Stripe Economics concurs with its "age of the solopreneur" finding — recent cohorts hit serious transaction volume faster, and the share of businesses reaching $1M cumulative revenue within a year was ~30% higher for the 2025 cohort than 2023 and ~3x higher than 2019. France too has record business creation, driven by micro-entrepreneurs. Tabarrok's explanation is the "devolution of power": one person armed with Stripe, Shopify, cloud software, automated bookkeeping, and now AI can do what once required a small staff, suggesting an emerging "experimental economy." Note: the headline figures measure registrations and platform-cohort revenue, not surviving employer firms, so the link to classic firm-dynamism metrics is suggestive rather than established.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Claude's Response:

The cross-source convergence is the post's real strength: registrations (Registered Agents), revenue-scaling speed (Stripe), and an international case (France) point the same direction, and the Stripe $1M-revenue-cohort metric matters most because it tracks actual output, not mere filings. The "devolution of power" mechanism is plausible and concrete — the unbundling of what once needed a staff into cheap software primitives, now with AI.

The gap is the slippage between "business formation" and "dynamism." The economic-dynamism literature (Decker–Haltiwanger; the Census Business Formation Statistics distinction between total and "high-propensity" applications likely to become employers) measures young-firm employment share and job reallocation, and the secular decline Tabarrok invokes is about employer firms and labor reallocation, not registration counts. Much of the post-2020 surge is nonemployer/solo LLC filings, some driven by tax, liability, or gig-classification motives rather than genuine new ventures, and Registered Agents Inc captures filings that can include shells and holding entities. A registration boom is not the same as reversing the employer-firm dynamism decline, and Tabarrok elides that distinction even though his Stripe evidence (about revenue, not registrations) is the part that would actually support it. He hedges appropriately at the close ("some of which will grow very large, very fast"), so this is optimism within his priors rather than overreach — but downstream readers should treat "dynamism is surging" as "formation and early revenue-scaling are surging," with employer-firm dynamism still unproven.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

The Lessons of Alan Greenspan's Life

Ryan Young · 2026-06-24 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Alan Greenspan's death this week at age 100.

Young, a Competitive Enterprise Institute economist writing in a libertarian register, frames Greenspan as both an "ideas person" (Ayn Rand's "Collective," Objectivism) and a "political person" (Ford Council of Economic Advisers chair, the 1982 Social Security commission, an 18-year Fed chairmanship). His verdict is mixed-positive: the 1983 Social Security fix was a tepid patch that left the unsustainable pay-as-you-go structure intact, and as Fed chair Greenspan's response to 1987's Black Monday — the liquidity injection that became the "Greenspan Put," a proto-quantitative-easing move — set a precedent that later normalized bailouts and QE. Young's central thesis is methodological: Greenspan's great success was continuing Paul Volcker's anti-inflation discipline and adding predictability via a de facto Taylor rule (high rates in booms, low in recessions, so the public can plan), and his great failure was never formalizing that rule and then abandoning it late in his tenure — betting on productivity gains to justify permanently lower rates, which Young blames for the 2007–08 crisis and the end of the Great Moderation. The framing lesson: Greenspan didn't deserve his "Maestro"/godlike reputation, but he turned sound ideas into concrete policy about as well as humanly possible without becoming a partisan hack.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Claude's Response:

A competent and ideologically legible obituary-reflection; the prompt rightly flags proportionate treatment, since its value to the roundup is modest. The through-line is a CEI house view — Fed discretion is the "biggest flaw," rules are the cure — and Greenspan is graded almost entirely on the rules-versus-discretion axis. Read against that prior, the piece is predictable (it reaches the conclusion the institution would reach) but honest rather than hagiographic about Greenspan's mixed record, which is to its credit.

The main analytic weakness is monocausality: pinning the 2007–08 crisis primarily on Greenspan's late-tenure low rates and his departure from the Taylor rule compresses a heavily contested macro-history — housing-finance structure, the global savings glut, regulatory and ratings-agency failures, securitization — into a single discretion-versus-rules story. That is the framework doing too much work; the Taylor rule is treated as a near-sufficient prophylactic ("the Fed's panicked overreactions… would never have happened") where it is at best one disputed input. The genuinely sharp touches are the "Greenspan Put" as the precedent that routinized bailouts, and the closing distinction the title actually promises — sound ideas into policy "without becoming a partisan hack" — which is more interesting than the macro post-mortem. No fundamental flaw; the piece is modest and self-aware about its subject's limits.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Tenuous Ties

Sam Pressler · 2026-06-24 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Original commentary — a write-up of Nobody to Call, Pressler's research project of interviews with 30 men without college degrees about friendship, community, and purpose.

Pressler reports that most of the 30 men interviewed were almost completely disconnected — no close friends, no community, no mentors — but argues the real story is subtler than a connected/disconnected binary: their lives ran on "tenuous ties," single relationships or groups that became single points of failure, so that ordinary life changes (a move, a job change, a death) tipped them from barely connected into outright isolation. He traces the mechanism through a post-high-school "friendship cliff" (the loss of school structure with no college or military to replace it) or a slower "drift," both accelerated as peers form families ("life-ing"). Work, often the only remaining structure, supplies "work friends" but rarely lasting friendship; a job change — or being fired, which "punishes twice" by also triggering depressive withdrawal — typically ends those ties. Pressler relocates blame from the men (who faulted their own lack of effort) onto a society that made a college degree the "golden ticket," eroded the material foundations of working-class life, and prized consumerized choice over commitment (citing Sandel's Tyranny of Merit, Case and Deaton's Deaths of Despair, and Pete Davis's Dedicated). His remedies are structural: alternative adult-transition structures (state service years, YouthBuild), re-embedded rites of passage (Journeymen Triangle, the College of St. Joseph the Worker), fair workweeks and living wages, and "activating" workplaces like Walmart to partner with the YMCA, men's groups, and houses of worship so that men who change jobs land in another web of association.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Claude's Response:

This is the social-capital / working-class-plight beat, and its central contribution is conceptual rather than evidentiary: "tenuous ties as single points of failure" is a genuine refinement on both the flat "men are lonely" discourse and the binary connected/disconnected framing. It names a mechanism — the fragility and concentration of ties, not their mere absence — that explains why men in similar circumstances diverge sharply after a single contingent event. The qualitative texture (named men, direct quotes) is the right evidentiary mode for establishing that the phenomenon exists and has a shape, and the cliff-versus-drift distinction and the "structure without friendship" observation about work are well drawn.

The limits are in the inference, not the description. The sample is 30 men, purposively recruited, with no comparison group or baseline, so the interviews establish the texture of the phenomenon, not its prevalence or its causes. The essay's load-bearing move is the pivot from description to blame allocation — "This is on us... we designed a society" — which it makes by citing three books rather than demonstrating the causal chain from the interview data. Notably, Pressler overrides the men's own explanation (they blamed their lack of effort) with a structural one; that is a legitimate sociological move, but the piece asserts the men are wrong about their own lives without adjudicating the question, when some of the described dynamics (not making the effort, depression-driven self-seclusion) are at least partly agentic, and the structural and individual accounts are not mutually exclusive.

The strongest objection lands on the prescription. The policy menu is a grab-bag whose link to the specific diagnosed mechanism is loose: fair workweeks and living wages address the precarity the piece persuasively ties to fragile ties, but service years and rites-of-passage programs rebuild adult-transition structure, and the workplace-activation idea sits in visible tension with the essay's own finding that work-based ties evaporate on a job change. Pressler half-acknowledges this (the "another web of association to catch them" hedge), yet the deeper implication of his own argument is that structures layered on top of precarity will themselves be tenuous unless the precarity is addressed first. The piece is humane and analytically sharper than most loneliness commentary; its softer half is the move from a well-supported description to a confidently assigned cause and a somewhat scattershot remedy. No fundamental flaw — the core observation holds; the causal superstructure is the contestable part.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Minnesota “Antifa” Terrorists Charged

Ken Klippenstein · 2026-06-23 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: the federal indictment, unsealed the prior week, of 15 Minnesota anti-ICE protesters as alleged "Antifa" members and thus domestic terrorists, announced by US Attorney Daniel Rosen.

Klippenstein frames the case as "pre-crime" — arrests made before or without regard to any completed crime — and as one of the first to explicitly cite National Security Presidential Memorandum 7 (NSPM-7, signed September 2025, three days after Trump designated Antifa a terrorist organization) as directing the arrests through Joint Task Force Vanguard. He notes Rosen could not define "Antifa" at the press conference and conceded that bodily harm "is not the measure" of the crime, while the operative "indicators" (anti-Americanism, anti-capitalism, anti-Christianity, "extremism on migration") are broad enough to sweep in millions. He then stacks a record of collapse: the north-Minneapolis "snow-shovel" case that turned out to be two Venezuelan DoorDash drivers (charges dismissed with prejudice; two ICE agents placed on leave for apparently lying under oath); roughly 18 Minnesotans whose assault-on-officer cases were dropped, one ICE agent's affidavit recurring in about ten; Chicago's "Broadview Six," tossed for prosecutorial misconduct, with 16 of 22 area impeding-an-officer cases dismissed; and five straight acquittals in Los Angeles. His conclusion: "national security" has been redefined to mean almost anything the government wants, displacing gas, rent, and healthcare as a society's priorities.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Claude's Response:

This is Klippenstein on his core beat — document- and record-driven adversarial national-security reporting — and the reportorial spine is solid. NSPM-7 and Task Force Vanguard exist; Rosen's press-conference quotes are damning on their own terms (a prosecutor who cannot define the group he is charging and who says bodily harm "is not the measure"); and the litany of collapsed cases is backed by denominators (16 of 22 in Chicago, five of five acquittals in LA, ~18 in Minnesota) rather than pure anecdote, which insulates it from the usual cherry-picking charge.

The piece's central engine is selection-as-argument: Klippenstein assembles a track record of failed prosecutions to pre-judge the brand-new 15-defendant indictment, whose specific merits are unadjudicated and largely undisclosed. The move is mostly fair — the pattern of dismissals is real and the resulting skepticism is earned — but it substitutes the base rate of past failures for engagement with what these 15 are actually accused of (conspiracy to impede or injure federal officers; Rosen's claim they were charged "for what they did," which Klippenstein deftly undercuts by noting the most vivid evidence shown was a video of defendant Kyle Wagner's violent rhetoric). A careful reader should hold open that a subset of an indictment can have merit even when the surrounding enforcement push is abusive and most adjacent cases collapsed; the frame makes that distinction hard to see. The scare-quoted "Antifa" throughout does real argumentative work — asserting the category is constructed and empty — and Klippenstein earns most of it (Rosen's non-definition, the "I'm Antifa!" moms and grandpas, the Portland "leader of Antifa" who was a random guy). The strongest opposing view he doesn't fully dispatch: a conspiracy charge does not require completed harm, so "no one was hurt" and "pre-crime" are not by themselves disqualifying — the live question is whether an actual agreement to commit a crime existed, which neither Rosen's weak presser nor Klippenstein's record-of-failures resolves. Net: well-sourced, rightly alarmed about NSPM-7's elastic "indicators" and the civil-liberties stakes, with one analytic soft spot — treating the prior collapse rate as near-dispositive of a case not yet tested. Not Straussian; the thesis is stated outright.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

How Liberals Misread MAGA

Gregory Conti · 2026-06-22 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Two books — Laura K. Field's Furious Minds: The Making of the MAGA New Right (Princeton, Nov. 2025) and Harvey Mansfield's Where Harvard Went Wrong (Encounter, 2026).

Conti credits Field's intellectual history of the "MAGA new right" for documenting overlooked strands (right-wing responses to the January 6 riot, the personalistic "Independent Charismatic" Protestant churches, and the failed "realignment," which he glosses as "privatize and deport" — "neoliberalism in one country" rather than a post-neoliberal, culturally conservative turn). But he argues the book fails as a guide to the Trump era on three grounds. First, tone: written in a "fit of pique," opening with a Nazi-likening epigraph, substituting Bentham's "passion-kindling appellatives" and guilt-by-association for primary-source exegesis (a missed strength, since Field is a political theorist by training). Second, it blames the right alone for polarization while ignoring left intolerance, and is itself Schmittian — calling Christopher Rufo "fascist buffoonery" is precisely the friend/enemy move Field condemns in conservatives. Third and most important, its tripartite typology (Claremonters / postliberals / National Conservatives) is too porous to bear weight and omits the right's live fault lines: religious vs. secular "Barstool" conservatives, the fraying "Judeo-Christian" consensus, and above all the tech right — the word "tariff" appears once and "tech right" never, despite AI accelerationism (Thiel, Yarvin, David Sacks, Marc Andreessen, AI Action Plan architect Dean Ball) being, in Conti's view, the most consequential MAGA cleavage. He then turns to Mansfield approvingly: three of the recently retired Harvard theorist's lifelong academic critiques — grade inflation, viewpoint diversity, and affirmative action — have "flipped from hopeless laments to conventional wisdom," the last via the Students for Fair Admissions ruling and the Office of Legal Counsel's disparate-impact opinion. Conti praises Mansfield's 1984 argument that affirmative action was doomed by its "underhandedness": advocated as indispensable in the abstract yet denied in every specific instance, it could never take public credit for its own operations and so could never be defended like an ordinary policy.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Claude's Response:

The essay's strongest stretch is the omission critique. "The word 'tariff' appears once and 'tech right' never" in a 400-page intellectual history of MAGA is a sharp, falsifiable-in-principle indictment, and the broader point — that a book organized around condemnation rather than comprehension skips the substantive intra-right arguments over foreign policy, trade, and AI — lands. The "privatize and deport" / "neoliberalism in one country" coinage is both a genuine concession to Field (the post-neoliberal realignment didn't happen) and a useful compression. Identifying religious-vs-secular and tech-right-vs-traditionalist (Musk's transhumanism vs. the religious right; DeSantis/Bannon/Hawley vs. White House accelerationism) as the live cleavages is plausible and forward-looking.

But the essay does some of what it accuses Field of. Conti faults her "passion-kindling appellatives" and guilt-by-association, yet leans on his own rhetorical jabs (the conservative-grievance memoirs that "would fill the library of Alexandria"; the Biden "chains" comparison) and on a one-line empirical claim — "social-science research attests that the left has become far less interpersonally tolerant than the right" — presented as settled and uncited while doing real load-bearing work. His defense of Rufo (nothing fascist about "a journalist who advocates for a colorblind ideal" or a duly appointed trustee) flattens a contested figure into maximally anodyne terms, the mirror image of the flattening he diagnoses in Field. And the "Islam is not a race, nor is Judaism" rebuttal to Field's Bannon passage scores a logic-chopping point against her loose syllogism while dodging her actual implication — that "economic nationalist" functioned as a euphemism.

The strongest counter Conti has to clear is that an intellectual history of a milieu — what these thinkers believe — is not obligated to be a policy or governance history of what is actually driving Trump-era rule. The tariff complaint partly conflates the two: a book about the MAGA mind can underweight the mechanics of Liberation Day without failing on its own terms. That objection lands for trade but not for tech, because the tech right (Thiel, Yarvin, the accelerationist abundance vision) is itself an intellectual movement, so its near-total absence is a fair hit on the book's own chosen ground.

The Mansfield half is appreciation more than review, and the connective tissue is thematic: Field is dismissive of Mansfield, who represents the substantive right she ignores, and on universities even she concedes the right "had a point." The affirmative-action "underhandedness" argument — a policy that could never boast of its own operation, unlike Social Security — is the essay's most elegant borrowed insight. But Conti presents Mansfield's "triumph" as vindication-by-events without registering that SFFA and an OLC memo are judicial and political victories, not intellectual ones, and that "the government is more committed than ever to race playing no role" is itself a contestable characterization rather than a neutral fact. The piece is a legitimate, substantive review essay; its weaknesses are rhetorical asymmetry and a few unargued empirical asides, not a collapse of the central case.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Beyond SAF and RSF: Why Sudan’s War Cannot Be Understood Through Two Actors Alone

Eglal Hamid · 2026-06-26 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: the diplomatic consensus treating Sudan's war as a two-party struggle between the Sudanese Armed Forces (SAF) and the paramilitary Rapid Support Forces (RSF), to be resolved by a ceasefire or power-sharing deal between them.

Hamid's claim is that when state institutions collapsed, governance did not disappear — it relocated to a "shifting spectrum" of sub-state and non-state actors, and a settlement blind to them will be disconnected from how Sudan actually functions. She walks four spheres of evidence, all sourced to regional research institutions. Services: Emergency Response Rooms (ERRs), civilian mutual-aid networks, organized kitchens, medical referrals, and water provision across Khartoum, Gezira, and Darfur. Dispute management: traditional authorities and customary judiya mediation kept markets open and aid convoys moving in Darfur and Kordofan. Economy: the war redrew trade geography toward Chad, Libya, and South Sudan, with checkpoints and transit fees now performing state functions, controlled by locally autonomous actors only nominally aligned with SAF or RSF. Most developed: the SPLM-N (Sudan People's Liberation Movement-North) under Abdelaziz al-Hilu runs courts and administration in South Kordofan and Blue Nile. The synthesis is a "widening gap between the state as a formal institution and governance as a set of practical functions" — authority now rests on the capacity to deliver services and regulate access, not on formal legal status.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Claude's Response:

This is a strong entry in the mechanism-first, materialist vein, and its value to the roundup is precisely the alternative epistemic supply chain it runs on: the entire argument is built from regional and specialist research — the Sudan Transparency and Policy Tracker, Small Arms Survey, Rift Valley Institute, Clingendael, the Conflict Sensitivity Facility, ACAPS, ACLED — that Anglophone op-ed coverage of Sudan never cites. The core reframe is a genuine contribution: shifting the question from "who controls the capital" to "who holds practical capacity" reorganizes the entire problem and exposes a real blind spot in a peace process still working from an early-war, two-actor map. The piece is also admirably disciplined against romanticism — it states plainly that these arrangements "should not be romanticised" and concedes that commanders' alignments are often opportunistic.

The structural weakness is the gap between diagnosis and prescription. Hamid is convincing that settlements ignore these governance structures but does not engage the hard problem her own evidence raises: how to incorporate dozens of fragmented, sometimes-armed local authorities into a national order without either legitimizing warlordism or institutionalizing the very fragmentation she describes. The "who governs in practice" frame is double-edged — some of what she counts as governance is checkpoint-and-transit-fee extraction (rent-seeking dressed as administration), and many of these structures exist only because of the war and may be incompatible with any recentralized state. Treating their persistence as a normative claim on inclusion glosses that tension. The strongest opposing view — that a war ultimately is settled by the actors who command the most force, and that local governance follows whoever wins the military contest — is not directly answered; the piece asserts the dispersal of authority but doesn't show it can survive a decisive military outcome. Still, the empirical observation is sound and the corrective to the two-actor frame is genuinely useful. Not flawed.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

How much deforestation is your country importing through trade?

Hannah Ritchie · 2026-06-24 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: a new dataset and Nature Food paper by Chandrakant Singh, Martin Persson and colleagues (2026) mapping commodity-driven deforestation embodied in trade, which Ritchie visualizes as an interactive Sankey diagram.

Ritchie — the Our World in Data researcher whose anti-doomer brief is to check alarmist intuitions against the numbers — pulls four findings from the data. First and headline: about 75% of global deforestation is driven by demand for products consumed domestically, not internationally traded, contrary to the common impression that imports dominate. Second, this is especially true of beef, the single largest driver, where Brazil's forest clearing mostly serves Brazilian demand. Third, rich countries do contribute meaningfully through imports — the UK's largest categories are now oilseeds (soy and palm), cocoa, and coffee — so the finding is not a clean exoneration of wealthy consumers. Fourth, the UK's imported deforestation has fallen by roughly two-thirds since the early 2000s, with similar declines in France and Germany but notably not in the United States. Ritchie is careful to flag the dataset's key limit herself: it measures "deforestation risk" — hectares embodied via national averages — not whether any specific item came from cleared land.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Claude's Response:

This is a chart-driven explainer built around someone else's dataset rather than original analysis, and it should be read as well-targeted myth-busting rather than a strong causal argument. The pattern is classic Ritchie: take a widely-held intuition ("your latte and burger are burning the Amazon") and show the data complicates it — most deforestation is domestic, so the trade-footprint story, while real, is a minority share. The move is sound, and her self-imposed caveat is the right one: "deforestation risk" from national averages is an exposure-accounting metric, not supply-chain traceability, so the numbers say how many embodied hectares sit in a country's trade, not whether a given product is implicated.

What keeps the piece honest is that she resists the over-correction too. She explicitly notes rich countries still contribute via imports, and the detail that US imported deforestation has not fallen (unlike the UK, France, Germany) is a needle pointed back at complacency rather than away from it. The one analytical subtlety the format leaves underdeveloped is the gap between consumption-accounting and marginal effect: "75% domestic" describes where demand sits today, but it does not establish that rich-world import demand is causally inert — a marginal change in import demand for soy or palm can still shift production at the deforestation frontier even when domestic demand is the larger bloc. The "three-quarters domestic" headline could be misread as "trade doesn't matter," and she guards against that only partly.

For the roundup's climate/energy theme, this functions as the quantitative, temperature-lowering counterpoint to the European heatwave commentary running in the same window — same domain, opposite register: where the Euro|topics digest treats the climate as moral emergency, Ritchie insists on getting the attribution arithmetic right before assigning blame. No fundamental flaw; the central claim is well-supported and she states its limits plainly.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Should People Avoid Whole-Body Screening Info?

Scott Alexander · 2026-06-23 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: expert pushback (covered in his prior post on Midjourney's full-body ultrasound scanner) recommending against whole-body screening, including existing MRI screening.

Alexander constructs an explicit cost-benefit model: per 1,000 healthy people screened by whole-body MRI, roughly 8 benefit (about 32 quality-adjusted life-years, or QALYs, gained) against costs of about $2.7M, 6,200 hours, and 5 QALYs lost to anxiety and biopsy harms — netting roughly $108,000 per QALY, right at the conventional ~$100,000 cost-effectiveness bar. He stress-tests it: a follow-up-refusal strategy wouldn't help (follow-ups are already cost-effective), and for a money-indifferent rich person the time-only trade looks favorable — until he notes the same logic would endorse spending years of daily doctor visits, which exposes how QALY math mismodels people's inconsistent valuation of time. He then itemizes ten ways the model could be wrong — order-of-magnitude inputs (sourced partly from AI estimates of weak studies), the cancer-only assumption, bad real-world doctors, irrational patient reactions to over-diagnosis, the worse economics of yearly rather than one-time screening, and unknown unknowns — concluding the true value could land anywhere from "fantastically useful" to "horribly counterproductive," and that historically such analyses come out worse than projected. Because an 8/1,000 effect is too small for available studies to detect, he endorses doctors' conservative default while preserving a narrow, non-evidence-backed case that a genuinely rich, rational, anxiety-immune person might be mildly net-positive. On Midjourney's ultrasound version he argues lower benefit (roughly half the cancers, possibly net-negative if it substitutes for MRI rather than adds), with cost the only plausibly decisive advantage, and rebuts "more data will fix the false positives" via an analogy that some harmless and dangerous findings are simply indistinguishable on the parameters ultrasound can measure.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Claude's Response:

The piece's value is not the $108,000 figure — which Alexander repeatedly disowns as built from order-of-magnitude inputs and AI-summarized weak studies — but the meta-argument it sets up. A tidy model landing right at the cost-effectiveness bar is precisely the situation where unmodeled factors and an unfavorable base rate (these clever analyses usually disappoint) should dominate, and where the absence of adequately-powered studies (you'd need hundreds of thousands of subjects to detect an 8/1,000 effect; we have only meta-analyses of low-quality studies on secondary endpoints like cancers-detected rather than lives-saved) means "net-positive in principle" can't license action. That reframing of the experts' recommendation — not "screening is bad" but "the case is on the edge, unverifiable, and the base rate is unfavorable, so default conservative" — is the genuinely useful contribution, and it is held to a high evidential standard.

Where to push: the rich-rational carve-out is nearly empty in practice. The essay performs even-handedness by granting that a money-indifferent, anxiety-immune, rationally-time-valuing person might benefit, then spends its closer dismantling membership in that set — the people who think they qualify outnumber those who do; the concierge doctor is paid per procedure; the longevity-influencer, biomarker, and MTHFR-mutation "bespoke medicine" pitches are grifts. The net practical upshot is that almost no real individual should read this as a green light, so the apparent balance resolves firmly toward the experts. That is defensible, but worth naming: the carve-out functions more as a rhetorical concession than a live recommendation.

This is characteristic Alexander — build the "spherical cow" model, then itemize why the cow isn't spherical — and the discipline is real. The "Mr. X / height to five decimal places" analogy is a clean rebuttal to AI-imaging maximalism: more measurement can't separate findings that are genuinely identical on size, shape, density, and location. The one place the rigor lapses into a joke is the life-hours digression, whose glib resolution ("you're an idiot, but go ahead") buries the more serious implication it surfaces — that whole-body screening competes against much higher-yield uses of the same time and money, so even a positive-in-isolation verdict may be the wrong call on opportunity-cost grounds. Net: honest, self-correcting, and it lands close to the medical consensus while explaining why the consensus holds rather than merely deferring to it.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Full Articles

Iran's aftermath: who lost, and the Vance turn

Iran has humiliated America’s military

Edward Luttwak · 2026-06-21 · Original →

Both those who criticize and those who loyally support Trump’s “deal” — it is hardly a sufficiently explicit agreement, indeed it remains vague on the key points — are carefully avoiding any mention of the critical factor that caused the abandonment of what could have been the last US-Iran war.

When the latest but probably not last Iran war started on 28 February 2026, the first victims, for once, were not innocent bystanders but rather Iran’s rulers and executioners, the supreme dictator Ali Khamenei; the Chief of the General Staff and head of Iran’s armed forces, General Abdolrahim Mousavi; the Minister of Defense, Aziz Nasirzadeh; the head of military research & development, Hossein Jabal Amelian; his predecessor Brigadier General Reza Mozaffari Nia, Esmail Khatib, the Minister of Intelligence; his deputy for Israel affairs, Yahya Hosseini Panjaki; some other officials, and also the private citizen Mojtaba Khamenei, son of Ali Khamenei but utterly unqualified for the emphatically non-hereditary supreme leader position. Mojtaba Khamenei has not been seen nor heard since the bombing, and is certainly mutilated if still alive.

Iran’s furious response to this mass decapitation was the launch of ballistic missiles — not just against Israel and US bases in the region but also against mere bystanders. Of the 1,471 launched missiles from the start on 28 February until 20 April, some 650 were aimed at Israel, 563 landed in the territory of the United Arab Emirates that has long helped Iran’s wrecked economy, 265 landed in Kuwait, where some US aircraft are based but which has always aided Iran, 215 attacked Qatar, which shares its vast natural gas field with Iran, a country that Qatar has never harmed, 194 struck Bahrain, the main regional base of the US Navy, and 135 were aimed at Saudi Arabia, in spite of its complete neutrality.

Iran’s very primitive missiles have been much less lethal than the German V2s of 1944 — some 3,225 of which killed more than 7,000 people in England and Belgium — but with their huge mass of metal they inflicted much property damage even if their warheads did not explode. The older and more abundant Kheibar Shekan weighs 17 metric tons with its 750kg warhead, the newer and larger Khorramshahr weighs close to 20 tons and delivers multiple warheads weighing up to 1,500kg.

What Iran achieved with its 650 missiles aimed at Israel was to damage many buildings and kill 27 civilians and one off-duty IDF soldier, though some 3,000 more were injured across the country, some of them seriously.

While Iran attacked civilians with its very inaccurate missiles, both the US and Israel relied on extreme precision to destroy underground missile assembly plants, detected missile launchers, rocket fuel depots and mixing plants. This entire subterranean industry was the result of many years of very high spending, largely funded by the diversion of the funds urgently needed to provide water supplies for Iran’s second city Mashad, historic Yazd and other cities but for Tehran above all, which may even have to be evacuated, according to Iran’s only elected leader, president Masoud Pezeshkian.

[

suggested reading##### Spycraft will decide Iran's fate

By Reuel Marc Gerecht](https://unherd.com/2026/06/spycraft-will-seal-irans-fate/?edition=us&ref=refinnar)

The dam-building and water supply budgets were not enough, so the nation-wide electrical generation funds were also diverted to the Revolutionary Guards’ missile industries, and also the funding needed to pipe the country’s unlimited natural gas supplies to every city. As a result, last winter and into March when the war started, horribly polluting mazout, residual oil, had to be burned to keep Tehran’s people from freezing, because neither electrical nor gas heating was sufficient in the winter months, even with global warming.

The regime was thus on the edge of systemic failure on the eve of the war, but has now been saved by the way the war was fought — and not fought. The mass decapitation of 28 February allowed surviving Revolutionary Guards officers to take over in Tehran, very much as the SS did in Berlin after Hitler’s death on 30 April 1945, eagerly killing anyone who wanted to surrender until Russian troops arrived two days later.

“No such invasion plan had even been suggested by anyone, so there was no justification for the pre-emptive ‘boots on the ground’ hysteria.”

But this time no troops were approaching Tehran, truly mission impossible because of geography alone if nothing else. Nobody in his right mind could contemplate an advance over 800 miles of desert and mountains on the only road from the Bandar Abbas on the Gulf coast to Tehran, with its 13 million people. If only a tenth still support the regime — that many are salaried in the army, police, part-time militia, Revolutionary Guards and the Bonyad foundations that own much of the economy — it would be enough to start a long season of urban warfare.

No such invasion plan had even been suggested by anyone, so there was no justification for the pre-emptive “boots on the ground” hysteria loudly manifest from isolationist backwaters and White House Vice Presidential staffers alike. As it turned out, however, the hysteria generated by wholly imaginary invasion plans had very real consequences, which in fact determined the outcome of the war till this day.

To understand how fears over a non-event brought about a veritable defeat, one must return to the other military action that occurred just before the start of the bombing, on 28 February. This already seems a long time ago, because everybody in the White House would have laughed at the suggestion that President Trump would ever come to terms with the Revolutionary Guards. Some 3,000 paratroopers of the rapid-response force of the 82 airborne division were sent to the Gulf, to join the 5,000 Marines of the 11th and 31st Marine expeditionary units already there.

They were more than enough to counter the entirely predictable — and predicted — Revolutionary Guard response to the bombing, which was to try to stop the flow of tankers and bulk carriers delivering crude oil, liquefied natural gas and fertilizer down the narrow seaways in the mostly shallow Persian Gulf from Iraq, Kuwait, Qatar and — partly — from Saudi Arabia, which can send half its oil by pipeline to the Red Sea, and the United Arab Emirates, which has a pipeline to the Indian ocean for half its oil.

[

More from this author##### Can Britain's military be saved?

By Edward Luttwak](https://unherd.com/2026/06/how-britain-maimed-its-military/?edition=us&ref=refinnar)

The Revolutionary Guards and Iran’s navy could not attack the tankers and bulk carriers with their warships, armed motorboats or submarines, all of which were efficiently sunk soon after the bombing started. But they could endanger navigation by dropping sea mines from any traditional dhow or fishing vessel, and even by attacking passing tankers with their anti-tank missiles. That would be enough to cause insurance companies to suspend their coverage, thereby stopping Persian Gulf supplies to the world economy.

That is where the airborne troops and Marines could be highly effective. With ample helicopter transport to take up positions for hours or days on uninhabited Persian Gulf islands and deserted coastal tracts to secure nearby navigable channels, and with strong tactical air support only minutes away to minimize combat risks, they were fully capable of defeating any interfering enemy forces.

By sending them into action, the US could have denied any oil shipments from Iran’s oil terminal at Kharg Island and from lesser terminals at Qeshm and Jask on the Indian Ocean beyond the Gulf, thus cutting off most of Iran’s oil export revenues. At the same time, those ground and air forces could minimize any interference with the tankers and fertilizer bulk carriers transiting from Iraq, Kuwait, the Emirates, Qatar and Saudi Arabia into the Indian Ocean, minimizing the war’s impact on global commodity markets.

Kharg Island and nearby Khargu Island have been left unmolested by American troops. (Satellite image from Maps4media via Getty Images)

Israel’s Prime Minister Netanyahu had asked only for assured resupplies of aerial munitions when it came to the pre-emptive bombing of Iran’s ballistic missiles, hoping to avoid the danger of an all-out attack that could overwhelm Israel’s defenses. He was surprised and pleased by Trump’s decision to join in and take command of the offensive. He did not pause to consider the possibility that he was forming an alliance with a weaker power, which would let him down.

By then, 28 February 2026, a total of 1,150 Israeli soldiers and local security officers had been killed since the war started on October 7, 2023, the equivalent of 39,100 dead US soldiers given the 1:34 population ratio.

Netanyahu, who was widely unpopular even before the war, was politically unaffected by the loss of more than 1,000 soldiers, but 1,150 killed in action — let alone 39,100 — would have quickly ended the Trump presidency via a fast impeachment with enough Republican votes, among vast coast-to coast mass protests. In other words, Netanyahu’s exclusive reliance on his personal dialogues with Trump, which left Israel’s Foreign Ministry and Intelligence a mere bystander, blinded him to the great change that had transformed the United States into a post-heroic country, no more willing to engage in combat than our NATO allies. Nor is it a political or even a cultural transformation that might be undone by strong leadership, but rather the unavoidable consequence of the collapse in female fertility, now down to 1.6. It means that very few American families have two or more boys, such that one might fall in combat without the family’s extinction. Almost every US combatant is now a “Private Ryan”.

That is why a prudent opposition to more wars in pursuit of quixotic aims — such as the 2003 Iraq war that I testified against before the Senate Foreign Relations Committee, seeing no chance of bringing democracy to Baghdad, and the Afghan war to modernize unwilling populations — has been transformed into a “boots on the ground” psychosis. Also present within the White House, it equates small-scale and short-lived commando actions with another endless war, nullifying the tremendous strength of US air power, allowing the Revolutionary Guards to win with very few fighters and very few small boats.

So this war fought by an irresolute President has not only allowed America’s worst enemies to come out the winners in spite of their utterly disastrous national policies, primitive missile engineering and clumsy military operations, but has also degraded US military strength down to European levels, because troops that cannot be risked in combat even for great gains are of little military value.

view comments
