So we get people adopting a very zero-sum view of immigrants or billionaires or believing that if we eliminate USAID or stop giving money to Israel that somehow all these tradeoffs will vanish.

By the same token, excessive negativism about the people currently holding political office lends itself to excessive optimism about the scope for change if we elect someone new. On the prices, for example, it’s not just that actually lowering the price level is probably impossible. The reality is that the Biden administration spent a lot of time thinking about ideas to reduce inflation and interest rates. What they kept bumping up against is that the main way to accomplish this is deficit reduction. I think that all things considered, they should have gone for it. But the counter-argument that all the feasible roads to deficit reduction are somewhat politically unpalatable isn’t crazy. The problem is that voters look at an inflation situation they think is bad and assume the bad situation is happening because bad people are running the government. If you just got some good people in there, they would fix things. So then Trump comes in and he’s just dealing with the same basic situation and for broadly similar reasons he doesn’t fix things.

Now there’s a lot of people on the left who feel that if they just overthrow “the Democratic establishment” and put one of their own into the White House, it will both slay MAGA and also solve all the fundamental problems of American life. That’s exactly how a lot of people felt 20 years ago, and it powered Barack Obama’s presidential campaign and took him all the way to the White House. I think Obama was a good president —maybe even a great one — but the people who projected the most unrealistic hopes onto him ended up sharply disappointed and today insist that he “betrayed” them on behalf of the corrupt establishment he was supposed to overthrow. What mostly happened is that he had to wrestle with reality.

My sense is that a lot of what plays to ideologues these days as hard-left politics is actually just a further manifestation of stealth democracy. You could imagine a world in which the rising clout of the left meant that more and more people were calling for a 25 percent VAT or for higher income taxes on the whole top half of the income distribution. There are real merits to this way of doing things, but it involves a fairly explicit tradeoff — in the Nordic countries, lots of banal consumer goods are less affordable than in the United States, but social goods like health care and child care and education are cheaper. But that kind of ethic of solidarity is absolutely not what the insurgent left is promoting. What they’re saying is that if we get rid of the corrupt politicians who are in the pockets of billionaires, we can just all get this stuff for free and not give anything up. This is just not really true, and it’s quite different from the practice of actually existing social democracies. But it’s appealing for the same reason that MAGA Twitter is always insisting to me that we can solve all housing problems and also balance the budget by deporting illegal immigrants.

Robert Bonasia: Is there a clear path for an urban Democrat to contend in swing states or nationally or are the primary electorates that choose them too left wing?

In Philadelphia the mayor is regularly labeled a “centrist,” but this mostly means she thinks heroin users should go to rehab instead of being allowed to shoot up on the street. She also signed sanctuary city legislation and heavy handed tenant protections into law without pushback. Keisha Lance Bottoms has a shot in Georgia, but that seems downstream of national political trends. In the past Ed Rendell went from being mayor of Philadelphia to governor of Pennsylvania, despite running in a strong midterm for Republicans. Is the only path now to be a Mamdani type, run for President and pray you get a nominated in a blue wave?

This is obviously a tough one. To get elected mayor of Philadelphia, you need to appeal to the median Democratic Party primary voter in the city of Philadelphia and you need to do so without relying on electability arguments. To get elected governor of Pennsylvania, you need to appeal to true swing voters. There’s just a big gap there. As you say, these kinds of cities routinely do elect moderate mayors relative to the local factional politics, but someone like that is probably still extremely progressive.

But I don’t think this is an ironclad law of politics. Mike Duggan served 12 years as a popular and successful mayor of Detroit, and I think he could have been a successful statewide politician. He decided he wanted to run for governor and also decided that he was actually too moderate to win a Democratic primary, so he was going to try to run as an independent. That didn’t work and he’s dropped out. But I think that was clearly poor decision-making on his part.

The key thing about Duggan, in contrast to Cherelle Parker in Philadelphia or my outgoing mayor in D.C., is that his political profile was as a successful reformer rather than as a “custodian of the establishment who fended off the leftists.”

Presumably at some point, the left faction will win a mayor’s race in Philadelphia. And then after some number of years in power they will stumble and lose control to a moderate. That person will then come in and do some moderate reformist stuff in a way that generates a very different statewide and national profile from the establishment custodian profile. Dan Lurie in San Francisco, for example, is clearly the kind of person who could do well in a swing state. His problem is that California isn’t a swing state, so it’s not clear that having a profile that’s mostly about being a moderate reformer is good for him. But Lurie is a guy who has plenty of progressive positions on the issues — it’s San Francisco — the difference is that he leads a newish moderate political regime that took over and enacted a bunch of noteworthy moderate changes.

Andrew: Why is it hard to get focused on results in politics? Like this is a big thread in abundance and once you start looking for it it’s everywhere.

We have checks we send out but actually doing the things isn’t nearly as important.

A fundamental reality of American politics is that the vast majority of elected officials are backbench legislators in safe seats who are looking for promotion to a better job.

Conditional on being a backbench legislator in a safe seat, there is really only one way to deliver “results” and it’s to do what Elizabeth Warren and Tim Scott did with the 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act: Find a partner across the aisle, roll up your sleeves, and do some stuff. You can be a moderate who crafts bipartisan deals or you can be a progressive icon who crafts bipartisan deals, but bipartisan deals are how things get done. It’s not that all legislation is bipartisan, but a lot of it is. And the partisan bills that pass are very leadership-driven processes with a lot of input from the White House. As a normal member of Congress, bipartisan dealmaking is the way to get things done.

I’d love to live in a world where Chris Murphy’s work on the bipartisan asylum reform bill that Trump killed in 2024 was a big boost to his presidential ambitions and his vague musings about the evils of neoliberalism were a hindrance. Realistically, though, it’s the opposite. The best thing Murphy has done to advance his career is hazy takes, and if he ever becomes a serious contender, his bipartisan legislative work is likely to become a vulnerability.

For Warren and the ROAD to Housing Act, there’s no real downside risk because the person who would attack a Democrat for doing this bill is Warren herself. Because she is literally Elizabeth Warren, nobody is going to complain that she sold out to banking interests and private equity. But there’s still no real tangible upside for her. She gets to know that she accomplished something on an important issue and I hope that’s reward enough for her.

But for the majority of politicians — especially House members and the thousands of largely anonymous state legislators and local legislative officeholders — the name of the game is to make all your party’s interest group stakeholders like you. That’s the way to build a case for yourself as you try to move up the ladder. And that’s antithetical to the spirit of getting things done. It’s true that the handful of members in swing seats have more incentive to get deals done. But the easiest way to be a moderate is just to say “no” to all of your party’s controversial ideas — to do less, not more.

So you’re left with a relatively tiny number of executive officeholders as the only real constituency for results. The governor wants to open the bridge. The mayor wants to tout falling crime. They need to care about results.

But the public is not always very good at causal attribution. One of the biggest differences between Zohran Mamdani’s successful run as a progressive mayor and Brandon Johnson’s failed run as a progressive mayor is that Johnson had to deal with the fallout of the Biden-era asylum situation and Mamdani didn’t. But it’s still the case that in terms of what you can actually control, an executive’s best bet is to try to deliver good results.

JohnFromNewHampshire: We’ve been powering aircraft carriers and submarines with nuclear reactors for decades. Seems like a huge environmental and economic win to let cargo fleets use the technology. Is there any hope for this?

Back during the Biden administration, there was a diplomatic agreement between the United States, the United Kingdom, and Australia under which the Australian Navy would buy some nuclear-powered submarines, so we got to witness an English-language debate about the value of this technology. What I learned is that separate from the national security issues around exporting military-grade nuclear reactors, there’s just a significant cost premium for nuclear propulsion over diesel. Countries like South Korea and Japan that almost certainly could either develop domestic reactor designs or else do an Australia-style deal to buy them have chosen to do neither of those things. The thinking is that given the budget constraint, it is better to have a larger number of diesel subs rather than a smaller number of nuclear ones.

The case for nuclear in a naval context is that nuclear propulsion has desirable performance characteristics —notably, it can run for years without needing to refuel — that are worth paying a premium for.

In the specific context of ballistic missile submarines used for strategic deterrence, this is extremely valuable and that’s why the U.S., the U.K., and France all have them. It is also why the People’s Republic of China built the Type 094 submarine and is developing its successor, the Type 096. So the ballistic missile subs are a clear case for nuclear propulsion. There’s then a decent case that if you want to build a fleet of attack subs to hunt and potentially destroy enemy ballistic missile subs, you need nuclear propulsion for that mission, too. But it’s debatable. The core use case really is nuclear deterrence. So even though the “carry nuclear warheads” and “use a nuclear reactor for power” aspects of a nuclear submarine fleet are conceptually separate, in practice they tend to go together. Australia is breaking that pattern, but it’s a borderline call.

The upshot is that there’s no reason to think civilian cargo ships would want to use naval reactor technology. The U.S. itself experimented with nuclear-powered cruisers and destroyers only to abandon the idea as not meeting a cost-benefit test.

For cargo, the case looks even worse. It would be cool, I suppose, to have a container ship that didn’t need to refuel. But the whole point of a container ship is to make regular stops at ports to load and unload. So there’s nothing really here that civilian shipbuilders would want.

It seems to me that the real relevance the nuclear-powered navy offers to civilian energy issues comes from another angle. These naval reactors are not subject to Nuclear Regulatory Commission oversight or any of its procedures for licensing reactor designs. And yet, the safety track record of the nuclear-powered navy is excellent. Which doesn’t mean we should just start rolling out naval reactors for civilian purposes — for starters, these reactors are designed to produce a lot of direct propulsion rather than electricity, so it wouldn’t even work — but it does go to show that a regulatory framework oriented around “getting to yes” on reactor designs can be perfectly safe and we don’t need the precautionary principle ethos that’s dominated the N.R.C. since its inception.

A New Jerusalem: I know we’ve been worried about the Road to Housing Act crippling the build to rent market. I sort of don’t understand why the build to rent market exists when the cost to buy versus rent is so high in most cities. Is this an artifact of some zoning distortion? That most of the current buy versus rent cost is from interest rates so developers aren’t pocketing the benefit?

Build-to-rent single-family housing exists because:

  1. Most residentially zoned land prohibits multi-family development.
  2. Since 2008, we’ve made it harder to get a mortgage.

(2) means lots of people need to rent and (1) means that even though we are building more apartments post-2008 than we were pre-2008, you can’t fully accommodate the demand through that channel. ROAD ultimately resolved in a happy place where build-to-rent is protected, and it also includes provisions that should help generate relief on both (1) and (2). I’m less bullish than Kevin Erdmann on the wisdom of going all the way back on (2) to where we were previously, but I do think everyone needs to recognize that there was an overcorrection there. Ultimately, though, the point is that people need to live somewhere.

If you want them to live in owner-occupied housing, you need to give them mortgages. If you want them to rent, then you need to legalize rental projects. Traditionally that has meant apartments, and nobody is more in favor of legalizing apartments than I am. But even in YIMBYtopia there’s lots of cheap land in America (in fact, in YIMBYtopia a lot of the land would get cheaper) and it makes more sense to develop that as detached homes. Why shouldn’t some of that inventory be rentals?

Chris: You’re mayor of New York. A developer comes to you with a plan: eminent domain a whole block of brownstones in, let’s say East Village, raze it, and replace it with a tower(s)-on-a-base development. You’d replace ~500 housing units with 3,000. You’d add retail space and enough underground parking to remove some nearby on-street parking. But you’d completely change the NYC aesthetic of the neighborhood. Do you do it?

No, of course not, and I think the specter of using eminent domain powers in this context reflects how far off the mark the basic politics of housing have become in the U.S. Nobody should force existing landowners to raze their properties or sell out to new developers. The incredibly radical change that we need to make to residential land use in the U.S. is to allow people to voluntarily sell their property to developers if they want to. On 90 percent of parcels in 90 percent of locations, the upshot of making that change will be — nothing.

Most people who own homes don’t particularly want to sell them.

And in most cases, the economics of demolishing a perfectly good home in order to build a different structure on the same location aren’t very appealing.

But it is also the case that there are some parcels of land that are currently so undersupplied with housing that a developer would be willing to make a really strong bid for a teardown prospect. And in any given year, some of the people who own parcels of land like that develop a strong motive to move to another location or to realize the financial gains of their housing investment. So you could go from a situation where there are no teardown re-developments in a given neighborhood to a situation where a few happen in any given year. Scale that across a lot of different neighborhoods in a lot of metro areas across a large country and you get a lot of additional housing. Scale it temporally for a long span of time, and you get a country where the built environment of neighborhoods changes faster but the sociological processes of displacement actually happen slower. You have more jobs, more tax revenue, faster economic growth, higher productivity, and so forth. But there’s no case for seizing people’s land in low-density areas and forcibly redeveloping it at higher density. There would be massive backlash to that and you’d eliminate the best arguments for YIMBYism.

Note that you picked out a specific case where I think almost anyone will agree that the forced re-development would be economically beneficial. But adopting that as a systematic approach would just recapitulate all the sins of urban planning as a genre — by ignoring market forces, you’d inevitably end up missing the mark on what the real “correct” scale of development is.

The critical thing that we need to do is to acknowledge that real estate markets feature a very complicated set of tradeoffs. Any individual non-billionaire is going to end up making some kind of compromise. Either you spend a larger share of your income on housing than is desirable, or you get a smaller place than is optimal, or you end up in a location that falls short of ideal in terms of commute logistics or some aspect of local amenities or whatever else. Markets let people work out not just “what would I like” but how much do I like X versus Y and how much I am willing to give up to get it. We should let neighborhoods evolve and change in response to shifts in demand, but not force them to change.

Thomas L. Hutcheson: What do, should Democrats DO if they win House and Senate?

What they should do is investigate Trump scandals while advancing a limited economic agenda of popular measures — a modest minimum-wage hike and a restoration of the health care funding that was cut under Trump — along with political reforms like bans on congressional stock trading and gerrymandering. But looking at how House primaries have gone, what they probably will do is immediately lapse into factional infighting about Israel.

What I learned as Chuck Schumer’s intern

Matthew Yglesias · 2026-06-23 · Original →

New York Senators Chuck Schumer and Hillary Clinton join New York Governor George Pataki at a press conference in 2002. (Photo by Brooks Kraft via Getty Images)

Twenty-five years ago, I was a summer intern in the office of New York Senator Chuck Schumer.

He was a sort of unusual case at the time. At the age of 50, he was relatively young for a senator and in his first term, having been elected to the seat in 1998. But he was first elected to the New York State Assembly when he was fresh out of law school at 23 years old and jumped to the U.S. House of Representatives when he was 29.

So he managed to be both a young senator and also a veteran politician.

It’s also hard to imagine given contemporary politics, but his 1998 victory over incumbent Republican Senator Al D’Amato was considered something of an underdog victory at the time. Politics was simply much less polarized. New York City had a moderate Republican mayor and the state had a moderate Republican governor, so there was no particular reason to think that a moderate Republican senator from Long Island couldn’t keep winning re-election indefinitely.

It was just a different time, politically, when basically every statewide office was considered at least potentially contestable if you could raise money, and the House battleground was much wider.

Part of the point here is just that I’m old.

My responsibilities as an intern included tasks like cutting articles out of print newspapers in order to fax a daily packet of clippings to the senator’s different offices around the state. I would also grab press releases hot off the printer and fax¹ them to various media outlets. Photocopiers and fax machines were also a big part of my job during my internship at Rolling Stone the previous summer.

Literally nobody would perform such tasks these days. But it occurs to me that this is a situation where age brings some wisdom.

Precisely because politics was so different back then, a much larger share of working politicians thought a lot about persuasion and about swing voters. Political staffing is a young person’s game, and for a while now the whole concept of trying to persuade swing voters and beat Republicans has been a niche pursuit that only a tiny minority of elected Democrats focus on. As a result, staffers don’t really receive even “summer internship brown bag lunch” levels of training in how it’s done.

Obviously a lot of what Schumer and my direct boss had to say about this is now as outdated as the list of speed dial² codes taped up to the wall by the fax machine. A lot of it, though, remains extremely relevant today.

The voters who matter don’t follow politics

Media consumption habits have changed enormously in the past 25 years. One constant, though, and something Schumer’s communications director really drummed into me, is this:

The media consumption habits of persuadable voters are very different from the habits of political junkies.

This is important to drill yourself on because the people who work in politics professionally are political junkies. Back in 2001, that meant staffers paying attention to publications like Roll Call and Congressional Quarterly and watching programs like CNN’s “Inside Politics” on weekday afternoons or the Sunday-morning interview shows.

Plenty of non-professionals watched those shows, too. But the point was that the kind of people sufficiently interested in politics to want to watch Tim Russert or Judy Woodruff interview politicians are very hard to persuade. People who care a lot about politics tend to have firm partisan convictions. Even if they’re cross-pressured or willing to be occasional crossover voters, the cross-pressures are probably deeply rooted.

As a politician, the people you really want to target with your casual messaging are people whose views about politics are pretty fuzzy.

The fuzziest people in the country back in 2001 were probably tuning in to local television news to get sports scores, weather forecasts, and service-y news. Nobody who works in politics professionally cares much what’s on WIVT in Binghamton. And the audience for WIVT in Binghamton probably doesn’t care that much about politics. But as a result, the highest-impact thing the senator’s staff could do was get him on WIVT in Binghamton saying or doing something popular.

One of our real messaging coups that summer was when we got Yankees manager Joe Torre and some local hospital executives to do an event with Schumer promoting blood donations, but also trying to help New York-area hospitals out with a serious policy problem that was playing out in D.C. at the time. The Joe Torre angle, probably more than the substance of it, attracted a lot of attention from local television stations. So we got the senator in front of an audience that doesn’t necessarily care about politics and had him talking about something that doesn’t play as partisan or “political” in a classic sense.

People like small wins

At that particular point in human history, Verizon —which at the time was the local telephone provider in the northeastern United States — was heavily promoting two special features. One was *66 (the “busy signal fix”), which you could dial if you were calling a friend and got a busy signal.³ The other was *69 (“not in time, press star six nine”), which was essentially a redial function. The idea that led to *69 was that someone might call you, but then hang up before you were able to make it to your phone and pick up. By hitting *69, you could call back whoever had called you.

The trick was, you’d get charged if you used those features. I believe the fees were disclosed in the fine print of the ads promoting the services, but they were not disclosed in real time when you used them.

Some friend of Schumer’s got hit with a whopper of a bill based on one of his kids going nuts with the *66, and he did something relatively insubstantial about it, like write a letter to the Federal Communications Commission asking them to promulgate regulations banning this. But it made for a big press conference. I helped stage one in New York City, and it got so much attention that the senator wound up taking it on the road and promoting the same initiative in several upstate cities.

Now, in retrospect, I would say that this was not really one of the most significant public policy issues in George W. Bush’s America.

I don’t even remember whether the F.C.C. did what we wanted. By the time this came around, the features in question were already somewhat obsolete since more and more people had call waiting and caller ID. Cell phones were, obviously, also exploding in popularity at this time and generally had those as standard features. So this whole thing really did not matter. Even at the time, it was clearly pretty trivial relative to the huge stakes around the Bush tax cuts, Medicare coverage of prescription drugs, abortion rights, and whatever else the Senate was working on at the time.

The problem-solving ethos

Critically, though, Schumer was okay with the wins being small.

In contemporary politics, the torch of calling out small scams and vowing to protect consumers has been carried forward by people like Elizabeth Warren, Rohit Chopra, and Lina Khan. Indeed, I think when she first arrived on the scene, Warren struck Schumer as a kindred spirit.

The difference, though, is that the modern-day anti-scam warriors wear their ideologies on their sleeve.

The Schumer approach to the “junk fee” issue would have been to ban hotels from charging non-optional destination fees and then say nice things about everyone when they stopped doing it. Maybe talk about how great and convenient online travel booking is compared to how things used to be, and that’s why it’s important to crack down on abusive ways of characterizing your prices.

The point is that swing voters don’t really like politics or care much about it, and they definitely aren’t interested in big-picture ideological clashes. You want to at least portray yourself as someone who woke up in the morning, heard about a problem, thought about it all day, and is now here with a solution. The people in the Warren tradition aren’t like that at all. To them, everything is a totalizing clash between their ideological vision and an implacable opposition driven by corruption.

As a result, instead of having the spirit of a “7 on Your Side” local television news segment, Biden-era regulators got a reputation as anti-business crusaders who made more enemies than friends.

In his 2007 book, “Positively American,” Schumer wrote about efforts to broker a bipartisan deal that would have opened more land in Alaska for oil drilling but also increased fuel efficiency standards on American cars. He laments that it didn’t come together because of interest group opposition on both sides.

This taps into how most Americans conceptualize corruption — they believe there’s a “problem-solving” mode that politicians could tap into if they wanted and make all the country’s troubles go away. They see the persistence of political conflict as a problem per se.

I of course get why Trump-era Democrats fetishize “fighters,” but I think it’s really important to understand that the purpose of fighting is to win, and you don’t win contested voters over by seeming stubborn. You don’t need to call it moderation, but there is a real difference between the problem-solving mode and the ideological-warrior mode.

We used to have politicians in this country

Everyone more or less knows that there are fewer swing seats and crossover voters than there used to be, and less ticket-splitting.

But I think people still underrate how much this has influenced the U.S. political system.

It used to be that even if there were plenty of safe House seats, any kind of statewide office was at least in principle contestable.

In 1998, when Schumer was making his first Senate run, New York had a Republican governor, New York City had a Republican mayor, and he was taking on a Republican incumbent. It was a solidly blue state in presidential politics at the time, but you just assumed that every statewide office would be visibly contested. As a result, basically every ambitious politician spent at least some time thinking about swing voters and persuasion — even if it wasn’t important for winning re-election, it was important for upward mobility.

Today’s politics isn’t like that.

In Michigan, specifically, a Democratic Party primary voter might say, “I like Abdul’s message, but I think Haley is the one who can win the seat.” But there aren’t a lot of states like that.

There are politicians like Gavin Newsom and Kamala Harris who’ve had very successful careers in Democratic Party politics without ever having beaten a Republican in a contested race. And increasingly there are people like Maria Cantwell and Schumer himself who did it once, back in the day, but will never have to again.

For most elected officials — and therefore most staffers and most campaign operatives — coalition-management is the only thing that actually matters, and it’s quite alien to imagine an incumbent sitting around worrying about persuasion. In retrospect, Schumer didn’t really need to be sweating all this small stuff in the summer of 2001. He never faced a serious opponent again. But at the time, it was reflexive — most people working in politics were persuasion-oriented and it was kind of in the water. Over the past 10 or 15 years, though, that’s become a niche pursuit, and there’s this huge cohort of members and operatives who never think about it.

1

Ask your parents.

2

Ask your parents.

3

Ask your parents.

The narrow path to saving the republic

Matthew Yglesias · 2026-06-25 · Original →

An American flag waves against a blue sky. (Photo by Grace Cary via Getty Images)

Not to be melodramatic, but I really believe the future of American democracy has been hanging in the balance the past few years.

January 6, as it played out, was more farce than tragedy. But normally, a president who becomes unpopular and loses power is discredited within his own political party. The fact that Donald Trump was able to survive not only electoral defeat but the specter of a failed putsch was an ominous portent for the country.

His ability to recapture the presidential nomination and then recapture the office itself is terrifying. And his second term has been a true “no guardrails” presidency.

It’s not that he’s suddenly a political superman who’s capable of anything; he remains quite constrained in his ability to accomplish all kinds of things. But the entire Republican Party has proven, time and again, that it will indulge Trump in essentially limitless levels of inappropriate conduct.

We have flagrantly unfit leadership at the F.B.I. We’ve seen repeated purges of senior military commanders. Bill Pulte is not only abusing power at the Federal Housing Finance Agency in previously unthinkable ways, but that willingness to abuse power was essentially his qualification for promotion to serve as director of national intelligence. With his pardons, Trump has made clear that there will be no legal accountability for any crimes conducted by his political allies, and he’s had many appointees across a range of agencies make clear that the White House can and will order prosecutions and regulatory crackdowns on his enemies.

The bad news for Americans’ well-being — but good news for the long-term future of the republic — is that Trump also massively fumbled the bag.

Rather than following a template for political success that would have allowed him to consolidate power, Trump has chosen overreach in almost every domain. And while his opportunistic promise to deliver a lower price level was never achievable, he was absolutely not forced to exacerbate inflation across multiple dimensions. These were voluntary blunders that have given the opposition a chance to beat him.

And the opposition has really needed good luck, because despite widespread alarm about these threats, the entire progressive movement has done less than nothing to respond to them.

While a hearty few souls have been organizing No Kings protests, the vast majority of activist energy has gone into a series of blue-on-blue primaries in safe districts, typically pitting an “establishment” politician whose views are already well to the left of the median voter against an insurgent whose views are identical on 90 percent of salient issues but who also says oligarchy and hates Israel. Even outside the circles of hardcore insurgents, the broad left-of-center media has displayed about 1,000 times more curiosity about what it takes to win primary elections in New York City than what it takes to beat Republicans in red-leaning areas.

But winning in red-leaning areas is what Democrats need to do.

If they can avoid blowing relatively easy races in Maine and Michigan, the decisive Senate race will be in Iowa or Ohio. With the Voting Rights Act gone, Republicans have been able to score a decisive advantage in gerrymandering and skew the House map heavily in favor of Trump, as the Senate already was. Democrats could easily win 52 percent of the vote nationally and see a Republican trifecta confirmed in place — except with Bill Cassidy and Mitch McConnell replaced by MAGA cultists.

The actual outlook, though, is better than that. Trump’s blundering and unpopularity, plus some good House and Senate recruiting, have put Democrats in a reasonable position to win.

Flipping both houses of Congress — and thus delivering a clear sign to American elites that this is not the time to surrender to authoritarianism — is not the most probable outcome, but it is on the table. To seize this chance, though, requires Trump opponents to focus on the thing that actually matters: not whether safe seat Democrats can be compelled to be even more uncompromising, but whether Democrats can win the key races.

The House is the states

Democrats have long been favored to flip the House, and their lead in the generic ballot is so large that all the gerrymandering probably won’t make a difference.

It is worth flagging, though, that with Virginia Democrats’ effort to gerrymander thrown out by the state’s own court and a bunch of red states in the South unleashing aggressive new gerrymanders thanks to the Supreme Court killing the Voting Rights Act, the landscape is now genuinely quite skewed. It would be historically unusual for an unpopular incumbent to see a big recovery between now and Election Day. But if Republicans can depress the Democrats’ generic ballot edge from its current range of six or seven points to something like four, the G.O.P. could potentially win the House.

The big issue here, though, is really 2028.

There are basically two things that could happen between this November and the end of the next cycle. First, there are several blue states that are not currently gerrymander-maxed but whose own state rules made it impossible to redraw maps in the time between the Supreme Court’s ruling and Election Day 2026. Over the course of two more years, though, Illinois will almost certainly draw an additional blue seat. New York will likely draw one and perhaps two additional blue seats. Maryland will probably create one.

But to get to within striking distance of even, Democrats will also need action from Virginia, Colorado, Minnesota, and Washington state, all of which are less certain for various reasons, including the fact that their ability to act likely depends on the midterms. Slow Boring is working with collaborators on some future donation recommendations that will include strategically targeted state legislative races to try to help with this, so stay tuned!

But another factor in play is Georgia.

Georgia did not join the red-state gerrymandering party, but it will have a new governor next year. If that governor is a Republican, Georgia will probably redraw. If the governor is a Democrat, it won’t. Democrats had a couple of strong contenders in the field, but the voters seem determined to go with former Atlanta Mayor Keisha Lance Bottoms, who isn’t a terrible nominee but seems like clearly the worst option. Nonetheless, Trump is unpopular enough that Bottoms has a decent shot at winning. There are also secret stakes in this race where if Bottoms wins, Jon Ossoff can run for president or V.P. without giving away a seat.

The Senate paradox

In the upper house, we have an odd situation in which Democrats seem to be in an unexpectedly strong position in some of the more challenging states, while a relatively easy race in Maine has become complicated and what ought to have been a layup in Michigan has turned into a clusterfuck.

First the good. Mary Peltola is genuinely favored to win Alaska’s Senate seat. Without her, the whole landscape would look very different. Peltola’s life would’ve been a lot easier if she’d run for governor, but she chose to run for Senate.

More than anyone else, she has “met the moment” in a real way, and Chuck Schumer talking her into it was a huge win. Every progressive NGO leader who ever gave her a hard time about anything while she was in the House should (but won’t) acknowledge that all their hopes and dreams hinge on her and make their apologies.

In Iowa, by contrast, the best candidate (Rob Sand) did run for governor, but Democrats still ended up with a really good candidate in Josh Turek, a Paralympian who holds a red seat in the state legislature. Zach Wahls should have done the right thing and stood down rather than turning this into a pointlessly expensive primary, but the voters chose correctly and the Sand-Turek ticket should have some complementarities and mutually reinforcing strengths.

In Texas, what I’ve seen so far from James Talarico suggests he is taking the obligation to win seriously. He’s seized the center ground on energy — touting “all of the above” in contrast to Trump’s efforts to kneecap renewables — and he’s moved toward the center on trans issues. He also appears to be collaborating with South Texas moderate Hispanics on a tactical level in a way that will hopefully unlock a good border message. This is a tough race that will cost a fortune and probably end in the election of crooked scumbag Ken Paxton, but the case for hope is real.

Ohio is the tough Senate race that I feel the most queasy about. Re-running Sherrod Brown makes some sense on paper but also strikes me as a little uninspired. Brown has moderated on cryptocurrency, which is good in the sense that it’s good to move to the center, but I think it’s a bit odd to treat his 2024 defeat as all about crypto spending. I was not a huge fan of his prior stance on this, but there are legitimate criticisms of the crypto industry and being anti-crypto was deeply consistent with Brown’s longtime populist persona.

To me, what separates the Sherrod Brown who won repeatedly in Ohio from the one who lost in 2024 is that the earlier Brown was a moderate on energy, while the 2024 Brown seemed like a deer in the headlights on Biden-era immigration issues. He’s polling okay in this race and I hope he pulls it out, but I don’t feel confident in his campaign’s approach.

Much has already been written about Maine, and because I’ll be in Hancock County Maine (where Graham Platner is from) for most of the summer, I’ll probably end up covering this a lot. I just want to note here that Maine had a ton of ambitious, age-appropriate, well-vetted politicians, absolutely none of whom ran for Senate. That includes guys like Troy Jackson, a Bernie factionalist whose politics I don’t really agree with but who would have brought everything to the table that Platner fans like without some of the downsides.

This brings us to Michigan, a true fiasco.

Gretchen Whitmer would have cleared the field and easily won this race. But she pulled an anti-Peltola and is throwing a promising political career away for no clear reason.

Meanwhile, nothing this cycle has caused me more heartache than this primary. We have Abdul El-Sayed, a Bernie factionalist, running not in one of the D+infinity House seats that are the left’s specialty, but statewide in a swing state where even small underperformance could be deadly. The establishment kinda sorta but not really consolidated around Haley Stevens as their candidate but, while Stevens is a moderate, she’s actually a weak electoral performer. Mallory McMorrow, the third force in the race, has more political skills than Stevens, but those skills have mostly been deployed in ugly fighting with Stevens.

I have friends in both the McMorrow and Stevens camps, and unfortunately instead of working together to fix the party, the narcissism of small differences is creating a situation where El-Sayed will likely win the primary.

It’s a massively frustrating situation. Even without Whitmer in the race, there were better options like Kristen McDonald Rivet (one of the top electoral performers in the House) and Detroit Mayor Mike Duggan (who instead chose to run for governor as an independent and then drop out). Maine is the bluest state on the 2026 map, but it’s tough for Democrats because Susan Collins is tough. Michigan should have been easy, but instead it’s become very hard — a true blunder.

Tactics versus strategy

I think the tactical efforts of Democratic Party leaders have been a little under-appreciated.

Hakeem Jeffries has his people collaborating on actual House votes, winning discharge petitions, and doing a surprisingly good job of influencing the news agenda. With the exception of one House race in California (pour one out for Jasmeet Bains), he’s managed to keep the factional infighting firewalled from the important races. Redistricting ended up being fucked, but that’s not his fault.

Schumer, I think, is guilty of a legitimate brain fart in Maine, where there were many better places to park his hopes and dreams than in Janet Mills.

But the Michigan situation, while a total nightmare, is not his fault. It’s not like he forgot to call Whitmer; she just chose to do the wrong thing. Iowa, Alaska, and Texas have all worked out quite well, and while I’m kind of gritting my teeth about Ohio, I do understand the play and I think this race is basically on Brown to read his old press coverage and talk to some steelworkers and people in the building trades about energy.

But as a guy whose whole thing is complaining about Democrats, I really do want to emphasize that 80 percent of what’s keeping Democrats in the game here is Trump blundering.

To that you can add some strong tactical optimizations around candidate selection and messaging emphasis. My moderate-aligned friends who work in ads and testing are all really happy with the direction the party is going in. I am less pleased, because I think operatives tend to massively overrate paid media and direct-to-camera videos relative to strategic position-taking.

But the national party has not made even token gestures at a rebrand. There’s nothing like the Republicans’ “young guns” from 2010 or the brand refresh Democrats did in 2006.

I think Democrats think they’ve changed their pitch on immigration after the Biden fiasco, but I genuinely don’t understand what they think is different about it. They know that “affordability” is their best talking point against Trump, but that has meant rebranding ideas that were mostly developed in Obama’s second term rather than an actual renewed focus on interest rates and productivity.

I’m trying to not be too whiny about this, because Weber specifically warns against the person who will “crumble when the world from his point of view is too stupid or too base for what he wants to offer.” The stakes are high, but there are real reasons for hope in these midterms and green shoots of the kinds of change that need to happen.

Democrats have under-reacted relative to what will maximize their odds in the midterms, and I really don’t want them to get complacent if they flip the House with two seats to spare. But there have been positive changes, and taking advantage of Trump’s screwups is a legitimate way to conduct politics. Relative to the disastrous situation the party was facing in the winter of 2024–25, things are looking up.

It’s going to take a bunch of small things all going right to make me feel good about this November. But it’s definitely possible if we all pull together and walk the narrow path to save the country.

↩ Back to article summary · ↪ See Claude's response

Ranking Joe Biden’s mistakes

Matthew Yglesias · 2026-06-25 · Original →

Former President Joe Biden and his wife are back in the public eye thanks to Jill Biden’s new book and Joe’s apparent difficulties raising money for his presidential library. Hunter Biden, meanwhile, has become a social media personality and even a guest on Candace Owens’s podcast. It’s a reminder that as much as the entire left-of-center community may want to simply memory-hole the entire Biden era, the family did serve for four years and their legacy matters.

At the same time, my strong sense is that among Democratic Party elites, a superficial consensus around being annoyed at Joe and Jill is masking deeper disagreements about what, exactly, they did wrong.

Jerusalem and I decided it might be fun to try to put together our rankings of the Bidens’ mistakes.

I don’t want to spoil the actual list — you’ll have to listen to the pod for that — but personally I take the somewhat contrarian view that the decision to run for a second term and then drop out too late to avoid an open primary was fine. It was maybe good, even. I’m also a defender of Biden’s approach to the Israel-Palestine conflict. This obviously was not perfect. Like every other president in history, he did not successfully resolve the conflict and generate a just and peaceful outcome. But the rapidly congealed conventional wisdom on the left that he was complicit in a genocide is, I think, both factually wrong and also a kind of cope — a way for progressives to sharply distance themselves from his administration without grappling with its substantive failures on domestic policy.

Jerusalem is less kind to Biden on those two points, but correspondingly kinder on some others. All told, I think this is the kind of conversation that Democrats really need to be having about the past administration — almost everyone these days agrees that the party needs to change and offer something different, but what exactly is that going to mean?

As for future episodes, in a couple of weeks we’ll be reviewing Jane Jacobs’s “The Death and Life of Great American Cities,” so grab a copy and read along!

The episode’s transcript will be available for paying subscribers after the paywall in this post.

WATCH THE EPISODE HERE

New episodes post every Thursday.

Jerusalem Demsas: Hi, I’m Jerusalem Demsas.

Matthew Yglesias: I’m Matthew Yglesias.

Jerusalem Demsas: Welcome to The Argument. As you can probably hear, I sound a little sick today, but that’s good because it’s on theme. Today we’re talking about Sleepy Joe Biden.

Matthew Yglesias: Yeah, he’s back, sort of. Ben Terris had this article in New York Magazine about Joe and Jill Biden trying to get back in the public eye, trying to make some money, trying to raise funds for a presidential library, do a book tour. And I would say the upshot of the article is that Democrats aren’t buying it.

Jerusalem Demsas: Nobody wants this.

Matthew Yglesias: Yeah.

Jerusalem Demsas: Democrats don’t want it. Progressives don’t want it. Former Biden staffers don’t want it. Jill Biden doesn’t even seem like she really wants it.

Matthew Yglesias: And I think that there is an element of cope by Democrats in this.

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah.

Matthew Yglesias: That it would be one thing if Joe and Jill Biden were being abandoned because Democrats, broadly speaking, are going in a radically different policy direction from the Biden administration; that’s, like, a valid reason to abandon somebody. But they’re actually not.

I feel like there’s this, like, personalistic piling on. As if, like, the Biden family single-handedly ruined everything, but also was right about almost every question of substance. And so we’re gonna, like, turn them into, like, the Omelas of the Democratic Party who need to, like, suffer for the sins of everyone without anything changing.

Jerusalem Demsas: You mean the child in Omelas?

Matthew Yglesias: Yeah, whatever it is.

Jerusalem Demsas: Most of the people in Omelas were, like, happy.

Matthew Yglesias: Yes, OK.

Jerusalem Demsas: They had great lives.

Matthew Yglesias: Yes, but the Democrats are like those people, right? Who are, like, going along and they’re like, “We’re right about everything, we’re vindicated about everything, we don’t need to change anything. It’s just all Joe and Jill’s fault.”

Jerusalem Demsas: So we’re gonna put them in the basement and throw poop at them, OK.

Matthew Yglesias: Yeah, and I don’t buy that. I mean, it’s not that I want to celebrate them, but, like, I want to condemn Bidenism. Not, like, Joe Biden as a man.

Jerusalem Demsas: So, like, while I have big policy disagreements with Joe Biden as well, I do think that it is perfectly coherent to be like, “If Joe Biden was, like, 30 years younger or — like, most of the problems by the end of the Biden administration would not have happened.

Matthew Yglesias: Well, I mean, I’m open to that, but I mean, I think… So, I think the conceit was we were going to try to, like, rank our complaints with Joe Biden.

Jerusalem Demsas: Yes. Top 10 reasons to hate Joe Biden.

Matthew Yglesias: And, I mean, I do agree, right? I mean, you know, 2024 election was lost, you know...

Jerusalem Demsas: I mean, the number one reason, I think, is just that, like, he should not have run again.

Matthew Yglesias: OK, so this is, like, my number one candidate. I mean, it was gonna come out here. You know, that, like, the terrible thing about Joe Biden is that he chose to run again.

I have a — that’s, like, the right thing to say. If I…

Jerusalem Demsas: Well, let me just give the case for it.

Matthew Yglesias: I just want to be clear.

Jerusalem Demsas: Donald Trump is, like, president of the United States because a man could not get over himself enough to realize — or he’s so sick that, like, someone around him should have done this. But they were, like, actively concealing this man’s decline from the American public.

They required a movie star, someone with absolutely no democratic kind of accountability reason to have to tell us anything, to reveal to us — George Clooney — that he was actually… This is not just, like, a one-off night where he, like, shat the bed in front of the entire country and the world, but that he was actually in this long-term decline.

He made every single one of his allies that stood up for him look like a moron. He made every single Democrat that they called on to say, like, you know — they called all these Democrats to be like, “Tell the press that he’s actually totally fine.” He discredited them. He discredited the whole party.

He made everyone in the entire left look like a complete moron for defending this person, all because he could not recognize that he was too old to run for president again. And, as a result, we have President Donald Trump, number two, way worse than number one.

Matthew Yglesias: So I want to express a nuanced view of this, right? So George Clooney is a hero.

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah.

Matthew Yglesias: He’s a great man and…

Jerusalem Demsas: Come on the pod, George.

Matthew Yglesias: And we love George Clooney. I liked his movie, Jay Kelly, that nobody really saw.

Jerusalem Demsas: I didn’t see it.

Matthew Yglesias: But I thought it was brilliant. It’s on Netflix. Everyone should check it out.

But the right thing to say if you are a Democratic Party politician is that Joe Biden erred by running for reelection at all, that he was too old, that they covered this up, that it denied the public, you know, the opportunity to have a real primary, etc.

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah.

Matthew Yglesias: That’s, clearly, like, the right thing to say. I don’t think it’s actually true, though, right? Now, clearly, Biden should have dropped out several days before he actually did. You know, they should have admitted that that debate made it untenable.

But I actually think that staying in the race too long to have a primary was good. That if there had been an open primary, if everyone had read Ezra’s column and been like, “This is correct, I’m not running for reelection, we’re gonna have an open nominating process”...

Jerusalem Demsas: What was that column, in March?

Matthew Yglesias: Whenever it was.

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah, yeah, OK.

Matthew Yglesias: All that would’ve happened is that Kamala Harris would’ve won the nomination, but she would have faced pressure from her left to tie herself even more to the mast of unpopular left-wing politics, and things would have been even worse. It’s high-minded and idealistic and, like, correct to say we should have had an open primary process, but it wouldn’t have accomplished anything.

Jerusalem Demsas: I guess I want to separate out two things. One, he shouldn’t have run way before. I’m talking about, like, this man should have been a one-term president, like he was saying, and should have been clear that he was doing that after like 2022. It should have been just obvious. And the second one is…

Matthew Yglesias: But that’s even worse. I guess what I’m saying is the more open the process, the worse it gets.

Jerusalem Demsas: I actually disagree about this. A) because I’m a democracy believer, I guess, unlike Matt Yglesias, who’s, I guess, for folks who are listening, just thumbs down democracy.

But I think this for a couple reasons. One, I think a couple of things would’ve happened. I think you are right that an open democratic primary process at that point in American politics and history would’ve been very focused on Gaza and Israel. I think I have, like, different views than you about how bad that would’ve been for Democrats. I think that’s clearly become an issue where acknowledging how bad Israel is now…

Matthew Yglesias: No, but to be clear, I want to bracket Gaza. But it’s just — it would’ve been everything, right? Like, we saw what Democratic primaries are like in 2020.

Jerusalem Demsas: But to be clear, they lost anyway. To be clear, like, both worlds, they’re lost.

Matthew Yglesias: I understand.

Jerusalem Demsas: They lose anyway. So the question is, if you’re gonna lose, especially at this point, would it have been better to have an open democratic primary process where, A), I think hopefully someone could have distinguished themselves by being like, I think, having better views on the economy, having better economic policy views, not being a Warrenite person who could have set themselves up better for 2028. Or B), the fact that those views would have been discredited is actually a part of the democratic process.

Like, right now we have this, like, the whole cope thing that you’re worried about, that you’re upset about, is that because everything is focused on Biden’s age, there’s no actual reckoning about the ideas that the Biden administration stood for. But that’s, like, the whole point of having an open process. Like, the democratic process literally lets you have those debates in public and invalidate points of view that we think are negative or positive.

Matthew Yglesias: But I guess I’m saying, like, you know, Biden steps aside.

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah.

Matthew Yglesias: And, like, Josh Shapiro and Gretchen Whitmer, like, could have challenged Kamala Harris for the nomination, but they chose not to.

Jerusalem Demsas: It was too late.

Matthew Yglesias: Well, I don’t know that it was.

Jerusalem Demsas: Well, like, they could or they should have done it, but, like, I just mean, like, I think they would have if it was 2022.

Matthew Yglesias: I think that the — well, so I sort of disagree with that. Like, I think the easiest way to dump Kamala would have been through a behind-closed-doors insider process at a convention rather than an open primary. I mean, she was just crushingly dominant position to win the nomination as the incumbent VP.

But, because she won it in a coronation, she at least did have the opportunity to distance herself. She chose to make the argument that…

Jerusalem Demsas: But it didn’t work.

Matthew Yglesias: Well, but it worked some. I mean, like, her numbers went way, way, way up. I guess all I’m saying is that, like, I think that there’s this, like, meta cope that, like, somehow you could have gotten a nominee other than Kamala Harris if Joe Biden had made different decisions. But, like, it was just always going to be her from the moment she was picked.

Jerusalem Demsas: I just think there’s, like…

Matthew Yglesias: And the concern that I always heard from people who were like, “Maybe Biden shouldn’t run again.” And it was like, “Well, if Biden doesn’t run again, we’re getting Harris.” And people were afraid of getting Harris.

Jerusalem Demsas: Well, we’ll figure out where to rank picking Kamala Harris as one of the top picks.

Matthew Yglesias: Well, let’s do it.

Jerusalem Demsas: But I want to just, like…

Matthew Yglesias: I think that’s another strong candidate, is, like, picking Kamala.

Jerusalem Demsas: But I just, I want to dig in here because I think, like, the point of an open — the point of democracy, not to go way too far back here — but the point of democracy is that, like, the process of the public reasoning allows us to get to better ideas. Like, by doing it —

Yes, if it had worked, if there was a closed-door process that had done this that was still somewhat democratic because you’re through, like, you know, you know, the selection mechanisms that have been laid out, like, yeah, that would have been fine. But clearly that wasn’t gonna happen because she’d consolidated that effort.

So, at that point, then it’s like, we should have an open process, not only because — not because I think it’s necessary that we’re going to get someone else. I think you’re right. Kamala Harris is the dominant choice of the voters at that point in time. But I think because it lets you force people into making cases for themselves and then arguing why those cases are actually good or bad.

Matthew Yglesias: OK, so I’m going to bracket that. My view is that democracy should exist between the parties rather than within them. And that the real moral of this story and of so much that’s crappy about American politics is that intra-party democracy is, like, a bad idea. But that’s probably a whole other podcast.

So, picking Kamala Harris.

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah.

Matthew Yglesias: This was a poor selection.

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah.

Matthew Yglesias: I rate this very high on the list.

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah.

Matthew Yglesias: The one thing that gives me some… I don’t know what it is about it…

Jerusalem Demsas: You’re on Team Jill Biden about this.

Matthew Yglesias: No.

Jerusalem Demsas: Jill Biden hates Kamala Harris too.

Matthew Yglesias: Well, this is the thing, is, like, I remember when Kamala Harris was selected.

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah.

Matthew Yglesias: And I said on the day that she was selected that this is a poor choice. I said on a podcast recorded two weeks before it was selected that I didn’t understand why anyone was saying Kamala Harris was a good choice.

I said on a podcast before that, that, like, if Joe Biden felt that he was cornered — that he had promised previously to select a woman, and then, because of George Floyd, he felt he had to select an African American, and there was only one African American woman who had traditional qualifications for the vice presidency — that Joe Biden should say “It was a mistake to have made that pledge” and he should select Cory Booker.

I know, also, that Joe Biden was resistant to selecting Kamala Harris, right? That’s how we ended up with this last-minute vetting of Susan Rice and Karen Bass, who were, I think, unsuitable for that role, is why they weren’t selected.

You know, I like Susan Rice. I know you hate her. But she’s not a politician. That didn’t make sense as an idea. That was a principal who was grasping at straws to evade this logic.

But I was really on an island when I was saying that stuff.

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah.

Matthew Yglesias: Democratic Party donors were really excited about the Kamala Harris pick. Again, I was doing a podcast with Ezra Klein at the time, and he was like, “Biden hit it out of the park with this Kamala choice.” And I don’t — I want to hear somebody say that, like, they were wrong, that the conventional wisdom in liberal America was wrong, that, like, the values and processes and ideas that led to Kamala Harris were a mistake.

Because again, if someone is saying, “My takeaway from the Biden experience is that, like, the conventional liberal approach to identity politics is broken,” then, like, I’m gonna stand up and cheer, right? But if they’re like, “Fuck Joe Biden, let’s keep everything the same,” it’s like, well, …

Jerusalem Demsas: I think you’re underrating how much that has actually permeated the party. I mean, I have to do this every time I talk about Kamala Harris. I did work for Kamala Harris on her 2019 presidential campaign in South Carolina.

Matthew Yglesias: Yeah. How’d that go?

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah, not great. I left politics and now I’m here ragging on Kamala Harris. And, I mean, I think at the time, a lot of people — I mean, the reason donors liked her is because she’s actually just someone who’s very charismatic in those kinds of rooms and is very comfortable with people in that kind of milieu, and, like, is just a very charismatic person in general in those spaces.

And, like, they liked her. She had very normal liberal opinions on most topics. She spoke really eloquently about, you know, reproductive rights and things that are really important to a lot of Democrats.

It also was, of course, a moment where people really wanted to elevate a non-white man, especially a Black woman, to higher levels of office. We saw this in multiple areas of governance and also, you know, the economy, etc.

And I just, I mean, my view of this is just: The impulse to look around halls of power and realize that they are, like, overrepresented by certain powerful groups is, like, a correct impulse. The decision then to go, “OK, the way that we fix this is to find any Black woman at all to elevate,” is, like, obviously fucking stupid.

And it’s stupid because it’s also, like, insulting to, like, the actual structural inequalities that don’t produce Black women who are with the qualifications to be vice president in higher numbers that are relative to their proportion of population. And I think that, like, the reason behind this is because, like, it’s actually quite difficult to solve these problems.

Most people now look back on that — most people, at least, that I talk to in the Democratic Party — look back on that process and are like, “This was obviously a huge mistake. This is obviously not how we should have done things,” from both perspectives.

Like, both the idea that, like, it was a bad choice in Kamala Harris, but also, like, really actually kind of insulting to Black women and to Black people in general that, like, the thing that you do to, like, help a population that has real concerns about inflation, about educational opportunities, about crime, whatever, is that you just, like, elevate some random person who happens to share some of their characteristics.

Like, I just think that that’s, like, not a perspective that people will anymore espouse openly. So I think you should take the win on this, Matt. Like, people agree with you now.

Matthew Yglesias: So, I got another sticky [note] and it says “identity over merit.” I’m trying to be provocative. I have a more nuanced view of what was going on here a little bit.

But I think that the Biden administration repeatedly did versions of this, right? Ketanji Brown Jackson was a perfectly reasonable Supreme Court selection. I think this is nothing like picking Kamala Harris for the vice presidency, but he pre-poisoned the well by not selecting an African American woman for the Supreme Court, but saying he was going to select an African American woman for the Supreme Court, then interviewing three African American women for the Supreme Court, then picking one of them.

And that — he put her on the Supreme Court, so it’s not, like, the most terrible thing you could do to somebody, but conditional on giving someone a Supreme Court appointment, it’s the worst way you could do that.

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah.

Matthew Yglesias: You know, George H.W. Bush always maintained with a straight face and total kayfabe that he did not pick Clarence Thomas because he wanted a Black man to replace Thurgood Marshall on the Supreme Court. He just said that he looked at a bunch of candidates and there was this guy and he really liked him and he thought he did good work and he put him on the bench.

And, like, identity has always been a consideration in judicial appointments, but that’s the appropriate way to do it. You know, there was for a while, there was a Jewish seat on the Supreme Court, but nobody ever said “I’m only considering Jews.”

And Biden would also do things, you know — it was Marcia Fudge, said she wanted to be agriculture secretary.

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah.

Matthew Yglesias: Because, actually, the Agriculture Department runs the food stamp program.

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah.

Matthew Yglesias: And that’s a very important issue for, you know, the urban poor, a constituency that she cares about a lot, and that she was tired of, you know, HUD being just, like, the Black job in the cabinet. And then Biden offered her HUD.

You know, Xavier Becerra, who has had a remarkable career resurgence and is gonna be governor of California, but he wanted to be attorney general, which is a job that he was qualified for. But Biden was so invested in the cutesiness of making Merrick Garland attorney general that he didn’t want to give Becerra the job that Becerra wanted. But he did want to have a Hispanic person in the cabinet. So he offered Michelle Lujan Grisham HHS Secretary, which is a job she was well-qualified for, but she didn’t want to be in the cabinet. So then Biden offers HHS to Becerra, which is not a job — he has no professional background in that.

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah.

Matthew Yglesias: Then the White House got really mad that Becerra wasn’t good at the job.

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah.

Matthew Yglesias: And so it’s this kind of double bind where, on the one hand, they were obsessed with identity. But also they wouldn’t actually treat the people they were elevating well, whether it’s how you would characterize them, giving them jobs that they actually want…

Jerusalem Demsas: I mean, this is, like, the classic — you are now espousing a classic woke opinion about glass ceilings, which is that people get put in, like Becerra, in particular, gets put in a position to fail.

Matthew Yglesias: Right. I mean, it’s — because he’s going to end up being governor because of Eric Swalwell, you know, all this other stuff, it winds up being fine. But I was really…

Jerusalem Demsas: Well, not fine for the administration or the massive program.

Matthew Yglesias: No, no, but I mean, I felt really bad for earlier when Becerra’s gubernatorial campaign was flailing.

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah.

Matthew Yglesias: I was sitting there thinking, you know, as a somewhat Latino person myself, like, this is a huge shame. This guy was attorney general of California. He would have been a front-runner for the gubernatorial nomination had his career not taken this bizarre detour into being a cabinet secretary whose boss then was shitting on him constantly.

And you also saw this with your good friend, Karine Jean-Pierre, who…

Jerusalem Demsas: Not my good friend, for listeners.

Matthew Yglesias: You wrote a funny article about her. It’s weird to — it’s not that weird to have a press secretary who you fire because you don’t think she’s doing that good of a job.

It’s really weird to have a press secretary who you maintain in a job while clearly and openly lacking confidence in that person’s ability to do the job — bringing, like, a white guy in to do half the briefings, leaking to Politico that, like, you were hoping to kick her upstairs to a job at Emily’s List. Like, it’s really — it’s both shabby treatment of these people as individuals and also a terrible way to run the government.

Jerusalem Demsas: I mean, it’s also — I mean, like, what’s so crazy about this is, like, there’s obviously an internal push by some, you know, high-ranking minority political bodies, whether it’s, like, CBC [Congressional Black Caucus] or CHC [Congressional Hispanic Caucus] or various members of, you know, the political establishment that’s tried to promote diversity in the ranks, that, like, you want to put forward your people in order to make sure that there’s still an impetus to diversify.

Like, you have to have, like, political groups that, like, care about — care about this in order to, like, create pressure for people to actually consider identity in some form or fashion. But, like, it’s kind of insane to go, like, “OK, we’re going to bow to the pressure of the CBC and do this, but then we’re going to, like, viciously insult the people that we’re actually elevating at their behest,” which indicates then that, like, you’re both kind of in their pocket, you resent being in their pocket, so you lose both the better — it’s, like, the worst of all worlds.

Now they’re also mad at you because you’re treating Karine badly. Like, there were, like, stories about how, like, people were offended that Karine Jean-Pierre was being treated badly by the press, by the president and his office and his staff.

There’s, like, tons of tension in D.C. among Black consultants and political staffers that these staffers are being treated badly, which is true. If you’re going to hire someone in that role, you shouldn’t be an asshole to them.

Matthew Yglesias: Right.

Jerusalem Demsas: But I think that, at core here, the thing I’m worried about is that there’s an overreading of this entire situation. Like, Donald Trump is president right now. His chief of staff is a woman. There are like — there’s a gay cabinet member for the first time. This is, like, actually, like, identity, like, you know, wins for these groups.

And, like, I’ll just note, there aren’t tons of stories questioning the competence of Susie Wiles to, like, I don’t know, like, destroy the country. Like, she’s clearly very, very good at helping them do that.

And I want to just impart to people, like, it is not bad to have a diverse cabinet. Like, that is not a problem. And that, like, when another Democratic president gets into office, the goal here should not be, “Oh, that was, like, a really shitty thing,” or whatever.

Because I’m going to these, like, fucking, like, left-wing things sometimes. I was invited to this, like, liberal event, and they were just like — it was like the least diverse thing I’ve ever been to in my life. I was like, I swear to God, the only Black person there. There was, like, 10% of people were women.

It was, like, kind of shocking to me. And I’m like, “Oh, it’s because, like, everyone has stopped giving a shit about this. Like, the woke era is over. Like, no one gives a shit about identity.” I’m like, this is, like, a complete incorrect overcorrection.

Matthew Yglesias: OK, but again, to the Trump point, right? I mean, Trump has a much less diverse cabinet and senior White House staff. You would expect — I mean, the Republican Party is less diverse, right?

If you — I’m not suggesting you do this — but if you just picked House Democrats at random and gave them cabinet jobs, you would end up with a fairly diverse cabinet. If you picked House Republicans at random, you would end up with a not very diverse cabinet.

But the elements of diversity in the Trump cabinet make a lot of sense, right? There are lots of people other than Scott Bessent who could be treasury secretary, but of possible jobs for Scott Bessent to have, treasury secretary is one of, like, three.

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah.

Matthew Yglesias: He’s a guy who could have a senior economic policy job or no job.

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah.

Matthew Yglesias: Scott Turner, HUD is the only job he could conceivably have. He is a legitimate housing guy. He’s not my favorite person in the universe, but he’s a completely reasonable resume-based HUD pick. It’s not…

Trump in his first term actually did the Biden thing and was like, “Here’s a prominent African American conservative. I’m gonna make him HUD Secretary instead of HHS Secretary or something.”

Jerusalem Demsas: To be clear, that’s, like, a longstanding thing.

Matthew Yglesias: Absolutely, yes. But, I mean, it’s, like, Trump broke with that, right?

Susie Wiles is chief of staff because she was his campaign manager, and people felt that she did a good job of imposing discipline on the campaign. It’s not, like, Donald Trump thought it would be a fun press release, and so he found a woman to be chief of staff. So…

Jerusalem Demsas: I will point out, though, like, the female cabinet secretaries have been the ones kind of being dropped one by one.

Matthew Yglesias: Yes, yes. No, no, Trump is glass cliffing them, and I think doesn’t get along well with women. There’s a lot of history there.

But just to say that there’s a difference between trying to be thoughtful, right? “I like this guy Becerra. What would be a good job to give him?” And this kind of flailing, you know, sort of stuff.

But in terms of staffing…

Jerusalem Demsas: Next sticky note.

Matthew Yglesias: I mean, I think — I wrote this down as Warren staffers — but, to me, this is the core of Bidenism. And when I talk about cope, like, this is what I am talking about.

We had an open primary contest in 2020. It was quite vigorous, and we saw very clearly that the donors to the Democratic Party, the small donors, they wanted Bernie Sanders. If they didn’t get Bernie Sanders, they wanted Elizabeth Warren.

The big donors wanted either Warren or Pete Buttigieg or maybe Kamala Harris. And all those people were well to the left of Joe Biden.

And the voters disagreed with the donors. They disagreed with the staffers. They disagreed with the media.

Like, no columnists that I know of were supporting Joe Biden. No big Twitter personalities were supporting Joe Biden. But the voters of the Democratic Party wanted Joe Biden.

He then immediately fires his campaign manager after winning the primary, which is bizarre. He brings on Jen O’Malley Dillon, who was the architect of the Beto O’Rourke presidential campaign.

Ron Klain comes in, a longtime Biden guy, but had been on the outs, but he gets back in. And his idea is to create this — not say, like, “We won the argument. This is the new moderate Bidenist Democratic Party,” but to create this Biden-Warren fusion in which all the key economic regulatory jobs go to Elizabeth Warren protégés.

And they really were — having won the primary on the basis of, “I was Barack Obama’s vice president,” they empower all these people who feel very strongly that the Obama administration deliberately let all these criminals off the hook, was a cesspool of corruption, etc., etc., etc. And there’s been no reckoning inside the Democratic Party about this, right?

And it’s, like, Obama doesn’t acknowledge it publicly in any kind of way, but there was this brain transplant of the old thinkers running economic policy in favor of their intra-party critics under the guise of continuity. And I think this is, you know, just a really important question. Like, “Do we want to continue on this trajectory or do we want to revert or do something else?”

And I think those people — I think the Bidenist Warrenists — are sort of ground zero for just, like, “Oh, this guy was too old and we just got to, like, swap in a younger-looking skin suit on Bidenism.”

Jerusalem Demsas: This is one of those things that’s, like, conventional wisdom in D.C., but I’m not sure is, like, well understood everywhere, is just how much power Warren-adjacent orgs/Warren’s office itself had over the hiring and appointments of senior and mid-level people in the administration.

Like, there was just, like, an effort — both, I think there’s, like, a good faith effort at the end of every contentious primary to, like, bring the party together. Like, you want a unified party. It’s bad vibes if, like, you still have the old person, like, your old, you know, sniping at you, saying you’re horrible or whatever. You want a unified anti-Trump coalition going into the general election. That’s all normal. You bring some people in. Obama did this, like, Clinton did this. Everyone does this.

This went further. This is, like, after the general election ends, after the transition — during the transition process, when you’re having decisions about who to appoint for various mid-level positions like deputy undersecretaries, etc. — is that, like, there’s an outsized impact that not just, I think, literally these offices and groups had, like groups like Revolving Door Project, etc., but, like, the ideas they espouse.

So, like, this idea that individuals who had worked in the private sector were necessarily, you know, suspect. That, like, if you had ever worked at, like, a McKinsey, if you had ever worked at a consulting firm, which based — or, you know, even in industry, like, if you’d worked in, like, oil, or gas, or you’d worked in even more, like, banking or whatever it is.

And there’s, like — I want to step back here. Like, obviously it’s the case that it’s very, very bad to have, like, regulators completely co-opted by the industries they’re supposed to be regulating. Like, no one actually wants there to be, like, a complete revolving door here.

But there’s a difference between, hey, like, “bankers and people who are going to be bankers once they leave the administration should not be in charge of all the banking regulatory bodies,” and “we shouldn’t have anyone with subject matter expertise working in banking regulation.”

And I heard from, you know, someone who was in Treasury, you know, on background tell me about how, like, they had trouble during the SVB, Silicon Valley Bank, crisis to finding someone who could just, like, walk them through — internally, walk them through — what was literally going to happen — that evening, that night, the next day — as this crisis was unfolding because they didn’t have people who were working in, you know, banking in the administration in those offices.

And so, I just, I think this is one of those things here where this is such an under, I think, discussed point because many of the people who are criticizing Biden still continue to have these views.

Matthew Yglesias: Right. And, you know, I mean, to get detailed on this, right?
