So you had a number of people appointed to prominent jobs at FTC, at Commodity Futures Trading Commission, other kinds of things like this throughout the administration. But you also had, Pramila Jayapal’s chief of staff was running the Office of Presidential Personnel.

Jerusalem Demsas: And Pramila Jayapal is the CPC —Congressional Progressive Caucus’ head.

Matthew Yglesias: Right.

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah.

Matthew Yglesias: And that’s a very eccentric decision-making, you know, and it has a lot of influence over the paper flow of who gets nominated for low-level jobs.

And so, you know, you would wind up seeing people who had been, like, OG Biden primary campaign staffers, at a time when that was a very unfashionable campaign, and they would end up working at the Department of Transportation or elsewhere in the outer realms of the galaxy, whereas people who were more wired with the left of the party would be in the White House or in the power agencies. It’s a very strange way to construct an administration.

I think it’s a profound difference of opinion as to whether one’s take on Biden being too old was that everything about the construction of the Biden administration was amazing, but the guy at its head lacked the vim and vigor to sell this amazing agenda.

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah.

Matthew Yglesias: ‘Cause these are two things that sound the same as text. My version of “Biden is too old” is that Biden, as an old guy, did not have the vim and vigor to stand up for himself to a certain level.

He had this top level of old chums of his, because to be clear, I mean, it’s not like it was only leftists in the administration, but it was a very thin layer of people who he had known personally for a very, very long time. And then he just ceded all kinds of ground on lower-level decisions and did not seem very personally engaged with the details of policymaking that was coming forth in this administration.

Jerusalem Demsas: I mean, this is actually, I think, I would categorize this as a different reason to hate Joe Biden, which is that he just didn’t give a shit enough about policy.

And I know that it’s so funny because the entirety of the Biden administration is kind of revolt against economists, revolt against the wonks. So there are all of these columns written during the Biden administration about how economists have lost cachet, etc., because, like, the Vox wonk white paper style of governance of the Obama years is super out of vogue and embarrassing.

But, like, I just want to, like, point out here that, like, even the left critiques of Biden almost entirely rest on the fact that this guy was completely disconnected from policy, whether we’re talking about foreign policy where, like, I think that he’s actually more connected than anywhere else, but, like, domestic policy in particular.

Like, this guy was just, like, did not, like, he was not the one negotiating with Joe Manchin. He’s, like, out to lunch when they’re doing the big IRA [Inflation Reduction Act] negotiations. He’s completely devolved that down, like, multiple layers away from him.

And I’m just, like, that’s not how the Obama administration worked because Obama personally was invested in the details of what was going on of his signature economic policy bill.

Matthew Yglesias: Right. So this, I think we’re agreeing here, but this is the debate that’s, like, not in the Ben Terris article. It’s not in these takes about how everyone’s past Joe Bidenism.

Are we saying “this was great, but the frontman wasn’t doing enough salesmanship”? Or are we saying that it was actually, like, a deeply flawed construction, in which the frontman’s lack of engagement with what they were doing is the problem?

And that brings us to a couple other things I’ve written down here...

Jerusalem Demsas: Just for folks — I want to be clear to audio folks, Matt has been sticking these stickies…

Matthew Yglesias: I’ve got stickies all around. I’m just, you know, I’m adjusting to podcasting as a visible medium.

Jerusalem Demsas: He’s on the mic. He’s on…

Matthew Yglesias: So this one says open borders, which, you know, that’s an exaggeration, but clearly something went wrong with immigration policy in the Biden years.

Jerusalem Demsas: It’s so funny. I think the biggest problem with Joe Biden — I mean, sorry, number one will be that he chose to run again, even though you have different views. I think my number two is, like, the “he didn’t care enough about economics” thing.

My number three is, like, not taking immigration seriously enough. Like, one of the most damning parts of the entire — I mean, the Demsas thesis on immigration during the Biden years is that, like, disorder was, like, the biggest problem. And, like, it’s very hard to manage, like, millions of people trying to come into the country.

But, like, you can do better and worse jobs of it, as you can witness mayors like the Denver mayor who was much more able to handle the inflows of migrants versus the Chicago mayor — which, for folks who are not geography heads, Denver is closer to the southern border than Chicago. And so was handling a much more difficult flow of people, but didn’t have the kind of, like, insane issues that Brandon Johnson did in Chicago of, you know, creating basically, like, a race war between existing African Americans who are upset about resources being taken away for migrants.

But my point is the Biden administration did not take seriously enough the need to be engaged in the resettlement process of migrants coming across the border. There’s reporting that’s done by, I believe, Reuters — we’ll put that in the show notes — that, like, they basically thought, the administration thought, that if they didn’t take ownership of resettling them, then they wouldn’t be blamed for where they ended up and how it ended up.

And I’m just, like, this is, to me, like, the most brain-dead thing on the planet because it’s just, like, obviously people are gonna blame you if you’re currently president of the United States no matter what happens.

And I don’t think you could have solved all of the problems, but, like, if you had spent more time getting resources to cities and states such that, like, when people were coming in, New York wasn’t having to, like, scramble to figure out where to put these fucking people, that actually would’ve made a massive difference in how the migrant crisis was experienced on the ground.

Obviously, I’m pro-letting in lots of immigrants. It’s just, you gotta do it in a way that doesn’t spur backlash.

Matthew Yglesias: My best article that I wrote in the Biden administration was at the beginning of March 2021. And I was saying, you know, I’d gotten this — people pitch me stuff sometimes.

Somebody at the White House was like, “We want you to write about how this huge surge of people at the southern border, like, isn’t our fault, that Republicans are lying about this.”

And I was like, OK, I mean, sure, it’s not your fault, whatever. Stuff happens, right? I mean, there’s a lot of external events in the world. But what do you want to have happen here? Like, what is your policy objective?

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah.

Matthew Yglesias: And it was like, bleh.

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah.

Matthew Yglesias: You know? Because they didn’t want to say, “We’re immigration restrictionists who don’t want these people to come. It’s not our fault and we are trying to stop it.” Because that would be too right-wing. And at the time, they were very focused on placating everyone. But they also obviously didn’t want to say, “Everybody is welcome.”

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah, open borders.

Matthew Yglesias: Because while it wasn’t “their fault,” quote unquote, it wasn’t, like, not not their fault, right? I mean, if they had just said, “Please, everybody come, like, we’re opening the floodgates,” like, even more people would’ve come and it would’ve looked bad. And they had no desire to — like, they didn’t actually want more people to come.

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah.

Matthew Yglesias: But they also didn’t want to say they didn’t want more people to come. They didn’t want to stop people from coming. They didn’t want to facilitate their arrival.

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah.

Matthew Yglesias: They were just annoyed that this was happening because they had these aforementioned economic policy ideas that they wanted to do. And so, it was like, “maybe the weather will change and people will stop coming.” You know, who knows?

Jerusalem Demsas: Maybe we’ll go into a recession and people will stop coming.

Matthew Yglesias: And then Greg Abbott out-politics-ed them.

Jerusalem Demsas: Yes.

Matthew Yglesias: And was like, “No, I will not let this just be some shit that I am dealing with. I am going to ratchet up the salience of this as high as possible.”

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah.

Matthew Yglesias: Send people around. You know, they could have said, “Right, OK, Abbott is sending people to all these different blue cities, but they have these very different characteristics,” right?

And so, you know, Brandon Johnson’s a little disorganized, but also, Chicago was just, like, just poorer, much poorer than Denver or New York. On the other hand, less expensive land and objective housing constraints, right?

Jerusalem Demsas: Lots of housing.

Matthew Yglesias: Chicago plus an influx of financial resources would arguably benefit from a lot of migrants coming in. New York, in theory, could, but you would need to —

Jerusalem Demsas: I mean, they figured it out. That’s what’s so crazy. People don’t — they’ve literally just, like — it stopped being a problem after a couple of months.

Matthew Yglesias: Well, it cost them incredible sums of money.

Jerusalem Demsas: Well, yes, but I just mean, like, it wasn’t, like, migrants living on the floors of Midtown.

Matthew Yglesias: Sure, sure, sure, sure, sure. But yeah, you know, I mean, New York has, like, big housing problems, land use constraints, etc., etc., etc. You could imagine a world in which an influx of asylum seekers, like, rebuild Chicago.

Jerusalem Demsas: Sure.

Matthew Yglesias: Right? But, you know…

Jerusalem Demsas: In fact, 60,000 Ukrainians came in and no one noticed.

Matthew Yglesias: You would have to try to do it. The other obvious one is that the American Rescue Plan was too big.

Jerusalem Demsas: This is another reason.

Matthew Yglesias: It caused inflation.

Jerusalem Demsas: OK, another reason for Joe Biden hating Joe Biden is that ARP was too big.

Matthew Yglesias: But nobody wants to say this.

Jerusalem Demsas: But I feel like the — like, isn’t the economics of this kind of like: It was a small impact relative to the larger forces causing inflation? Because, like, inflation happened all over the world. ARP, obviously, only in the United States.

There was — I should have looked this up before this podcast — but there was some paper that was estimating the amount of inflation that occurred as a result of the ARP, and it was, like, pretty modest, right?

Matthew Yglesias: Well…

Jerusalem Demsas: Wasn’t it? Am I misremembering?

Matthew Yglesias: Well, I’m gonna say two things about this. I will see people saying, “Oh, there’s all this research saying the ARP didn’t really spark inflation.”

Jerusalem Demsas: Well, it’s just, like, moderate.

Matthew Yglesias: Yeah, but you look at it, and they’re saying, “OK, well, you know, inflation went up to what, like, 8, 9%?”

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah.

Matthew Yglesias: And they’re saying, you know, maybe, like, 2 percentage points of that was ARP? But that’s the difference between winning and losing in 2024, I think, really, really clearly, right?

The other argument that people make is that we saw similar amounts of inflation globally, which is true. But the shock to natural gas prices induced by the Russia-Ukraine war was much more severe in Europe than it was in the United States because there’s physical constraints on how much liquefied natural gas can be exported from the U.S. to Europe. So, the fact that there was about the same amount of inflation is kind of a coincidence. The higher levels of fossil fuel production in the United States insulated us.

I’m not gonna, you know, argue for, like, Biden’s wishy-washy energy policies being, like, a top-tier policy error, but I think that this was a significant mistake.

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah.

Matthew Yglesias: The Biden administration went soft on enforcing oil sanctions against Russia.

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah.

Matthew Yglesias: Because they were concerned about the impact on the global economy, but they raised drilling fees in the United States and blocked oil infrastructure projects in Alaska.

Jerusalem Demsas: I mean, this is, like, a classic thing where it’s, like, easier to do your policy aims in foreign policy because there’s just, like, less congressional oversight and less, like, press oversight ‘cause no one gives a shit. And so you end up seeing, like, true preferences of elected officials in the foreign policy space.

Matthew Yglesias: But, I mean, I spoke to somebody at the NEC, and I asked them, I was like, “Why don’t you go easier on domestic drilling and harder on international sanctions enforcement? You get the same climate outcomes, a better economic outcome, a better foreign policy outcome.” And he told me the green groups don’t lobby on sanctions enforcement.

Jerusalem Demsas: Yes, exactly.

Matthew Yglesias: I’m like...

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah.

Matthew Yglesias: That’s, like, a really irresponsible way to run the government in my opinion.

Jerusalem Demsas: I agree. I want to return to ARP, though, because it seems like the best figure is probably around 3 percentage points, but that includes CARES, which I think you can’t blame on the Biden administration.

Matthew Yglesias: Sure.

Jerusalem Demsas: I’ll say this, I have a lot more empathy for the ARP mistake, in part because, like, every single — including myself, and I think most economists at the time — were basically saying “We do not want to repeat the mistakes of the Great Recession...

Matthew Yglesias: Yeah.

Jerusalem Demsas: …and understimulate the economy.”

Like, there was, I think, not as well understood a phenomenon that, like, you know, economists are like, “OK, yeah, unemployment’s bad, inflation’s bad. We’re not really sure which is worse. Unemployment seems really, really, really bad. Let’s really try to avoid that even if we get a little bit more inflation,” particularly because we’re coming from a context where we’d had really low inflation for a long time.

Everyone had kind of, like, memory-holed how bad inflation is, both politically and just, like, literally bad for people, it is. And also, like, it doesn’t actually make that much sense to economists who are like, “OK, like, your prices go up, but, like, your wages go up. So are you really that worse off?” And the American people were like, “Yes, this is the fucking worst thing that’s ever happened to me. I don’t care if my wages go up. My wages go up because I’m a good worker. Prices go up because you’re bad at the economy.”

Matthew Yglesias: Well, so…

Jerusalem Demsas: And so, like, you end up in a situation where I actually think, like, not realizing that this was the incorrect paradigm fast enough was an error. I think by the time we’re getting to 2023, like, they’re still a little bit too behind the curve doing the whole, like, “The economy’s actually doing really well guys.” Like that whole vibe is really bad.

But I’m not sure I would blame them for ARP. Were you saying that at the time, that ARP was stupid?

Matthew Yglesias: No, I endorsed ARP. I remember when Obama was president — I will violate anonymity because it just reflects well on him — I was arguing with Jason Furman, and I was like, “You guys should do more inflation.” And he was like, “We absolutely should not do more inflation.”

And I was like, “But unemployment will be lower, and yeah, prices will be higher, but wages will match up.” And he was like, “You young people do not understand how much the market would hate that.”

Jerusalem Demsas: Is Jason Furman older than you?

Matthew Yglesias: Yeah.

Jerusalem Demsas: OK.

Matthew Yglesias: Not like a lot, a lot older, but he was like, “No, people will get really fucking mad if that happens.”

Jerusalem Demsas: He was right, wow.

Matthew Yglesias: And I was like, “That’s stupid, that doesn’t make sense, blah, blah, blah.” He was completely right.

Jerusalem Demsas: Was Jason Furman against ARP?

Matthew Yglesias: Yes, he was.

Jerusalem Demsas: Wow.

Matthew Yglesias: Not a coincidence.

Jerusalem Demsas: How about that? Wow.

Matthew Yglesias: He wrote against it. He called me up on the phone. He was like, “Matt, you’re still wrong about this.” And he was completely right.

I struggle to blame them for ARP because there was a lot going on and a lot of considerations on the other side. I think it became clear quite quickly…

Jerusalem Demsas: How soon?

Matthew Yglesias: …that they were mistaken about this.

Jerusalem Demsas: You mean before 2023?

Matthew Yglesias: Yeah. Not saying they shouldn’t have admitted they were wrong, because nobody wants that. But there was no pivot, right?

I mean, this goes back to the left-wing economic staffing, right? That there was no, like, “Huh, maybe Jason and Larry were right about this.”

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah.

Matthew Yglesias: “Huh, maybe the decision to, like, stiff-arm traditional neoclassical economic policy advice is not working out that well.”

Jerusalem Demsas: Yes.

Matthew Yglesias: There was no — there was, like, an executive order to create, like, a whole-of-government antitrust crackdown. There wasn’t a revisiting of the concept that ditching low consumer prices as an enforcement goal for antitrust policy might be a mistake.

Jerusalem Demsas: Yep.

Matthew Yglesias: And there was definitely no, “We want every agency to run through the regulatory books and bring to us as many things they can find, as obscure as they might be, that will help bring prices down.”

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah.

Matthew Yglesias: And so what I’ve heard from people who tried to run exercises like that is that, you know, they weren’t allowed to propose anything to the White House that labor groups or environmental groups would get upset about. And so, that then meant you’re committing to not doing anything on cost of living, which was the number one issue.

So, we’ve been dwelling on left-wing staffing in the administration, left-wing policy choices. I want the defeat of Joe Biden and the discrediting of Bidenism to lead to, like, the dawning of abundance and sound return to economic practice.

Instead, it’s been seen as discrediting moderate Democrats. Leftists are running amok, now more so than ever before. And I think that’s clearly because in most people’s minds, the salient factional dispute in the Democratic Party right now is about Israel.

I’ve written “Gaza” here, right? But, like, far and away, the number one criticism of Joe Biden that is articulated by people, beyond being old, is that he was, like, complicit in a genocide done by Israel, and that we need to wash our hands of Bidenism, meaning the U.S.-Israel alliance, and that we need to turn to leftists who, in fact, were running Biden administration policy on, like, most issues that most people care about, and just, like, also give them control over the U.S. relationship with this tiny country in the Middle East.

Jerusalem Demsas: I don’t know, I think Biden did shit the bed on Israel. That’s pretty clear.

Matthew Yglesias: But, like, do you think that Biden was complicit in Israel undertaking a genocide?

I think that if I said — I think that if I, you know, in debate, whatever — if I said, like, “No, like, I think that’s, like, a weird slander,” then people would say, like, “No, fuck you. Like, you’re on the ash heap of history, along with everybody else.”

Jerusalem Demsas: I think what’s, like, really obvious is that really early on, Israel began acting in ways that were, like, genuinely horrifying. That it was, like, known that they were gonna have some sort of reaction to October 7th, obviously — an incursion that ended up killing and hurting, like, tons of people in Israel.

But, like, I just want to be clear here, like, once you were getting credible reporting and images coming into the United States of what they were doing to children, defenseless civilians, etc., it’s just, the entire posture of it being, like, “We’re still friends with Israel.” Like, it’s just insane to me.

Like, from the very beginning, like that — I mean, again, I was already more left-wing on this issue. So, like, that was…

Matthew Yglesias: Like, I don’t — ‘cause, like, really, like, I don’t understand this at all.

Jerusalem Demsas: Understand what?

Matthew Yglesias: This viewpoint, which I admit, like, I’m alone. I’m, like, lost on an island here. But this is, like…

Jerusalem Demsas: What do you mean? Like, I think — I mean, again, like, in general, like, if I’m witnessing another country in the world bombing civilians, I’m, like, upset when I notice the U.S is...

Matthew Yglesias: But what do you mean by bombing civilians? So, OK…

Jerusalem Demsas: What do you mean? People were dying — civilians.

Matthew Yglesias: There’s a dense urban environment.

Jerusalem Demsas: Yes.

Matthew Yglesias: Right?

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah.

Matthew Yglesias: There’s a war that is happening in the dense urban environment.

Jerusalem Demsas: Yes.

Matthew Yglesias: And there’s a policy by the government of Egypt, which is that, like, refugee outflows will not be permitted from this dense…

Jerusalem Demsas: I also think that’s bad.

Matthew Yglesias: I know, but — I know you think that’s bad because you’re, like, a reasonable person. But the conventional wisdom, right, throughout this war, on the left, to be, like, a good person on the left, right, is that, like, don’t say anything about Egyptian policy.

Jerusalem Demsas: But I did. That’s why you’re talking with me.

Matthew Yglesias: Sure, no, no, but I’m just saying, like, what’s going on here, right? That, like — and if Israel was to advocate for this, that would be, like, even worse, right? Now they’re doing double genocide.

Jerusalem Demsas: I just think that at the front, like, the person who has to justify the value and efficacy of a bombing campaign that will kill tens of thousands of people is the person doing the bombing campaign. Like, yes, what Hamas did is horrible, they’re a horrible terrorist organization who, like, did this horrible thing on October 7th.

But then Israel has to go, “OK, like, what is the value of us doing this, like, mass bombing campaign on Gaza? Is that going to lead to less terrorism? Is that going to lead to a geopolitical situation that will be safer for Israelis and Jews?”

Like, I think clearly not. I think it’s clearly gone worse for everyone except for the most right-wing parts of Israel. And now, as Trump has betrayed Netanyahu with Iran, it’s, like, not even close.

Matthew Yglesias: I’m not talking about Iran, though, right? I mean, because, again, like, the Biden administration did not give Netanyahu what he wanted on Iran.

Jerusalem Demsas: Yes.

Matthew Yglesias: OK, so I want to be clear about this because I think the war with Iran has rightly made people more critical of Israel than they were before.

But the Biden administration was preventing that from happening, right? And there’s a view of Biden that he was just, like, Netanyahu’s poodle and was doing everything that he wanted, but he very specifically resisted pressure to go do this.

So, I think we’re left with the question of, like, what — where does the moral culpability lie for this? And I know, like, I’ve lost the argument and will be run out of town on a rail if I say this, but, like, it’s Hamas’ fault. Like, they could have surrendered at any time and ended this war.

And I think that people who are motivated, right, if you try to think about it, like, in a rational way, like, if your concern was with the human welfare of the people of Gaza, you should be, like, concerned that the people who created this military conflict were insisting on fighting to the last civilian. And not taking this view that it’s, like, outrageous that Israel would fight back in a defensive war.

Jerusalem Demsas: I think that you’re getting too — I think that your focus is so much on, like, these, like, very unrepresentative activists that you’re seeing online or etc. But, like, I think the majority opinion from, like, most people who are not super involved in politics is that it’s very, very bad to witness a government killing children. And, like, when you see images like that, that’s really bad and that you should not want that to happen.

And then you have Joe Biden getting up there and saying, “Hey, Israel is a state that has been America’s partner for a long time, and we’re going to stand with them.” And, like, obviously he’s doing — I’m not, like, denying that there was this pressure campaign behind the scenes to push Netanyahu to do different things. But, like, A), like, why is your public posture this way about a government that’s enacting these kinds of policies?

Matthew Yglesias: I just think they’re fighting a war. Like, I just, like I don’t…

Jerusalem Demsas: I think war’s bad. Do you think — I mean, like, seriously, I don’t understand. Like, I think it’s bad when people are fighting wars, especially ones that have, like, no clear endgame that is going to actually lead to long-standing peace.

Like, there’s no — at no point did Netanyahu articulate an actual strategy for, like, ending the war in a way that would lead to peace. Like, that’s not what he’s doing.

Matthew Yglesias: Yes, I agree with that. But, like, Hamas also didn’t.

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah. And Hamas is an awful terrorist organization.

Matthew Yglesias: I mean, I guess, but I mean, I just, like, to me, like, there is a war. Like, a long-standing war that has been going on here that has two sides to it.

Jerusalem Demsas: Yes.

Matthew Yglesias: And where, like, now, the progressive view, to be, like, in good standing in the Democratic Party, is to just: Be on Hamas’ side.

Jerusalem Demsas: I do not think that is true. I think there are some people…

Matthew Yglesias: That’s how it reads to me. And not, I mean, not, like, obscure activists, because, like…

Jerusalem Demsas: Like, Hasan Piker is obviously, right? Like, he’s made this controversial claim.

Matthew Yglesias: But, like, who’s winning all these DSA primaries everywhere? Like, this is what’s happening. Like, this is what…

Jerusalem Demsas: That is true. But I just think, again, like, you’re too focused on this, like, one — like, I’m not saying they’re not, like, powerful or whatever, but, like, I’m saying, like, most people who are opposed to Israel are not, like, “I like Hamas.” Like, I don’t know what the polling on Hamas is, but I think if you were to, like, ask the American public, like, “How do you feel about Israel?”

Matthew Yglesias: Well, I understand that that’s not their subjective view. By the same token, literally everyone who is pro-Israel is not, like, “I enjoy killing Palestinian children.”

Jerusalem Demsas: Sure.

Matthew Yglesias: Right? But Israel’s critics pin them with the deaths of Palestinian children because that is what is occurring.

Jerusalem Demsas: I think that nation-states have…

Matthew Yglesias: Regardless of whether or not that’s their subjective goal here. And so, by the same token, right, the people whose reaction to October 7th is to do anti-Israel protests the next day because, you know, the counterattack is too brutal — like, they are just— they are taking sides in a conflict.

Jerusalem Demsas: Yes.

Matthew Yglesias: Right? And, like, the critique of the Biden administration is that it was wrong to take Israel’s side in the conflict, that they should have taken the view that it’s illegitimate for Israel to fight back.

Jerusalem Demsas: I think that, like, this — the big problem for Biden is that, obviously, the conflict did not begin during the Biden administration. Like, this is, like, a long-standing conflict between Israel and Palestine, the Middle East, like, for decades and decades. And so, like, obviously you’re, like, now we’re starting this — the clock — on October 7th, and that’s, like, the situation that Biden had to deal with.

But I think that the idea that nation-states should be held to a different standard than, like, terrorist actors is, like, not insane to me. Like, I think when a terrorist actor does something terrible, I’m, like, “Yeah, that’s a terrorist actor that we’re trying to eradicate that does not have the access to international banking, that does not have access to buy weapons from the United States government or any government. Like, that is part of an illicit web of, you know, bad actors that we actively try to sanction, that we actively try to find through our, you know, various DOD arms.”

And, like, that’s different than Israel where it’s, like, yeah, you are a nation-state that is ostensibly supposed to be a democracy that is currently allowing — not even with Gaza — like, it’s currently allowing members of your country to, like, incur into the West Bank and, like, steal people’s land and beat them. And it’s just, like, you’re already behaving in ways that are far beyond what we allow liberal Western democracies in good standing to behave.

And then on top of that, you’re now going to start a war that’s going to create political problems with the United States where you have no endgame. And I’m just, like, why would I be on your side?

Matthew Yglesias: I’m so torn about this. Because, like, I feel so critical of Israel.

Jerusalem Demsas: Yes, I know.

Matthew Yglesias: And yet, I feel that what is standard conventional wisdom — like, entry stakes into progressive politics at this point — is, like, so over-the-top hysterical that, like, I just don’t know. Like, I don’t know where I’m left. But, like, words mean things, right? And, like, you keep dodging this, but just, like, the discourse of the day is genocide.

Jerusalem Demsas: Mm-hmm.

Matthew Yglesias: Right? And that is a weighty concept, right? Like, legally speaking. And the goals of the boycott, divestment, sanctions movement, which, again, this is becoming quite mainstream. Lots of Democratic Party elected officials, and increasingly so.

There’s primaries happening in House races pitting totally down-the-line orthodox progressives against also-down-the-line orthodox progressives who also want to sanction Israel, where that’s, like, the only issue. And it’s, like, really, really big.

And I just, like, I don’t know. You know, I’m, like, I’m trying to, like, rank Biden flaws here, right? And, like, I don’t want to say that their Israel policy worked out great because it obviously didn’t, right? It didn’t achieve, like, any of its own goals.

Like, a lot of people died and it ended completely unsatisfactorily. And yet, I just feel like a wildly overblown criticism has become conventional wisdom while we’re, like, “Oh, remember inflation that destroyed Western democracy? Like, eh, should get a pass on that one because…”

Jerusalem Demsas: I don’t think that’s a fair read of what’s — I think most people, A), even the entirety of the DSA left is, like, “We only talk about cost of living now. Mamdani has shown us the way. We talk about cost of living now” because, like, everyone recognizes that’s, like, a number one issue.

And, like, secondly, like, to be clear, like, even abstracting away from, like, the literal policy merits and what actually happened in Israel and the Middle East and U.S. policy, etc., like, on the politics, that’s also been proven correct. Like, that being anti-Israel is not actually the anchor around the weight of candidates as it was expected to be.

Matthew Yglesias: I think we see this all the time online, with human beings, right? Just, like, individual people. And it’s, like, when people decide that a given person is, like, one of the bad guys, they then — because there’s a lot of people out there, right? — some of the people criticizing the bad guy will be criticizing the bad guy in terms that are not factually accurate.

Jerusalem Demsas: Yes.

Matthew Yglesias: Right? And then, because that person is the bad guy, the people who agree that that person is the bad guy don’t want to, say, step up and correct the other people. And we saw this, like, so much of what…

Jerusalem Demsas: I mean, this is the problem with populism. This happens with…

Matthew Yglesias: Yes. And cancel culture is a very vague term. But, to me, that was sort of the essence of it, was like, if we have decided that so-and-so is a bad person, then you should not stand up for that person against any kind of criticism, whether the criticism is accurate or not.

And I feel — and it wounds me as a Jewish person — that, like, Israel has become the, like, social media’s main character, right? Where, like, because Bibi Netanyahu is, like, a bad guy, and because the sort of post-2009 trajectory of Israeli handling of the Palestinian issue is not really defensible, that it’s like anything you say about Israel is fair game. And nobody is supposed to stand up for them in any kind of way. And it’s weird nitpicking to actually care about the specific accuracy of claims that are being made.

And then this has then metastasized into intra-Democratic Party factional politics because all this shit we were talking about before, progressives don’t want to acknowledge, right? So it’s all getting piled in to this idea that there was some, like, really obvious better way to resolve the Gaza War, which, like, I don’t think stands up to a ton of scrutiny. Like, what were you gonna do that was going to lead to a happy outcome of this?

Like, this is a classical difficult policy problem that has befuddled people for many generations because there’s a lot of supernatural considerations in play over control of the Holy Land. And…

Jerusalem Demsas: Supernatural considerations?

Matthew Yglesias: Sure. And I think it’s, like —well, I mean, I do. I think supernatural considerations.

Jerusalem Demsas: You mean religious considerations?

Matthew Yglesias: Yeah. Like, I think Chuck Schumer and Zohran Mamdani, two nice, secular New Yorkers, could probably, like, sit down and, like, just draw lines on a map in, like, all kinds of different ways and be like, “This is fine,” right? Just as, like, Jews and Muslims coexist, like, quite well in the United States of America. But they’re, like, fighting over the Holy Land in a way that’s, like, not that amenable to compromise.

We’ve, like, veered off topic of Joe Biden here.

Jerusalem Demsas: We’re enacting a microcosm of the broader problem in which Israel takes over the entirety of the conversation.

Matthew Yglesias: I just — I think that a lot of the policy problems of the Biden administration had, like, relatively straightforward fixes.

Jerusalem Demsas: Yes.

Matthew Yglesias: And that this one is, like, actually hard.

Jerusalem Demsas: I don’t think that anyone believes that there’s, like, a straightforward fix to the problem of Gaza and Israel and the West Bank. People don’t want to be on the side of the bad guy, right? It’s almost, like, exactly what you were saying.

Like, it is, like, really bad to, like, witness a country bombing another country into mud. And then, like, TikTok is taking off during the 2020s. And so you get, like, visceral videos all the time of — we didn’t talk about the TikTok ban. That’s another reason…

Matthew Yglesias: Oh yeah.

Jerusalem Demsas: …to be pissed off at Joe Biden.

But visceral videos all the time of, like, children and women and families and civilians getting their homes bombed to the ground. And I think my point here is just that, like, I actually think there was really low levels of effort by the Biden administration to distinguish the fact that they were not on that side, that they could have taken, to make people feel like, “OK, I now have faith that you were making diplomatic efforts to actually help the people of Gaza not be in this situation.”

And instead, it was, like, “We’re wrapping our arms around Bibi, we’re not doing…” Like, that, to me was, like, a massive tactical political error regardless of what was going on behind the scenes, and, like, made me feel less trusting of the Biden administration that they were actually taking the problems of Gaza seriously.

Matthew Yglesias: OK, so how high would you advocate for putting this?

Jerusalem Demsas: Israel on this list? I mean, I think that, like, number one to me is the economy.

Matthew Yglesias: OK.

Jerusalem Demsas: Number one. Where’s yours? Is that number one for you too?

Matthew Yglesias: Yes.

Jerusalem Demsas: Number two is him running again.

Matthew Yglesias: OK.

Jerusalem Demsas: I think I have to put Israel at number three just because it’s, like, massively destroyed, like, the Democratic Party, and also, like you said, has helped spur antisemitism...

Matthew Yglesias: Yeah.

Jerusalem Demsas: …across the entire country.

Maybe number four is not taking immigration seriously enough. Are you agreeing with all of these or is this just my list?

Matthew Yglesias: No, I’m just writing it down.

Jerusalem Demsas: Number five is gonna be Kamala.

Matthew Yglesias: OK.

Jerusalem Demsas: I feel like I’m just sort of listing them now. What else do I have here? I’ve forgotten them all.

Matthew Yglesias: So, we had energy stuff, we had the specifics of the rescue plan. I don’t know how you sort of parse all that out.

Jerusalem Demsas: ARP is, like, down number 10. I mean, I don’t even think I’ll put that on the list. Like, 10?

Matthew Yglesias: OK, so you think ARP is fine. But, so you’re thinking, like, economic staffing?

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah. I mean, basically, my view is like, I blame you for something where, like, if the method that you — if the pathway that got you to that solution was bad, I blame you for that solution.

But, like, ARP, I’m, like, you listened to experts and economists and tried to solve a problem, and then it didn’t turn out great? That’s just, like, life, you know what I mean?

Matthew Yglesias: Yeah, right. So, OK, I guess, like, I would go closer to what I think is, like, the mass electorate view of this.

Jerusalem Demsas: The what electorate?

Matthew Yglesias: Like, the electorate’s view — the mass public’s view of this, right? Like, that just, like, the economy was really bad. People were upset about that, right? So I think that the construction of the economic team.

I would put the immigration stuff number two, ‘cause, like, that was the other thing people were really mad about. I might even put the immigration stuff ahead of the economy just because it felt, at the time, quite idiosyncratic.

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah.

Matthew Yglesias: To me. I mean, this economic stuff I didn’t agree with, but there was, like, a lot of people pushing for that. So I don’t really know.

I would categorize the sort of identitarian staffing, like, more broadly than just Kamala Harris, but put it up there, you know, high at number three.

I do think, you know, we have to give some consideration to the rescue plan as, like, a serious error.

I mean, it’s true that a lot of people were for it. I was for it. I can’t be too down on it, but, like, really the top people from the prior Democratic administration were, like, warning them against this course of action. There was something happening with the legislative politics that didn’t quite make sense.

You know, I guess, like, yes. I mean, despite whatever my passion on this, I mean, I think that their handling of Gaza belongs on the list. It clearly did not work out the way that they wanted it to. They didn’t achieve what they were trying to achieve.

I guess I should say that I think that other aspects of the Biden foreign policy, like, actually did work out well and constitute the sort of main redeeming quality of the administration, actually.

Jerusalem Demsas: I mean, without Gaza, Biden’s foreign policy would’ve been considered a return to tradition.

Matthew Yglesias: Yeah, I mean, the defense of Ukraine worked well. I think the…

Jerusalem Demsas: They’re still going.

Matthew Yglesias: …handling of the sort of — handling of China was pretty good on the whole in terms of these things. So that’s another reason I go a little soft on the Gaza situation.

Jerusalem Demsas: What are our biggest — you wrote them down. So what are our biggest disagreements?

Matthew Yglesias: I don’t know. Are we disagreeing?

Jerusalem Demsas: No, I mean, like, on our lists.

Matthew Yglesias: Well, running again. I mean, you have that up as number two. You stand with the conventional wisdom. I think that that was good. That going through the tape, dropping out at the last minute.

Jerusalem Demsas: You got to write this take out. You gotta write “Biden was right to run again.”

Matthew Yglesias: I think I wrote it at the time.

Jerusalem Demsas: Did you really?I don’t think so. That would’ve been a…

Matthew Yglesias: But, you know, it was, like, you know, he dragged it out for, like, two weeks too long, or however many that was. And I feel bad for Andrew Bates who had to do some embarrassing tweets.

Jerusalem Demsas: And now is getting dragged by Jill Biden anyway.

Matthew Yglesias: Right. I mean, like, that’s…

And I think that their post-dropout behavior, which we didn’t really talk about that, I guess I would sub that in.

Jerusalem Demsas: Well, ‘cause they’re egomaniacs.

Matthew Yglesias: Yes. And so, some of this is, like, Jill Biden clapping back at Andrew Bates for being a little critical of their resurfacing. But also, you know, not just an ego — a smart, rational egomaniac would have said to Kamala Harris, “My legacy depends on you winning this election.”

Jerusalem Demsas: Yes.

Matthew Yglesias: “No matter what you say or do for the next 100 days, if you win, my legacy will be secure as the man who saved the country from Donald Trump, who saved the independence of Ukraine, who saved the Western alliance. Those are the things that I care about most — healing the soul of the country, restoring the faith of the West — and all of that requires you to win.”

**Jerusalem Demsas: “**So throw me under the bus.”

Matthew Yglesias: “You should say or do whatever you think it will take to do that. And I’m gonna get on the phone with Jen Dillon, with everybody there in Wilmington, and I am gonna have this exact same conversation with them. I will be really mad at you if you lose, but whatever it takes to win, go do that.”

And he didn’t do that, right? I mean, we don’t know, like, exactly what he said, but it is clear that, like, they felt that there would be downside to breaking with the Biden White House, that some kind of counter-messaging would come forth, that he really wanted her to run as his successor in a way that is just — like, it’s shortsighted.

You know what I mean? Because in the grand scheme of things, nobody’s going to care. What happened now is that because she lost, he failed.

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah.

Matthew Yglesias: And because he failed, nobody wants to fund his stupid fucking presidential library. And, like, but I think that that is a post-dropout problem more than a decision to run again.

Jerusalem Demsas: I do think that, like, most of my ranking stems from the idea that, like, I blame — blameworthiness to me is assigned based on, like, the process by which someone lands a decision.

So, like, to me, the process by which someone lands “that you cannot even bad-mouth me to win the presidency and save us from Donald Trump,” is, like, the same — this is the same process that lands on, like, “Let me run again.”

Like, it’s just, you are — to be president, you have to be somewhat of an egomaniac, so you can’t really blame someone for that. Like, that’s just kind of, like, what the whole situation is. But I just think that at some level here, the lack of long-term thinking was based in the fact that they were so sensitive to slights from the Obama years.

Like, all of that to me is just so central to understanding Biden’s entire — the economy, his hatred of economists, his hatred of wonks, his distaste for, like, Obama-type people, like, his team’s distaste for those kinds of people, that type of policymaking. It all stems from this whole, like, “people are being mean to the Bidens”-type of energy.

And everything that flows from that, I find so — I mean, I know this is not, like, rational of me, whatever — it’s, like, it’s so irritating that, like, you would put that above, like, “How many people am I helping? How many people’s lives am I saving?” It’s just, like, it’s insane to me.

Matthew Yglesias: I guess I would frame this a little bit differently, but, like, there’s a curious way — ‘cause I think everybody in big-time politics, every president, is a bit of an egomaniac. But it’s, and this is just, I think, like, the main thing that is misunderstood by the broad public about the Biden administration, is that I don’t think you could get a world in which Joe Biden is not an egomaniac, in which any president is not an egomaniac.

But the egomania that I wanted Biden to have is the one where he’s like…

Jerusalem Demsas: Long-term.

Matthew Yglesias: No, no, no, but not just long-term — I mean, there’s that at the end — but at the beginning, to be like, “Guys, I was too old. I do stutter. I’m not charismatic. The donors didn’t like me, and I beat all you motherfuckers anyway. And show me — like, yes, I want you all on the team, but I want everybody who backed Warren, everybody who backed fucking Pete, to explain to me why I beat you.”

You know what I mean? “Show me that you understand,” right? Because if you did that…

Jerusalem Demsas: You want LBJ-style egomania?

Matthew Yglesias: Well, no, but you know what I mean? Because the reason why he beat them was that continuity with Obama was popular, that beating Trump at any cost was popular, that cultural moderation was popular, that there was, in fact, no demand for this kind of change.

But, instead, Biden seems to have directed his egomania at a desire to get over on Obama.

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah.

Matthew Yglesias: Right? And to embrace the critics of continuity because that was going to show that he was even bigger, right? That he was, like, a bigger and better change agent than his predecessor, rather than that he was a smarter politician than all the people that he beat, right?

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah.

Matthew Yglesias: And, like, you know, so it’s true that there’s, like, then many different things happen. But I think that, like, the reckoning that we keep, like, not having in the Democratic Party was with this, like, the voters were like, “We want continuity with Obama.” And then the continuity-with-Obama guy was like, “Nope, we’re doing a hard break with Obamaism.” And then the voters hated that. And now we’re, like, still…

Jerusalem Demsas: Doing it again.

Matthew Yglesias: Yeah, we’re, like, going in circles. Let’s do a quick peer review. We’ve gone on for a long time.

Jerusalem Demsas: This one is a fun one. So, I actually talked to the coauthor of this paper for my old podcast at The Atlantic, but it’s called “Bachelors Without Bachelor’s“— great name— “Gender Gaps in Education and Declining Marriage Rates.”

So, there’s obviously this kind of, like, conventional wisdom, kind of probably best understood through JD Vance’s “childless cat ladies” comment, right? That, like, the people, the women, who aren’t getting married are these, you know, girlbosses getting their PhDs and degrees and living in the big city and, you know, 10 cats, and they’re too invested in their careers, they don’t care enough about family.

What these economists show is that, like, that’s actually just not correct.

Matthew Yglesias: Yeah.

Jerusalem Demsas: That the people who are not getting married — like, that college-educated women largely have seen their marriage rates stay pretty stable — that it’s non-college-educated women that have seen their rates of marriage decline. For non-college women, marriage rates have cratered from 78.7% to 52.4%.

That’s big. It seems largely driven by the fact that basically what’s happening is, like, the best-earning non-college-educated men are now marrying college-educated women, which, you could imagine this, this is, like, a nurse marrying a plumber, right? Like, nurses have to have a college degree, plumbers don’t.

So, like, that’s kind of that relationship we’re imagining here. And that means that, like, the non-college-educated men who would’ve been marrying non-college-educated women are, like, taken up.

Matthew Yglesias: Sure.

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah.

Matthew Yglesias: Yeah, I mean, there’s a great paper. It’s a good corrective to the sort of culture war framing of this. I wrote a piece, you know, about this.

Social conservatives who are concerned about the declining marriage really want to hector what they perceive of as — I mean, we did the Feminine Mystique episode, right?

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah.

Matthew Yglesias: And so, it’s like, the sound critique of what Betty Friedan is doing is that, like, she is very, like, monolithically focused on high-achieving, highly educated women. And so, social conservatives are, like, just doing the same thing in reverse, right? And they’re like, “No, no, no. All you people who read Feminine Mystique and, like, went to go become girlbosses, like, you need to be tradwives instead.”

But, like, the girlbosses are continuing to be married at a fairly high rate, just to the most high-achieving men.

Jerusalem Demsas: Yes.

Matthew Yglesias: Right? The thing that’s happening is that the least-educated, least-economically dynamic women who are potentially partnering up with, like, the most fucked-up, unimpressive men are opting out of marriage. And that’s just, like, a completely different — I think, like, a very genuine social problem, actually.

Jerusalem Demsas: Yes.

Matthew Yglesias: And, like, worthy of social conservative concern. And notably isn’t, like, was, like, the blind spot of second wave. It’s not, like, the fault of second wave feminism, but there was just so much discourse in there about, like, high-achieving people.

But all social problems, like, primarily accumulate at the bottom ends of the socioeconomic spectrum, right? And — but, like, people who read prestigious publications are very interested in, like, our own lives.

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah.

Matthew Yglesias: And we just, like, tend to neglect the problems of, you know, people of below-average earnings, below-average education, below-average intelligence, below-average conscientiousness. But, like, usually when bad things happen, that’s who suffers.

And so, we’re seeing both, like, you’re left without good marriage prospects, but also…

So, it’s understandable, you don’t want to marry bad partners. But, like, also, being a poor single mom is pretty bad. Dying alone is not great. It’s a bad situation. And we should think more about that.

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah. I mean, I think it’s another point in the whole — even if average marriages are better off than average people who remain single, like, it’s the marginal marriage seems potentially, like, not actually, like, good for the people involved.

So, basically, like, the paper looks at both employment and prison, and it finds that where non-college men have jobs, non-college women marry more. So it’s not just, like, they want people with degrees, it’s like, “Do you have a job?”

And the effect is pretty strong in the worst area. So in places where male employment is low, only 45% of non-college women are married. But in places where male employment is high, like, in the best places, like, 66% of non-college women are married. That’s, like massive, it’s like a 20-point difference.

Matthew Yglesias: Yeah, yeah.

Jerusalem Demsas: Huge. So, like, there’s, like, a clear pathway here. Like, jobs for, like, non-college-educated men seem, like, extremely important if your concern as a social conservative is to increase marriage rates. It’s like, do these men have jobs — good-paying jobs?

And also, like, prison — they look at this too. Places with less incarceration go with more marriage too. So it’s basically, like, you want people to not have gone to prison and to have a job.

Matthew Yglesias: Well, I think you could think about the causation there in different kinds of ways, right? Because, like, probably, you know, you can reduce your prison — your incarceration rate — by, like, letting all the murderers out of prison earlier. I’m not sure the guy who, like…

Jerusalem Demsas: I think that might actually work.

Matthew Yglesias: It might or might not.

Jerusalem Demsas: At, like, increasing marriage rates. It would have other problems, but…

Matthew Yglesias: Well, I mean, I don’t know. right.

Jerusalem Demsas: I mean, the men would literally be outside.

Matthew Yglesias: Yes, but I mean, I think that probably the guy who was, like, just imprisoned for three years for murder is not a highly desirable marriage prospect.

Yeah, you know, so I mean, I just think…

Jerusalem Demsas: Depends on your market.

Matthew Yglesias: You know, and again, with the unemployment, you know, it’s hard to say exactly what’s happening.

Jerusalem Demsas: Have you seen the reality TV show Love After Lockup?

Matthew Yglesias: I haven’t.

Jerusalem Demsas: So, there’s a whole…

Matthew Yglesias: I’m really missing out.

Jerusalem Demsas: There’s a whole market.

Matthew Yglesias: But yeah, I mean, the point is you have this kind of negatively selected population, right? That where outcomes for men are bad, women are not that interested in marrying men with bad outcomes, which has sort of always been the case. But as women’s economic prospects improve, there is less incentive to, like, settle for somebody who sucks, which is, like, that’s good.

Jerusalem Demsas: I mean, I think the social conservative position here is something that, I think, often is unstated, is that, like, marriage has sometimes been the mechanism or the institution by which men have stopped sucking in these ways that we’re talking about.

And so, like, they’re like — I think sometimes that the actual policy aim is that, like, “OK, if women will just marry some of these subpar men, then they won’t be as subpar anymore.”

And I think that that’s actually what’s going on here. ‘Cause I think your model of this is, like, “Oh, they’re super confused. Why are they focused on the girlboss instead of focused on the fact that women are marrying men with jobs and that have all these characteristics that are positive?”

I think it’s ‘cause they just want them to do that.

Matthew Yglesias: I have maybe a darker interpretation of that, but we’ll leave it for the audience.

Jerusalem Demsas: Go ahead. No, no, that’s a tease.

Matthew Yglesias: No, no, no, I mean, I think that just as Betty Friedan is aware that she’s talking about a minority of the population, but she just cares a lot about those outcomes, I just think cultural conservatives are, like, authentically want to reverse these elite phenomena.

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah.

Matthew Yglesias: And they’re marshaling social statistics that are only tangentially related. I mean, just as, again, remember Betty Friedan with her soft Korean War POWs, right? When people are trying to win these culture war arguments, they…

Jerusalem Demsas: say crazy things, yeah.

Matthew Yglesias: They tend to reach, right, for, like, more ammunition because it sounds petty to, like, admit how obsessed everyone is with a small number of highly educated people.

Linda Hirshman, I always give credit to for being, like, the one person who will say, “I actually care a lot about elite outcomes because it sets the tone for all of culture and society.” But, like, that’s, like, a bad move in the discourse, to just own up to it. So I think we get a lot of…

Jerusalem Demsas: This is what I say on affirmative action and everyone hates it.

Matthew Yglesias: There you go. Yeah.

Jerusalem Demsas: OK, I think that’s a great place to close off. In a couple weeks we’re gonna be reading The Death and Life of Great American Cities by Jane Jacobs. So if you want to pick up that tome and read along with us, feel free.

We’ve gotten a lot of suggestions from people about books they want us to read. So if you have those, feel free to us on Substack or on Twitter and we’ll get back to you if that’s a book we’re gonna pick.

But, as always, like, comment, subscribe, tell people about the podcast. We’re growing and we’re trying to meet new people.

Matthew Yglesias: Bye.

↩ Back to article summary · ↪ See Claude's response

What is Gavin Newsom doing?

Silver Bulletin · 2026-06-23 · Original →

Newsom campaigning for the Biden-Harris ticket in Michigan in 2024. Getty Images.

Following the successful passage of his redistricting referendum in California last November, California Gov. Gavin Newsom began the year as the frontrunner for the 2028 Democratic nomination. But polls suggest that he’s slumping. According to Race to the White House, Newsom’s support among prospective Democratic voters in 2028 has fallen from 25 percent in January to 15 percent today. That's a fairly big shift, given that not all that much has happened. The California gubernatorial primary was kind of a mess, with Newsom not endorsing a candidate until Xavier Becerra was the lone Democrat left standing. Still, that’s local news and this is a national primary. And redistricting has remained a big story, even if it ended on a slightly bitter note for Democrats.

But these early polls do have some predictive power, believe it or not. Prediction markets have tracked with them, with Newsom’s probability of winning the nomination declining from 35.5 percent on Jan. 1 to 23.3 percent today.

No one opponent has emerged as the obvious alternative. Bettors see the field as wide open, with the top six candidates having less than a 60 percent combined probability of winning the nomination. But it’s worth noting where the biggest gains have come from: Kamala Harris and Georgia Sen. Jon Ossoff. Each presents a challenge for Newsom. Harris, who might or might not be serious about running again, is from the same state, and it’s hard to find much daylight between her and Newsom on policy. And Ossoff, you could argue, mogs Newsom in the “good-looking white guy” category, only with youth on his side and the credential of actually having won elections in a swing state.

Newsom's odds have declined in prediction markets

Polymarket probability of winning 2028 Democratic nomination, Jan. 1 vs. June 23

If you ask me, though, the reason for Newsom’s decline is simple: this is reversion to the mean. He doesn’t have a particularly persuasive argument for being the nominee, and his early support may reflect name recognition as much as anything else.

Newsom is as “establishment” as it gets

As the governor of the nation’s largest state¹, Newsom is the logical “next-in-line” candidate for the establishment faction of the Democratic Party. He’s perhaps also the best-known Democrat who isn’t Harris or 80+ years old (Joe Biden, Bernie Sanders). But this comes at a time when that establishment is highly unpopular, including with Democratic voters.

Last year, Newsom made a few conspicuous moves toward a more “heterodox”, centrist side by inviting people like Charlie Kirk on his podcast and shifting to the right on transgender athletes. And in January, he announced his opposition to California’s proposed wealth tax that might appear on this November’s ballot.

These moves, although not necessarily popular with Democratic primary voters, might suggest a candidate trying to break out of the establishment box. But Newsom’s strategy has been a little contradictory. Who was one of his most recent podcast guests? Hunter Biden, the pardoned son of the former president.

Indeed, as Axios notes, Newsom has made a show of embracing Biden and his legacy — somewhat ironically given that Newsom essentially ran a shadow campaign against Biden in 2022. The Biden admiration isn’t new, however. Last October, Newsom described Biden as “one of the most successful presidents in the last century.”

We’ve written a lot about Biden and his legacy at Silver Bulletin, and I’ve urged Democrats to get over it and move past him. Newsom is conspicuously doing the opposite.

Because it’s not as though voters are warming to Biden’s legacy, as they sometimes do for former presidents. In fact, CNN just released a poll of favorability ratings for living presidents. Only 30 percent of voters had a favorable impression of Biden, actually lower than the 38 percent approval rating that Biden left office with. By comparison, Donald Trump had a 34 percent favorability rating in the poll, Bill Clinton was at 38 percent, George W. Bush was at 42, and Barack Obama is the only actually popular former president with a 57 percent positive rating.

As you might imagine, voters who do hold a favorable view of Biden are nearly all partisan Democrats. Among self-identified Democrats in the poll, Biden has a 71 percent favorability rating. But it’s just 51 percent among voters who merely lean toward the Democratic Party.² And it’s just 20 percent among independents — compared to 56 percent for Obama. (It probably hasn’t helped that the Biden family is constantly making news in the wrong way, by re-litigating the Democratic Party’s decision to push Biden aside.)

Only hard-core Democrats still like Biden

Favorability ratings, CNN/SSRS poll, May 7-31, 2026

True, every Democratic candidate is going to face difficult decisions on issues like trans rights or policing where the median Democratic primary voter is well to the left of the median general election voter. Biden is a strange hill to die on, however. A 71 percent favorability rating is far from unanimous support even among Democrats. It’s not like Biden is particularly well-liked by the left, either. And if 2028 is a “change election”, like most elections are, it’s hard to see why you’d embrace an 83-year-old whose legacy voters just rejected in 2024 when his vice president went head-to-head against Trump.

As an aside, I’d note how popular Obama remains with Democrats — a 96 percent favorability rating! Now and then, I’ll see some contrarian take from a self-described liberal on Twitter or Bluesky claiming that the base wants to move beyond Obama. But that’s deeply out of touch with the polling. Obama is basically the only Democrat who still unifies the various factions of the party, including younger voters and more left-wing ones. Indeed, Obama has his fair share of imitators, including Ossoff and Pennsylvania Gov. Josh Shapiro, who can mimic his cadence.

So it’s at least a little bit weird that Newsom describes Biden as “one of the most successful presidents in the last century” when far more Democrats would describe Obama that way. In the CNN poll, 30 percent of voters listed Obama as the president they admire most; just 1 percent of respondents said that of Biden.

But let’s get back to the question posed by the headline.

Newsom is running as a factional candidate of the #Resistance Libs

In my piece on Heather Cox Richardson, I identified three main factions of the Democratic Party. First, The Left, who have candidates they prefer to Newsom like Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez. Second, the more centrist-y Abundance Libs. Newsom has been friendly with them at times, going on Abundance co-author Ezra Klein’s podcast for instance. But they tend to have a lot of critiques both of Newsom’s performance in office — Abundance is basically a book about the failures of governance in California — and his electability.

Last but by no means least — they’re possibly the largest faction — are the #Resistance Libs. They’re progressive but usually not “far left”. Demographically (like Richardson), they’re older, college-educated and female-leaning. But they tend to really root for the blue team. They’re less ideological than they are partisan, a distinction that sometimes gets lost. They’re the group that tends to feel Biden was unfairly railroaded out because of what they think of as overblown media fixation on Biden’s age.

Although Biden isn’t popular with pretty much anyone — even among Black voters, only 53 percent have a favorable view of him — you can find some echoes of the #Resistance Lib constituency in the CNN poll on Biden. He’s relatively more popular with women than men, with voters aged 45 or older, and with college-educated voters who frequently consume political news, all #Resistance Lib hallmarks. (Note that the numbers in this table are among all voters, not just Democrats.)

Biden's comparative strengths are with older, college-educated women

Favorability ratings, CNN/SSRS poll, May 7-31, 2026

Meanwhile, while none of the factions were happy about 2024, they each have different theories of the case for how Democrats can get a better result in 2028. The Left thinks the party should move, well, left, especially on distributional and economic issues. The Abundance Libs think the party should tone down culture war issues that poll poorly and prove that they can govern more effectively.

And the #Resistance Libs? They basically deny that Democrats have a substantive problem at all. Instead, their leading theories of 2024 are that Democrats lost because 1) they didn’t fight hard enough, both against Trump and against unfair media coverage and 2) because Kamala Harris was a Black woman.

Newsom resolves each of these concerns, although the white guy part is left unstated. HE’S A FIGHTER WHO TWEETS IN ALL CAPS LIKE TRUMP. AND HE’S A WINNER.

To be clear, Newsom’s redistricting play was both substantive and smart, and he deserves a lot of credit for it. Also to be clear, the Republican Party is likely to have a hell of a mess on its hands in choosing its nominee in 2028 and will face massive headwinds unless Trump magically becomes more popular in the second half of his lame duck term. Newsom can win and is probably no worse than a toss-up against JD Vance or whomever.

But Newsom’s electability argument is weak. Polls find that Democratic voters see Newsom as highly “electable”, but his margins in California have been underwhelming, and the Golden State is the furthest thing from a swing state. True, I don’t expect primary voters to be making Wins Above Replacement calculations, but candidates like Ossoff (and Shapiro, depending on how his re-election goes) obviously have stronger arguments in the electability department by virtue of over-performing in swing states.

So my theory is that Newsom’s noisy support for Biden is intended as a signal to the most partisan #Resistance Libs — as an effort at reassuring them that they were right about 2024.

The most effective signals are often costly signals, however. Most voters are extremely skeptical about Biden’s performance in office. The better strategy is probably to invoke some nostalgia for the one Democratic president (Obama) that voters actually do have warm feelings for, while also promising change, generational and otherwise. But someone like Ossoff is in a stronger position to make this case than Newsom.

Maybe Newsom realizes this: that he’s never going to effectively rebrand himself as anything other than a capital-E Establishment Democrat, so he might as well embrace it. It might not be the worst choice as a matter of tactics. But it’s also a sign of weakness. Newsom is never going to be the favorite candidate of the Left (especially after opposing the wealth tax) or the Abundance Libs. But if he can hold the #Resistance Libs, he’ll have a high floor in the Democrats’ highly proportional primary system.

What happens from there? Maybe the same thing that happened for Biden in 2020 and John Kerry in 2004. The rest of the party rallies around the early frontrunner as the “least-worst” choice after an early primary win or two, fleeing to safety when presented with alternatives that seem equally problematic. Newsom has more raw political talent than Kerry or Biden, frankly. And it’s a formula that has worked before in the Democratic primaries — but also one that tends to produce highly triangulating and thoroughly mediocre general election candidates.

1

And, indeed as the architect of a series of policies that have caused voters to leave California.

2

In the CNN poll, the “lean Democrat” column in the crosstabs included both capital-D Democrats and independents who said they lean toward the Democratic Party. I was able to estimate the numbers for the “lean Democrat” group alone through extrapolation based on the sample sizes of each group.
